Sender: news@digitalconcerthall.com
Date: 2026-01-16 16:48:30+00:00
Subject: =?utf-8?Q?Live:_Kirill_Petrenko_conducts_Mahler?=
  =?utf-8?Q?=e2=80=99s_Eighth_Symphony?=

Sat, 17 January 2026, 19:00 (Berlin)


“Symphony means constructing a world with all the technical means at one’s disposal.” Nowhere did Gustav Mahler realise this credo more impressively than in his Eighth Symphony. With eight soloists, three choirs and a gigantic orchestra, it goes beyond anything that came before. At the same time, Mahler brings together fundamental ideas of intellectual history in this profusion of sound – from medieval hymns to the conclusion of Goethe’s Faust. After 15 years, the Berliner Philharmoniker are bringing the work back to the concert stage under the baton of Kirill Petrenko.

Live

Sat, 17 Jan 2026, 19:00 (Berlin)

Sat, 17 Jan 2026, 13:00 (New York)

Sun, 18 Jan 2026, 03:00 (Tokyo)


Repeats

Sun, 18 Jan 2026, 12:00 (Berlin)

Sun, 18 Jan 2026, 06:00 (New York)

Sun, 18 Jan 2026, 20:00 (Tokyo)


Exclusive insights: “Upbeat” – Gustav Mahler’s Symphony No. 8

Principal oboe Albrecht Mayer presents his personal view of Mahler’s Eighth, and we also gain an insight into the rehearsal work of chief conductor Kirill Petrenko.


Petr Popelka successfully made the leap from double bass to the conductor’s desk, and his debut with the Berliner Philharmoniker was met with rapturous applause. The concert began with Dvořák’s sombre, fairy-tale-like Wood Dove, a work from Popelka’s homeland, Czechia. Soloist Gil Shaham then gave a powerful performance of Alban Berg’s Violin Concerto. In Schumann’s Spring Symphony, which concluded the programme, “the conductor kept the excitement level high, teased out dynamic subtleties and really threw himself headlong into this music. The sound blossomed and bloomed in every note!” (Tagesspiegel).


Goethe’s Faust fascinated not only Mahler, but entire generations of artists. Among them was Robert Schumann, who used the material as the basis for a work that shifts captivatingly between incidental music, cantata and secular oratorio. Daniel Harding presented Scenes from Goethe’s Faust with the Philharmoniker and the Rundfunkchor Berlin – Christian Gerhaher sang the title role “devilishly well” (Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung).


Welcome the Berliner Philharmoniker into your home – on your TV, computer, tablet or smartphone. Live or on-demand from our archive.

                                                Digital Concert Hall vouchers


The Digital Concert Hall is the online concert venue of the Berliner Philharmoniker. Here, their concerts are broadcast live and are also available as recordings in a video archive a few days later. The Digital Concert Hall is available on televisions, computers, smartphones, tablets and streaming devices. Its streaming partner is Internet Initiative Japan Inc. (IIJ).

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-17 15:24:56+00:00
Subject: Noble Lesbian Savage Fights For Humanity

Excerpts from my piece:
When people talk about totalitarianism, they think of gulags, George Orwell, secret police knocking on the door at night, and coercion, usually under threat of pain or death, to surrender one’s liberty.
Huxley’s novel begins when one Mustapha Mond, who controls Western Europe for the global government, hears of one John the Savage, a white man raised on an Indian reservation in the American Southwest. Brought back to London, the Savage, whose mind was formed on the collected works of William Shakespeare, inexplicably refuses to join the joyful collective, which is like an endless Carnival Cruise, without the mass brawls.
You all know what happens next. The Savage declines to join the perfect society, where everyone is cared for and peace reigns, because he believes that suffering, including the right to be unhappy, is key to being free, key to being human. More from my piece:
The acclaimed show, which wrapped up its inaugural run last month on Apple TV, and has become the most-watched show in the streamer’s history, updates Huxley in the same geographical setting where the Savage grew up: the Southwest. In so doing, Pluribus makes the philosophical themes running through the nearly century-old novel graspable—and unsettling—for a contemporary audience.
Carol, who thinks she’s the only one in the world who is both immune to the virus and willing to fight for humanity’s right to be unhappy (thus free), discovers she has a confederate — a tough Catholic man from South America:
The real Savage emerges later in the Pluribus narrative: Manousos Oviedo (Carlos-Manuel Vesga), a Colombian living in Paraguay, who has from the beginning refused all contact with both the Others and the immune community, declining Carol’s outreach. Without giving away too much of the plot, let’s say that Manousos suffers intensely at one point, and is taken by the Others to a hospital for treatment. When he leaves, the medical Others smile and tell him his treatment was free. Furious, Manousos angrily writes an IOU, declaring, “Everything has a cost!”
There’s many kinds of Catholics, but I grew up with the kind of Catholic that accepts that life is hardship and suffering and strife, and in a way, that makes it worth living. I don’t think he approaches suffering and strife in the same way as other people who haven’t had that Catholic thing given to them from birth. It’s a way of seeing life in which you go, This is hard, but life is hard.
John the Savage had Shakespeare; Manousos Oviedo has the catechism as a way of life.
I’ll quote no more from the piece, except to say that it goes on to talk about its particular relevance to our time — an era and a culture in which people are willing to surrender the liberty and their humanity to be relieved from discomfort and anxiety. 
By the way, the copy editor changed my reference to the “noble Savage” to “ignoble Savage” — which is the opposite of what I intended. I’ve asked for a correction in the copy online.
A word about the comments section in yesterday’s newsletter. Apparently more than a few of you are angry at me for having questions and concerns about the way ICE is doing its job. I have long believed, and said here, in deportation. I broadly support what Trump is doing, and I certainly despise the way these activists are going about their protests. But some of you have decided that I’m now some sort of liberal because I raised concerns about SOME of ICE’s tactics — even though I cited polls showing that a majority of Americans are against what ICE is doing.
Gang, come on. This is the kind of purity spiral we associate with the woke Left. If we want the goal of deporting as many illegals as possible to succeed, then we cannot afford to lose the public on this — and we are losing the public. We have to be able to self-criticize, and self-correct, to reach our goals. If we can’t, we are going to find our heads handed to us in the next election — just as the Democratic Party’s enslavement to the demands of the puritanical activist Left helped cost them the last election. 
Beware purity spirals! To be a conservative is to try to live in the real world. I’m pro-life, as you know, but during the last right-wing Polish government, a conservative Catholic judge made a pro-life ruling regarded as extreme by many Poles. It brought huge protests into the streets, and likely caused that government to be voted out in a close election. Now the country is governed by the left-wing Eurocrat Donald Tusk. 
There’s a very good chance the Republicans are going to lose the House in the midterms; I’m not sure about the Senate. If so, it will likely because of the economy, not ICE raids. But our side can’t afford to lose votes. Moral considerations aside, if you want the mass deportations to continue, then you need to be concerned about how these raids are going down, and how our side is losing the propaganda war. In a democracy, it is not enough to be right; you also have to be persuasive.
Anyway, in this comments thread, I’d rather talk about Pluribus. If you haven’t watched it, spend the weekend binging — it’s a bit slow, but it builds. Carol and Manousos are such terrific characters, and the final episode of Season 1 has one hell of an ending.

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-18 02:23:21+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=94=AE_Exponential_View_#557:_Starlink,_Iran_&_abundance;?=
 =?UTF-8?q?_AI=E2=80=99s_exploration_deficit;_AGI_vibes,_aliens,_Merge_Lab?=
 =?UTF-8?q?s_&_re=E2=80=A6?=

“I love the sharpness of your insights and perspectives.” — Satya C., a paying member
Good morning from Davos. I got into Switzerland yesterday for a week of meetings and, if past years are any guide, a lot of conversations about AI. As in previous years, I’ll be checking in live most days from the Forum to share what I’m hearing behind the scenes.
And now, let’s get into today’s weekend briefing… 
An antidote to forced scarcity
In both cases – authoritarian or merely dysfunctional – centralized infrastructure created demand for decentralized workarounds: solar panels in Lahore and Starlink dishes in Tehran. In corrupt or dysfunctional systems, power usually flows from dependency, not from legitimacy. Control the grid, control information, control money and compliance follows. When hardware breaks that dependency, only legitimacy remains – and these states have little. Energy, intelligence and coordination underpin growth; two of the three are now slipping beyong centralized control.
Escaping the explore-exploit trap
My own experience hits a related wall. I’ve used AI tools extensively for research on my new book; it’s excellent at spotting cross‑field patterns. But safety theatre has hobbled them. When I test plausible tech scenarios with well‑understood trends, models smother answers in caveats for every stakeholder — permitting bodies, developing economies, future generations, and so on. The labs bake this in during post-training through reinforcement learning, to optimize for hedge and cover. That’s the opposite of exploration. So we have tools that synthesise brilliantly, if bureaucratically, but flinch from the leaps that research needs.
Feel the AGI yet?
The AI applications of 2026 and 2027 will be doers. They will feel like colleagues. Usage will go from a few times a day to all-day, every day, with multiple instances running in parallel. Users won’t save a few hours here and there – they’ll go from working as an IC to managing a team of agents.
By that standard, long-horizon agents such as Claude Code would already qualify. The models provide the brain and the scaffolding – memory, tools and decision-making – lets them act. Together, they can figure out a great deal.
As long-time readers know, I think the term “AGI” is too blurry to be meaningful, let alone carry much practical weight. Unburdened by that term, Sequoia is pointing out something many of us feel. We can expand our cognitive capacity for mere dollars a day. It feels like a watershed.

Subject: Weimar America & A Smoldering World

Good morning from London Heathrow. Logged on to see what’s happening in the world, and wondered if it was too early to start drinking.
Given the heinous crimes they were about to commit, there is a tendency to think that the members of the Einsatzgruppen were all bloodthirsty sadists. It’s a comfortable way of thinking about the history, but it’s wrong. Whatever else motivated these killers, it wasn’t mass psychopathy. Two of the four commanders of the Einsatzgruppen, Arthur Nebe, head of the Criminal Police, and Walter Stahlecker, who had led security forces in Norway, volunteered for the job.9 The others were appointed, including the intellectual Otto Ohlendorf of the SD. He wasn’t the only cerebral commander within the ranks of the killing squads. In Einsatzgruppe A, eleven of the seventeen most senior leaders were lawyers; nine held academic doctorates.
The point is, people can be psychologically motivated to commit unspeakable atrocities. The entire book is about how the National Socialists achieved that. Thus:
Alfred Metzner, a driver for one of the Nazi commanders [in Poland], remembered that “pregnant women were shot in the belly for fun and then thrown into the pits… . And Walter Mattner, an officer charged with organizing a ghetto clearance, recounted his actions to his wife in October 1941: “I aimed calmly and shot with confidence at the women, children and numerous babies… The babies flew in great arcs and we shot them to pieces in the air before they fell into the ditch and the water … Oh, Devil take it! I’d never seen so much blood, filth, flesh. Now I understand the expression ‘blood-drunk’.”
Many such cases. 
Sat down in the lounge to spend the day waiting for my evening flight to Budapest. Opened X and saw this. Click on it to see the video:
Here they are at the club, doing same (that’s Andrew Tate’s brother Tristan, I believe):
“Hitler was f—king cool,” Fuentes has said. You know, the man ultimately responsible for his agents throwing Jewish and Polish babies into the air and shooting them to shreds before they hit the ground.
There is not enough irony in the world to legitimize what these scumbags in Miami did. What Fuentes and his growing group of followers among the influencers are doing is conditioning people to see Jews as subhuman. It’s more than a little strange that black people like Kanye West, Sneako, and Tristan Tate participate in this, as it was the same rationale that allowed “decent” white Southern men to whip, rape, and kill slaves, and after the war, lynch black men. But this is where we are in Weimar America. 
Do you think this is funny? Do you think this means nothing? When I was in DC last fall, a Zoomer conservative told me of a party in which affirming that you are an anti-Semite was the password to get in. This person is a white Christian. Her boyfriend is as well, and he’s so disgusted by what he’s seeing and hearing in his circles that he’s thinking of leaving politics entirely, and getting out of Washington.
Meanwhile, leftist mobs are rampaging through Minneapolis: 
When Trump invokes the Insurrection Act, this will be why. And when I talk about “Weimar America,” I’m not just talking about the neofascist young Right. 
According to The Nazi Mind, dehumanizing the Other is the first step to preparing yourself to kill them. This is happening on the Left and the Right. This was Spain just before the civil war broke out there in the mid-1930s. 
Potential repercussions: If only a few of these materialise - it will be catastrophic for the USA.
 
Europe will immediately demand the closure of every U.S. military base on the continent. Ramstein in Germany, Aviano in Italy, Lakenheath in the UK. 
The US’s ability to project power into the Middle East and Africa vanishes overnight. They will be evicted from the very soil they helped liberate and defended for decades, and forced to retreat to their own shores as a fortress nation, isolated and friendless. Perhaps Trump may even forge new alliances with Russia and China.
 
Economically, the European Union is the largest single market in the world, and they will immediately be compelled to respond to it, under Article 47.2 TFEU - which is the EU’s own mutual defence treaty and framework.
Europe will likely move to call in U.S. debt and dump their dollar reserves, sending the value of our currency into a death spiral.
To be maximally charitable and avoid serious concerns of real corruption, Trump is still running his comms and public image game like it's Trump45, but since Trump45 was a "thing," we've all been dragged through 5 years of INTENSE political and psychological warfare and destabilization. 
As a result, there's a divide. The sober majority doesn't want the kind of "thumb in the eye," "own the libs" style that made Trump45 fun anymore. We want an assurance through Trump47 that things can and will "go back to normal." 
We want a steady hand and clear leadership, and we're getting some of that, no doubt, mixed with a lot of dumb memes, official government shitposts, reckless (and captured?) influencers, bad decisions hidden behind "bad advisors," grifters, idiots, and what is obviously another huge psyops against us, this one "on our side," that the White House is either blind to or participating in (granted, "the White House" is like 5000 people, some of whom are on the less-sober side who like and promote this destabilizing idiocy).
The population is not the population we had in 2016 that was sick and tired of effete, pompous, elitist pseudopolitics. It liked "owning the libs" because it was funny and cathartic. Seeing the pomposity punctured and exposed was necessary and valuable at the time, destabilizing as it was to certain things.
In 2025, going into 2026, we have a different population. We're freaked the hell out and want to feel like we're going to get back on track, and Trump and his team are (for reasons beyond my comprehension that also must include corruption) have decided that we're in for another long season of psychological abuse that will result in a longer, darker season of even more of it that follows when the Democrats take back over as a result.
This is true, and far too many Americans are too drunk on self-regard to understand how this hurts our own country’s interests:
This is also true:
If hypocritical European leaders cared even a fraction as much about the sovereignty of their own countries, and its being compromised by mass migration, as they do about Greenland’s borders being threatened by the US, everything in Europe would change for the better. 
Really does feel like the world is on fire, doesn’t it?

Sender: johnathanbi@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-18 16:02:50+00:00
Subject: Christian Scholar Wrestles With Uncomfortable Evidence

1. Jesus never claimed to be God
2. His prophecy of imminent apocalypse failed
3. The Gospels contradict each other
4. The historical evidence for resurrection is inconclusive
These are the beliefs of my guest, Princeton’s Dale Allison, after a lifetime of study.
These are not uncommon positions among New Testament historians as methods of historical criticism have overturned orthodox belief. What is uncommon here is how Allison has responded to his new beliefs. Whereas most scholars who arrive at these positions become atheist, Allison remains a practicing, devoted christian who follows Jesus. In a way, his religious practice has become even stronger due to these beliefs.
In this interview, you are going to learn how Professor Allison, after much struggle, came to these uncomfortable critical positions and what Christianity means for him now.
Topics:
This Substack is reader-supported. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a paid subscriber.

Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-18 22:38:01+00:00
Subject: Chaos in Minnesota

The situation in Minneapolis is heating up. I just returned from the city, having spoken at an event in which Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent uncovered new details about the Somali fraud scams.
One of the most interesting things I learned from Bessent was that federal investigators are starting to look at Minnesota’s politicians and their relationship to Somali fraud. This is crucial because everyone on the ground in Minnesota, from state legislators to citizen observers, told me it was an “open secret” that politicians knew this was happening. They were, at a minimum, complicit in the fraud.
According to the state legislature’s fraud committee, whistleblowers have been filing reports since 2019, raising questions about impossible financial statements and about the Somali fraud schemes that penetrated these programs. These complaints were suppressed; whistleblowers were turned away, and some faced retaliation. In our interview, Bessent spoke of “disturbing tapes” of Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison in meetings with political donors who asked for fraud investigations to be halted.
The real question is: Were these politicians going along with this for votes as an implicit quid pro quo? Or were they doing it for more nefarious reasons? Was there a dirty deal of some sort?
To be clear, the evidence we’ve uncovered so far suggests a mixture of stupidity and complicity, but there certainly is the possibility of conspiracy. During the event, Bessent announced that his department would soon release funds for bounties to try to get some Somali fraudsters to turn in their compatriots and, potentially, turn in Minnesota politicians.
The trap has been set. And now, we aren’t the only reporters looking around...

Teperman assumirá em fevereiro como novo CEO da Constância
Roberto Teperman assumirá em fevereiro próximo a função de CEO da Constância Investimentos, em substituição a Cassiano Leme que passará a atuar como presidente do Conselho de Administração da gestora, com foco no desenvolvimento da empresa no longo prazo. A Constância tinha, segundo o último ranking Top Asset publicado por Investidor Institucional, ativos sob gestão no total de R$ 1,62 bilhão em 30 de junho de 2025...

Pedrinha reforça time comercial da MAG
A MAG Investimentos contratou Luiz Fernando Pedrinha para reforçar sua equipe comercial, como responsável pelo relacionamento com os fundos de pensão. Com mais de 20 anos de experiência no atendimento a clientes institucionais, Pedrinha atuou nos últimos três anos e meio na Schroders Brasil, gestora inglêsa que anunciou em setembro do ano passado sua decisão de encerrar as operações no Brasil...

Fachesf ainda não decidiu sobre participação em nova entidade
A Fachesf, fundo de pensão patrocinado pela Companhia Hidro Elétrica do São Francisco (Chesf), publicou nota em seu site afirmando que seu Conselho Deliberativo ainda não tomou nenhuma decisão em relação à participação na nova entidade que a Eletrobras, atualmente Axia Energia, está criando com o objetivo de incorporar as fundações patrocinadas por suas controladas...

VWPP divulga rentabilidade de planos em dezembro/25
A VWPP, fundo de pensão da Volkswagem do Brasil, publicou o desempenho dos seus dois planos de Contribuição Variável (CV) em dezembro de 2025. Segundo divulgação da fundação, todos os perfis superaram o CDI nos 12 meses de 2025, embora no mês de dezembro, especificamente, apenas o perfil conservador tenha superado essa referência em ambos os planos...

Funpresp-Jud publica rentabilidade de perfis em novembro/25
A Funpresp-Jud, fundo de pensão dos servidores públicos federais do poder judiciário, divulgou a rentabilidade de novembro seu Plano de Benefícios (PB), por perfis. No mês, o perfil Horizonte Protegido teve uma rentabilidade de 1,04%, o perfil Horizonte 2040 rendeu 1,27% e o perfil Horizonte 2050 teve rentabilidade de 1,49%...

Sender: contato@informativo.mundobt.com.br
Date: 2026-01-19 12:01:14+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?B?Q2hlZ291IG8gQlQgTmV3cyBkZSAyMDI2IPCfkpo=?=

Bodytech


Como não abandonar o treinonas primeiras semanas?

Criar uma rotina não é simples. Os primeiros dias, semanas e até meses costumam ser os mais desafiadores e, antes que o hábito esteja consolidado, a chance de desistência é maior.

Quando o objetivo é incluir exercícios, mais movimento e uma rotina ativa no dia a dia, esse processo pode ser ainda mais difícil, especialmente para quem está começando agora, no início do ano.

Pensando nisso, conversamos com o diretor técnico da Bodytech, Eduardo Netto, que compartilhou dicas práticas para ajudar a tornar esse caminho mais leve e consistente.

Clique aqui 

“Em 2026, o compromisso é com uma vida mais ativa e mais constante.”

Suar emagrece?

Existe uma correlação indireta entre o suor e o gasto calórico. Exercícios mais intensos geram mais calor e, consequentemente, mais suor, mas suar não significa, necessariamente, queimar mais calorias.

É possível suar intensamente em uma sauna sem gastar quase nada de energia. Por outro lado, alguém que treina pesado em um ambiente frio, mesmo suando pouco, pode ter um gasto calórico elevado.

Quer saber mais? Leia agora em nosso blog!

por Haemin Sunim na Amazon

No verão, se hidrate e durma bem 

Pode até não parecer, mas sono e hidratação estão diretamente ligados. A desidratação, por exemplo, aumenta a liberação de vasopressina, hormônio responsável pela retenção de líquidos,

o que pode fragmentar o sono e prejudicar sua qualidade.

Ao mesmo tempo, noites mal dormidas reduzem a sensibilidade à sede e aumentam a perda de fluidos ao longo do dia, criando um ciclo negativo que impacta tanto o descanso quanto o equilíbrio do corpo. E no calor, esses efeitos são potencializados.

Entenda mais sobre o tema 
aqui!

O que esperar ao fazer parte do#mundoBT?

Ao fazer parte do #mundoBT, você pode esperar variedade, tecnologia e acompanhamento de qualidade em cada etapa da sua jornada. São mais de 170 atividades na grade, além de aplicativos que facilitam os treinos dentro e fora da academia, como o app da Bodytech e o BTFIT.

Além disso, a experiência é completa dentro das unidades, com atendimento de alto padrão, estrutura moderna, programas específicos - BT Care, BT Express e BT Acqua - e serviços que tornam a rotina mais prática, como Kids Room, aluguel de armário, rouparia, wi-fi, avaliação funcional e bioimpedância.

Você sabe o que é Coenzima Q10?

A CoQ10 é uma substância essencial para o funcionamento das células, atuando diretamente na produção de energia e na proteção contra o estresse oxidativo.

Durante a prática de exercícios, seu papel é ainda mais relevante, já que a CoQ10 atua nas mitocôndrias, participando da produção de ATP, principal fonte de energia do corpo.

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-19 12:25:10+00:00
Subject: Minnesota Church Attack & Civil War

When I use the term “Weimar America,” I’m not talking about the Nazification of the United States. In fact, it was the formal accession to power of the National Socialists in 1933 that ended Germany’s Weimar Republic. No, I’m talking about the similarities between what happened in Germany, 1918-33, and what is happening in the US today — that is to say, the analogous events that produced mass psychosocial instability, resulting, ultimately, in a turn to totalitarian government.
What happened at church in Minneapolis yesterday is pure Weimar America. Look:
What is the FACE Act? According to Wikipedia:
Here, courtesy of one of the activists, are some of those responsible for this. US Attorney General for Civil Rights Harmeet Dhillon has already announced a federal investigation of this incident. I expect these people will all be receiving visits from the feds. Hope so:
You all should be aware that an important line — legally and psychologically — has been crossed here, with this invasion of a place of worship during services. 
The beginning of the process that led to the Spanish Civil War in the 1930s was the widespread desecration, in 1931, of churches and convents by left-wing anarchists and other anticlerical groups, shortly after the fall of the monarchy. They even pulled the corpses of dead nuns out of their coffins and dragged them onto the street to mock:
This is how much the Spanish anarchists hated the Catholic Church, and Christianity. The new left-wing Republican government responded passively. This initiated a cycle of violent retribution. By 1936, it was war.
The desecration of what one’s opponents hold sacred is such a critical event because it is felt, and felt very deeply, as an attack on the target group’s very identity. History is filled with examples of militants desecrating holy sites as part of a broader war. For example, Serbian forces damaged or destroyed 1,000 Bosnian mosques in the 1990s, in an attempt to intimidate and to erase Muslim presence there. We know how that ended. 
I will never forget what a friend of mine, an American officer who served there later as part of a UN peacekeeping force, told me about going into a wooden barn in which the Serbs massacred hundreds of Bosnian Muslim men in a mass shooting. The shooting had happened some time earlier, but he said the walls were covered with a black slime — mold feeding off the blood and viscera embedded in the walls. He said it was the most horrible thing he had ever seen. 
What these fools don’t understand is that things like invading and disrupting a church service compels many Christians and others, who might have been doubtful about the Trump administration’s approach to immigration enforcement, to rush towards Trump for protection from the mob. They are hardening sides. Frankly, I hope the feds swoop in with force and start mass arrests, starting with Don Lemon. Again: the protesters crossed a bright red line yesterday in going into that church.

Anbima pede cautela em mudanças na fiscalização dos fundos
Em resposta à declaração feita na manhã desta segunda-feira (19/1) pelo ministro da Fazenda, Fernando Haddad, de que os fundos de investimento atualmente sob responsabilidade da Comissão de Valores Mobiliários (CVM) deveriam passar a ser regulados e fiscalizados pelo Banco Central, a Associação Brasileira das Entidades dos Mercados Financeiro e de Capitais (Anbima) publicou uma nota defendendo que “qualquer mudança deve ser conduzida de forma cuidadosa, transparente e tecnicamente aprofundada”...

Focus projeta Selic caindo neste ano e estável nos anos seguintes
A projeção de inflação de 2026 segue em queda, descendo de 4,05% na pesquisa Focus feita pelo Banco Central e divulgada na segunda-feira passada (12/1) para 4,02% na pesquisa divulgada nesta semana. As projeções para os anos de 2027, 2028 e 2029, porém, mantiveram-se estáveis no mesmo comparativo, em 3,80%, 3,50% e 3,50% respectivamente (ver quadro abaixo)...

A Unifinance possui cerca de R$ 4,2 bilhões em ativos sob assessoria, aplicados exclusivamente em produtos da Azimuti e de sua controlada, a AZ Quest...

Fachesf fecha 2025 com planos superando as metas
A Fachesf, fundo de pensão dos empregados da Cia Hidrelétrica do São Francisco (Chesf), fechou o ano de 2025 com todos os seis planos rentabilizando acima das suas respectivas metas atuariais/índices de referência. Já em relação ao mês de dezembro, isoladamente, três planos fecharam acima da meta e três abaixo...

Ibovespa fecha estável em dia de movimento fraco, e dólar recua
O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta segunda-feira (19/1) praticamente estável, com leve alta de 0,03%, aos 164.849,27 pontos, em pregão de baixa liquidez por conta da ausência de investidores norte-americanos devido ao feriado norte-americano em memória de Martin Luther King...

Lucas assume diretoria de administração fiduciária da Apex
Walter Lucas é o novo diretor de administração fiduciária e controladoria de fundos da Apex Group, empresa provedora de serviços financeiros. Com mais de 39 anos de atuação no mercado financeiro, Lucas esteve entre 2024 e 2025 na Qore DTVM e nos 38 anos anteriores no Citi, onde exerceu as funções de vice-presidente e responsável pela área de administração fiduciária...

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-20 12:43:45+00:00
Subject: A Euro-Right Case Vs Trump's Greenland Antics

Voting trends in most of Europe favor the nationalist right. That should make Trump happy. It aligns with the principle laid out in his National Security Strategy late last year, which framed strong European sovereignty as necessary to defend the West. And yet now, in his MAGA-lomaniacal determination to impose his will on the world, Trump may tear apart that civilizational unity. How will any self-respecting European conservative fail to stand up to the bullying from the White House?
Of course, it would be nice if the European leaders cared half as much about guarding the borders of their own countries as they do about defending the borders of Greenland from the Sultan of MAGA. But it’s not difficult to understand their reaction to the Greenland gambit. There are a number of conservative Europeans who generally support Trump, but who are now being forced to reconsider their position—because they are patriots.
With his bellicose posturing on Greenland, Trump is putting his natural political allies in Europe in the position of losing ground with voters, or having to take strong anti-American stances to maintain their popularity. A wiser and more patient approach would be to play the long game, and count on changing European populist politics to deliver Greenland to American ownership through diplomatic means. But that’s not how Trump rolls.
I make a point that I personally didn’t really grasp until I moved to Europe:
Living here over the past few years, I’ve learned things that Americans who have never lived abroad simply can’t understand. It is impossible to overstate the influence the United States has beyond its borders.
What these questioners meant is that they had been freed from one hard tyranny—the Soviet Union—but now found themselves subject, in a softer way, to one coming from the United States. It generally played out culturally. One Slovak Catholic told me that U.S.-funded NGOs were at war with anything in his country that violated American (and Western European) ideas of progress. For example, in 2023, then-USAID chief Samantha Power made a well-publicized visit to Budapest, where she announced a $20 million donation for “pro-democracy” NGOs. Conservative Hungarians took this as an attempt to undermine a democratically elected government in a NATO ally.
I look forward to polls of European voters, to take their temperature. Hard for me to see how you win people over to your side in the long term by humiliating them, and reminding them of their own weakness. I’m not talking about Greenland per se — European publics will never favor that, whatever their governments say or do — but about electing governments aligned broadly with the stated US long-term strategic goal of consolidating and defending the West. 
Here in Hungary, there’s an election coming up in the spring, and it’s going to be close. On the one hand, Orban’s closeness with Trump should be a big bonus for him, given that Hungary no longer has a hostile administration to deal with, and indeed has an ally in resisting Brussels’ bullying. On the other hand, if I were the opposition leader Peter Magyar, I would go all in on “European patriotism” — this, even though it’s clear that should he win, he will turn Hungary into a vassal of Brussels’s managerial liberals. Bottom line: I want Orban to win re-election, just as I want the RN, the AfD, Reform UK, and a right-wing coalition to win in Poland. I fear this Trump thing puts all that in danger. I hope I’m wrong. We will see.
However paradoxical the notion of a nationalist Internationale, many had pinned their political hopes on Trump fostering just such a coalition — a hope fanned by the NSS. Instead, Trump has tainted by association every European populist group that has ever given his regime so much as the time of day.
Last point — this from today’s WSJ:
Only 16% of Europeans view the U.S. as an ally that shares the same values, down from 21% in 2024, according to a recent survey conducted in November by the European Council on Foreign Relations, an international think tank.
The decline in the U.K., long the closest U.S. ally in Europe, was stark: down to 25%, from 37% a year earlier.
Europeans also have their pride and don’t like to see their countries being pushed around, said Jérémie Gallon, a French foreign-affairs expert and head of Europe for trade-and-policy consulting firm McLarty Associates.
“People are starting to feel that the sense of humiliation, and vassalization, is at a point that is unacceptable,” he said.
It is all so unnecessary. Of course the US-European relationship needed to be recalibrated. Of course J.D. Vance’s Munich speech was an occasion of brutal truth-telling. But I’m telling you, the US is going to miss Europe when it’s gone, more than the “screw the Euroweenies” people on the American Right understand. 
The only way European civilization is saved is by changing demographics and the recovery of Christianity. Politics has a key role to play in that. Trump is making it much harder on the politicians in Europe who stand a chance at reversing its steep decline. That is all.

Subject: =?utf-8?Q?The=20math=20that=20most=20quantitative=20teams=20skip=20=28but=20shouldn=E2=80=99t=29?=

Simpler is deeper

Most statistical approaches describe randomness in a "probabilistic" framework, with distributions, conditional independence, copulas,...

In financial applications, we use a simpler "linear" framework, with mean–covariance classes, partial uncorrelation, projections...

Understanding the connections between the two frameworks, and how decision theory operates in them, shape how we model, estimate/learn and optimize -  from risk to uncertainty, from data to beliefs.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-01-20 17:02:25+00:00
Subject: The Checkup | =?UTF-8?B?VGVuZMOqbmNpYXM=?= em biotecnologia para 2026

The CheckupTendências em biotecnologia para 2026

Por Roberta Arinelli, Editora-executiva de Saúde  • 19.01.25

 
 Olá! Seja bem-vindo à The Checkup, a newsletter semanal de Biotech and Health da MIT Technology Review Brasil.

Às terças-feiras, trazemos temas essenciais sobre saúde, biotecnologia e inovação para você acompanhar as principais tendências da área.
Nesta edição, o destaque é a análise de Jess Hamzelou, repórter sênior da MIT Technology Review, sobre as principais tecnologias disruptivas de 2026, explorando a lista das 10 Breakthrough Technologies, com foco especial nos avanços em biotecnologia, explorando não apenas o seu potencial científico, mas também custos, limites regulatórios e dilemas éticos que devem moldar os próximos anos.
 Na curadoria científica da semana, três destaques. Um estudo internacional usa IA para revelar os fatores que explicam as desigualdades globais na sobrevida ao câncer, pesquisadores do MIT identificam uma proteína intestinal capaz de neutralizar bactérias resistentes, e cientistas elucidam o mecanismo da dor muscular associada às estatinas.
 Neste episódio do podcast Biotech and Health, Camila Pepe e Carolina Abelin conversam com o pneumologista Paulo Pitrez sobre uso de IA para identificar subtipos de asma infantil associados a maior risco de infecções respiratórias.
Boa leitura!

 
As tendências em biotecnologia para acompanhar em 2026

  Na última semana, a MIT Technology Review publicou sua lista anual das 10 Breakthrough Technologies. Como sempre, ela reúne tecnologias que ganharam destaque nas notícias no ano passado e que, para o bem ou para o mal, prometem causar impacto nos próximos anos. São as tecnologias às quais você realmente deveria estar atento.
 A lista deste ano inclui tecnologias que devem transformar o setor de energia, a inteligência artificial, as viagens espaciais e, claro, a biotecnologia e a saúde. As inovações biotecnológicas para 2026 envolvem a edição dos genes de um bebê e, separadamente, a “ressurreição” de genes de espécies antigas. Também incluímos uma tecnologia controversa que oferece aos pais a chance de rastrear embriões em busca de características como altura e inteligência. Aqui está a história por trás das nossas escolhas em biotecnologia. 
Um bebê editado por base!
Em agosto de 2024, KJ Muldoon nasceu com um distúrbio genético raro que permitia o acúmulo de amônia tóxica em seu sangue. A doença pode ser fatal, e KJ corria risco de desenvolver distúrbios neurológicos. Na época, sua melhor chance de sobrevivência envolvia aguardar um transplante de fígado.
Ressurreição genética
A empresa fez uma afirmação ainda mais dramática no mês seguinte, ao anunciar que havia criado três lobos-terríveis (dire wolves). Esses animais impressionantes, de pelagem branca como a neve, teriam sido criados a partir de 20 alterações genéticas no DNA de lobos-cinzentos, com base em pesquisas genéticas de ossos antigos de lobos-terríveis, segundo informou a empresa na época.
Pontuação de embriões
A fertilização in vitro (FIV) envolve a criação de embriões em laboratório e, normalmente, a atribuição de uma “pontuação” com base na probabilidade de crescimento bem-sucedido antes de serem transferidos para o útero de uma pessoa. Até aqui, nada de controverso.
 Recentemente, porém, essa pontuação de embriões evoluiu. Laboratórios conseguem retirar algumas células de um embrião, analisar seu DNA e rastrear algumas doenças
genéticas. A lista dessas doenças está crescendo. E agora algumas empresas estão indo ainda mais longe, oferecendo aos futuros pais a oportunidade de selecionar embriões com base em características como altura, cor dos olhos e até QI.
Leia mais sobre a lista das 10 Breakthrough Technologies
O que você acha que está faltando na lista?


 
 Pela web

IA revela os fatores ocultos que moldam a sobrevida ao câncer no mundo
 Pesquisadores transformaram a IA em uma nova lente para compreender por que as taxas de sobrevida ao câncer variam de forma tão acentuada entre países. Ao analisar dados oncológicos e informações sobre sistemas de saúde de 185 países, um modelo de
machine learning identificou quais fatores, como acesso à radioterapia, cobertura universal de saúde e força econômica, estão mais fortemente associados a melhores desfechos em cada contexto nacional.
O estudo combina dados do Global Cancer Observatory (GLOBOCAN 2022) com indicadores da Organização Mundial da Saúde, Banco Mundial e Nações Unidas. A análise utiliza a razão mortalidade/incidência como medida da efetividade do cuidado oncológico e emprega métodos explicáveis de IA para mostrar, país a país, quais investimentos em saúde têm maior potencial de impacto real na redução da mortalidade.
 Os resultados revelam que não existe uma solução única. No Brasil, a ampliação da cobertura universal de saúde aparece como o principal motor para melhorar os desfechos; na Polônia, o destaque vai para radioterapia e renda per capita; no Japão, a densidade de centros de radioterapia; e nos EUA e Reino Unido, a força econômica geral. Já na China, persistem desafios relacionados aos gastos diretos dos pacientes, mesmo diante de avanços no financiamento do sistema. Segundo os autores, a principal contribuição do trabalho é transformar desigualdades globais em roteiros acionáveis de política pública, permitindo que gestores priorizem investimentos de forma mais precisa e equitativa. 

Proteína intestinal reforça a defesa contra bactérias no trato digestivo
 Pesquisadores do MIT identificaram uma proteína do trato gastrointestinal capaz de neutralizar uma ampla gama de bactérias e, ao mesmo tempo, fortalecer a barreira de muco que reveste o intestino. Conhecida como intelectina-2, essa lectina se liga a açúcares presentes tanto no muco quanto na superfície de microrganismos, atuando de forma dupla na proteção da mucosa intestinal.
 Nos experimentos, a intelectina-2 mostrou capacidade de entrecruzar moléculas de mucina, tornando a camada de muco mais estável e resistente. Quando bactérias conseguem ultrapassar essa barreira, a proteína passa a se ligar diretamente às membranas microbianas, aprisionando os patógenos e inibindo seu crescimento. Com o
 tempo, muitos desses microrganismos acabam se desintegrando, indicando um efeito antimicrobiano direto, inclusive contra bactérias resistentes a antibióticos.
 Os autores destacam que essa atuação em dois níveis (reforço da barreira física e controle direto de patógenos) ajuda a explicar como o intestino mantém o equilíbrio entre proteção contra infecções e convivência com a microbiota benéfica. O achado abre caminho para novas abordagens terapêuticas em doenças inflamatórias intestinais e para o desenvolvimento de estratégias antimicrobianas inspiradas no próprio sistema imune humano.

Cientistas explicam por que estatinas causam dor muscula
 Pesquisadores identificaram um mecanismo molecular que ajuda a explicar por que muitos pacientes abandonam o uso de estatinas devido a dor, fraqueza e fadiga muscular. O estudo mostra que algumas estatinas podem se ligar inadvertidamente a uma proteína essencial das células musculares, desencadeando um vazamento anormal de cálcio que compromete o funcionamento normal do músculo.
 Usando criomicroscopia eletrônica, a equipe observou que a sinvastatina se conecta a regiões específicas do receptor de rianodina (RyR1), abrindo um canal que permite a liberação inadequada de cálcio dentro da célula. Esse desequilíbrio pode enfraquecer diretamente as fibras musculares ou ativar processos bioquímicos que levam à degradação progressiva do tecido muscular, um achado que oferece uma explicação de longa data para os efeitos adversos associados às estatinas.
A descoberta abre caminho para estratégias mais seguras de controle do colesterol, seja por meio do redesenho de estatinas que não interajam com essa proteína muscular, seja pelo uso de fármacos capazes de bloquear o vazamento de cálcio. A inovação representa um passo importante para melhorar a adesão ao tratamento e reduzir um dos principais limitadores do uso dessas drogas amplamente prescritas.

   
 Podcast #240


Asma infantil: como a IA pode antecipar riscos e crises

Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-20 18:31:10+00:00
Subject: Data Contracts: A Missed Opportunity

Over the last couple of years, the data industry has been having a conversation about data contracts that never quite went where they needed to.
There was no shortage of activity around the topic. Definitions were proposed and refined. Conceptual boundaries were drawn and redrawn. Data contracts were compared to APIs, governance frameworks, data mesh primitives, and ideas teams already “sort of” implemented in practice. 
The discussion was energetic and well-intentioned, but it tended to stay at the level of classification rather than construction.
What was largely absent was sustained engagement with the engineering consequences of taking data contracts seriously. Questions about enforcement, evolution, compatibility, and failure modes appeared only briefly before the conversation moved on. The result was an industry consensus that data contracts were “interesting,” without a shared understanding of what it would actually mean to build platforms around them.
In hindsight, this matters—not because the debate was unproductive, but because of what happened in parallel.
The Shift Happening Elsewhere
While the data community was debating what data contracts were, the software engineering world was converging—quietly and pragmatically—on a different organizing principle: specifications as the primary unit of system design.
This wasn’t a philosophical shift so much as an operational one. As systems became more distributed, more automated, and more interdependent, informal agreements stopped scaling. Documentation drifted. Assumptions diverged. Human coordination became the bottleneck.
The response was not more process, but more precision.
APIs began with schemas rather than code. Infrastructure moved from scripts to declarative specifications. Compatibility rules were automatically encoded and enforced. In these systems, the specification was no longer an artifact produced alongside the system—it was the system.
More recently, AI agents have accelerated this trend. Agents do not operate on intent, convention, or context. They operate on explicit, machine-readable, and verifiable data. Where specifications exist, agents can reason deterministically. Where they do not, agents approximate—and approximation is rarely acceptable in core infrastructure.
This is where the connection to data contracts becomes unavoidable.
Data Contracts as Specifications, Not Concepts
Viewed through the lens of spec-driven development, data contracts stop looking like a data-specific innovation and become a familiar pattern applied to a different domain.
A properly implemented data contract is a specification:
It defines structure, semantics, and invariants
It establishes compatibility guarantees over time.
It is versioned, validated, and enforced programmatically.
It creates a stable interface between independently evolving systems.
This is exactly what spec-driven development has optimized for in software engineering.
The difference is not conceptual—it is operational. Software engineering treated specifications as executable constraints. The data industry often treated contracts as descriptive artifacts. As a result, contracts were discussed as governance tools or communication mechanisms, rather than as interfaces with failure semantics.
That framing limited how far the idea could go.
Where the Two Worlds Diverged
Spec-driven systems force early clarity around hard problems.
How does change propagate?
What is allowed to evolve independently?
What breaks compatibility, and how is that detected?
Where does enforcement occur, and what happens when it fails?
In software systems, these questions are answered in code and tooling. In data systems, they were often answered socially. Producer–consumer agreements existed, but they lived in tickets, meetings, and tribal knowledge rather than in executable form.
Many teams compensated by building partial solutions: strong schemas, upstream quality checks, and informal SLAs. These patterns worked, but they relied heavily on human intervention. They were resilient, but not legible to machines.
As long as humans were the primary integrators, this was manageable. As soon as AI agents enter the workflow, it becomes a constraint.
Why Spec-Driven Thinking Changes the Next Phase
AI agents make an implicit demand of data platforms: make your rules explicit.
Agents can generate schemas, propose transformations, reason about compatibility, and enforce policy—but only if the platform exposes contracts in a form they can execute against. Without that, agents revert to inference, which introduces uncertainty precisely where determinism is required.
This is the practical implication of spec-driven development for data engineering. It’s not about adopting a new paradigm. It’s about recognizing that the platform already behaves like a system of interfaces—and formalizing those interfaces accordingly.
Teams that have already internalized contract discipline will find this transition incremental. Teams that have not will experience it as friction.
What We Should Do Next
At this point, the terminology matters less than the mechanics.
Whether we call them data contracts, data interfaces, or executable schemas, the path forward is the same:
Treat schemas as specifications, not documentation
Encode quality, semantics, and compatibility as executable rules
Enforce contracts at clear system boundaries, preferably early.
Version data interfaces with the same rigor as APIs
Make ownership and accountability explicit and machine-readable.
This is not about adding process. It is about making systems legible to other systems.
Closing Thought
The original data contracts conversation wasn’t wrong. It just stopped too early.
Spec-driven development has shown that explicit, enforceable interfaces are not optional in complex, automated systems. Data platforms are now at that same inflection point.
Data contracts were never the destination.
They were the missing layer that would have made everything else easier to build.
The opportunity is still there—but only if we’re willing to treat contracts as infrastructure, not ideas.
References

Subject: Now on SACD: Kirill Petrenko and the Berliner Philharmoniker

Now on SACD: Kirill Petrenko and the Berliner Philharmoniker – the beginning of a partnership

In June 2015, the Berliner Philharmoniker elected Kirill Petrenko as chief conductor, and he took office in 2019. Our edition presents award-winning key recordings from this exciting phase on SACD for the first time. Performances of Beethoven’s Seventh and Ninth Symphonies, Tchaikovsky’s Symphonies Nos. 5 and 6, and works by unjustly forgotten composers such as Franz Schmidt and Rudi Stephan reveal the first important programme outlines and the intense music-making of this partnership.

These special recordings are being released for the first time as a compact SACD edition with a 108-page accompanying book.

Caderno do MPS explica novo marco dos investimentos dos RPPS
O Departamento de Regimes Próprios de Previdência Social (DRPPS), instância do Ministério da Previdência Social, publicou nesta terça-feira (20/1) um caderno digital intitulado “A Nova Estrutura de Tipos de Ativos na Regulamentação dos Investimentos dos Regimes Próprios de Previdência Social (RPPS)”. O documento tem a finalidade de apresentar as principais alterações introduzidas pela nova resolução que alterou o marco regulatório dos investimentos dos RPPS, incluindo a atualização da classificação dos tipos de ativos, os novos limites prudenciais e os impactos práticos para a revisão das Políticas de Investimentos dos RPPS...

Fundo de pensão da Dinamarca zera exposição à títulos dos EUA
O fundo de pensão dinamarquês AkademikerPension decidiu zerar, até o fim deste mês, sua exposição a títulos do Tesouro dos Estados Unidos, hoje estimada em cerca de US$ 100 milhões. A entidade administra aproximadamente US$ 25 bilhões em recursos de professores e profissionais acadêmicos na Dinamarca...

Nota da Capef informa aos participantes sua exposição ao BRB
Frente aos rumores de mercado que o BRB (Banco de Brasília) pode vir a enfrentar dificuldades por conta dos negócios que tinha com o Banco Máster, a Capef (fundo de pensão dos funcionários do Banco do Nordeste do Brasil) publicou nota em seu site informando sua exposição aos ativos do BRB. Segundo a nota, a fundação adquiriu Letras Financeiras (LFs) do BRB em 15/05/2024 e 26/07/2024, com 88% dos vencimentos marcados para 14/12/2027...

Funsejem divulga rentabilidade dos perfis em dez/25
A Funsejem, fundo de pensão do grupo Votorantim, divulgou a rentabilidade de dezembro dos quatro perfis de investimento do plano Votorantim Prev. No mês, o perfil Conservador rendeu 1,11%, o Moderado 0,91%, o Agressivo 0,82% e o Super-Agressivo 0,66%, enquanto em 12 meses os mesmos perfis renderam 14,00%, 16,29%, 19,77% e 23,10%....

Ibovespa renova máximas em oposição à Nova York; dólar sobe
O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou esta terça-feira (20/1) em alta de 0,87%, aos 166.276,90 pontos, renovando o recorde de fechamento e contrariando o desempenho negativo das bolsas de Nova York. O avanço foi sustentado pela entrada de fluxo estrangeiro em mercados emergentes, em meio ao aumento das tensões tarifárias e geopolíticas entre Estados Unidos e Europa, o que tem estimulado a busca por diversificação e favorecido o Brasil via operações de carry trade, diante do elevado diferencial de juros...

PRhosper divulga retorno de planos em nov/25
A PRhosper, fundo de pensão dos funcionários do grupo Rhodia, divulgou a rentabilidade de novembro dos seus três planos: Básico, Suplementar e SolvayPrev. Os três possuem perfis de investimento semelhantes, sendo que o perfil Flex 0 não tem renda variável, o Flex 15 tem 15% de RV e o Flex 30 tem 30% de RV. Veja abaixo as rentabilidades do mês e do acumulado de 12 meses até novembro...

February 11th, 2026 | 10 AM EST

Hi folks,

How do you move your DevOps and SRE practices beyond reactive monitoring toward predictive, automated delivery?

AI is fundamentally changing the landscape of operations, requiring senior practitioners to rethink how they manage CI/CD pipelines and system reliability. The challenge is no longer just observing system health, but integrating AI agents and generative models to detect issues and respond at machine speed, minimizing customer impact while accelerating delivery.


- Predictive Incident Detection: How to implement AI-driven strategies that identify risks before they escalate.
- Intelligent Observability: Connecting signals from code changes, runtime behavior, and user impact to enable fast, automated decisions.
- AI-Driven Remediation: Practical approaches for automating root cause analysis and adjusting configurations or rolling back releases.
- Continuous Resilience: Using AI agents to validate system architectures and test resilience directly in production.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-01-21 15:03:43+00:00
Subject: Data centers =?UTF-8?B?ZXN0w6Nv?= redesenhando o mapa do Brasil

[ARTIGO ENERGISA] 

 Data centers e interiorização:a nova geografia da infraestrutura Expansão dos data centers no Brasil traz desafios regulatórios e estruturais, mas impulsiona desenvolvimento regional, sustentabilidade e investimentos. 

 


 A expansão dos data centers tem se tornado um tema central na agenda tecnológica e econômica global, representando um pilar fundamental para a digitalização e o avanço da inteligência artificial. 
O Brasil possui vantagens competitivas relevantes, como uma matriz energética predominantemente limpa e uma localização estratégica que favorece a atração de investimentos internacionais. 
Neste cenário, o país busca superar desafios estruturais e regulatórios, como a complexidade da legislação, limitações na infraestrutura elétrica e a escassez de mão de obra qualificada, para consolidar sua posição como um hub tecnológico sustentável. 
 Em parceria com o Grupo Energisa, o artigo destaca a importância da descentralização dos data centers para promover o desenvolvimento regional e a inclusão social, além da necessidade urgente de um marco regulatório específico com incentivos fiscais e investimentos robustos para fomentar a inovação e o crescimento econômico. 

Entenda o papel dos data centers no desenvolvimento tecnológico e sustentável do Brasil. 

 


Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Subject: Uma nova aposta para combater as

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 21.01.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  E se a próxima grande virada da Inteligência Artificial não vier de um novo modelo, mas de um freio inesperado? Em 2026, tentar prever o rumo da tecnologia ficou mais difícil do que nunca. Três incógnitas comandam o jogo. Os grandes modelos de linguagem vão continuar avançando ou vão desacelerar? A impopularidade da IA vai travar a expansão de data centers? E a regulação vai se organizar, ou vai virar um cabo de guerra entre big techs e proteção ao público, especialmente crianças? Há ganhos na ciência, mas chatbots ainda prometem mais do que entregam. 

 

POR: JAMES O´DONELL

Por que previsões sobre IA são tão difíceis

 E por que estamos prevendo, afinal, o que vem a seguir para a tecnologia, em 2026?
 A Inteligência Artificial deixou de parecer um assunto de nicho e virou conversa de família, entre relatos de psicose induzida por chatbot, medo do custo energético dos data centers e a dúvida sobre crianças com acesso irrestrito. A pergunta inevitável é o que vem depois, se a tecnologia ainda pode ser melhor. 
 Só que prever os próximos passos ficou mais difícil, mesmo para quem costuma acertar tendências.
 Três incógnitas travam o mapa de 2026. Os grandes modelos de linguagem vão continuar ficando mais inteligentes, ou o avanço vai desacelerar? A reação pública, cada vez mais hostil, vai barrar a expansão de data centers? E a política vai conseguir organizar a regulação sem virar um emaranhado, entre a pressão das big techs e iniciativas para proteger usuários, especialmente crianças? 

 
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Provavelmente, há mais material de abuso infantil online agora do que nunca antes

3. Como está indo a proibição, na Austrália, das redes sociais para crianças?
4. Cientistas estão encontrando formas mais objetivas de identificar doenças mentais

5. Um novo videogame prende você em um centro de golpes online

The Big Story


Espaço MIT Alumni do Brasil

Outubro 2025



 O aplicativo Newsco nasceu para resolver um problema comum a quem vive de informação, o excesso de notícias e dados espalhados, que atrasam decisões. Criado em 2023 por Gabriel Cantu e Lucas Bastianik, ele combina curadoria editorial e Inteligência Artificial para entregar economia, política e negócios com clareza e rapidez. 
 A evolução do negócio veio com a Newsco Pro, que junta em um só lugar notícias de centenas de veículos, resumidas com a metodologia de brevidade inteligente, dados do mercado financeiro, de fundos a renda fixa e variável, e informações corporativas inseridas pelas próprias empresas. Tudo é organizado por recomendação e por agentes de IA generativa, que coletam dados, comparam cenários macroeconômicos, analisam carteiras e montam relatórios sob medida.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br

Subject: A IA saiu da fase de testes, mas
 =?UTF-8?B?c2VndXJhbsOnYSBjb21ww7VlIHN1YSBlc3RyYXTDqWdpYT8=?=

[PODCAST] Escalar IA requer dados, regras e segurançaNo podcast da MIT Technology Review Brasil com a Palo Alto Networks, você entende como dados, governança e proteção sustentam a adoção em escala da IA 



A Inteligência Artificial entrou nas organizações com pressão e entusiasmo, mas a fase de testes não garante a sua escalabilidade. 
O ponto decisivo, agora, vai além de "ter IA": como manter a operação com dados bem organizados, governança consistente e proteção adequada para evitar que a inovação se torne um risco permanente?  
Com o aumento da adoção de sistemas autônomos, também aumentam as ameaças, que se tornam mais rápidas e personalizadas. As empresas que adotarem uma postura preventiva, utilizando IA para se defender da própria IA, definindo regras claras para o uso autorizado e para a atuação de agentes em nome de pessoas e equipes, terão largado na frente.
 No novo podcast da MIT Technology Review Brasil, em parceria com a Palo Alto Networks, Rafael Coimbra conversa com Fernando Fujihara, sócio-líder de engenharia de software, IA e dados da Deloitte, e Marcos Pupo, presidente da Palo Alto Networks para a América Latina, sobre os desafios de segurança, governança e dados na implementação de Inteligência Artificial. 
 
Conheça os  participantes:  

Marcos PupoPresidente da
Palo Alto Networks para
a América Latina



Fernando FujiharaSócio-líder de engenharia
de software, IA e dados
da Deloitte



Rafael CoimbraMediador, editor-executivo
da MIT Technology
Review Brasil

 Veja nesta edição: ⯀ Por que dados e segurança são essenciais para transformar pilotos promissores em iniciativas escaláveis 
⯀ Como a IA reduz o tempo de ataques de dias para horas e, em alguns casos, para minutos 
 ⯀ O que caracteriza o uso não autorizado de IA nas empresas e por que a visibilidade deve vir antes do bloqueio 
 ⯀ Como a falta de integração entre ferramentas de cibersegurança dificulta a resposta às ameaças, e como plataformas unificadas podem melhorar essa situação 
 ⯀ Como os agentes de IA ampliam o debate sobre responsabilidade, ética e a necessidade de supervisão humana 

Saiba o que muda quando a IA sai da prova de conceito e vira infraestrutura do negócio

Previc apresenta à Abrapp as prioridades da autarquia no ano
News Investidor Institucional ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌

‍Olá Tolis Vossos ‍

Previc apresenta à Abrapp as prioridades da autarquia no ano
A Superintendência Nacional de Previdência Complementar (Previc) realizou nesta quarta-feira (21/1) uma reunião com a Associação Brasileira de Previdência Complementar Fechada (Abrapp), em Brasília, na qual apresentou os principais pilares de sua agenda para 2026, reforçando o papel da autarquia como indutora da governança, da transparência e do fortalecimento do sistema de previdência complementar fechada...

Em Davos, Bessent minimiza decisão de fundo de pensão dinamarquês
O secretário do Tesouro dos Estados Unidos, Scott Bessent, minimizou nesta semana a decisão do AkademikerPension, fundo de pensão dos professores e acadêmicos da Dinamarca, de zerar sua exposição a títulos da dívida americana. Segundo Bessent, o movimento do fundo de pensão dinamarquês é financeiramente irrelevante para os Estados Unidos...

Ibovespa sobe mais de 3% com postura menos belicista de Trump
O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta quarta-feira (21) em alta de 3,33%, aos 171.816,67 pontos, renovando tanto o recorde de fechamento quanto a máxima intradiária, de 171.969,01 pontos. Foi a primeira vez, desde abril de 2025, que a Bolsa brasileira registrou uma valorização diária superior a 3% e o maior avanço percentual desde abril de 2023...

Mudança na fiscalização de fundos exige análise técnica, diz Ibre
O Instituto Brasileiro de Relações com Investidores (Ibri) defendeu nesta quarta-feira (21/1) que a proposta do ministro da Fazenda, Fernando Haddad, de transferir a regulação e fiscalização dos fundos de investimento da Comissão de Valores Mobiliários (CVM) para o Banco Central, “deve ser analisada minuciosamente sob todos os ângulos, de forma transparente e com embasamento técnico”...

Banco Central decreta liquidação extrajudicial do Will Bank
O Banco Central decretou nesta quarta-feira (21/1) a liquidação extrajudicial do Will Bank, controlado pelo Banco Master cuja liquidação foi anunciada pelo regulador em novembro de 2025. Segundo o BC, a liquidação do banco digital foi adotada um dia após o Mastercard suspender o uso de seus cartões na rede devido a não cumprimento de suas obrigações financeiras, evidenciando sua insolvência e deterioração das condições financeiras...

Postalis supera meta anual no PostalPrev, mas não no BD
O Postalis, fundo de pensão dos funcionários dos Correios, divulgou a rentabilidade de seus dois planos, o PostalPrev (CV) e o Plano BD, em 2025. De acordo com a fundação, no ano o CV rendeu 13,52% e superou sua meta atuarial de 8,88%, mas o BD rendeu apenas 2,63% e ficou abaixo da meta “devido ao impacto da reavaliação de um fundo complexo”, explica nota da fundação. A nota, porém, não especifica qual era a meta do BD no ano...

O que as empresas mais inovadoras do Brasil estão fazendo diferente  
Governança, estratégia e cultura, na prática, nos cases vencedores do Innovative Workplaces 2025 




 
 Inovação, hoje, não é ter boas ideias, é construir capacidade de execução, com estratégia, governança e cultura alinhadas ao negócio.

 
 Foi com esse olhar que o Innovative Workplaces 2025 reconheceu 20 empresas pelas melhores práticas em inovação no país, na quarta edição da iniciativa, durante o EmTech Brasil 2025.

 O ponto em comum entre as vencedoras não é uma fórmula única, é a maturidade para transformar decisões em impacto mensurável. A coletânea Business Cases 2025 reúne casos de sucesso das empresas vencedoras, destacando critérios, aprendizados e caminhos que podem ser adaptados à realidade de diferentes setores.

É um conteúdo para lideranças e times que buscam repertório sólido, visão estratégica e referências aplicáveis, sem depender de tendências passageiras.

• Como a execução foi desenhada, do modelo de governança ao portfólio de iniciativas, com decisões claras sobre prioridades e trade offs;
 • O papel da cultura e das pessoas para sustentar eficiência operacional e experiência no trabalho, sem perder velocidade;
• Onde a Inteligência Artificial entrou de verdade como capacidade organizacional, conectada a métricas e rotinas de gestão;
 • O que muda por setor, com destaque para finanças e seguros, tecnologia e telecom, serviços, varejo e consumo, e como adaptar as lições ao seu contexto.

Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-22 13:02:07+00:00
Subject: The Return of the "Jewish Question"

Rufo: David, I’m excited to talk about your Compact essay, which I thought was important and did not get enough attention, on the return of the Jewish question, or the “JQ.”
There’s been an avalanche of insanity over the last couple of years about Jews, Israel, and Israeli and Jewish Americans. I think the piece that you wrote was the piece that needed to get written. To help us frame this conversation, would you mind summarizing the essay’s main points and telling us a bit more about its origins?
Azerrad: This subject has been on my mind for some time, but I suppose conditions needed to get really bad for me to think that it warranted an argument.
The essay examined the two central claims of those who are obsessed with the Jewish question. One is that Israel and Jews in America control U.S. foreign policy, and that therefore America is at the service of Israel and Israel is responsible for all of America’s wars—Exhibit A, of course, being the Iraq War. To investigate that claim, I looked at the best argument I could find, which is a chapter in John Mearsheimer and Stephen Walt’s book, The Israel Lobby and U.S. Foreign Policy.
The second pillar of the argument is not a foreign policy one; it’s a domestic one. Many today look at America’s problems and the state of our politics and ask, ‘Whose fault is it?’ In many corners of the internet, the answer is the Jews. There, I looked at the argument that Jews are supposedly responsible for every horrible left-wing movement in America and the West, basically since Karl Marx, along with all sorts of degenerate social trends. There, I engaged with, again, the best argument I could find—which is not Nick Fuentes or Candace Owens, but Kevin MacDonald’s book, The Culture of Critique.
In the essay, I acknowledged that these arguments have some limited basis in reality—as most conspiracy theories do—and acknowledged that there is an undeniable appeal to blaming the Jews for everything. But I then proceeded, as best as I could, to demolish the arguments. Both claims, I argue, are premised on what I called selective noticing. You just notice the Jewish footprint on the Left and in foreign policy, and you completely ignore, for example, the Jewish footprint on the Right. Or, more importantly, you ignore all the factors that have nothing to do with Jews that explain our current predicament. That’s basically the essay.
Lomez: You mentioned at the start it would take a lot for you to even sit down to write this, to acknowledge that these questions around Jewry, international Jewry, organized Jewry, or whatever term you want to use, are worthy of a debate and are worthy of a response. What exactly were you seeing that compelled you to write this? 
And secondly—and I’m not sure I have my own answer to this—why now? Why are we seeing, over the last two to three years and perhaps reaching a boiling point now, these questions about Jewish influence becoming so salient? To be sure, it’s not like this is all brand new—it’s been around for a long time in fringe corners of the Right. And it’s been a siloed part of American discourse for a long time. Why has it leaked out into the broader conversation now?
Azerrad: Very good questions. I teach at Hillsdale’s Washington, D.C., campus, so I’m in contact with a lot of young people on the Right. I’ve noticed in recent years that this has become an issue in a way that it just wasn’t before.
I’ve been living in D.C. since 2008. I’ve been involved with the Right for my whole career, including work at the Heritage Foundation, where I did all the educational programs for the interns. These questions just never came up. But in recent years I’ve noticed that some of the young men with whom I’m in contact—and it is always men—mention things. They mention names and websites.
Another factor was the trifecta of Tucker Carlson, Nick Fuentes, and Candace Owens. I don’t pay much attention to Candace Owens. When she shows up on my X feed, she just seems completely unhinged. Fuentes was, until quite recently, not on many people’s radars, at least among people our age. But then Tucker gave him a two-and-a-half-hour platform. Of course, Tucker himself, in the last one or two years, has been drifting in that direction.
I’m Jewish, which I mention in the essay. I’ve never once encountered anti-Semitism. I’m not on high alert for it, but I just noticed that this argument was out there. And I noticed how quickly this stuff can rot the brains of highly promising young people. That’s what really offends me—not as a Jew, but as a conservative. We’re outgunned, we’re outmanned, and our country is under assault. Every young man who goes down this rabbit hole is someone we’re losing for the cause of reclaiming our country. So that’s why I wrote the essay now...

Paredão de gelo ameaça


 A MIT Technology Review Brasil publica semanalmente infográficos que aprofundam os assuntos do momento através da análise de dados. Toda quarta-feira, um novo tema dentro do universo da tecnologia e da inovação é apresentado para além do óbvio.
