Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-25 21:31:37+00:00
Subject: Sunday In Budapest

I know it’s Sunday, but I also know that tomorrow morning’s newsletter will be preoccupied with the horrors of Minneapolis. I wanted to share some spiritual things with you today, before they’re drowned out by the coming week’s cacaphony.
Something incredibly good happened to me on Saturday, a blessing I’ve spent the last four years praying for. For the sake of family privacy, I won’t say what happened, only that it left me on my couch afterward praying tears of joy, and thanking God. I know too that many of you have been praying for me for some time. Well, those prayers were answered, at least in part, and in a way that suggests a good way forward. Going to the liturgy this morning was different than it has been in ages. I had so much weight lifted from my shoulders. 
Kontakion 1
Everlasting King, Thy will for our salvation is full of power. Thy right arm controls the whole course of human life. We give Thee thanks for all Thy mercies, seen and unseen. For eternal life, for the heavenly joys of the Kingdom which is to be. Grant mercy to us who sing Thy praise, both now and in the time to come. Glory to Thee, O God, from age to age.
Ikos 1
I was born a weak, defenseless child, but Thine angel spread his wings over my cradle to defend me. From birth until now Thy love has illumined my path, and has wondrously guided me towards the light of eternity; from birth until now the generous gifts of Thy providence have been marvelously showered upon me. I give Thee thanks, with all who have come to know Thee, who call upon Thy name.
Glory to Thee for calling me into being
Glory to Thee, showing me the beauty of the universe
Glory to Thee, spreading out before me heaven and earth
Like the pages in a book of eternal wisdom
Glory to Thee for Thine eternity in this fleeting world
Glory to Thee for Thy mercies, seen and unseen
Glory to Thee through every sigh of my sorrow
Glory to Thee for every step of my life’s journey
For every moment of glory
Glory to Thee, O God, from age to age
I got to thinking during the liturgy how much of a trial these past few post-divorce years have been, and how unfaithful I have been, in terms of despairing. But God is merciful. The time of trial is far from over, but I can see the dawn coming after this weekend. Don’t lost hope!
Kontakion 3
It is the Holy Spirit who makes us find joy in each flower, the exquisite scent, the delicate colour, the beauty of the Most High in the tiniest of things. Glory and honour to the Spirit, the Giver of Life, who covers the fields with their carpet of flowers, crowns the harvest with gold, and gives to us the joy of gazing at it with our eyes. O be joyful and sing to Him: Alleluia!
Ikos 3
How glorious art Thou in the springtime, when every creature awakes to new life and joyfully sings Thy praises with a thousand tongues. Thou art the Source of Life, the Destroyer of Death. By the light of the moon, nightingales sing, and the valleys and hills lie like wedding garments, white as snow. All the earth is Thy promised bride awaiting her spotless husband. If the grass of the field is like this, how gloriously shall we be transfigured in the Second Coming after the Resurrection! How splendid our bodies, how spotless our souls!
Glory to Thee, bringing from the depth of the earth an endless variety of colours, tastes and scents
Glory to Thee for the warmth and tenderness of the world of nature
Glory to Thee for the numberless creatures around us
Glory to Thee for the depths of Thy wisdom, the whole world a living sign of it
Glory to Thee; on my knees, I kiss the traces of Thine unseen hand
Glory to Thee, enlightening us with the clearness of eternal life
Glory to Thee for the hope of the unutterable, imperishable beauty of immortality
Glory to Thee, O God, from age to age
Tarot In The French Quarter
Soon after, she and her two friends — all of them Catholic — went into nearby St. Louis cathedral. Geraldine began to experience weird reactions — blasphemous things against Jesus Christ, and, interestingly, St. Joan of Arc, whose statue is outside on Jackson Square. 
Geraldine and her friends went back home, but she sank into possession. Eventually she was delivered through the power of God via exorcism. It’s a terrifying story, but a hopeful one.
What I love about the way Father Martins tells these stories is that he wants people to understand the scary reality of this stuff, but he also wants people to understand both that there is rescue in Christ, and second, how they should be living as Christians to keep the door closed to demonic attack. Above all, The Exorcist Files is pastoral. 
Father Martins uses this case to strongly warn listeners against tarot cards or any form of divination, which the Bible forbids. We don’t know why some people can mess with this stuff and suffer no apparent spiritual ill effects, and others become demonically afflicted, but it happens, and no good can come from it. At the end of this two-part episode, Father Martins speculates that the tarot card reader might not even have understood the malevolent powers he was channeling. But they are real.
What’s interesting too is that Geraldine, like her friends, was and is a believing Catholic. But her faith was not strong enough to keep her from the tarot stunt, nor was it strong enough to keep her from sleeping with her boyfriend. It came out in the exorcism that this particular sin was part of the bondage the demon claimed over her. Father Martins says we can’t tell ourselves that things like this are not really serious; in the spiritual world, they are, and can lead to spiritual disaster. 
It brought to mind this quote from Aldous Huxley that a Catholic friend texted to me over the weekend:
From Ends and Means: An Inquiry into the Nature of Ideals:
“I had motives for not wanting the world to have a meaning; and consequently assumed that it had none, and was able without any difficulty to find satisfying reasons for this assumption. The philosopher who finds no meaning in the world is not concerned exclusively with a problem in pure metaphysics. He is also concerned to prove that there is no valid reason why he personally should not do as he wants to do. For myself, as no doubt for most of my friends, the philosophy of meaninglessness was essentially an instrument of liberation from a certain system of morality. We objected to the morality because it interfered with our sexual freedom… There was one admirably simple method of confuting these people and justifying ourselves in our erotic revolt: we would deny that the world had any meaning whatever.”
This was 100 percent me during my college years, as I reckoned with whether or not to become a Christian. I saw in retrospect that almost all of what I thought at the time was genuine intellectual struggle with the concept of Christianity was really about me trying to find some rational way out of having to abandon the “erotic revolt” that I so deeply desired. Even now, as a Christian well into middle age, I struggle with how much sexual purity really matters to God. 
I don’t want to give you the false idea that I’m some sort of Yankee tomcat, prowling the streets of Budapest. Rather, it’s the case that we live in a thoroughly eroticized world, one in which even believing Christians like me can be tempted to think that God really doesn’t care as much about impurity as all that. Father Martins’ podcast reminded me that it really does matter, and the spiritual battle for purity in our bodies as well as in our souls is worth fighting. 
He has some very powerful words at the end, assessing how the mere belief in God, and in the Catholic faith, did not protect Geraldine from the Enemy:
Geraldine’s case is revealing in what it teaches us about relationship. Geraldine, Kevin, Emily, and Rachel were sincere Christians. They all had a relationship with Christ, but their faith was too shallow and lukewarm to protect them from evil. It didn’t shape their choices enough to keep them from sinning, such as engaging in a harmless tarot reading on Bourbon Street, or [not] preserving the marital act for marriage. 
What makes a Christian a Christian is not belief in Christ. If that was all it took, then the Devil, who knows the truth perfectly, would be the greatest Christian. What makes a Christian a Christian is his obedience to Christ. 
… Most of you listening will never experience what the Church calls possession. But all of us, without exception, are engaged in the same spiritual warfare, though at a lower temperature. We are daily tempted towards “harmless compromises”: a little curiosity about forbidden things, a little contempt for prayer, a little resentment towards the holy. The Enemy’s strategy is always to make sin look trivial, and repentance look dramatic. In reality, it is the other way around. Sin is what destroys us; repentance is simply coming home.
A little contempt for prayer. How often do I choose not to say morning prayers, because I’m too eager to get online and see what happened overnight? How often do I make short work of evening prayer, because I’m sleepy, and have spent too much time on the Internet, or reading, or whatever? A lot, is the answer. Father Martins reminds us that these seemingly trivial decisions could have very serious consequences for our souls. 
That double episode of The Exorcist Files is also pretty great for the role that St. Joan of Arc plays in this contemporary spiritual battle — especially a relic whose authenticity Father Martins doubted, until the demon possessing Geraldine shrieked at it. As Father says in one of these episodes, the saints aren’t simply some sort of friendly “ghosts” for our contemplation, but great victors in the long spiritual war against the Devil, fighting for us from heaven. 
Judaism And Supersession
I am an Anglican theologian who has been to the Holy Land 25 times and have friends there who are Arab Christians, Muslims, Catholics, and Orthodox. I have written and edited four books and many articles on these questions of the Land, Jewish roots, and Zionism.
You make some good points about Christian Zionism and the plight of Arab Christians in the Holy Land. You are right that the patriarchs remember the many evangelicals and Pentecostals who try to re-convert Arab Christians to their own brand of Christianity, and that many Christian Zionisms (there are many varieties) suggest that the restoration of Jews to their Land was not only prophesied by the Bible but also prophesies the current state of Israel and that this return and state are necessary for an End Times scenario they think imminent.
You are also right to say that some (I would not say “most”) evangelical and Pentecostal Zionists ignore Arab Christians and seem to be concerned only for Jews in the land. And that these Arab Christian leaders in the Land have to negotiate a minefield because of their Islamist neighbors, that their flocks are in daily danger of being accused of not supporting the plight of their fellow Palestinians, and that Pizzaballa is far better than his predecessor.
But would you care to listen to where your snapshot might not be complete?
Second, it is necessary in this contentious and political debate to define what is meant by “Christian Zionism.” You describe it as the belief that God has restored the Jews to their ancient homeland as part of an End Times scenario. You support the state of Israel’s right to exist but believe this Christian Zionist idea has no “theological” basis.
Most people use the term “Christian Zionism” to refer to the 19th-century evangelical movement called dispensationalism that is focused on a “rapture” of true Christians off the planet sometime before the end of the world, and teach a detailed schedule of events they think the Bible says will happen between now and the end of the world.
But there is a “new Christian Zionism” that rejects a rapture and is agnostic on End Times schedules. It argues, however, that God has not ended his eternal covenant with the Jewish people and that the land promise is integral to that covenant. So that the land is still a Holy Land, and that this is taught in not just the Old but also the New Testament.
Even Pizzaballa’s Church has affirmed these two things. Vatican II’s Nostra Aetate quotes Romans 9 to affirm that today’s Jews retain the “sonship and the glory and the covenants and the law and the worship and the promises,” and in 1991, Pope John Paul II referred to the return of Jews to the “mountains of Israel” in the last centuries as a fulfillment of Ezekiel’s promise.
Perhaps Pizzaballa is thinking only of evangelical dispensationalism when he and his colleagues denounce Christian Zionism as “damaging” ideology that “mislead[s] the public.”
So at least one of the major players here (Rome) has actually endorsed the ideas 1) that God’s covenant with the Jewish people is ongoing and was not ended after the time of Jesus, and 2) that the massive return of Jews to the Land starting in the 18th century was prophesied by Scripture.
Now, we scholars in the movement called “the New Christian Zionism” do not say that the current state of Israel is prophesied by the Bible or that it is the last state before the eschaton. But we do agree that the last century has proved that the Jewish people need a state to protect them.
And you agree, Rod, that the state of Israel has often protected Arab Christians from their Islamist neighbors.
I will never forget the day twenty-five years ago when I replaced my replacement theology. I was leading a church tour of Israel, and we were standing on the ruins of a third-century AD synagogue at Capernaum, just a stone’s throw from the sparkling waters of the Sea of Galilee.
Our guide read from a passage I had read hundreds of times but always skipped over: “Jesus said to the crowds and his disciples, ‘On Moses’ seat have sat the scribes and the Pharisees. Everything therefore they might say, put into practice and protect’” (Matt. 23:2–3a; my emphasis and translation).
I was shocked, not only that I had never noticed, but more importantly, that Jesus was praising all the teachings of the scribes and Pharisees. This was just before Jesus launched into a long tirade against what most Christians are used to hearing about: Pharisaic hypocrisy. But this overlooked prelude made me realize suddenly that my previous thinking about Jesus rejecting first-century Judaism needed scrutiny, especially my assumption that God had transferred his covenant from the Jewish people to (what rapidly became) the Gentile Church.
More:
And what about Paul? Did he believe his Jewish brothers who had rejected Jesus thereby lost their Chosen People status? Quite the contrary. In his last major statement on the Jewish people more than twenty-five years after his conversion, he said these Jesus-rejecting brothers “are [note the present tense] beloved because of the fathers,” the patriarchs. Their “gifts and calling are irrevocable” (Rom. 11:28–29).
“Calling” was a technical Jewish term for God’s choosing Abraham’s seed to be his Chosen People, and “gifts” for first-century Jews like Philo the Alexandrian philosopher and Josephus the historian always included the land promise. Lest there be doubt that Paul still believed in the land promise, Luke tells us in Acts of the Apostles that when preaching in a synagogue in Antioch of Pisidia (now Turkey), Paul told his audience that “after destroying seven nations in the land of Canaan, God gave this people Israel their land as an inheritance” (Acts 13: 16b–17, 19). This was more than twenty years after his Damascus road encounter, and Paul could not have been more explicit that he continued to hold to the land promise.
One more quote:
Typically they declare, as Mattson does, that Christian Zionists are presumptuous to find any relation between the Bible and the current state of Israel. Mattson is right, strictly speaking, but not as much as he supposes. Consider the apostle Peter’s prophecy in his second speech in Jerusalem, that the apokatastasis is still to come (Acts 3:21).This was the word used by the (Greek language) Septuagint (the Bible for the early church) that one day there would be a return to the land by Jews from the four corners of the earth (Jeremiah 16:15; 24:6; 50:19; Hosea 11:11).
Many scholars said these Old Testament prophecies referred to the return to the land after exile in Babylon. But Peter was speaking after the resurrection of Christ. He was predicting a future worldwide return to the land—which did not occur in significant numbers for more than seventeen centuries.
Historians have documented a massive return of Jews to the land starting in the eighteenth and then flooding in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Only a prejudiced mind (recall Procrustes’s iron bed) would deny the possibility that this unprecedented return to the land was a fulfillment of not only Old Testament but also New Testament (think Peter’s) prophecy.
I won’t quote the piece at length, so do read it yourself. McDermott is not a “dispensationalist” — a Protestant who believes that the Jewish return to the Land of Israel is part of some Rapture scenario. Nor does he believe that the State of Israel per se is fulfillment of Bible prophecy (but he does say that the Jewish people re-gathered on the land God gave their forefathers have a right to protect themselves, thus the Jewish state.)
Doing a little online research just now, and I see that some Protestant scholars interpret that line of Jesus’s to mean “until non-Jewish rule over Jerusalem comes to an end” — which it did in 1967. I see that the Orthodox Christian consensus is less literal, believing that “the times of the Gentiles” will not be fulfilled until the Second Coming. The Catholic consensus is about the same.
A quote from Israel Matters:
One takeaway from this book is that we Christians are closer to Jewish Israel than we have thought. I like to compare it to the relationship between Scripture and the Church. Christian theologians often say that these two things are mutually informing. Scripture teaches and corrects the Church, and the historic Church teaches its newer members how to understand Scripture. Error usually comes from individual Christians who think they can understand the Bible without being taught by the great holy men and women who came before them, whose accumulated wisdom has shaped the Great Tradition. It is this Tradition that must inform all reading of the Bible. And the Holy Spirit continues to develop that Great Tradition as it further unpacks that blinding revelation that came to Israel and the apostolic church. 
So Scripture informs the Church, and the Church guards and guides the meaning of the Bible. We can now say something similar regarding Israel and the Church. Each informs the other. Israel’s godly and wise rabbis have inestimable riches to share with those Christians who are willing to mine the depths of the rabbinic writings. The vast majority have nothing to do with Jesus as Messiah, but instead with what God has revealed of himself in the first three-fourths of the Christian Bible known as the Old Testament. Their God is our God. We have things to share with them about the identity of the Messiah. But our disagreement on that issue should not prevent us from sitting at the feet of Israel’s sages, as did some of the Christian Fathers, such as Jerome.
Our link with Israel is even closer than this would suggest, however. Christians already believe that the saints of the Old Testament, most of whom are Jews, are in the kingdom of heaven and therefore in communion with us, as the creeds teach. No doubt we are in communion with the Jews of Israel who worship Jesus as Messiah. What about the myriads of Jews down through history who have loved the God of Israel—who Jesus taught us is the only true God—but who have been unable to
see Jesus as Messiah? These are the Jews of whom Paul seems to have been writing when he said that God sent a “hardening on part of Israel” (Rom. 11:25). 
In many cases this was because “Christians” were killing Jews in the name of “Christ.” Can we blame them for not seeing this Christ as their friend? Before he went to Damascus, Paul did not have Christians attacking him, yet he tells us later that God had mercy on him because he had “acted ignorantly in unbelief” (1 Tim. 1:13). If God had mercy on Paul because of his ignorance, might God not have mercy on Jews who are ignorant of Jesus because of “Christian” hatred of Jews in the name of “Christ”?
Jesus taught that no one comes to the Father except through him, and Paul said that to be saved, one must confess with the lips that Jesus is Lord and believe in the heart that God raised him from the dead (John 14:6; Rom. 10:9). Could Jews who have loved their Father in heaven—Jesus’ Father!—but could not see Jesus because of Christian hatred for them in the name of a proclaimed “Christ”—could these Jews have had Jesus revealed to them in some way and time known only to God? Could they—in some way and time only God knows—have confessed with their lips and believed in their hearts?
Jesus hinted that there were hard cases in which someone who has not had revelation from the Holy Spirit and as a result speaks against Jesus can be forgiven: “Whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the age to come” (Matt. 12:32). We do not know what will happen in these hard cases where Christian hatred has blinded Jewish eyes. But we can leave these hard cases to Jesus. In the meantime we can say that we are in communion with all of Israel who love our heavenly Father, who is the God of Israel, and who are on the way to knowing the Son as well as the Father.
Be patient with me here, I’m learning about something I hadn’t thought much about! I guess I’ve always had this intuition that anyone who hates the Jews as Jews (as distinct from despising a bad person who happens to be Jewish) also hates God, who chose them as His people. Now I need to be better informed, theologically, from all sides.
There’s just so much to read! I’m overwhelmed by the things I want to know, and the things I want to read, versus the things I must read for the work I am contracted to do. But who knows? Maybe this is part of it — part of helping me to understand what this new emanation of Jew-hatred in America means, at a deep spiritual level.
Imagine that: a young Zulu man and believer in Jesus comes to the heart of central Europe, discovers ancient Christianity on the banks of the Danube, and accepts as his baptismal name the name of one of the greatest Christian saints — a man of Africa. What a world! Pray for my new friend Anthony as he begins his Orthodox journey.
OK gang, back here tomorrow, to descend into the darkness that is today’s Minneapolis — and which might be a bellwether for tomorrow’s America. I don’t look forward to writing Monday’s newsletter, I tell you what.

---
Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-26 04:12:24+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #254

LLMs are transforming software development, but integrating them into real projects can be tricky when models don’t understand your codebase, pipelines, or conventions.
Join Dagster on Tuesday, January 27th, for a practical look at data engineering best practices, common pitfalls, and live demos of LLM developments.
Debate Alert: Ontology vs Context Graphs vs Semantic Layers: What Will AI Need in 2026?
What are Context Graphs, and why are builders, VCs, and operators calling it the next $1T opportunity? Join The Great Data Debate to get answers to questions the data & AI industry is so curious about right now:
Where does context materialize in practice? Who owns context as meaning evolves?
Semantic layers, ontologies, context graphs - what should data teams build in 2026?
Where should that context live: in the warehouse, inside agents, or in a dedicated context layer?
Join Bob Muglia(former CEO, Snowflake), Karthik Ravindran(GM, Microsoft), Tony Gentilcore(Co-founder, Glean), Prukalpa Sankar(Co-founder, Atlan), and Jaya Gupta(Foundation Capital) for an open discussion on what data teams should actually build next.
Mark Rittman: Why We’ve Tried to Replace Data Analytics Developers Every Decade Since 1974
Perhaps the recurring dream of replacing data analytics developers isn’t a mistake. Perhaps it’s a necessary optimism that drives tool creation.
Developers are always hungry for the next big abstractions. The article is an excellent reminder that Semantic layers didn’t make metric definition trivial, but they made it more maintainable. Self-service BI didn’t remove IT from the equation, but it expanded who could explore data.
Alibaba: AI Trends Reshaping Data Engineering in 2026
Alibaba writes that data engineering is evolving from data movement to building intelligent, autonomous systems in which data serves as a continuously learning capability. The field is getting reshaped by unified data–AI platforms, self-healing operations, context engineering for AI agents, real-time and multimodal pipelines, and privacy-first approaches such as synthetic data and federated learning.
Thoughtworks: The state of data mesh in 2026: From hype to hard-won maturity
As with any scientific theories, it starts with a half-finished, widely debated theory and matures over time. Data Mesh and Data Contract are two such theories; though widely adopted by many companies internally, they still have much room to mature.  ThoughtWorks writes an excellent article about the current state of data mesh adoption. 
Sponsored: The AI Modernization Guide
While 75% of enterprises experiment with AI, traditional data platforms are becoming the biggest bottleneck. Learn how to build a unified control plane that enables AI-driven development, reduces pipeline failures, and cuts complexity.
- Transform from Big Complexity to AI-ready architecture
- Real metrics from organizations achieving 50% cost reductions
- Introduction to Dagster Components: YAML-first pipelines that AI can build
Anthropic: Demystifying evals for AI agents
AI agent development faces evaluation gaps as systems evolve from single-turn prompts to autonomous, multi-step tool use. The article defines agent evals around task outcomes rather than transcripts, using repeated trials, hybrid grading methods, and reliability metrics such as pass@k and pass^k to capture non-deterministic behavior. Starting with small, failure-driven task sets and layering automated evals with production monitoring and human review enables teams to iterate faster, adopt new models safely, and maintain consistent agent reliability.
Booking.com: AI Agent Evaluation - practical tips at Booking.com
Evaluating autonomous AI agents is challenging because simple prompt-based metrics fail to capture whether user intents are reliably fulfilled. The article presents a dual approach combining black-box evaluation focused on task completion using LLM-as-a-judge with glass-box evaluation that inspects tool selection, syntax, and intermediate agent decisions. Benchmarking agents against simpler baselines and measuring consistency across repeated queries helps teams justify added complexity, control costs, and assess production readiness.
LinkedIn: Reimagining LinkedIn’s search tech stack
Traditional keyword-based search struggles to understand user intent, handle natural language queries, and bridge vocabulary gaps at scale. The article describes LinkedIn’s shift to a semantic search stack built on LLM-based query understanding, embedding-based retrieval, and small-language-model ranking, supported by LLM judges, model distillation, and continuous relevance measurement. 
Stefan Kecskes: Kafka Dead Letter Queue Triage: Debugging 25,000 Failed Messages
The DLQ pattern in Event processing is well-known and somewhat widely adopted. However, I’m delighted to read about what happens after a message enters a failed state for the first time. Discard or fix it? The author provides practical tips for analyzing DLQ messages, cautions, and resiliency practices before rebroadcasting the message. 
Teads: The End of the Dashboard as We Know It: Designing for Insight in the Age of AI
Static dashboards fail to support decision-making because they require users to interpret large volumes of data manually. The article argues for AI-driven dashboards that act as proactive, conversational assistants by focusing on scenarios, personalized insights, and transparent recommendations rather than fixed screens. Measuring success by decisions enabled instead of data displayed reframes dashboards as adaptive systems that guide action and improve human–AI collaboration over time.
Flipkart: High-Risk, High-Scale: Guaranteeing Ad Budget Precision at 1 Million Events/Second
Real-time ad budget enforcement at scale is risky because latency can cause advertiser overspend, revenue loss, and delayed mobile events to be miscounted. The article describes Flipkart Ads’ architecture that separates real-time enforcement from batch settlement using Apache Flink, stateful deduplication with RocksDB, and event-time processing with watermarking. Prioritizing availability over strict consistency enables the system to process nearly one million events per second while ensuring accurate budget capping and final financial reconciliation.

Sender: newworkinphilosophy@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-26 13:05:56+00:00
Subject: "The Obligation to Restrict AI in Student Writing" - Mark Robert Taylor
 (Calvin University)

I have had students attempt to submit essays containing AI-generated text as their own. Call this student practice “AI appropriation.” I do not allow AI appropriation in written assignments. I believe that every teacher and every university has a strong obligation to forbid AI appropriation. This is because AI appropriation is morally parallel to plagiarism. Accordingly, universities and teachers who allow students to use AI in cases where they would forbid dependence on other persons are in a position parallel to allowing plagiarism.
When we look at the essential problems with plagiarism that make it impermissible in an educational context, we find that AI appropriation has exactly the same problems. Let’s look at three. The first reason it’s wrong for teachers to allow plagiarism is that letting somebody else write for the student trades formative learning for short-term productivity; it is a refusal to enter fully into the student-teacher relationship. Written submissions represent the student to the teacher by conveying rich and unique information. When a student submits someone else’s writing, he has removed himself from the teacher’s view. She cannot help, guide, or assess him, and feedback becomes pointless. The plagiarizer is “shutting-down the possibility of being educated” (Sadler 2007, 285). If teachers allowed plagiarized essays, the implication would be that productive results are all that matter; the education of the person behind those results is irrelevant. Rather than a social relationship aimed at personal development, the educational dynamic would be reduced to evaluating source-independent output.
The situation is the same for AI appropriation. Teachers cannot understand or evaluate a student if the words submitted originate from elsewhere. Students might instruct the AI in certain ways—just as a plagiarizer would instruct a paper mill author—and this might lead to an essay loosely aligned with the student’s actual opinions. But, in either case, the slight amount of information that leaks through is largely irrelevant and insufficient for the teacher’s use in meaningfully relating to the student as a teacher.
The second reason that allowing plagiarizing essays is unethical is that it undermines the usefulness of educational institutions. Writing shapes students and their thinking, and plagiarism replaces writing. In schools that allowed plagiarism, students’ words would not reflect their abilities. Those who should be informed by students’ degrees would be misled, and students otherwise inclined to do their own work might feel coerced into purchasing essays to keep up with peers who do.
Likewise, the student who is allowed to submit AI-generated essays will leave the university with a degree which falsely testifies to his abilities. Other students have done the hard work of writing, but the AI appropriator has not practiced that skill or the careful thinking required for it. His grade on written assignments means nothing about him. Further, the effect of allowing AI appropriation creates a similarly coercive environment for students who would prefer to write on their own.
A third reason allowing plagiarism is wrong is that plagiarism is parasitic on the social expectation that those around us are using words originating from their inner lives. If I send a love letter with a poem, the receiver assumes I invented it. If an employer receives a cover letter from an applicant, she assumes the applicant, and not somebody else, wrote it. Allowing plagiarism would thus encourage deception because it takes advantage of others’ trust that people are writing for themselves.
AI appropriation likewise violates this social obligation to authenticity. Much of the value of AI’s text output depends on our shared assumption that people’s words are their own. Many of those who appropriate text from AI know that doing so is deceptive and for this reason strongly want to avoid having their use of AI be detectable (Seetharaman and Barnum 2024). AI appropriation, in many cases, is useful to the user only so long as others assume that it is not occurring.
This trio of reasons represents the core of what is wrong with allowing plagiarism and AI appropriation. Some might want to add that plagiarism (unlike AI appropriation) is also a kind of “literary theft.” But this is non-essential, since theft precludes consent and plagiarism can occur when the author consents—or, indeed, when the plagiarizer is the author.
We can conclude that plagiarism runs contrary to education’s basic aims. This is true even when allowing for diverse views on the purpose of education—whether dissemination of knowledge (Bakhurst 2020), formation of students as rational persons (Siegel 2016), developing intellectual virtues (Pritchard 2022), or other, societal, goods (Anderson 2007). Since there is no relevant educational difference between plagiarism and AI appropriation, it would be just as unethical for a school to allow students to submit AI generated essays as it would be to allow them to submit plagiarized essays.
Finally, one might accept everything I’ve argued, but conclude that, practically speaking, we cannot detect AI appropriation, so we cannot justify forbidding its use (Otsuki 2020; Darby 2023; Hicks 2023). This is a common response. Yet, it is important to see that the assertion that teachers have no reliable way of detecting AI appropriation is simply false. If a failing freshman submits a graduate-level essay with independent research, surely we know he didn’t write it. Some recent studies have found that teachers cannot reliably distinguish between random samples of text which are AI-generated versus student-authored (Fleckenstein et al. 2024; Jakesch, Hancock, et al. 2023). But these studies show only that there is nothing in the style or composition of AI-generated text which makes it detectable out of context. Teachers are never in the abstract position of assessing random papers in isolation. We are always assessing our own students in our own courses. In that position, we have calibrated expectations for what particular students should and shouldn’t know. When a student submission radically exceeds the norm, or uses obscure technical terminology, then a conversation is in order. Teachers do not have to be able to detect which submissions were produced by AI, they only need to detect whether it was likely written by their own students. In this way, detecting AI appropriation is no different from detecting plagiarized essays.
Educators are obligated to treat AI contributions to the student writing process the same way they would treat and attribute human contributions. One practical advantage of this approach is that it piggybacks on a clear system of norms which are already in place within universities; teachers and students already know how to treat plagiarism. If we want to know whether a given use of AI should be permissible, we only need to ask, “Would it be alright if another human provided that help or did that work?” If a student is permitted to talk over essay ideas with a classmate, she ought to be permitted to discuss them with AI. If a student is not permitted to submit an outline which her classmate wrote, she should not be permitted to submit an outline which was AI-generated. The way forward is clear. We need only keep reminding ourselves that the nearest precedent for using AI for help with a written assignment is not using word processers—it is asking another person to help write the paper.
Works Cited
Pritchard, Duncan. 2022. “Cultivating Intellectual Virtues.” In Handbook of Philosophy of Education. Routledge.
Seetharaman, Deepa, and Matt Barnum. 2024. “OpenAI Withholds Tool to Nab Students Who Cheat.” Wall Street Journal, Eastern Edition (New York, N.Y., United States), August 5.
Thanks for reading New Work in Philosophy! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-01-26 14:04:01+00:00
Subject: A IA pode mesmo nos ajudar a descobrir novos materiais?

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 26.01.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  A Inteligência Artificial promete acelerar a descoberta de novos materiais, de baterias melhores a semicondutores e ímãs mais eficientes. O problema é que inventá-los segue sendo lento e difícil, e sugerir estruturas no computador não é o mesmo que fabricar algo novo e útil no laboratório. Empresas como a Lila Sciences treinam modelos com literatura científica e os conectam a laboratórios automatizados para encurtar o ciclo de testar e aprender. Mas faltam avanços decisivos e anúncios como o da DeepMind já foram criticados por “novidades” que, no fundo, são pouco inéditas ou inviáveis. 

 

POR: CASEY CROWNHART

 A IA pode mesmo nos ajudar a descobrir novos materiais?

Há novo interesse e financiamento para a área, mas ela está aguardando seu momento de avanço
 A julgar pelas manchetes recentes, parece que a Inteligência Artificial vai resolver tudo, mas, na descoberta de novos materiais, ainda há um longo caminho. A promessa é enorme para a tecnologia climática, que depende de baterias melhores, semicondutores e ímãs mais eficientes. O desafio é que criar materiais é um processo lento e caro, e a área não vê muitos saltos comerciais há décadas. 
É aí que entram empresas como a Lila Sciences, que treina modelos com a literatura científica e os conecta a laboratórios automatizados para acelerar a rodada de imaginar, testar e aprender com resultados reais. O entusiasmo existe, mas falta o “momento de avanço”. Afinal, sugerir estruturas é uma coisa, fabricar e demonstrar novidade e utilidade é outra. 

  
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Big Techs estão contraindo dívidas para financiar aspirações em IA
2. Avalanches de desinformação impulsionados por IA representam uma ameaça à democracia
3. Estamos prestes a começar a ver mais robôs em todos os lugares
4. Algumas pessoas parecem ser especialmente vulneráveis à solidão

5. Este acadêmico perdeu dois anos de trabalho com um único clique

Citação do dia

 
“Na velocidade com que a IA está progredindo, eu acho que teremos uma IA mais inteligente do que qualquer humano ainda este ano. No mais tardar, no próximo”
  The Big Story


Do fogo ao feed: como a tecnologia reprogramou o que comemos

 Janeiro 2026

 

 Cozinhar parece banal, mas foi um dos maiores saltos tecnológicos da humanidade. A neurocientista Suzana Herculano-Houzel sustenta que o fogo “pré-digeriu” a comida, aumentou em até 30% a energia disponível e liberou combustível para o cérebro crescer. Com mais neurônios vieram linguagem, memória e planejamento. 
 A mesma inteligência que reduziu a fome global, no entanto, também criou um sistema alimentar cheio de contradições. Da genética e dos transgênicos ao CRISPR, de cofres de sementes no Ártico a fazendas verticais e rastreabilidade via blockchain, a comida virou uma cadeia hiperindustrial. No fim dela, ultraprocessados dominam o prato, impulsionam doenças crônicas e reforçam a dependência de remédios para reparar danos.

Subject: Como sua empresa incorpora GenAI ao =?UTF-8?B?bmVnw7NjaW8/?=

Em qual cluster sua empresa está?

A resposta dá à liderança clareza para priorizar apostas, incrementar ganhos e reduzir riscos na adoção de IA.

 A discussão sobre IA generativa nas empresas costuma girar em torno de ferramentas e projetos-piloto, mas os dados indicam que o diferencial real está na forma como a tecnologia é incorporada à estratégia do negócio. 
 A pesquisa GenAI na prática organiza as empresas em três clusters que reúnem padrões de adoção, prioridades e níveis de maturidade no uso dessa tecnologia.
Esses perfis apontam processos distintos, que vão desde a institucionalização dos sistemas autônomos, como infraestrutura corporativa, até o uso voltado à inovação e à geração de receita.

[Special Edition] Sua empresa já decidiu as metas de sustentabilidade para 2026? Na nova Special Edition, você entende o que é preciso para transformar estratégia em resultados



O ano de 2026 já está em curso, e pensar em
 metas de sustentabilidade passa por transformar objetivos em resultados reais.

Para muitas empresas, o foco deixa de estar apenas na definição de compromissos e passa a apontar para a construção das condições necessárias para que essas metas avancem na prática.
A nova Special Edition “TI Verde: do discurso à ação”, produzida pela MIT Technology Review Brasil em parceria com a NTT DATA, analisa
por que apenas parte das organizações consegue transformar intenção em resultados mensuráveis.
Com base em dados e evidências, o estudo mapeia os fatores estruturais que permitem que a sustentabilidade se consolide na prática corporativa.

 
Conheça os principais fatores para transformar objetivos de sustentabilidade em ação:
 


Metas
mensuráveis Permitem priorizar investimentos, garantir orçamento e acompanhar resultados ao longo do tempo.



Liderança presente
do C-level O envolvimento da alta gestão amplia a implementação de políticas e dá escala à agenda sustentável.

 

TI integrada à estratégia O uso de dados e a otimização da infraestrutura conectam sustentabilidade a eficiência operacional e redução de impacto.

Subject: =?utf-8?Q?=f0=9f=8e=82_Happy_birthday,_Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart!?=

Happy birthday, Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart!

On 27 January 1756, a musical prodigy was born in Salzburg: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. By the age of five, he was already composing with an ease that seemed almost playful, as if ideas flowed to him naturally. More than two centuries later, audiences around the world continue to be captivated by the immediacy, emotional clarity and humanity of his music. Mozart’s works have been firmly embedded in the repertoire of the Berliner Philharmoniker since the orchestra’s earliest days. Join us in celebrating his 270th birthday and explore outstanding interpretations from our archive, featuring chief conductor Kirill Petrenko alongside world-class guest artists.


So often in Mozart’s sacred music, a particular depth and sense of reassurance comes to the fore – qualities that have lost none of their power over time. The writer Hermann Hesse, in his novel Steppenwolf, once described the transcendent feeling of redemption Mozart’s music evoked in him: “I was reminded of eternity, of the stars. For an hour I could breathe again, could live, without suffering torment or fear.” For the 2023 Europakonzert in Barcelona’s Sagrada Família, Kirill Petrenko devised a programme that was both ceremonial and life-affirming. Mozart’s Coronation Mass was paired with the Symphony No. 25 and the motet “Exsultate, jubilate”, featuring soprano Louise Alder.



Flowing melodic lines, playful elegance, a lyrical warmth and, beneath the surface, moments of poignant tension: these elements define Mozart’s unmistakable style. In his own time, he astonished audiences not only as a composer but also as a brilliant improvising pianist. Today, his piano concertos stand as some of the finest achievements of Classical composition. Our playlist invites you into this remarkable sound world, with performances by leading artists including Mitsuko Uchida, Daniel Barenboim, Emanuel Ax, Maria João Pires and Maurizio Pollini.

Sender: johnathanbi@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-27 17:02:02+00:00
Subject: Venezuela, Greenland, and the Return of Geopolitical Expansion

I gave this lecture months before Venezuela & Greenland, but it’s now more relevant than ever. It’s on Machiavelli’s case for conquest: why great nations should pursue foreign expansion.
In his Discourses, Machiavelli wrestles with this choice of stability (Sparta/Venice) or conquest (Rome). While he identifies advantages & disadvantages of both, he ultimately sides with conquest as the better strategy. Machiavelli warns us that our modern obsession with peace is a lot more dangerous than first meets the eye. For easy times make soft men, and soft men make hard times.
We moderns want to reply to Machiavelli that conquest itself is morally repugnant: that even if it is successful, the conqueror becomes corrupted through the process. Machiavelli flips this logic on its head: it’s those who know only peace who are morally decadent, becoming soft and effeminate. In other words, what’s so shocking about reading Machiavelli is not just THAT he urges us to conquer but WHY. He believes that there is something spiritually & morally healthy in conquering others:
Danger cleanses your soul. War brings sobriety. And violence is the best moral teacher.
Let me be clear. I do not agree with Machiavelli’s prescriptions of geopolitical expansion. A return to the 20th century is neither possible nor desirable. But, I do think Machiavelli’s insights are a serious challenge to our modern complacency and, perhaps, overvaluing of peace. I will end this lecture by developing this critique and point to other avenues of expansion that are available to us today.
Topics We Cover:
To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a paid subscriber.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br

Subject: The Checkup | Sarampo detectado em =?UTF-8?B?w6FndWFz?= residuais

The CheckupSarampo detectado em águas residuais

Por Roberta Arinelli, Editora-executiva de Saúde  • 27.01.25

 
 Olá! Seja bem-vindo à The Checkup, a newsletter semanal de Biotech and Health da MIT Technology Review Brasil.

Às terças-feiras, trazemos temas essenciais sobre saúde, biotecnologia e inovação para você acompanhar as principais tendências da área.
Nesta edição, o destaque é a análise de Jess Hamzelou, repórter sênior da MIT Technology Review, sobre a vigilância do sarampo por meio de águas residuais, uma estratégia que pode antecipar surtos, revelar subnotificação e apoiar decisões rápidas de saúde pública em um cenário de queda da vacinação e reemergência global da doença.
 Na curadoria científica da semana, três destaques: sensores criados por IA para detectar câncer precocemente, uma nova estratégia para regenerar cartilagem e conter a osteoartrite, e evidências de que traços de transtorno do déficit de atenção e hiperatividade na infância impactam a saúde ao longo da vida.
 No episódio do podcast de Biotech and Health da última semana, Camila Pepe e Carolina Abelin conversam com Mauricio Barreto, pesquisador da Fiocruz Bahia, sobre o uso de tecnologia para enfrentar os impactos das mudanças climáticas na saúde.
Boa leitura!

 
Sarampo nas águas

À medida que as taxas de vacinação caem e os surtos continuam, cientistas têm experimentado outras formas de identificar rapidamente novos casos e impedir a propagação da doença. E eles estão começando a ver algum sucesso com a vigilância de águas residuais (esgoto).
 Afinal, o esgoto contém saliva, urina, fezes, células de pele eliminadas e muito mais. Pode-se considerá-lo uma amostra biológica rica. A análise de águas residuais ajudou cientistas a entender como a Covid-19 estava se espalhando durante a pandemia. Ainda é cedo, mas essa abordagem já começa a nos ajudar a compreender melhor o sarampo.
Ainda assim, estima-se que cerca de 95 mil pessoas tenham morrido de sarampo apenas em 2024, a maioria crianças pequenas. E os casos estão aumentando na Europa, no Sudeste Asiático e na região do Mediterrâneo Oriental.
 Os esforços de saúde pública para conter a disseminação do sarampo, que é extremamente contagioso, normalmente envolvem monitoramento clínico em ambientes de saúde, juntamente com campanhas de vacinação. Mas os cientistas também começaram a olhar para o esgoto.
 Junto com diversos fluidos corporais, todos nós eliminamos vírus e bactérias no esgoto, seja ao escovar os dentes, tomar banho ou usar o banheiro. A ideia de procurar esses patógenos no esgoto para monitorar doenças existe há algum tempo, mas ganhou força de verdade durante a pandemia de Covid, quando os cientistas descobriram que o coronavírus responsável pela doença era eliminado nas fezes.
Desde então, ela e seus colegas desenvolveram um teste para identificar o RNA do vírus do sarampo. Eles o testaram em duas estações de tratamento de esgoto no Texas entre dezembro de 2024 e maio de 2025. Em cada local, a equipe coletou amostras duas ou três vezes por semana e as analisou em busca de RNA do sarampo.
Há resultados ainda mais promissores vindos de uma equipe no Canadá. Mike McKay e Ryland Corchis-Scott, da Universidade de Windsor, em Ontário, e seus colegas também vêm testando amostras de esgoto em busca de RNA do sarampo. Entre fevereiro e novembro de 2025, a equipe coletou amostras de uma estação de tratamento de esgoto que atende mais de 30 mil pessoas em Leamington, Ontário.
 Esses testes de esgoto são um tanto limitados. Mesmo que detectem o sarampo, eles não informam quem tem a doença, onde exatamente as infecções estão ocorrendo, nem quantas pessoas estão infectadas. McKay e seus colegas começaram a avançar nesse ponto. Além de monitorar a grande estação de tratamento, a equipe usou absorventes internos (tampões) para absorver esgoto de um ramal lateral de um hospital.
 Em seguida, compararam os resultados dos testes de sarampo com o número de casos clínicos naquele hospital. Isso lhes deu alguma ideia da “taxa de eliminação” do vírus. Quando aplicaram esse parâmetro aos dados coletados na estação de tratamento de Leamington, a equipe obteve estimativas de casos de sarampo muito mais altas do que os números oficialmente reportados.
 Sempre haverá limites para a vigilância de águas residuais. “Estamos olhando para o conjunto de resíduos de toda uma comunidade, então é muito difícil obter informações sobre infecções individuais”, diz Corchis-Scott.
 Wolfe também reconhece que “ainda temos muito a aprender sobre como usar melhor essas ferramentas para que sejam úteis”. Mas sua equipe no WastewaterSCAN vem testando esgoto em todo os Estados Unidos para sarampo desde maio do ano passado. E os resultados são publicados online e compartilhados com autoridades de saúde pública.
McKay diz que sua equipe parou de testar para sarampo porque o surto em Ontário “foi declarado encerrado”. Ele afirma que os testes seriam retomados se e quando um novo caso único de sarampo fosse confirmado na região, mas também acredita que suapesquisa apresenta um forte argumento para a manutenção de um sistema de vigilância de águas residuais para o sarampo.
 McKay se pergunta se essa abordagem poderia ajudar o Canadá a recuperar seu status de eliminação do sarampo. “É como se fôssemos um pária agora”, diz ele. Se sua abordagem puder ajudar a limitar surtos de sarampo, ela poderia ser “uma boa ferramenta de saúde pública no Canadá para [mostrar] que estamos com tudo em ordem”.
Situação do sarampo no Brasil
 Devido a ausência de registros de novos casos de sarampo no Brasil em 2015, o país recebeu em 2016 a certificação de eliminação do vírus, mantendo-se sem casos confirmados em 2016 e 2017. Entretanto, a partir de 2018, o vírus voltou a circular, impulsionado pelo intenso fluxo migratório e pela queda das coberturas vacinais. Em 2019, após mais de 12 meses de circulação contínua do genótipo D8, o Brasil perdeu a certificação de país livre do sarampo.
No mesmo ano, foram confirmados cinco casos, sendo quatro associados a viagens internacionais e um caso autóctone, sem fonte de infecção identificada
Em 2025, o Brasil confirmou 38 casos de sarampo, distribuídos no Distrito Federal (1), Rio de Janeiro (2), São Paulo (2), Rio Grande do Sul (1), Tocantins (25), Maranhão (1) e Mato Grosso (6), evidenciando a dispersão geográfica do vírus no território nacional.
 Apesar disso, o Ministério da Saúde afirma que registros esporádicos de Sarampo não interferem a manutenção da recertificação de 2024.


 
Pela web

Sensores criados por IA abrem novos caminhos para a detecção precoce do câncer
 Pesquisadores do MIT e da Microsoft desenvolveram sensores moleculares projetados por IA capazes de identificar sinais iniciais de câncer com alta sensibilidade. A estratégia utiliza nanopartículas revestidas por peptídeos que reagem à atividade excessiva de proteases (enzimas frequentemente hiperativas em células tumorais) liberando sinais detectáveis na urina. A abordagem pode permitir testes simples, potencialmente realizados em casa, e ajudar a identificar diferentes tipos de câncer ainda em estágios iniciais. 
 O avanço foi possível com o desenvolvimento do modelo de IA CleaveNet, capaz de desenhar peptídeos altamente específicos para proteases associadas ao câncer, superando métodos tradicionais de tentativa e erro. Ao focar enzimas-chave, como a MMP13, o sistema gera sensores mais precisos, ampliando o sinal biológico e reduzindo custos experimentais. Além do uso diagnóstico, os peptídeos criados por IA também podem ser incorporados a terapias direcionadas, liberando medicamentos apenas no ambiente tumoral. A descoberta abre caminho para uma nova geração de testes precoces e tratamentos mais precisos contra o câncer. 

 Uma nova estratégia para regenerar cartilagem e conter a osteoartrite
 Pesquisadores identificaram uma estratégia capaz de reverter a perda de cartilagem associada ao envelhecimento e prevenir o desenvolvimento de osteoartrite após lesões no joelho. O bloqueio de uma proteína ligada ao envelhecimento, a enzima 15-PGDH, restaura a cartilagem hialina funcional em articulações de camundongos idosos e melhora significativamente a mobilidade. Amostras de cartilagem humana obtidas em cirurgias de artroplastia também passaram a regenerar quando expostas ao tratamento.
  A enzima 15-PGDH, chamada pelos autores de “gerozima”, aumenta com a idade e degrada a prostaglandina E2, molécula essencial para o reparo tecidual. Ao inibir essa enzima, houve espessamento da cartilagem, proteção contra osteoartrite após lesões semelhantes à ruptura do ligamento cruzado anterior e melhora funcional dos animais. A regeneração da cartilagem não envolveu células-tronco, mas sim a reprogramação genética dos condrócitos, que retornaram a um perfil mais jovem e regenerativo.
Ensaios clínicos de fase 1 com inibidores de 15-PGDH já demonstraram segurança em humanos para outras indicações.

 Traços de TDAH na infância podem impactar a saúde ao longo da vida
 Pesquisadores mostraram que sinais de transtorno do déficit de atenção e hiperatividade (TDAH) na infância estão associados a piores desfechos de saúde física na meia-idade. O estudo acompanhou quase 11 mil pessoas dos 10 aos 46 anos e identificou maior risco de múltiplas condições físicas e de incapacidade relacionada à saúde entre aqueles com traços elevados de TDAH na infância.
 O trabalho aponta 14% mais chance de relatar duas ou mais doenças físicas aos 46 anos, como enxaqueca, dor nas costas, câncer, epilepsia ou diabetes, além de maior probabilidade de limitações funcionais, especialmente entre mulheres.
 Os pesquisadores apontam que esses desfechos refletem uma combinação de fatores, incluindo maior prevalência de problemas de saúde mental, IMC elevado, tabagismo e menor acesso a diagnóstico, acompanhamento médico e suporte ao longo da vida. Os achados reforçam a necessidade de estratégias de saúde pública e modelos de cuidado contínuo que considerem o TDAH não apenas como um transtorno do neurodesenvolvimento infantil, mas como uma condição com implicações sistêmicas e duradouras para a saúde.

Com capital externo, bolsa bate recorde e dólar cai a R$ 5,20
O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta terça-feira (27/1) em alta de 1,79%, aos 181.919,13 pontos, renovando o recorde de fechamento. O avanço foi sustentado pela continuidade do forte fluxo de capital estrangeiro para ativos brasileiros, impulsionado pelo diferencial de juros e pela estratégia de carry trade, em um pregão de expectativa em relação às decisões de política monetária no Brasil e nos Estados Unidos. Em janeiro, a Bolsa já acumula valorização de 12,91%...

Previc fará cinco eventos para discutir mudanças na Resolução 26
A Superintendência Nacional de Previdência Complementar (Previc) realizará a partir do dia 6 de março uma série semanal de cinco seminários virtuais, na qual abordará de forma detalhada as alterações e aperfeiçoamentos da Resolução Previc 26/2025. Os primeiros quatro eventos acontecerão em março, nos dias 6, 13, 20 e 27, e o quinto em 10 abril...

Alagoas Previdência fecha 2025 com rentabilidade acima da meta
A Alagoas Previdência, regime próprio de previdência do estado de Alagoas, encerrou 2025 com uma rentabilidade anual de 11,63%, superando sua meta atuarial do mesmo período, que ficou em 8,75% (IPCA + 4,30%). O resultado, o melhor do instituto desde 2022, foi obtido com 100% dos recursos aplicados em renda fixa, distribuídos entre títulos públicos, fundos referenciados e pequena parcela em ativos prefixados...

Capef divulga rentabilidade dos planos em 2025
A Capef, fundo de pensão dos funcionários do Banco do Nordeste do Brasil, divulgou a rentabilidade de seus dois principais planos em dezembro do ano passado. O plano de Benefício Definido (BD), com patrimônio de R$ 4,30 bilhões, fechou dezembro com rentabilidade de 0,66% no mês e 10,18% em 2025, para metas atuariais de 0,66% e 9,35%, respectivamente. Já o plano de Contribuição Variável (CV I), com patrimônio de R$ 3,19 bilhões, rendeu 0,97% no mês e 12,64% em 2025, para metas de 0,76% e 9,48%, nessa ordem...

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-01-28 09:03:32+00:00
Subject: We're Losing European Nationalist Conservatives

Good morning from the train from Bratislava, the capital of Slovakia, to Budapest. Just got a message that I left my Kindle behind at the hotel. Honestly, it is impossible for me to travel without losing stuff. I hate myself for that. Anyway, I love the word “Bratislava”; it makes me think of these two delightful personalities of my youth:
No need to go deep into it here. Y’all have heard it from me here in recent days. Let’s just say that my people in Bratislava are shocked and angry at Trump over his behavior. Shocked, because they really supported him — and this is very unusual in Europe, you see, even in relatively conservative Slovakia. They really had high hopes that he would change things for the better, especially after JD Vance’s Munich speech, which thrilled them. 
But now they don’t know what to think. I had several different conversations about it, and the basic theme that emerged was something like this: 
Yes, Trump tells us Europeans some difficult truths, things we need to hear. But he does it so crudely and insultingly that he makes Europeans feel belittled and humiliated. Why? How does this help America, or conservatives like us, who have for a long time been fighting a lonely battle for the kind of change Trump says he wants? 
One Slovak conservative told me that Americans should not be surprised if Europeans turn toward China now. Another said to me that he had so much hope a year ago, when Trump first took office, but now is pretty discouraged. 
It’s as if the only people who exist in their minds are the Enemy Libs, whose tears taste oh so sweet, and Team MAGA, who deserves its performative thrills (which include making libs cry). Hey, I have a taste for lib tears too, at times, but more than anything, I want to achieve goals, not simply enjoy the misery of my enemies (and honestly, I don’t seriously savor political sadism). There’s a whole world of people out there who are unsure quite what to think, but who are persuadable — which it’s how it’s supposed to be in a democracy.
Matt Walsh tweeted this in satirical disrespect of the whiners who complain about ICE enforcement methods:
I want our immigration laws to be enforced, but I want the government to do it without cutting advertisements that make it look like it’s in love with its own punitive powers, without constantly dissembling and shouting about internal enemies and acting like its own errors don’t exist, and without enforcement operations creating a police-state atmosphere for a major metropolitan area that lasts for weeks.
To Walsh and other advocates of permanent confrontation, maybe even that reformulation sounds like hapless surrender. But to me, these people are constructing a dream palace where polling and mainstream opinion don’t matter at all, where just showing strength is enough to carry waverers and anxious normies with you, where a magical final victory awaits that doesn’t require compromise or coalition management.
The woke weren’t bound by polls and public opinion, comes the reply. Yes, and look what it got them: a decisive electoral defeat and a generational opportunity for conservatism, which the Trump administration has been giving away, piece by piece and controversy by controversy, since the “vibe shift” days of late 2024.
And Yet, The Dems Still Suck
But if Democrats are to succeed in excising the Trump malignancy from the body politic, their party faces a major hurdle: public distrust, if not downright animosity.
Yascha Mounk, a political scientist at Johns Hopkins, summed up the Democratic dilemma in an email responding to my queries:
Democrats would be extremely foolish to think that the temporary advantage given to them by Trump’s unpopularity amounts to a permanent fix of their deeply rooted image problem. The party’s favorability ratings remain at record lows.
And while Democrats may temporarily be de-emphasizing some of the rhetoric that made them so unpopular, most voters do not believe that they have had a real change of heart about wokeness or D.E.I. — much less that they have a coherent set of political ideas to fill the resulting vacuum.
Put another way, Democrats — and their liberal allies — must persuade voters to have faith in the legitimacy, fairness and strength of the party, its institutional supporters and its network of advocacy groups. They need to be able to restore the left’s claim to the mantle of free and fair elections, neutral administration of justice, individual rights and an end to corruption.
More:
The authors tracked key word usage in Democratic platforms from 2012 to 2024 and found the frequency of the word “hate” increasing by 1,323 percent; “white/Black/Latino/Latina” by 1,137 percent; “L.G.B.T./L.G.B.T.Q.I.+” by 1,044 percent; and “equity” by 766 percent.
Over the same period, usage of “father/fathers” fell 100 percent; “crime/criminal” by 30 percent; “responsibility” by 83 percent; “middle class” by 79 percent; and “veteran” by 31 percent.
And:
“Here I stand, in 2026, and America’s long arc of liberalism appears to have bent straight into the dirt,” Smith wrote.
Like many liberals of the old school, Smith continued,
I watched with concern as the quest to end discrimination against Black Americans evolved into a desire to institutionalize discrimination against white Americans in universities, nonprofits, government agencies and many corporations — something the liberals of the 1990s swore they would never countenance. …
I watched as the gay rights movement gave way to a trans movement that was deeply out of step with both America’s beliefs and civil rights law. …
After decades of mass incarceration, a loose alliance of progressive D.A.s, judges and anti-police protesters shifted blue cities toward far more permissive policies toward property crime, public drug markets and low-level assaults and harassment. The progressivism that emerged in the 2010s seems to view anarchy as a form of welfare, believing that the best way to help the poor and unfortunate was to allow the worst and most violent among them to terrorize the rest of them without restraint or reprisal.
The sociologist Musa al-Gharbi tells Edsall:
Democrats have little credibility because they have been supporting lots of censorship on everything from insufficiently progressive views on race and gender, to suppressing views on contentious political issues in the name of fighting “misinformation” and more.
The party that represents these people, or at least will not emphatically condemn them, will never, ever have my vote. I think many of them would actually kill people like me if they could. This does not mean I have much love for the GOP. It means that if I have to choose between a party that hates people like me, and one that, for all its many flaws, does not — well, there’s not much choice, is it? 
Do better, Republicans! We cannot let these people be voted back into power. 
Today In Weimar America
Here’s some good news from Publisher’s Weekly, about new book deals:
So this is happening! Thank you, Tom and Gary. The title is suggested by the publisher. I’m keen on Warning To Weimar America, but they didn’t care for it. We’ll negotiate, but I can live with that one if it comes to it, because it’s accurate.
Once again (for new subscribers): when I compare conditions in contemporary America to 1920s Germany, I’m not claiming that America has suffered traumas like the loss of a major war that killed two million of our men, or hyperinflation that wiped out the savings of tens of millions, or the Great Depression. Nevertheless, as David Betz keeps saying about how we in the West are ripe for civil war, I’m saying that the psychosocial conditions in the US today are eerily parallel to what existed in the Weimar Republic, which led ultimately to Germany succumbing to totalitarianism. 
One thing I see from reading Weimar histories — and this is very clear in the excellent German noir series Babylon Berlin — is that the far left was highly active in 1920s Germany, trying to destabilize the democratic Weimar Republic. Hitlerism remained unpopular as late as 1928, when the Nazis polled a measly 2.7 percent in national elections. It took further economic catastrophe (the Depression), the paralysis of the Bundestag, and the masses becoming fed up with social disorder and cultural decadence, for Nazism to catch fire in the popular mind. 
The point is not to look at the Left and say, “Look what you made us do.” The point is to examine the conditions that made left-wing and right-wing extremism seem plausible to more and more people in a particular time and place, as the only way out. Folks, we are there in America today. We really are. 
The other side in the moral conflict here doesn’t have to prejudge matters of fact. The Border Patrol officer or officers who shot Pretti might have been wrong. But the presumptions of civilization are on law enforcement’s side. If there is violence between officers and someone else, officers have the superior right to use force. The attempt to negate laws through mob actions and systematic harassment campaigns – which is what the anti-ICE activists have been conducting – are wrong in themselves, whether or not the laws are entirely right. This is because, as everyone knows, America is not a totalitarian society, elections do matter and immigration enforcement is necessary for any country. It’s a basic form of the rule of law; it is not fascism.
In fact, the use of mobs to intimidate designated enemies and overawe the forces of law is historically characteristic of fascism and other revolutionary movements. The anti-ICE movement may not be fascist, but if it were really so sensitive about anything that bears the faintest similarity to fascism, it would have to renounce its present tactics. That won’t happen because while the anti-ICE activists and their allies in the media and politics do truly hate Donald Trump and anything that defends our immigration laws, they don’t actually believe that fascism has come to America. It may be Trump they hate, but it’s America they’re angry at – the left considers the country quite racist and wicked enough on its own terms, without its having to owe any debt to Mussolini, while the NeverTrumpers are cynics who are happy to ally with ideologues who aspire to revolution, as long as doing so promises to hurt the man (and the MAGA movement) they resent.
More:
Trump’s presence in the White House is intolerable because he doesn’t defer to progressives in using the power voters have given him. Choosing not to enforce the law is as much an assertion of power as enforcing it – it’s an assertion of the power of left-wing ideology over constitutional government, in defiance of voters’ control.
Democrats are brazenly open about their contempt for immigration laws, as seen not only in Joe Biden’s performance as president but also in their declaration of “sanctuary” cities and states, where local authorities will not cooperate in enforcing federal law. The Democrats can afford to be so bold because they have power bases outside elected office, in the courts, the media, and in the streets. The protesters are a wing – an armed wing, in the case of Pretti – of a para-government. This unconstitutional alternative government is what is laying claim to power in the Minneapolis tumult.
Are you seeing in the liberal media condemnation, or even disquiet, over the Minneapolis mobs? Of course you aren’t. Even the kinds of liberals who would not join that mob see no real problem with them. They really are saying to the Right, “Look what you made us do.” They have contempt for democracy and the rule of law when democracy and rule of law produces results they hate. I have criticized in this space the overreach of ICE, but let’s not forget that these mobs in Minneapolis are doing what they do in defense of foreigners who have broken the law. 
Once again, to my liberal readers: do you see why this kind of thing drives normal people to Trump? What are people supposed to think about mobs, backed by opposition political leaders, who engage in abusive and, well, fascist tactics (the use of force and the realistic threat of violence to intimidate political opponents)? As a Trump-supporting conservative, but not an uncritical one, I see how Trump’s overreach sours normal people on him and his party — and that’s why I want him, and his administration, to change their tactics. Not their strategic goal — deportation is just, legal, and necessary — but their tactics. Nevertheless, these Antifa operatives and their fellow travelers are utterly repugnant, and a clear and present threat to law and order, as well as to democracy. 
Decent people on both the Left and the Right have a responsibility to be aware of what’s happening in Weimar America, and choose to act accordingly. If you only see the problem as one of The Other Side, you are deluding yourself. In 2020, when I published Live Not By Lies — it came out during Covid and after the Summer of Floyd, the latter of which catalyzed a spasm of leftist, anti-white radicalism within captured institutions, it was definitely a problem primarily of the Left. I believe it still is mostly a problem of the Left, but by no means exclusively — and, as we are seeing now, the rise of a radicalized young vanguard among politicized right-wing elites is a real thing. 
In one of the Weimar histories I just finished, the historian said that Hitlerism had special appeal to young German men of the 1920s, because they had only known great instability from the war and its aftermath, and they were terrified about their futures. If you are a white male in your mid-twenties now, you grew up in post-9/11, woke America, and with the Internet. You are open to this stuff in ways that older people aren’t, in part because you have likely experienced first-hand how much contempt the Left in power within institutions has for you. You have no memory of the Before Times, when even the liberals were actually liberal, not illiberal leftists. And you will have seen how useless establishment Republicans, terrified of being called racist, were at protecting your interests, and defending a liberal democratic social order. 
Don’t forget what my Zoomer right-wing friend active in DC politics told me last fall when I asked him what the Groyper and Groyper-adjacents of his generation want: “The don’t want anything. They want to tear it all down.” 
What A Single Good Man Can Accomplish

Subject: Em Davos, tudo era sobre IA e Donald Trump

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 28.01.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  Em Davos, o assunto era só um. Na verdade, dois: Inteligência Artificial e Donald Trump. A cidade foi ocupada por “casas” de big techs e debates lotados sobre IA com CEOs descrevendo ganhos reais, de bilhões em eficiência a prontuários automatizados, e o avanço do comércio com agentes. Nos bastidores, Trump pautou piadas, tensão e medo, e a oposição subiu o tom.

 

POR: MAT HONAN

Tudo o que falaram em Davos é sobre IA e Donald Trump

Os dois tópicos dominaram as conversas oficiais e paralelas
 Em Davos, na Suíça, bastava puxar conversa para cair sempre nos mesmos temas: Inteligência Artificial e Donald Trump, presidente dos Estados Unidos. A cidade foi tomada por “casas” temporárias na Promenade, quase todas de big techs, sinal de como a tecnologia capturou a economia global. 
 No Centro de Congressos, foram apresentados casos concretos, bilhões em economia de custos, prontuários automatizados que devolvem tempo a profissionais de saúde e a promessa de um comércio com agentes que, em 2026, pode comprar por você com base em hábitos e preferências.

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Trabalhadores de tecnologia estão pressionando chefes para condenar o ICE
2. FBI está investigando chats do Signal que rastreiam agentes federais
3. Grok não é seguro para crianças ou adolescentes
4. Futuro da cidade futurista da Arábia Saudita está em risco

5. Influenciadores gerados por IA estão ficando cada vez mais surreais

Citação do dia

 
“A humanidade está prestes a receber um poder quase inimaginável, e não está nada claro se nossos sistemas sociais, políticos e tecnológicos possuem a maturidade para exercê-lo”
  The Big Story


Do fogo ao feed: como a tecnologia reprogramou o que comemos

 Janeiro 2026

 

 Cozinhar parece banal, mas foi um dos maiores saltos tecnológicos da humanidade. A neurocientista Suzana Herculano-Houzel sustenta que o fogo “pré-digeriu” a comida, aumentou em até 30% a energia disponível e liberou combustível para que o cérebro crescesse. Com mais neurônios vieram linguagem, memória e planejamento. 
 A mesma inteligência que reduziu a fome global, no entanto, também criou um sistema alimentar cheio de contradições. Da genética e dos transgênicos ao CRISPR, de cofres de sementes no Ártico a fazendas verticais e rastreabilidade via blockchain, a comida virou uma cadeia hiperindustrial. No fim dela, ultraprocessados dominam o prato, impulsionam doenças crônicas e reforçam a dependência de remédios para reparar danos.
