Sender: news@digitalconcerthall.com
Date: 2026-02-06 12:57:17+00:00
Subject: New: Sir Simon Rattle and Ian Bostridge at the 2011 Lucerne Festival

New: Sir Simon Rattle and Ian Bostridge at the Lucerne Festival

At the 2011 Lucerne Festival, the programmes focused on the theme of night. The Berliner Philharmoniker and Sir Simon Rattle contributed likewise: Benjamin Britten’s Nocturne, sometimes meditative and dreamy, but mostly dark and frightening, explores this nocturnal theme. The soloist in the evocative composition for tenor and small orchestra was Ian Bostridge. Simon Rattle paired the piece with Anton Bruckner’s unfinished Ninth Symphony – a moving “farewell to life”.


Please note: The post-production of the concert from 30 January 2026 with Kirill Petrenko and Janine Jansen has unfortunately been delayed. Thank you for your patience!

Paavo Järvi conducts Bruckner’s Second Symphony

A delicately shimmering violin tremolo, beneath which the cellos swell into a sweeping melody – even the opening bars of this Second Symphony bear the unmistakable signature of Anton Bruckner. Paavo Järvi and the Berliner Philharmoniker demonstrated in 2019 just how captivating the emotional roller-coaster, sudden breaks and suspenseful pauses are in this composition, which is often unfairly dismissed as an early work – our recommendation of the week.


“I bring music out into the world,” says Lang Lang, whose career is not only defined by dazzling concert performances. Time and again, the star pianist can be seen on unusual stages, whether at the opening ceremony of the Olympic Games or together with the heavy metal band Metallica. In collaboration with the Berliner Philharmoniker’s Education Programme, he organised the 1000 Fingers workshop in 2010, in which he rehearsed and performed a piece by Schubert with 100 piano students of all ages.


Welcome the Berliner Philharmoniker into your home – on your TV, computer, tablet or smartphone. Live or on-demand from our archive.

                                                Digital Concert Hall vouchers


The Digital Concert Hall is the online concert venue of the Berliner Philharmoniker. Here, their concerts are broadcast live and are also available as recordings in a video archive a few days later. The Digital Concert Hall is available on televisions, computers, smartphones, tablets and streaming devices. Its streaming partner is Internet Initiative Japan Inc. (IIJ).



Berlin Phil Media GmbH

Leipziger Platz 1

10117 Berlin

Germany

Managing directors: Olaf Maninger, Marcus Forchner

Registered in: Berlin-Charlottenburg, Germany

VAT-ID: DE260 440 601

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-02-06 14:03:37+00:00
Subject: Por que os chatbots =?UTF-8?B?ZXN0w6NvIGNvbWXDp2FuZG8=?= a verificar
 a sua idade

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 06.02.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  A preocupação com crianças conversando com chatbots de Inteligência Artificial reacendeu a pressão por verificação de idade nos Estados Unidos. O debate gira em torno de leis que exijam checagem, seja para sites adultos, seja para proteger menores em chats, e a disputa virou quem será responsável por isso. A OpenAI planeja estimar a idade por sinais de uso e aplicar filtros, mas erros podem exigir selfie ou documento por meio de uma empresa chamada Persona, levantando críticas sobre vieses e privacidade. 

 

POR: JAMES O'DONNELL

 Por que os chatbots estão começando a verificar a sua idade

Confirmar quais usuários são crianças é politicamente delicado e um pesadelo técnico
 A preocupação com crianças conversando com chatbots de IA fez a verificação de idade voltar ao centro do debate nos Estados Unidos. Durante anos, bastava informar uma data de nascimento; algo nada complexo, sendo a informação verdadeira ou não. Agora, estados avançam com leis que exijam checagem, seja para sites com conteúdo adulto, seja para proteger menores em chats. 
 A disputa deixou de ter foco apenas na verificação, observando também quem vai assumir o ônus técnico, político e jurídico. A OpenAI diz que pretende estimar automaticamente a idade com sinais de uso, como horário do dia, aplicando filtros para reduzir a exposição a violência e a conteúdo sexual. Quem for classificado como menor de idade pode ter de confirmar identidade com selfie ou documento via a empresa Persona, um caminho criticado por vieses e riscos de privacidade.  
 
 
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. As novas ferramentas de programação da Anthropic estão abalando os mercados
2. Configuração da Apple impediu FBI de acessar o iPhone de repórter
3. A interrupção em um data center, no mês passado, afetou todas as categorias do TikTok
4. As Big Techs estão despejando bilhões em IA na Índia

5. YouTubers estão assediando mulheres usando câmeras corporais

Citação do dia

 
“Nós ouvimos muitos dizerem que não querem ter nada a ver com IA”
The Big Story


 Desafios e impactos da crise climática no sistema alimentar

Janeiro 2026



 Enquanto você lê esta frase, o mundo já acumulou mais alguns milhões de dólares em danos ambientais. De acordo com a sétima edição do Global Environment Outlook, do PNUMA, de 2025, a produção de alimentos e o uso de combustíveis fósseis custam ao planeta US$ 5 bilhões por hora, cerca de R$ 27 bilhões. 
 A combinação de perda de biodiversidade, solos degradados, poluição e desperdício ameaça safras, eleva preços e reduz a qualidade nutricional, e o aquecimento pode encolher o PIB global em 4%, até 2050. Na COP30, em Belém, a Declaração de Belém e o Pacote Belém defenderam inserir fome e justiça social nas metas climáticas, com revisão em 2028. 

   


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil

 Últimos dias para confirmar presença no WebinarUm encontro com especialistas para compreender o novo tabuleiro geopolítico da energia e seus desdobramentos estratégicos.

 


 
Faltam poucos dias para um dos encontros mais estratégicos
da agenda do Energy Summit 2026. 
 O Webinar de Geopolítica da Energia vai reunir especialistas e lideranças para um encontro estratégico que irá aprofundar como os movimentos geopolíticos estão redesenhando o setor energético global, e o papel do Brasil nesse novo cenário.
 Os participantes terão acesso a análises de especialistas e lideranças, sobre como a geopolítica influencia diretamente a produção, distribuição e consumo de energia, destacando os impactos nas cadeias globais e as tendências que moldam decisões estratégicas no médio e longo prazo.
 Confirme sua participação agora e tenha acesso a insights exclusivos, visão qualificada de mercado e debates que antecipam decisões críticas para os próximos anos.

Subject: Shadow AI: o ponto cego que pode custar caro

[PODCAST] Seu time usa IA sem você saber e isso é motivo para se preocuparNo podcast da MIT Technology Review Brasil com a Palo Alto Networks, entenda o problema da Shadow AI nas empresas e como
retomar o controle



  A IA já está sendo usada dentro das empresas, 
mas nem sempre pelo caminho oficial

 Quando equipes recorrem a ferramentas por conta própria, para ganhar velocidade de entrega, o que parecia só produtividade vira risco. Dados sensíveis podem circular fora das políticas, decisões podem ser tomadas sem rastreabilidade e a empresa perde visibilidade do que realmente está acontecendo.
 O resultado é um ponto cego. A conta vem em forma de incidente, compliance ou desgaste reputacional. Mapear onde esse uso começa e por que se espalha é o primeiro passo para recuperar o controle com agilidade.
No podcast "A Virada de Chave da Segurança na Era da IA", da MIT Technology Review Brasil com a Palo Alto Networks, você entende como identificar o problema e quais ações ajudam a reduzir o risco.


Marcos PupoPresidente da
Palo Alto Networks para
a América Latina


Fernando FujiharaSócio-líder de engenharia
de software, IA e dados
da Deloitte


 Rafael CoimbraMediador, editor-executivo
da MIT Technology
Review Brasil

  Veja nesta edição: ⯀ Por que dados e segurança destravam a escala além do piloto
⯀ De que forma a IA encurta ataques de dias para horas e, às vezes, minutos
 ⯀ Como a fragmentação de ferramentas atrasa a resposta e o que muda com plataformas unificadas
⯀ Como agentes de IA elevam o risco e exigem supervisão humana

 Confira o episódio e descubra como escalar IA sem criar vulnerabilidade

---
Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-07 13:25:29+00:00
Subject: Noonan Elegizes A Paper That Once Was

Noonan says the decline, perhaps fatal, of the Post under owner Jeff Bezos, who just laid off 300 people there, is a catastrophe. She writes:
But the Post’s diminishment, which looks like its demise, isn’t just a “media story.” Reaction shouldn’t break down along ideological lines, in which the left feels journalism is its precinct and is sad, and the right feels journalism is its hulking enemy and isn’t sad. Treat it that way and we’ll fail to see the story for its true significance. The capital of the most powerful nation on earth appears to be without a vital, fully functioning newspaper to cover it. That isn’t the occasion of jokes, it’s a disaster.
I fear sometimes that few people really care about journalism, but we are dead without it. Someday something bad will happen, something terrible on a national scale, and the thing we’ll need most, literally to survive, is information. Reliable information—a way to get it, and then to get it to the public. That is what journalism is, getting the information.
You have to think of it as part of your country’s survival system. Maybe the government will or won’t tell you the truth about what’s going on, maybe the Pentagon will or won’t, but if you know you’ve got this fabulous island of broken toys, professional journalists working for a reputable news organization, you’ve got a real chance of learning what’s true.
It takes years to make good reporters—people who are trained, who love getting the story so much, who love the news so much, that they will wade into the fire, run to the sound of the guns. They are grown only in newsrooms, not at home with laptops. They are taught by older craftsmen and professionals, through stories and lore.
The Post’s greatness and expertise can’t easily be replaced and perhaps can’t be replaced at all, or at least not for decades of committed building.
Noonan is writing about journalism — or rather, Journalism — in an idealistic way. This is how I, who graduated from journalism school in 1989, was trained to think about the profession. It’s both noble and practical. I still believe that journalism is massively important, especially in a democracy. Where I dissent is in Noonan’s genuinely felt glorification of the Post.
For journalists of my generation, the Post was iconic. Watergate, mostly, but also because it was just a great paper. When I graduated, my motto was, “Style section by 30!” — meaning, my professional goal was to be hired by the Post’s terrific features section by the time I was thirty. I didn’t meet that goal, and the Style section, once the best of its kind, has been a shell of itself for many years. My point is, Noonan is eulogizing an institution and a profession that hasn’t existed for ages. I don’t say that mockingly; I too wish we still had that kind of journalism. But then, I wish we still had a lot of good things.
She blasts Jeff Bezos for not using his vast fortune to prop up a paper that fewer and fewer people read (this, by the Post’s own demographic research). I can sympathize with that; if I had Bezos Money, I wouldn’t mind losing a tiny bit of it each year to subsidize a money-losing enterprise that served the public good. But does the Post do that? Why has the paper lost so many readers over the years?
The Internet is no doubt the main reason. But it is surely also the fact that the Post served as the parish newsletter for the Church of Good Liberals. I sometimes joke that I subscribe to The New York Times so I can know what the regime’s official story is. (By “regime,” I don’t mean the Trump administration, heaven knows; I mean what Curtis Yarvin calls “the Cathedral” — the ruling class that makes up the Deep State and that runs most American institutions.) A Kremlinologist back in the Cold War would read Pravda for the same reason. But that’s unfair to the Times, which, for all its many, many faults, remains a great paper (though one severely limited by its own biases). 
I entered journalism in its final golden years, before the Internet hit newspapers like an asteroid. The idea of seeking the truth and trying to be fair was still taken seriously in newsrooms. I imagine it still is, but only in the ideological terrarium in which journalists work. 
What I saw over my years of working within the daily newspaper profession (1989-2010) was a degradation of professional standards and the rise of ideology. It’s a familiar story, one I needn’t go into here. To sum it up, I came to see newsrooms of people who all thought more or less the same way — liberal, progressive, secular — while telling themselves that they were the only truly open-minded people in town. 
This surely has a lot to do with my own becoming religious, and more conservative, in the 1990s. That made me a definite outsider within newspapers. (Even when I worked for the New York Post, the fact that I was a religious and social conservative made me an outsider — but journalists at the NYP, to their eternal credit, never took themselves with as much self-regarding solemnity as the non-tabloid journalists I worked with.) As an outsider, I was able to see with the kind of clarity that only outsiders in any institution can, how blind so many of my colleagues (but not all!) were to their own biases.
What grated me, and the few other conservatives I got to know in newsrooms during those years), was the sense of crusading moralism within the profession, and the absolute confidence that they were Correct About Everything.
When I was at the Dallas Morning News, for example, I saw a lot of good reporting. But I also saw a deep conviction, assumed by most but never articulated, that any news that might in some way affirm the worldview of conservatives, especially religious conservatives, should not be reported.
1. Yes, I did use the word, in this context: A student asked me if I thought her high school’s administration was right to suspend a classmate of hers for using the word in a video she’d made in eighth grade. I said “Did she actually call someone a “(offending word”? Or was she singing a rap song or quoting a book title or something?” When the student explained that it was the student, who was white and Jewish, sitting with a black friend and the two were jokingly insulting each other by calling each other offensive names for a black person and a Jew, I said “She was suspended for that? Two years later? No, I don’t think suspension was warranted. Somebody should have talked to her, but any school administrator should know that 12-year-olds say dumb things. It’s part of growing up.”
And, from a man who was a reporter based on South Africa for a time in his career (the American teenagers had accused him of defending blackface):
3. The question about blackface was part of a discussion of cultural appropriation. The students felt that it was never, ever appropriate for any white person to adopt anything from another culture — not clothes, not music, not anything. I counter-argued that all cultures grow by adopting from others. I gave examples — gunpowder and paper. I said I was a San Franciscan, and we invented blue jeans. Did that mean they — East Coast private school students — couldn’t wear blue jeans? I said we were in Peru, and the tomato came from Peru. Did that mean that Italians had to stop using tomatoes? That they had to stop eating pizza? Then one of the students said: “Does that mean that blackface is OK?” I said “No, not normally — but is it OK for black people to wear blackface?” “The student, sounding outraged, said “Black people don’t wear blackface!” I said “In South Africa, they absolutely do. The so-called colored people in Cape Town have a festival every year called the Coon Carnival* where they wear blackface, play Dixieland music and wear striped jackets. It started when a minstrel show came to South Africa in the early 1900’s. Americans who visit South Africa tell them they’re offended they shouldn’t do it, and they answer ‘Buzz off. This is our culture now. Don’t come here from America and tell us what to do.’ So what do you say to them? Is it up to you, a white American, to tell black South Africans what is and isn’t their culture?”
None of this mattered. The Times forced him to resign. Great Awokening stuff. So, when Peggy Noonan writes about the making of a journalist in this way — “They are taught by older craftsmen and professionals, through stories and lore” — I think of poor Donald McNeil. 
Long before the Great Awokening, when I was at the News, a black colleague went to our editor and accused me of “creating a hostile work environment” because I called a Pakistani mob that burned down a Danish consulate “savages.” I had been friends with that guy, and really liked him. He didn’t come to me — he went straight to the editor and used the kind of language that pricks up the ears of HR Karens. I removed the comment in which I said that from the editorial board blog, and told our editor I was doing it under protest. I told her that there is no way at this newspaper, given its HR department, that I could get a fair hearing in the case where a black man says a white conservative is “creating a hostile work environment” (by calling a fanatical Islamic mob burning down a consulate over a cartoon “savages”!).
I made it my business to stay as far away from that previously valued black colleague after that. He had my career in his hands, and he knew it. Many such cases.
Now, you tell me how this kind of environment relates to the Woodward & Bernstein nostalgia in Noonan’s column. Hey, I wish American newspapers were like she depicts them! She’s absolutely right about the importance of proper journalism to democracy. But I wish these same Post reporters and fellow travelers lamenting the Bezos savaging of their institution had thought about how they themselves were running the institution. 
They included MGIPOC (Marginalized Genders and Intersex People of Color mentorship program); Mi Gente (Latinx employees at NPR); NPR Noir (black employees at NPR); Southwest Asians and North Africans at NPR; Ummah (for Muslim-identifying employees); Women, Gender-Expansive, and Transgender People in Technology Throughout Public Media; Khevre (Jewish heritage and culture at NPR); and NPR Pride (LGBTQIA employees at NPR).
All this reflected a broader movement in the culture of people clustering together based on ideology or a characteristic of birth. If, as NPR’s internal website suggested, the groups were simply a “great way to meet like-minded colleagues” and “help new employees feel included,” it would have been one thing.
But the role and standing of affinity groups, including those outside NPR, were more than that. They became a priority for NPR’s union, SAG-AFTRA—an item in collective bargaining. The current contract, in a section on DEI, requires NPR management to “keep up to date with current language and style guidance from journalism affinity groups” and to inform employees if language differs from the diktats of those groups. In such a case, the dispute could go before the DEI Accountability Committee.
In essence, this means the NPR union, of which I am a dues-paying member, has ensured that advocacy groups are given a seat at the table in determining the terms and vocabulary of our news coverage.
And:
In recent years I’ve struggled to answer that question. Concerned by the lack of viewpoint diversity, I looked at voter registration for our newsroom. In D.C., where NPR is headquartered and many of us live, I found 87 registered Democrats working in editorial positions and zero Republicans. None.
So on May 3, 2021, I presented the findings at an all-hands editorial staff meeting. When I suggested we had a diversity problem with a score of 87 Democrats and zero Republicans, the response wasn’t hostile. It was worse. It was met with profound indifference. I got a few messages from surprised, curious colleagues. But the messages were of the “oh wow, that’s weird” variety, as if the lopsided tally was a random anomaly rather than a critical failure of our diversity North Star.
Berliner’s essay is about how all this affected NPR’s coverage of the issues. I would not be surprised if some wrongthinker inside the Washington Post could tell a similar story about how that paper changed over the years. I could be wrong! But I doubt I am.
Before the Internet, a lot of people subscribed to the paper because it was the only way to get in-depth news. Many liberal journalists flatter themselves that people won’t subscribe to the paper because they can’t handle the truth (I’ve heard this, multiple times). It never occurs to them to think that maybe the people don’t value the product, because they don’t trust it. 
Me, I’m a conservative, but I want to be told true and important things about the world, even if they go against my own personal narrative. I wouldn’t value a conservative paper that was written to edit out the things that trouble conservatives. Just tell me as best you can about what’s going on in the real world, and I’ll figure it out. The problem with our papers today is they are written and edited by liberals and progressives, many (but not all) of whom have little concern about the world outside their own narrow confines. 
I was told by a reader this week that one of the Norwegian journalists who interviewed me last weekend said that I shouldn’t have been invited to Norway at all, because I’m associated with J.D. Vance and Viktor Orban. That attitude is extreme, but I’ve seen a somewhat milder version of it among American liberal journalists. They just don’t see any reason to be curious about people unlike themselves, or fair.
Is any of this relevant to the fall of the Washington Post? I think so, though I hesitate to make any big, definitive pronouncement, given that I was a subscriber for a relatively short time, and just checked in with the paper from time to time. I have no doubt at all that somebody with my views — religious, conservative, mildly but skeptically Trumpy — could never get hired there, even though I’ve written two New York Times bestsellers. And if I did, I wouldn’t want to work there, because I wouldn’t want to have to deal with the kind of bullshit I sometimes dealt with at my last newspaper, in which right-thinking liberals would literally turn their backs to me if passing me in the hallway (this really happened once), or I would have to walk through a minefield waiting for a privileged minority to accuse me of making them anxious, and then having to go into a Kafkaesque HR process in which I would probably lose, being a white male conservative. 
Noonan writes:
The Post’s greatness and expertise can’t easily be replaced and perhaps can’t be replaced at all, or at least not for decades of committed building.
I do not know the Washington Post, so I could be wrong here. But my guess is that the expertise that made the Post a great newspaper back in the day has been driven out of the paper by a progressive culture of militant hostility to anything that doesn’t fit the preferred Narrative, or at least self-satisfied incuriosity. As a young journalist, I dreamed of one day working at The Washington Post. I am certain there are good journalists still working there. But I would say something that wasn’t true in 1989, when I started working in daily newspapers: that they are places where curiosity about the world goes to die. You end up becoming less a reporter than a curator of Narrative. And God forbid you step outside the lines; the mob within the paper will make sure you are punished, maybe even ruined professionally. 
I think about the greats of journalism past. Mencken. Liebling. Royko. So many others. Could they last five minutes among the Jacobins in newsrooms today? They were messy men, but if living teaches you anything, it’s that greatness and wisdom doesn’t always come in a neatly tied package. Good journalists are interesting journalists, and that means they are rarely saints. The fact that Donald McNeil’s 40+ years at the Times did not save him when some punk-ass teenagers accused him said a lot, not just about the Times, but about American journalism. Had he been a veteran of the Post, I doubt they would have handled that controversy any differently. Maybe I’m cynical, but I doubt any major American paper would have. These are the times in which we live.
Anyway, Noonan is right about one thing: losing good newspapers is a catastrophe for American democracy. But this is not Jeff Bezos’s fault. Look, if you spend time on X, and you follow the right accounts, you learn things that really happened in the world, and that really matter, that never make it into the pages or our respectable daily papers. Why don’t the people who report and edit the papers care about things like this? 
Beats me. But fewer and fewer people care about them, so they end up in situations like the Post today, with some whining that Bezos ought to continue subsidizing them out of the goodness of his heart, even though their readership is declining, and talented public-minded people like Peggy Noonan writing elegies for a model of journalism that died a long time ago.

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-02-07 15:01:52+00:00
Subject: O que o presente veste

Conhecimento que veste ideias 


 A maneira como nos vestimos acompanha transformações que vão além da moda. Novas dinâmicas de trabalho, rotinas mais flexíveis e a mistura entre vida pessoal e profissional criaram um cenário em que roupas precisam responder a diferentes contextos no mesmo dia. A Reserva observa esse movimento e propõe peças pensadas para adaptação, mobilidade e consistência de estilo, sem perder autenticidade.
 Nesse cenário, moda deixa de ser apenas expressão estética e passa a dialogar com informação, repertório e visão de mundo. Entender o que usamos também é entender o tempo em que vivemos. A parceria com a MIT Technology Review Brasil nasce dessa convergência, conectando criatividade, inovação e reflexão para ampliar o olhar sobre comportamento, tecnologia e cultura contemporânea.
 
Descubra como a coleção traduz esse encontro entre design e pensamento atual e revela novas formas de vestir o agora.

 
 


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil
 Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-07 20:55:48+00:00
Subject: The Mysteries of Jeffrey Epstein

The federal government recently released millions of documents related to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein, and everyone is scrambling to find an angle.
My own way of looking at the ever-evolving Epstein scandal is that, on the surface, one might see signs of the most advanced and all-encompassing global conspiracy, but when one starts to go down all these pathways, the trail quickly starts to fall apart. When one gets away from the grand theorizing and into the granular detail, a completely different story begins to emerge—one that, in my view, is actually rather banal.
Beneath the surface, the Epstein saga is fundamentally one of philistinism, hedonism, and self-enrichment. Jeffrey Epstein was able to ingratiate himself within circles of power. He used the perception of his own wealth and power to build more wealth and power. He then deployed this system of prestige to legitimate his desires for young women—and, in some cases, underage women.
What Epstein did was morally wrong. We can unequivocally condemn his actions, especially when his abhorrent conduct involved minors. But it’s still quite a different story than what some of the online conspiracies would suggest.
What Epstein’s Emails Tell Us About Liberal Elites
The most revealing thing about the Epstein files is that they give us a look into the world of liberal elites. I want to highlight a couple of email exchanges that illustrate the point...

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-08 03:14:17+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=94=AE_Exponential_View_#560:_The_$1_trillion_panic;_my_f?=
 =?UTF-8?q?avorite_AI_analysis_tool;_intention_economy,_CAR-T_therapy_&_?=
 =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=A6?=

Hi all,
Welcome to the Sunday edition #560. This was a week of overreactions. Wall Street panicked about $650 billion in AI spending. Sam Altman and Dario Amodei traded jabs.
But this is exactly what Exponential View is for… We go beneath the noise to the forces that move markets, technologies, and societies. 
Today, I’ll unpack what investors got wrong in their panic, the model upgrades that matter more than the benchmarks suggest, and what I’ve learned from living with agents that never sleep (including an unveiling of my favorite thinking tool… which I built for myself.)
Markets overreact
For capital markets, this uncertainty isn’t just about who might win in a well-defined game; it’s about the type of game that’s being played. Markets use tools that assume relatively stable competitive structures and roughly linear growth in order to price company-level cashflows over three- to ten-year horizons.
So when I look at this week’s sell-off, I see investors who haven’t yet experienced the breakthrough moment of watching an AI agent compress 10 hours of tedious work into 40 minutes. Once that realisation moves from early adopters to Main Street, the $650 billion won’t look like reckless spending. It will look like they didn’t spend enough.
See also, I spoke about this live on Friday in conversation with Matt Robinson , Jaime Sevilla  and our Hannah Petrovic :

While you watched the Super Bowl…
This was the week Anthropic and OpenAI went full contact. First, my take on the model upgrades – and then what the Super Bowl quip is really about.
A C compiler capable of building Linux is a genuinely hard engineering problem. Carlini had tried the same experiment with earlier Opus models. Opus 4.5, released just months ago, could pass test suites but choked on real projects. Predecessors before that could barely produce a functional compiler at all. Opus 4.6 completed a two-week engineering task. Every increment on autonomous execution is a step change in real-world outcomes.
OK, now let’s turn to the Super Bowl… 
Anthropic serves an expensive product to rich people. … [We] are committed to free access, because we believe access creates agency.
Living with new beings
with a new AI-driven workflow in place, I ran the steps through a series of modular prompts and automation scripts. The system parsed, filtered and structured the inputs with minimal human intervention. A light edit at the end and it was done in 15 minutes.
And yet, instead of feeling triumphant, I felt… unsettled. Had I missed something? Skipped a crucial step? Was the result too thin?
It wasn’t. The work was complete and it was good. But I hadn’t emotionally recalibrated to the new pace.
For high-consequence work – investment due diligence, thorny analysis, or divergent exploration – I now turn to Clade, a multi-agent system I built where AIs argue with themselves until better answers emerge. Here’s how…...

---

Pasta fagioli—literally “pasta and beans”—is a rustic Italian
soup that’s easy to make but big on flavor. It starts with
pancetta and vegetables simmered in wine and a tomato-based
broth, then finishes with tiny pasta, beans, and lentils. What
really makes this version special is the way the broth is
enriched: a portion of the soup is puréed and stirred back in,
giving it a hearty texture and deeper, richer taste.

Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-09 04:54:05+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #256

What assets do best: The Dagster Children's Book
We’re excited to share something a little unexpected from the Dagster team: What assets do best, a children’s book about data assets! Perfect for kids and data-loving grown-ups alike, you’ll learn how assets work together, adapt to change, and give teams a complete view of their data.
Watch the narrated story, find out where you can snag a free book IRL, and print & play puzzles!
Alexander Shereshevsky: Graph RAG in 2026 - A Practitioner’s Guide to What Actually Works
Graph RAG adoption stalled after early hype because high indexing costs and unclear performance trade-offs limited production use. The article explains when Graph RAG outperforms vector search, how teams reduce costs with selective graph construction, and why hybrid vector–graph architectures deliver the best results.
Mark Rittman: So, Just How Relevant is Multi-Touch Attribution for Marketers in 2026?
Marketing attribution struggles in 2026 as privacy controls, regulations, and cookie loss remove large portions of user-level data. The article explains how teams adapt by combining deterministic identity for logged-in users, server-side tracking, and triangulation across MTA, MMM, and incrementality testing. Prioritizing authentication, tracking micro-conversions, and owning raw event data enables more reliable attribution in a privacy-first environment.
Pinterest: Next Generation DB Ingestion at Pinterest
Legacy batch-based ingestion pipelines created high latency, operational complexity, and compliance gaps across Pinterest’s data ecosystem. The article explains how Pinterest built a unified CDC-based ingestion framework using Kafka, Flink, Spark, and Iceberg to stream database changes and efficiently upsert them into analytical tables, reducing data latency from days to minutes while lowering compute costs and improving reliability at the petabyte scale.
Sponsored: Dagster Running Dagster
In this upcoming session, find out how Dagster's data team has increased its capacity, along with best practices for data modeling that work well with AI assistants. We'll also demo a real case where our Compass Dagster+ integration identified the root cause of a Postgres-to-Snowflake pipeline that was failing 40-50% of the time.
Netflix: The Data Canary: How Netflix Validates Catalog Metadata
Although this article is not specifically about the data warehouse, it demonstrates how data corruption can occur without a code change and still disrupt the system. The article describes how Netflix built a data canary system that validates new catalog metadata using side-by-side clusters, chaos-based testing, and customer-centric behavioral metrics. By detecting corruption within minutes and blocking the release of unsafe data, Netflix applies code-level deployment rigor to high-velocity data pipelines.
Uber: Introducing uFowarder - The Consumer Proxy for Kafka Async Queuing
Kafka consumer services struggle to scale reliably when direct protocol access introduces head-of-the-line blocking, inefficiency, and operational complexity. The article explains how Uber built uForwarder, a gRPC-based Kafka consumer proxy that resolves head-of-line blocking, improves hardware utilization, isolates traffic, and supports delayed processing. By abstracting Kafka internals behind a push-based interface, uForwarder increases reliability and efficiency across thousands of consumer workloads.
Pierce Lamb: Agentic Search over Graphs of Long Documents (or LAD-RAG++)
Possibly one of the best reads for this week for me. Vanilla RAG struggles with long, structured documents because static chunking loses layout, relationships, and cross-page context. The author reviews LAD-RAG++, which constructs layout-aware document graphs and employs agentic retrieval to explore structural and semantic connections dynamically. Engineering improvements in memory control, graph pruning, and cost-efficient processing make graph-based RAG practical for high-recall question answering over dense professional documents. 
Halodoc: Halodoc’s Layered Data Validation Strategy for Building Trust in the Lakehouse
Data quality issues in healthcare analytics demand stronger guarantees than generic validation frameworks can provide. The article explains how Halodoc built a custom, configuration-driven validation pipeline with four-layered checks that combine time-bound reconciliation, AI-generated structural tests, and business-rule enforcement across the Lakehouse. By integrating LLMs into validation and surfacing failures in real time, the system reduces incidents, increases trust in analytics, and supports reliable clinical decision-making.
Booking.com: Beyond Prompt Engineering: How We Used Supervised Fine-Tuning for Travel Recommendations
Prompt-based LLMs struggle to deliver fast, privacy-safe, and personalized travel recommendations at production scale. The article explains how Booking.com used supervised fine-tuning on an open-weight model with parameter-efficient techniques, contextual inputs, and carefully designed labels to combine conversational understanding with behavioral signals.
Pranav Mehta: Clickhouse Internals: A Deep Dive into ClickHouse Distributed Connection Pooling
ClickHouse operators may misinterpret connection-retry warnings as leaks when distributed queries encounter transient network errors. The article explains how ClickHouse reuses pooled TCP connections for distributed tables and why idle timeouts in spiky workloads produce harmless “Broken pipe” warnings. 
All rights reserved, Dewpeche Private Limited. I have provided links for informational purposes and do not suggest endorsement. All views expressed in this newsletter are my own and do not represent current, former, or future employers’ opinions.

Reunião do Cofom discute inovação, intercooperação e micropensões

A Comissão de Fomento da Previdência Complementar Fechada (Cofom) realizou na última quinta-feira (5/2) sua primeira reunião do ano, dia 5/2, em Brasília. O colegiado, formado por representantes do governo federal, participantes/assistidos, entidades fechadas de previdência complementar (EFPC) e patrocinadores/instituidores, discutiu as soluções de fomento voltadas a questões como acessibilidade, atratividade, eficiência, sociedade, inovação, além de ações de intercooperação entre as EFPC. A deliberação sobre os temas ficou para a próxima reunião, prevista para o fim de fevereiro...

Os trabalhos, coordenados pelo Comitê de Gestão de Riscos (CGR) da autarquia, terão início em 9 de fevereiro de 2026 e prazo estimado de até três semanas para sua conclusão...

Elos publica rentabilidades de 2025

A Elos, fundo de pensão dos funcionários da Eletrosul, divulgou a rentabilidade de seus cinco planos em 2025. Dos cinco, três possuem perfis de investimento e dois não. A melhor rentabilidade de dezembro/25 foi alcançada pelo plano CD Eletrosul, no perfil Protetor, que rendeu 1,18%, enquanto a pior do mês foi também do CD Eletrosul, no perfil Visionário, que rendeu 0,80%. Já o melhor retorno de 2025 foi obtida pelo plano Elosprev, no perfil Visionário, que rendeu 16,85%, enquanto o pior do ano foi do CD Eletrosul, no perfil BPSD, que rendeu 10,46%...

Sender: newworkinphilosophy@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-09 13:13:10+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=9CNon-Player_Characters_in_the_Real_World:_A_Threefold_P?=
 =?UTF-8?q?roblem_for_Theodicies=E2=80=9D_-_Netanel_Ron_(Hebrew_University?=
 =?UTF-8?q?_of_=E2=80=A6?=

Did you ever wonder if someone you met is a real person or just some character that God animates? Did you ever wonder if there is someone who is living life as your neighbor, experiencing your neighbor’s life “from the inside,” or if your neighbor is more like a non-conscious robot who acts and looks like a human but is really just a puppet playing their role?
“NPC” is a term used in video games and in tabletop role-playing games like Dungeons and Dragons. While some characters in these games are controlled by real-life human players, in some video games there are characters who are controlled by the game itself, and they operate upon a preprogrammed set of behaviors. Likewise, in tabletop role-playing games, sometimes there are characters who are controlled by the storyteller, rather than by one of the players. These characters are called “non-player characters”, or “NPCs” for short. NPCs often serve as adversaries that players must contend with, or as allies that assist players in their quests, but in many games there are also countless NPCs designed simply to populate the game’s virtual environment and make it realistic.
Theodicies are philosophical arguments aimed at explaining why a very powerful (maybe even “all-powerful”), very knowledgeable (maybe even “all-knowing”), and very (maybe even perfectly) good God would let there be so much evil and suffering in our world. One of the most popular strategies for theodicies is to try to point out good things that the existence of evil and suffering is necessary for, good things that might make it all worthwhile in the long run. In the paper, I show that some theodicies face a problem because the good things that they argue that evil and suffering are necessary for are really things that can come about even if real people and real animals hardly ever suffer and bad things mostly happen only to NPCs. I call these theodicies “NPC-inviting theodicies” since they invite the thought that God should make NPCs exist in the real world to bring about plentiful goods with hardly any real suffering.
A lot of so-called “free-will theodicies” are NPC-inviting theodicies. These theodicies argue that evil and suffering are a result of human free will, and they argue that God gave us free will, despite the bad things it leads to, because it is also necessary for some very good things. For example, people freely choosing to love God and people freely choosing to act virtuously. Now imagine a world where God created only one real person – “Player One” – and the rest of the world is filled with NPCs. In this kind of world, Player One can exercise their free will to freely choose to love God and freely choose to act kindly towards others (NPCs who Player One does not recognize as such), all without anyone being harmed if Player One chooses to act evilly once in a while. If God repeats this in many worlds, with a different person being Player One and living among NPCs in each world, many people can freely choose to love God and freely choose to act virtuously without anyone else being harmed when they don’t. This makes these free-will theodicies NPC-inviting.
In the paper I discuss a few more NPC-inviting theodicies, including the “soul-making theodicy” famously championed by John Hick, and I present a threefold problem for these theodicies which I name the “NPC problem.” The most obvious aspect of the NPC problem is probably what I call “the bizarre aspect,” which simply points out that it is quite bizarre, even unpalatable, for a theodicy to imply that we should expect for there to be many real-life NPCs among us, disguised as fellow conscious creatures. Yet this is what we should expect if we take an NPC-inviting theodicy seriously.
Another, more subtle aspect of the NPC problem is what I call “the refutation aspect.” God presumably aims to minimize unnecessary pain and suffering, so it would be natural for God to protect the Player One of each world and keep them away from harm. This should be easy for God, since there is only one person for God to focus his providence on in each world. Now, if someone is very unfortunate and they live a wretched life full of suffering, they know they can’t possibly be the Player One of their world. This means, however, that if an NPC-inviting theodicy is correct, that person must be an NPC. We cannot know for sure about other people whether they are real people or NPCS, but people do know this about themselves. If someone knows that they are not an NPC, yet they suffer far more than they might have suffered if they were Player One, they have first-person access to evidence that refutes NPC-inviting theodicies.
In the paper I also discuss a “dark aspect of the NPC problem” and show how NPC-inviting theodicies harbor a dangerous potential to provoke dark thoughts that smack of racism, classism, and ableism. Afterwards, I consider four possible responses to the NPC problem, one aimed at showing why God wouldn’t fill the world with NPCs even if an NPC-inviting theodicy is true, and the rest aimed at resolving only the refutation aspect and the dark aspect of the NPC problem. One response argues that God wouldn’t create NPCs because that would be deceitful on God’s part, and another response argues that God wouldn’t place Player One in a world full of only NPCs because God would want Player One to have “real friends” and not just relationships with NPCs. I argue that these responses can only help NPC-inviting theodicies if the value of God being not deceitful or the value of “real friendships” is so high that it outweighs the enormous amounts of suffering that God can prevent if he utilizes NPCs, and this seems unlikely.
Thanks for reading New Work in Philosophy! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

---
Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-02-09 14:03:20+00:00
Subject: O que =?UTF-8?B?w6kgdW0gcGFyw6JtZXRybz8=?=

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 09.02.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  Parâmetros são os números que moldam o comportamento de um grande modelo de linguagem, como botões ajustados durante o treinamento. Eles começam aleatórios e, a cada erro, um algoritmo os corrige em milhares de rodadas, com custo enorme de computação e energia. Três tipos dominam o sistema. Embeddings codificam cada token em listas de milhares de valores que capturam significado e proximidade entre palavras. Pesos e vieses, nos transformers, refinam esse sentido no contexto. Na saída, o modelo escolhe o próximo token por probabilidade, com ajustes como temperatura, top-p e top-k. 

 

POR: WILL DOUGLAS HEAVEN

Grandes modelos de linguagem têm muitos parâmetros. Mas o que é um parâmetro?

 Eles são os números misteriosos que fazem seus modelos de IA favoritos funcionarem. O que eles são e o que eles fazem?
 Um editor acorda às 4 da manhã com a pergunta que move a corrida dos grandes modelos de linguagem: o que é um parâmetro? No fundo, são números que, ajustados durante o treinamento, viram os botões que determinam como o modelo responde. Tudo começa com valores aleatórios, um algoritmo mede os erros e recalibra esses números repetidas vezes, um processo que soma quatrilhões de cálculos e consome muita energia. 
Três famílias de parâmetros fazem a mágica: embeddings, pesos e vieses. Embeddings transformam cada token em uma lista enorme, muitas vezes com 4.096 números, colocando palavras em um espaço de significados onde “mesa” fica perto de “cadeira”. Pesos e vieses, dentro dos transformers, modulam esses vetores para dar contexto e resgatar sinais fracos. 

 
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Os riscos apresentados pelo uso de estatinas são menores do que pensávamos
2. A Rússia está usando o rigoroso clima de inverno como arma
3. Apostadores geopolíticos estão investindo na probabilidade de guerra
4. Seu próximo aumento salarial pode ser aprovado por IA

5. Os frequentadores de cinema já se cansaram da IA

Citação do dia

 
“Sou totalmente a favor de remover pessoas ilegais, mas arrancar caras de cima de cortadores de grama na Califórnia e deixar o caminhão e o equipamento simplesmente lá, parados? Definitivamente, não é trabalhar com mais inteligência”
  The Big Story


Da genética ao prato de comida

Janeiro 2026



 Por décadas, a saúde foi explicada quase que exclusivamente pela biologia. Só que o prato pesa tanto quanto o gene, e o território define o que chega a ele. Em bairros onde frutas e verduras são raras, ou caras, os ultraprocessados viram norma; os chamados desertos alimentares. No Brasil, dados oficiais de 2024 indicam que cerca de 25 milhões de pessoas, 1 em cada 3, nos grandes municípios analisados, vivem nessas áreas.  
Um estudo do MIT, ao vasculhar milhões de itens de cardápios, reforça que não é falta de opção, e sim a baixa qualidade do que se vende todos os dias, que se associa à obesidade e outros riscos. Fora das cidades, a desigualdade continua, com produtos mais açucarados no hemisfério sul e mais agrotóxicos permitidos.

Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: johnathanbi@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-09 16:02:36+00:00
Subject: The Overwhelming Evidence for Miracles Suppressed By Authorities

The supernatural is real: levitation, reincarnation, near death experiences, remote viewing…
Not only do these “impossible” phenomena occur, they are well documented and often by secular scholars nonetheless. There are entire labs out of Duke, Stanford, Princeton, University of Virginia that have come up with compelling evidence of the supernatural that cannot be absorbed into a materialist frame.
This is the claim of my guest Princeton’s Dale Allison in his new book: encountering mystery.
So why haven’t we heard any of this? When I read Allison’s impressive survey of this phenomena my first reaction was not shock but anger: why haven’t I in my 20+ years of education ever been made aware of this radical data and the Copernican shift it seems to suggest?
Dale’s answer is that this has all been systematically suppressed by not just atheists but the religious as well. This data is threatening not just for the materialist worldview but most religious traditions as well. In fact you are going to hear how atheism itself is an outgrowth of certain sects of Christianity.
In this interview you are going to learn about all the mind-blowing empirical data for the supernatural and what this tells us ontologically about the world we live in.

Orgão do TCU não vê “desconformidades” nos investimentos da Previ
News Investidor Institucional

10/02/2026

‍Olá Tolis Vossos ‍

‍‍Veja o que há de novo

Orgão do TCU não vê “desconformidades” nos investimentos da Previ

No início de 2025, o Tribunal de Contas da União (TCU) iniciou uma auditoria contra a Previ, fundo de pensão dos funcionários do Banco do Brasil, para investigar suposta má gestão dos investimentos da entidade e as causas de um déficit de R$ 14 bilhões em seu Plano 1, acumulado nos primeiros onze meses de 2024. O ministro do TCU Walton Alencar Rodrigues, responsável por solicitar a auditoria, ainda não publicou o acórdão do processo. No entanto, um relatório do órgão técnico encarregado de analisar as principais denúncias, datado de 30 de setembro de 2025, não identificou irregularidades nos processos de investimento da entidade...


Boletim Focus projeta nova queda da inflação ao final deste ano

O boletim Focus, publicado semanalmente pelo Banco Central, projeta nova queda do IPCA de 2026, passando de 3,99% na pesquisa da semana passada para 3,97% na pesquisa desta semana. As projeções para a inflação de 2027, 2028 e 2029 mantiveram-se estáveis em 3,80%, 3,50% e 3,50%, respectivamente...


Ibovespa renova máxima e dólar tem menor nível desde maio/24

O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta segunda-feira (9/2) em alta de 1,80%, aos 186.241,15 pontos, renovando o recorde de fechamento pela décima vez em 2026. A alta foi impulsionada principalmente pela entrada de capital estrangeiro, em meio à rotação global de ativos que favorece mercados emergentes, após sinais vindos da China de menor apetite por títulos públicos dos Estados Unidos. O movimento destoa das bolsas internacionais, que operaram de forma mais cautelosa diante do enfraquecimento do dólar e das incertezas sobre economias desenvolvidas...


Multimercados têm captação líquida de R$ 17,3 bilhões em janeiro

A indústria de fundos fechou o mês de janeiro com R$ 75,3 bilhões de captação líquida, o segundo melhor desempenho mensal do setor desde 2021 informa a Associação Brasileira das Entidades dos Mercados Financeiro e de Capitais (Anbima). Os fundos multimercados registraram captação líquida de R$ 17,3 bilhões no mês, o melhor resultado desde junho de 2021...


SCPrev anuncia rentabilidade de planos em 2025

O SCPrev, fundo de pensão dos funcionários públicos de Santa Catarina, divulgou a rentabilidade de seu plano de mesmo nome em dezembro. No mês, o plano rentabilizou 1,10% para uma meta de 0,77%, enquanto no acumulado dos 12 meses de 2025 rendeu 13,81%, para uma meta de 9,84%. A carteira de investimentos do plano somava R$ 608,79 milhões ao final de dezembro...

As systems grow, observability becomes harder to maintain and incidents harder to diagnose. Agentic observability layers AI on existing tools, starting in read-only mode to detect anomalies and summarize issues. Over time, agents add context, correlate signals, and automate low-risk tasks. This approach frees engineers to focus on analysis and judgment. (Article)

-


-




Prevent AI-generated tech debt with Skeleton Architecture. This approach separates human-governed infrastructure (Skeleton) from AI-generated logic (Tissue) using Vertical Slices and Dependency Inversion. By enforcing security and flow control in rigid base classes, you constrain the AI to safe boundaries, enabling high velocity without compromising system integrity. (Article)

** | curso on-line |
Charles Dickens, uma introdução
------------------------------------------------------------
Este curso oferece uma introdução acessível à obra de Charles Dickens por meio de leituras orientadas, diálogos com a fortuna crítica e incursões biográficas que lançam luz sobre o processo criativo do romancista. Ao longo dos encontros, examinaremos a relação entre forma e contexto, a construção da metrópole vitoriana, o papel das instituições e as redes afetivas que estruturam os romances de Dickens. Destacando obras como Oliver Twist, Grandes esperanças e David Copperfield, as aulas combinarão recortes para leitura e contextualização histórica. O objetivo é apresentar chaves de leitura que aproximem a ficção dickensiana de leitores contemporâneos.


** Marcela Santos Brígida
------------------------------------------------------------

É professora de Literatura Inglesa na Universidade do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (UERJ), onde coordena o projeto de extensão Literatura Inglesa Brasil. Sua pesquisa concentra-se nas poéticas do storytelling na literatura e em outras artes, com foco nas adaptações e apropriações da ficção de Charles Dickens em obras do século XXI, nos Estudos da Performance e na literatura irlandesa contemporânea.
Programa:

Aula 1.
A vida de Charles Dickens e a Inglaterra vitoriana.

Aula 2.
Infância, pobreza e bem-estar social: entre Oliver Twist e David Copperfield.

Aula 3.
Burocracia, justiça e as instituições: entre A pequena Dorrit e Casa soturna.

Aula 4.
Afeto e legado cultural: Um conto de Natal.

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-10 12:38:30+00:00
Subject: Disquiet On The Weimar America Front

The film differs somewhat from the Remarque novel. For example, the novel’s scene that I found most powerful — Paul Bäumer, the protagonist, going home on leave, and experiencing the shock of the patriotic folks back home having no idea how bad things are at the front — doesn’t make it into the film. Plus, it adds a political-historical subplot, in which the Social Democratic politician Matthias Erzberger negotiates a surrender with the French high command, over the protest of a fictional warmongering German general. We see in that general’s final speech, before he orders a futile attack in the last hours before the armistice takes hold, the seeds of the “stabbed in the back” myth that allowed the German generals to avoid responsibility for the war in the eyes of the German people, and that later proved so useful to Hitler as he was building his movement. And Paul’s death is more dramatic in the film than the novel, but its essential futility remains true to the Remarque work.
I know it’s just a film not real life, but it is extremely detailed, and faithfully reproduces the things I’ve been reading from historians about what it was like for infantry on the front lines. It was good to watch this material visually reproduced; the horror of it all lands harder. Watching it really does help one understand where Hitler came from, in the sense that it is very, very difficult to imagine how any nation overcomes that kind of mass trauma. 
Two million German soldiers died in the war, and about 700,000 civilians, from malnutrition and disease. The French lost about 1.3m soldiers, but many fewer civilians, even though the war was fought on French territory.) In any case, conditions at the front were the same for all combatants. In 2018, I visited a French farm family in Picardy, whose land was part of the front back then. The Battle of the Somme was fought there. One million men in total died in that single battle. 
I took this photo in the forest behind the family’s fields:
The scars are still there. The father of the family told me that when his grandfather returned to their village from the fighting, there was nothing left, only rubble. He didn’t even know where his family home had stood. Imagine what that does to a people. The French more or less recovered; the Germans did not, because they bore the burden of losing the war (and thus the sense that all that death and suffering was meaningless), plus the crushing weight of Allied reparations, which contributed to the hyperinflation that impoverished millions. 
That’s my focus in this book project: how we Americans of today find ourselves in a psychosocial condition that resembles Germany of the 1920s, even though we have not suffered what they did in the Great War and its aftermath. To be specific, like the Germans of the 1920s, we collectively live in a state of precarity, fear, and anxiety about the future. Most of the old, structured assumptions and traditions by which we lived are gone. Trust in institutions has all but evaporated. Mass loneliness and atomization is a widespread phenomenon. We have reached the point where many, perhaps most, people don’t know who or what to trust, and prefer Narrative to Truth. 
All of that came to 1920s Germany as a result of the war and its aftermath. We have reached similar conditions without that vivid, visceral trauma. How? I think the acceleration of life over the past half-century has a lot to do with it, and so does the advent of the Internet. Of course there are actual historical events that contributed — 9/11, the failed wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, the 2008 financial crash, Covid — but mostly, I think, it was gradual. But here we are, in a comparable state of instability. 
Some people, when they hear I’m working on a book about the Weimarization of America, assume that my thesis is that America is headed towards Nazism. That’s not it. I don’t know where America is headed. I only know that the conditions are ripening for the emergence of some form of authoritarianism. It could be of the Left or the Right, or some new thing we haven’t yet imagined (e.g., the de facto corporate fascism of Communist China, producing material abundance while maintaining strict social control). I think the advent of AI, and its integration into daily life, will present future authoritarians with an incredibly powerful tool for managing the masses. 
The history books about Weimar focus mostly on politics and economics, but the complete breakdown of sexual mores in Weimar Germany, which signaled deeper disorder within society — well, we have them now in contemporary America. That kind of thing. Wait till you read the next item, below the paywall.

Subject: O que fazer quando o crime opera como =?UTF-8?B?aW5kw7pzdHJpYT8=?=

[Special Edition] 
A arquitetura
da verificação inteligente 
 Como hubs integrados protegem negócios
e fortalecem a confiança digital contra fraudes
em escala e em tempo real 
 


 
 Com a Inteligência Artificial ao alcance de todos, golpes ganharam escala, sofisticação e velocidade. E a confiança digital virou parte da infraestrutura do negócio.
 A nova Special Edition, “A arquitetura da verificação inteligente”, da MIT Technology Review Brasil, em parceria com a Certta, mostra por que a prevenção a fraudes está migrando de controles isolados para hubs de verificação que conectam sinais, dados e modelos para decidir em tempo real, com menos atrito para quem é o real usuário.

 
 
 
 
Nesta edição, você entende:

 


 ⯀ Como a verificação inteligente reorganiza a defesa em camadas e reduz a fricção, do cadastro ao pagamento
⯀ Por que voz, rosto e documentos sintéticos mudam a lógica de autenticação
 ⯀ O que diferencia sistemas que apenas reagem daqueles que aprendem e se adaptam
⯀ O desafio das pequenas e médias empresas, e o caminho para elevar maturidade sem complexidade inviável

 
Se o crime opera como indústria, a resposta precisa atuar como arquitetura integrada, contínua e capaz de evoluir. Esta Special Edition traz exemplos e conceitos para orientar decisões de produto, risco e tecnologia.

combate à fraude digital com a Special Edition

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br

Subject: The Checkup | =?UTF-8?B?QmViw6pzIGFww7NzIG8gY8OibmNlcg==?=

The CheckupBebês após o câncer

Por Roberta Arinelli, Editora-executiva de Saúde  • 10.02.26

 
 Olá! Seja bem-vindo à The Checkup, a newsletter semanal de Biotech and Health da MIT Technology Review Brasil.

Às terças-feiras, trazemos temas essenciais sobre saúde, biotecnologia e inovação para você acompanhar as principais tendências da área.
Nesta edição, o destaque é a análise de Jess Hamzelou, repórter sênior da MIT Technology Review, sobre um procedimento cirúrgico experimental que preserva a fertilidade de pacientes tratadas por câncer colorretal e já resultou no nascimento de bebês.
 Na curadoria científica da semana, três destaques: uma plataforma vacinal capaz de expandir células B raras e avançar no desenvolvimento de vacinas contra HIV e gripe, um fármaco experimental que redefine o relógio biológico e reduz o tempo de recuperação do jet lag, e evidências de que o vírus Epstein-Barr pode ter um papel direto na ativação imunológica associada à esclerose múltipla.
 No episódio do podcast de Biotech and Health, Camila Pepe e Carolina Abelin conversam com Marcelo Murilo, VP de Inovação, Tecnologia e Expansão da Benner, sobre o papel da tecnologia na redução de custos, na predição de riscos e na melhoria do cuidado em saúde.
Boa leitura!

 
O futuro da fertilidade depois do câncer

 A radioterapia e a quimioterapia podem ter efeitos colaterais bastante prejudiciais, que comprometem o útero e os ovários. Cirurgiões estão desenvolvendo uma possível solução: simplesmente “afastar” esses órgãos do campo de radiação durante o tratamento do câncer. Depois que o tratamento termina, eles podem recolocar o útero, junto com os ovários e as tubas uterinas, em sua posição original.
A paciente de Huber tinha 28 anos quando foi descoberto um tumor de quatro centímetros em seu reto. Médicos do Hospital de Sion, na Suíça, onde Huber trabalha, recomendaram um tratamento que incluía múltiplos medicamentos e radioterapia antes da cirurgia para removê-lo.
 Esse tipo de radiação pode destruir células tumorais, mas também pode danificar outros órgãos da pelve, explica Huber. Isso inclui os ovários e o útero. Pessoas que passam por esse tipo de tratamento podem optar por congelar os óvulos previamente, mas o dano causado ao útero significa que elas nunca poderão levar uma gestação adiante, acrescenta. Lesões no revestimento do útero podem dificultar a implantação de um óvulo fertilizado, e a musculatura uterina fica incapaz de se expandir, diz ela. 
Nesse caso, a mulher decidiu congelar seus óvulos. Mas utilizá-los no futuro seria complicado e a gestação por substituição (também chamada de “barriga solidária” no Brasil) é ilegal na Suíça. Huber então lhe ofereceu uma alternativa.
 Ela vinha acompanhando o trabalho de Ribeiro, oncoginecologista que atuava no Hospital Erasto Gaertner, em Curitiba, no Brasil. Lá, Ribeiro havia desenvolvido um novo tipo de cirurgia que consistia em deslocar o útero, as tubas uterinas e os ovários de sua posição na pelve e posicioná-los temporariamente na parte superior do abdome, abaixo das costelas.
Huber se lembra de ter visto Ribeiro apresentar o caso em uma conferência na época. Ela imediatamente percebeu que sua própria paciente era candidata ao procedimento e que, como cirurgiã com ampla experiência em histerectomias, poderia realizá-lo. A paciente concordou.
 Os colegas de Huber no hospital ficaram apreensivos, conta ela. Nunca tinham ouvido falar do procedimento. “Quando apresentei essa ideia ao cirurgião geral, ele não dormiu por três dias”, diz. Mas, depois de assistir a vídeos da equipe de Ribeiro, ele se convenceu de que a cirurgia era viável.
 Assim, antes do início do tratamento oncológico, Huber e seus colegas realizaram a operação. A equipe literalmente suturou os órgãos à parede abdominal. “É uma dissecção delicada”, diz Huber, mas acrescenta que “não é o procedimento mais difícil”. A cirurgia durou de duas a três horas. Os pontos foram removidos por meio de pequenas incisões cerca de uma semana depois. Nessa altura, o tecido cicatricial já havia se formado, criando uma fixação duradoura.
 A mulher teve duas semanas para se recuperar da cirurgia antes de iniciar o tratamento contra o câncer. Esse tratamento também foi bem-sucedido. Em poucos meses, o tumor havia diminuído tanto que não podia mais ser visto em exames de imagem.
 Como medida de precaução, a equipe médica removeu cirurgicamente a área afetada do cólon. Ao mesmo tempo, cortou o tecido cicatricial que mantinha o útero, as trompas e os ovários na nova posição e transferiu os órgãos de volta para a pelve.
 Cerca de oito meses depois, a mulher interrompeu o uso de anticoncepcionais. Ela engravidou sem fertilização in vitro e teve uma gestação em grande parte saudável, segundo Huber. Por volta do sétimo mês de gravidez, surgiram sinais de que o feto não estava crescendo como esperado. Isso pode ter ocorrido por problemas no suprimento sanguíneo da placenta, diz Huber. Ainda assim, o bebê nasceu saudável.
 Ribeiro afirma já ter realizado a cirurgia 16 vezes e que equipes de países como Estados Unidos, Peru, Israel, Índia e Rússia também já a realizaram. Nem todos os casos foram publicados, mas ele estima que existam cerca de 40.
 Desde o nascimento de Lucien no ano passado, um sexto parto foi anunciado em Israel, diz Huber. Ribeiro conta que também ouviu falar de outros dois nascimentos desde então. O mais recente foi o da primeira mulher que passou pelo procedimento. Ela teve uma menina há alguns meses, segundo ele.
 Nenhuma cirurgia é isenta de riscos, e Huber ressalta que existe a possibilidade de os órgãos serem danificados durante o procedimento ou de um câncer mais avançado se espalhar. O útero de uma das pacientes de Ribeiro falhou após a cirurgia. Os médicos ainda estão “na fase de coleta de dados para criar um procedimento padronizado”, diz Huber, mas ela espera que a cirurgia ofereça mais opções a jovens com alguns tipos de câncer pélvico. “Espero que mais mulheres jovens possam se beneficiar desse procedimento”, afirma. 
Ribeiro diz que a experiência lhe ensinou a não aceitar o status quo. “Todos diziam que não havia nada a ser feito [em relação à perda de fertilidade nesses casos]”, conta. “Precisamos continuar evoluindo e buscando respostas diferentes.”


 
 Pela web

Nova plataforma vacinal estimula células B raras e pode ampliar a proteção contra HIV e gripe
 Pesquisadores do MIT e do Scripps Research Institute desenvolveram uma plataforma de vacina baseada em partículas semelhantes a vírus construídas com um arcabouço de DNA, capaz de expandir significativamente populações raras de células B precursoras de anticorpos neutralizantes. Esse é um passo considerado crítico para vacinas eficazes contra vírus altamente mutáveis, como o HIV e o influenza.
 Em estudos pré-clínicos, a abordagem gerou até oito vezes mais células B “on-target” do que plataformas proteicas já testadas em humanos, ao reduzir respostas imunológicas indesejadas contra o próprio veículo da vacina. O uso de um arcabouço de DNA “silencioso” permite concentrar a resposta imune no antígeno de interesse e pode abrir caminho para estratégias de imunização em múltiplas etapas, além de aplicações em doenças infecciosas, neurodegenerativas e até dependência química.

 Novo fármaco redefine o relógio biológico e reduz quase pela metade a recuperação do jet lag
 Cientistas identificaram um novo composto, chamado Mic-628, capaz de avançar de forma confiável o relógio biológico, um dos maiores desafios no tratamento do jet lag e de distúrbios do ritmo circadiano. Ao atuar sobre a proteína reguladora CRY1, o fármaco ativa o gene Per1, sincronizando tanto o “relógio mestre” do cérebro quanto os relógios periféricos do corpo, independentemente do horário de administração.
 Em testes com camundongos submetidos a um modelo de jet lag, uma dose de Mic-628 reduziu o tempo de adaptação de sete para quatro dias. Por não depender de janelas rígidas de aplicação, ao contrário de estratégias como luz ou melatonina, o composto representa uma nova abordagem farmacológica para redefinir o ritmo circadiano, com potencial aplicação em viagens, trabalho em turnos e distúrbios do sono.

Vírus Epstein-Barr pode ter papel direto na esclerose múltipla
Pesquisadores identificaram novas evidências de que o vírus Epstein-Barr (EBV) pode atuar diretamente na ativação do sistema imunológico em pessoas com esclerose múltipla. O estudo encontrou um acúmulo expressivo de células T CD8+ direcionadas ao vírus no cérebro e no líquido cefalorraquidiano de pacientes com a doença, em níveis muito superiores aos observados no sangue.
 Os cientistas também identificaram a ativação de um gene viral exclusivamente em indivíduos com esclerose múltipla, sugerindo um mecanismo específico pelo qual o EBV pode contribuir para o dano imunológico característico da doença. Os achados reforçam o papel do vírus em doenças autoimunes e podem abrir caminho para estratégias terapêuticas voltadas diretamente ao EBV.

Ibovespa recua leve e dólar fecha a R$ 5,19
News Investidor Institucional

11/02/2026

‍Olá Tolis Vossos‍

‍‍Veja o que há de novo

Ibovespa recua leve e dólar fecha a R$ 5,19

O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta terça-feira (10/2) em queda de 0,17%, aos 185.929,33 pontos, refletindo a cautela dos investidores diante da divulgação do payroll, o relatório oficial de empregos dos Estados Unidos, e da repercussão dos dados de inflação no Brasil. Apesar do recuo, o índice permaneceu próximo do nível recorde e acumula alta de 15,39% em 2026. O desempenho esteve em linha com o exterior, onde as bolsas de Nova York encerraram o dia sem direção única, à espera de indicadores relevantes...


Anbima propõe ajustes nas regras prudenciais de ativos virtuais

A Associação Brasileira das Entidades dos Mercados Financeiro e de Capitais (Anbima) está propondo ao Banco Central ajuste no limite de exposição aos tipos de ativos virtuais e tokens para fins de classificação prudencial. O pedido foi em resposta à consulta pública sobre o tratamento prudencial de ativos virtuais. “A segurança do mercado de ativos virtuais depende de regras que assegurem a integridade das operações ao longo de todo o seu ciclo”, comenta Eric Altafim, diretor da Anbima...


Forluz publica resultado dos seus três planos em jan/26

A Forluz, plano de pensão dos funcionários da Cemig, divulgou os resultados de seus três planos em janeiro. O Plano A, de Benefício Definido (BD), rendeu 0,96% no mês, para uma meta atuarial de 0,78%, enquanto os planos B e TaesaPrev, ambos de Contribuição Definida (CD), renderam 1,19% e 1,89% no mesmo período, para metas de 0,76% e 0,68%, respectivamente...


DFPrevicom publica resultados de 2025

O plano DF-Previdência da DFPrevicom, fundo de pensão multipatrocinado dos servidores públicos do Distrito Federal, fechou 2025 com rentabilidade de 13,64% para uma referência atuarial de 8,43 (IPCA + 4%). No mês de dezembro, isoladamente, a rentabilidade foi de 0,81% para uma referência atuarial de 0,67%...
