Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-26 11:10:17+00:00
Subject: Are There (Spiritually) Righteous Gemstones?

I’ve also heard privately from some readers who say it’s all a hoax, and from others (parents of these non-verbal autists) who say don’t believe the debunkers, this stuff is real, and I’ve seen it. 
My own view is that a) it’s probably real, and b) if so, the only real question is whether or not this phenomenon is of God. I’m inclined to believe that it is, but I don’t know that, so I’m still reading and listening. 
My hope was to convey that God is the source of everything, that His spirit, being, as stated multiple times throughout Scripture and very specifically in John 1, is the source of all, the Word is the source of all and without the Word, nothing that is - is. And nothing that He made is bad, as stated in 1 Timothy 4:4 and through prayer and the word of God it is sanctified, for the earth is the Lord’s, and all its fullness (1 Corinthians 10:26). Also in Romans 14 Paul teaches that nothing is unclean of itself, but it is our actions, our intentions, our beliefs that make something pure or impure.
One of you readers, who identifies himself as not religious or conservative, commented:
As for stones being transmitters of energy, or even some spiritual significance of sort, I can’t discount that as well.  Various gemstones have been described in detail as part of apocalyptic revelation.  Think of components of New Jerusalem, as well as gems on the breastplate of the ancient Israelite High priest. Divine chariot throne in apocalyptic revelations and gems associated with it are often green (i.e. Emerald-like) in color. This is part of ancient Jewish mystical tradition, that is also replicated in Eastern Orthodox iconography, from Libya to Russia. That is why so often Jesus Christ/Virgin Mary is circumscribed in a green circle in Icons. Take what you will, and while I understand the perspective you are coming from, I believe there is more to gems having some deep spiritual quality, although the conventional New Agers may get the substance of it wrong, and twist it in a convenient way. I admit I haven’t done any deep dive into this topic though. Urim and Thummim were used in divine communication in the Ancient Israelite temple priesthood.
I had only thought of gems as decorative. Can any of you tell me more about how gems were regarded by the ancient Hebrews, as well as in the ancient Church? Grok tells me:
The Urim and Thummim (Hebrew: אוּרִים וְתֻמִּים, often translated as “lights and perfections” or “revelation and truth”) hold significant importance in the Hebrew Bible (Old Testament) as a divinely sanctioned means of obtaining guidance directly from God.They were sacred objects placed inside (or upon) the breastplate of judgment (חֹשֶׁן הַמִּשְׁפָּט, ḥōšen ha-mišpāṭ), a key part of the high priest’s garments. This breastplate, described in detail in Exodus 28:15–30, was a square pouch-like item attached to the ephod, adorned with 12 precious stones representing the tribes of Israel. The instructions specify: “And in the breastplate of judgment you shall put the Urim and the Thummim, and they shall be on Aaron’s heart when he goes in before the Lord” (Exodus 28:30; see also Leviticus 8:8).Their primary significance lies in their role as an oracular device—a legitimate, priestly method for inquiring of YHWH (the Lord) and discerning His will on critical matters affecting the nation, such as military decisions, leadership, or guilt/innocence. This function is explicitly tied to the high priest:
Joshua was to consult the high priest Eleazar “by the judgment of the Urim” for guidance on leading the people (Numbers 27:21).
In Moses’ blessing on the tribe of Levi, the Urim and Thummim are highlighted as belonging to “your godly one” (Levi/the high priest), emphasizing their role in divine mediation (Deuteronomy 33:8).
They appear in several narratives where leaders “inquire of the Lord” (often implying the Urim and Thummim), such as in battles or crises (e.g., 1 Samuel 23:9–12; 30:7–8; possibly Joshua 7; 1 Samuel 14:37–45).
The Bible does not describe their physical form or exact mechanism in detail, leading to various interpretations (e.g., two stones or lots for yes/no answers, illuminated gems, or letters on the breastplate stones lighting up/protruding to spell answers). What is clear is that they represented reliable divine revelation through the high priest, distinct from forbidden practices like divination or necromancy.
Also put the Urim and the Thummim in the breastpiece, so they may be over Aaron’s heart whenever he enters the presence of the LORD. Thus Aaron will always bear the means of making decisions for the Israelites over his heart before the LORD. (Exodus 28:30)
From what I can tell — remember, this is a diary in the form of a newsletter, not a scholarly paper! — it is not clear that the Urim and Thummim were gems, or that they were used narrowly as receivers of energy, though they were clearly used as a form of God-approved divination by the High Priest. I had assumed that the gems on the priestly breastplate were purely ornamental. Do we have reason to think otherwise? Let me hear from you in the comments. 
If that theory is true, then one naturally wonders: why now? One possibility: we were only able to perceive the image on the Shroud Of Turin after the invention of photography, when the photonegative of a picture a photographer took of the Shroud revealed the figure of a man who had been scourged and crucified. Infranco speculates that the autism phenomenon, which has always been with us (I guess), but which has exploded in modern times, could be part of God’s plan to prepare us. I dunno, it’s all speculation. 
But I think this is important: Infranco talks about how in Scripture, we see a connection between radical suffering, and God manifesting through those who suffer. St. Paul’s willingness to take beatings and persecution for the sake of the Gospel is the paradigmatic example, but there’s also this, from the Gospel of John, Chapter 9:
As he went along, he saw a man blind from birth.  His disciples asked him, “Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?”
“Neither this man nor his parents sinned,” said Jesus, “but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him. …”
It is well within the Christian tradition to see those who radically suffer in their bodies can be used by God to manifest His own glory. It is therefore within Christian orthodoxy to consider that God is using the radical suffering of these severely autistic people to tell us something about Himself. 
If so, this is why the first half of The Book Of Heaven is so important. It’s about the radical suffering of Katie Asher, Houston’s mother. She somehow kept her faith throughout unspeakable suffering, much of it at the hands of Houston’s father, who was an adulterer and a violent drunk. If you believe that Houston really is a “herald,” then that gives particular meaning to the suffering that not only he suffers, but that his mother suffered, and does suffer as caretaker for Houston, with all his difficulties.
I also note that in that Blurry Creatures podcast, Infranco points out that the Nazis considered the disabled to be examples of life that has no meaning. This is how they justified euthanizing these “useless” people. It could be that God is using these nonverbal autists to remind us all that all life is sacred, and nobody is useless. This, at a time of rising acceptance of euthanasia. 
Final point: at the 1:42 point in the Blurry Creatures podcast, Infranco says that the Telepathy Tapes creator made a deliberate decision to edit out the religious aspects of this experience. He judges this decision charitably, saying maybe the show’s producer wanted to be more “inclusive.” Maybe. The reason I decided to quit listening to TTT was because I was informed yesterday by a trustworthy source that this had happened, over the objections of some of the participants in the podcast. That, plus at least two of you writing to me privately that Season Two degenerates into New Age slop. 
Still, maybe I’ll finish the first season, just to know better what I’m talking about … but while also knowing that editorial decisions were made to delete the Jesus stuff. 
I expect that Katie will join the discussion in the comments today. Please subscribe if you want to participate. I remind you all that I will increase the price of this newsletter from $6/month to $7/month — the first price increase in two years. This Substack is my primary source of income, and the US dollar has declined meaningfully against European currencies in the past few months. ...

---
Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-26 12:02:35+00:00
Subject: Inside the Trump Administration's War on DEI

For 60 years, the U.S. Department of Justice’s Civil Rights Division was a racialist, hard-Left institution, whether the president happened to be a Democrat or a Republican. Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon is the first person to change that dynamic and champion a new theory of civil rights law based on the principle of colorblind equality. In the following interview, Dhillon outlines the work that the Trump administration is undertaking to reverse the Left’s decades-long weaponization of civil rights law, explains how the administration is handling captured left-wing institutions, and responds to emerging arguments on the Right that colorblind equality does not go far enough to confront the Left.

Informe de Rendimentos XP






















Olá, Investidor! Tudo bem?
O seu Informe de Rendimentos e relatórios auxiliares estão disponíveis para download no app e site da XP.





Para efetuar o download, o passo a passo é simples.





Pelo app:
1. Acesse sua conta no aplicativo XP. Clique em "Seu Nome" no canto superior esquerdo da tela;
2. Selecione "Imposto de renda" na seção "Mais informações"; consulte seus "Relatórios" do ano de 2025;





Pronto! Você já pode baixar seu Informe de Rendimentos e relatórios auxiliares.








Pelo site:
1. Acesse sua conta no site XP. Na seção "Minha Conta", selecione "Imposto de renda"; 
2. Clique em "Download"; na seção "Relatórios"; consulte seus arquivos do ano de 2025;

















Pronto! Além de ter acesso ao seu Informe de Rendimentos, você poderá consultar os Relatórios auxiliares.

A decisão foi tomada após dois meses de avaliações, que incluíram análises quantitativas e qualitativas, diligências presenciais e uma avaliação aprofundada de aspectos como governança, risco, estrutura operacional, tecnologia, práticas ESG e capacidade global de gestão...


SRPC lança Coletânea de Normas das EFPC atualizada até dezembro

A Secretaria de Regime Próprio e Complementar, órgão do Ministério da Previdência Social (MPS), está disponibilizando a nova versão da Coletânea de Normas das Entidades Fechadas de Previdência Complementar (EFPC), com leis, decretos, resoluções, instruções e portarias atualizados até dezembro de 2025. A anterior tem os dados atualizados até setembro de 2025. Para acessar a nova coletânea, no formato digital, com 634 páginas...


Ibovespa cai mas segue acima dos 191 mil pontos; dólar sobe 0,27%

O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta quinta-feira (26) em leve queda de 0,13%, aos 191.005,02 pontos, refletindo a realização de lucros nas ações de grande capitalização após uma sequência de altas recentes. Sender: architect-newsletter@mail.infoq.com
Date: 2026-02-27 09:09:54+00:00
Subject: The Software Architects' Newsletter February 2026

[InfoQ Live Logo]

The Software Architects' Newsletter
February 2026

Welcome to the InfoQ Software Architects' Newsletter! We bring you essential news and experience on emerging patterns and technologies from industry peers each month.


This newsletter explores software architecture through multiple lenses, including technical, organizational, human, economic, and operational. From Conway's Law and socio-technical systems to developer experience, governance, and AI-augmented design, it examines how different perspectives shape architectural decisions and long-term outcomes. Understanding these lenses helps architects reason more clearly about trade-offs, constraints, and changes at scale.

News

OpenAI Scales Single Primary PostgreSQL Instance to Millions of Queries per Second for ChatGPT


WhatsApp Deploys Rust-Based Media Parser to Block Malware on Three Billion Devices


The effort traces back to 2015's Stagefright vulnerability, which showed how attackers could hide malware inside seemingly innocent image or video files. Those malicious files targeted bugs in Android's media libraries, and apps like WhatsApp couldn't patch the underlying OS. At the time, WhatsApp had a C++ library called "wamedia" that checked MP4 files for conformance issues before sending them.

LinkedIn Re-Architects Service Discovery: Replacing Zookeeper with Kafka and xDS at Scale


Uber Moves from Static Limits to Priority-Aware Load Control for Distributed Storage


The design protects Docstore and Schemaless, which are built on MySQL and serve traffic through thousands of microservices, supporting over one hundred seventy million monthly active users, including riders, Uber Eats users, drivers, and couriers. By prioritizing critical traffic and adapting dynamically to system conditions, the system prevents cascading overloads and maintains performance at scale.

Working with Code Assistants: the Skeleton Architecture


By enforcing patterns such as dependency inversion and template methods, architects retain control over system behaviour while allowing AI to accelerate implementation. The model shifts engineers toward defining constraints, information flow, and non-functional requirements. Ultimately, Skeleton Architecture enables teams to leverage AI productivity gains without sacrificing architectural integrity or long-term maintainability.


Case Study

You’ve Generated Your MVP Using AI. What Does That Mean for Your Software Architecture?


AI-generated code acts largely as a “black box”, making implicit architectural decisions that teams may not fully understand, control, or maintain over time. This approach introduces risks around technical debt, sustainability, and integration with existing systems, particularly when quality attribute requirements (QARs) such as scalability, security, and performance must be met.

As a result, architecture becomes more empirical. Instead of designing systems primarily up front, teams must focus on validating AI-generated architectures through experimentation and architectural testing, including performance, usability, resilience, and security testing. These validation activities help determine whether the system satisfies business and technical requirements.

Architectural decision-making remains critical, but it should shift toward clearly articulating trade-offs and constraints in prompts so AI can generate appropriate solutions.

Architects must also consider long-term maintainability, because AI-generated code may be difficult to evolve or repair. Ultimately, AI accelerates implementation, but increases the importance of defining architectural qualities, validating outcomes empirically, and ensuring systems remain sustainable as AI tools evolve.





Upcoming Events

Conference: March 16-18, 2026








About InfoQ

Senior software developers rely on the InfoQ community to keep ahead of the adoption curve. One of the main reasons software architects and engineers tell us they keep coming back to InfoQ is because they trust the information provided and selected by their peers.

We’ve been helping software development teams adopt new technologies and practices for over 20 years through InfoQ articles, news items, podcasts, tech talks, trends reports, and QCon software development conferences.

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-27 09:22:08+00:00
Subject: Beginning Of The End Of UK Liberal Democracy

Normally local by-elections in Britain are nothing for non-British people to care about. But I want you readers, most of whom are not Britons, to understand what just happened in the UK. It signals nothing less than perhaps the beginning of the end of British democracy, and the germination of the seeds of civil war. This is a sign.
Yesterday, the Gorton and Denton constituency held an election. It was a total catastrophe for the Labour Party, for which this had been previously a completely safe seat. The far-left Greens party crushed them:
Despite flooding the area with ministers and 1,000 activists, Labour did not even have the consolation of second place with its candidate trailing in behind Reform's Matt Goodwin. Nigel Farage complained of 'cheating' after reports of so-called 'family voting' from independent observers.
The team assessed two significant aspects of electoral integrity – family voting (where two voters either confer, collude or direct each other on voting) which breaches the secret ballot. The team also assessed the impact of the requirement for voters to show ID before they are issued with a ballot paper.
2023 saw the enactment of the Ballot Secrecy Act, which made the practice of family voting more clearly a breach of the secret ballot, making it more enforceable by staff in polling stations. Signage is now available to discourage the practice. Signage was only seen in 45% of the polling stations observed.
The observer team saw family voting in 15 of the 22 polling stations observed, some 32 cases in total, nine cases in one polling station alone. The team observed a sample of 545 voters casting their vote – meaning 12% of those voters observed either caused or were affected by family voting.
Commenting John Ault, Director of Democracy Volunteers said;
‘Today we have seen concerningly high levels of family voting in Gorton and Denton. Based on our assessment of today’s observations, we have seen the highest levels of family voting at any election in our 10 year history of observing elections in the UK.’
We are Balkanising our country, moving beyond citizenship as our primary political identifier and instead relating to one another as members of antagonistic tribes whose territories happen to overlap.
The Green Party’s behaviour in the run-up to yesterday’s by-election should place that party beyond the parameters of democratic decency.
At one point, their candidate, Hannah Spencer, told her Reform opponent, Matt Goodwin, that the Manchester Arena bomb had happened ‘because people like you are dividing people’.
More:
Does this really need spelling out? No democracy can flourish if its people lack common identity and shared allegiance.
… We have moved from being a cohesive nation, in which almost everyone accepted certain norms – equality before the courts, parliamentary democracy, religious pluralism, free speech – to one in which we ourselves are teaching groups of our own citizens to be separate and resentful.
We might have handled immigration differently, with more manageable numbers. But our real error was to turn our backs on British patriotism.
During the 20th century, most settlers arrived in Britain in positive spirits. People don’t abandon their family and language to go to places they despise.
But we taught their children that Britain was rapacious, reprehensible and racist. No wonder some of them turned against the country of their birth.
UnHerd’s Jonny Ball explains why this election signals Labour’s collapse and the end of Britain’s traditional politics:
Gorton and Denton is a sign of things to come — the realignment of our politics around new poles. What we’re witnessing isn’t just the shattering of our two-party system, but a seismic chapter in the strange death of Labour Britain. The former voter blocs of the governing party, already in an unhappy marriage, living under the same roof but barely speaking for many loveless years, finally have splintered and gone their separate ways.
More:
Labour’s fractured base is represented perfectly across this Mancunian constituency. In Denton, to the east, the old, post-industrial white working-class; and to the west, in Gorton, the Muslim and ethnic-minority vote, living cheek-by-jowl with the declassé elements of Britain’s urban liberals. Here was the tripartite alliance of the contemporary Left in all its glory, fused together awkwardly just like the constituency itself, with a motorway in the middle. Labour has not lost an election here since 1931. Yet today, each of its tribes has found its own reasons to despise Starmer’s Labour: a project that was never comfortable in its own skin.
Ethnic bloc voting is, of course, not uncommon in the United States, though we hate to admit it. But it was unknown in Britain until now. The Greens are far left on social issues, but also radically pro-Muslim. This is what the French call “Islamo-gauchisme” — a tactical alliance between Muslims and far-leftists, who are allied only in their hatred for the West and what it traditionally stands for. Everybody with a brain in their heads knows that Muslim voters for the party have no interest in the party’s pro-LGBT agenda, or its radical environmentalism. The leftists are useful idiots. The Muslims are cunningly biding their time. 
It’s about gender and generation too. According to a YouGov poll published last month, 44 percent of British women aged 18-24 would vote Green. That’s almost half the youngest voting-age cohort in the country! Almost one out of three young men in that age cohort also prefer the Greens, meaning that overall, 37 percent of Britons between the ages of 18 and 30 support this radical de facto Islamist party.
Pressure will be overwhelming for Prime Minister Keir Starmer to resign, but that only means Labour will choose his replacement. They don’t have to call a general election until 2029. One can only hope that Starmer will call a general election now, while the country still has a chance of being saved by Reform UK. Labour and the Tories, who between them governed Britain for the 20th century until today, are fading; the future belongs to Reform and the Greens, battling it out. The center is disintegrating. Both Tories and Labourites euthanized themselves. 
Meanwhile, I saw this chart yesterday for the United States, which does not bode well:
This is what you get when, as in Britain, the ruling class in academia and the media train a whole generation to hate their country. Where do you think this is going to go? There were far more Republicans who were proud to be American even under the Biden administration than there were Democrats under Biden!
I remind you that these are Weimar conditions. 
Houellebecq At 70
Houellebecq is one of the most important writers of our time, precisely because he is also one of its most merciless chroniclers: an author whose work constitutes both a diagnosis and a symptom of a civilisational condition of exhaustion that he describes with clinical precision, yet never fully transcends himself.
More:
Since his breakthrough with Whatever and especially The Elementary Particles, Houellebecq has relentlessly described a world in which humanity, after the collapse of its traditional structures of meaning, is left in a condition of emotional, sexual, and spiritual atomisation and alienation.
Liberalism—understood not merely as an economic system but as an anthropological principle—has penetrated every sphere of life, as gaining the love of another becomes a competitive sport, sexuality is commodified, and self-realisation becomes a stressful obligation. Thus, the modern individual, far from being liberated, appears in Houellebecq’s novels as a lonely consumer whose freedom consists mainly in choosing between different forms of emptiness.
This diagnosis runs with almost monomaniacal consistency throughout his entire work. Platform portrays the attempt to compensate Western spiritual barrenness through commodified sexuality; an experiment which culminates in a violent collapse.
The Possibility of an Island takes our modern-day avoidance of suffering to its logical conclusion by imagining a posthuman future in which humans replace themselves with their technically optimised successors to escape their suffering, only to discover that, along with their pain, any meaning disappears also.
You don’t read Houellebecq for spiritual uplift. On the other hand, I have experienced some spiritual uplift from reading him, simply because it is encouraging to read a novelist who tells the damn truth about the world we have created without God. Houellebecq longs for God, but he simply cannot find faith. 
The key moment in his best-known novel, 2015’s Submission, where his stand-in, the dissolute professor François, stands before the medieval statue of the Virgin inside the cave church at Rocamadour, and has a mystical experience while young Catholics around him are praying. Yet lacking courage, François convinces himself that he must have just eaten something that didn’t sit right with him. He returns to Paris, and by the end of the novel, converts cynically to Islam, to guarantee himself a permanent job and three wives under the new Islamist order. He is not really a Muslim, not in his heart; he just lacks the wherewithal to resist, and besides, wants nothing more than job security and regular sex. The soft-Islamists now in control of the country, by democratic means (!), don’t care; they just want power. 
Ultimately, Houellebecq’s fiction points to a fundamental incoherence in modern, liberal political thought. You don’t get sexual freedom without the sort of economic emancipation free markets allow (it’s hard to multiply sexual partners when, say, you’re totally beholden economically to a spouse. That is, at least not without significant danger to yourself—just read some 19th-century social novels and you’ll see what I mean!). At the same time, you don’t get economic freedom and self-determination without a loosening of the moral constraints that material necessity used to hold in place. In any case, whatever side you’re on politically, the most important thing to understand as far as reading MH is concerned is that both of these visions—human flourishing understood either as economic or moral-sexual liberation—are materialistic and reductive.
And, rather obviously, they also fail adequately to address human beings’ metaphysical needs, which liberalism is content to leave up to the individual. Religion’s purpose, as I see it, is to order collective life sub specie aeternitatis, but you don’t get that when the hard work of metaphysical consolation becomes a private affair. In the vacuum, alternatives inevitably arise, some of the most pernicious of which we see today: ethnic and racial identitarianism, religious extremism and terrorism, and a tolerance and even embrace of totalitarian rhetoric across the political spectrum. I’m synthesizing a bit on Houellebecq’s behalf, but I think this vision can help us make sense of much of the tension we’re seeing today.
More:
In the case of Christianity in Europe, I think the question to ask is something like this: can a civilization maintain its identity if it sheds its native religion? Houellebecq doesn’t think so, and neither do I. This isn’t a political or polemical point. Imagine taking as an anthropological platitude the claim that human beings will be religious and, moreover, that civilizations are built upon the metaphysical systems they create (or which are revealed to them, to give credit to the metaphysical on its own terms). It’s obvious from such an assumption that the collapse of the metaphysics entails the eventual collapse of everything else. This should be deeply alarming to anyone who cares about the West’s tradition of humanitarianism, which emerges—and it would be wonderful if we could all agree on this—out of the original Judaic notion of imago Dei and later from Christian humanism. Secular humanism has been running for quite some time on the fumes of the Judeo-Christian religious inheritance, but it’s not clear how much longer that can go on.
Honestly, it’s frightening to think what a truly post-Christian West would mean for our basic institutions. I’m not stumping for Christianity here; I just happen to have the intellectual conviction that the analysis of human society begins with religion. If you incline toward Marxian thinking, which looks at things in the diametrically opposed way, you’re going to hate what I’m saying. But that’s how I see it.
What just happened yesterday in Great Britain is Houellebecqian. The Muslim voters who came out for the Greens know exactly what they want. They are, shall we say, metaphysically confident. The idiot white Britons who voted with them find meaning in hating everything traditional in their country. Britain long ago allowed its Christianity to atrophy. The radical Left ally with the same kinds of people that will jail or kill them should their party come to power, because the — the white left — are driven by hatred of their home and all it means. They are essentially nihilists. 
Civil war is coming to Britain. Mark it. Rupert Lowe MP’s rape gang inquiry heard this testimony:
The Green Party platform calls not only for opening the borders, but for abolishing the police. Think about that — and think about how the Greens are the most popular party among under-30 Britons. Then again, I’m not sure why we should be worried about abolishing the police. When it came to protecting white English girls from Muslim rape gangs, the British police abolished themselves.
Here in Hungary, we are facing a national vote in April. Viktor Orban, the man who prevented Hungary from becoming like the rest of Western Europe — balkanized and trending Islamist — is on the ballot facing a former member of his own party, Peter Magyar, who has the backing of Brussels. The same mainstream European establishment that has overseen the slow Islamization via mass migration of most western European countries, including Britain, is doing everything they can to stop Orban. Please, American readers, as you take in the news reporting from Europe, understand what you are not being told. There’s a Narrative the media are upholding.
AI And The Jobs Of The Future
Twitter co-founder Jack Dorsey says his technology firm Block is laying off almost half its workforce because artificial intelligence (AI) “fundamentally changes what it means to build and run a company.”
The layoffs will mean headcount at the company - which owns Square, CashApp and Tidal - will fall to less than 6,000 from 10,000.
Where will those people find jobs, with “the majority of companies” soon to reach the same conclusion and “make similar structural changes”? I don’t think we are remotely prepared for the social turmoil the coming wave of unemployment is going to cause. 
[Manny Medina:] There are two areas that I think will be vibrant in the short to medium term. One is energy. The other is healthcare.
Outside of the sun, the most powerful source of energy is the atom. I’ve been telling my kids, you should really figure out how to get into nuclear. My oldest kid just got a job at TerraPower, a company founded by Bill Gates that works on applications of nuclear power. It took him a few years, but he listened to me.
One of the other shortages we’ll continue to have is healthcare, specifically around cancer. My 19-year-old decided to go into nuclear medicine, which is using nuclear isotopes to treat cancer. It’s superdangerous, but it’s very effective.
With the younger ones, I have more flexibility in seeing what’s coming out, but also a lot more stress in that I can’t see the future that far out. The part I want to make sure of is that they don’t look at AI as a threat. They should just figure out how to do something amazing.
Just figure out how to do something amazing, kids! C’mon!
This, from Jaime Teevan:
Metacognitive skills will be very important—flexibility, adaptability, experimentation, thinking critically, being able to challenge things. Developing critical-thinking skills requires friction, doing things that are hard, doing deep thinking.
For that, a traditional liberal-arts education is really important. That’s true at a macro level, at a time of extreme disruption. And it’s also interestingly true at a micro level. Think of what AI does. It used to be that communicating with a computer was deterministic: You press this button, and this thing happens. Now it’s based on natural language, providing context, expressing intent and thinking critically.
Well, it’s a good thing we have preserved a liberal arts sector in the universities that teach critical thinking skills. Ha ha! No, we turned the liberal arts and humanities faculties into woke madrassas, where the last thing any professor wants is for students to think critically (as opposed to regurgitating the politically required answer). 
Constantinople In The Sixth Century
I WANT TO GO TO THERE!
Why Does America Feel Worse Than Europe?
As an American, when you go to a European city or an Asian city — or even to Mexico City — and you see pretty buildings and peaceful clean streets and there are nice trains and buses everywhere, what you are seeing is a lack of crime. The lack of crime is why people in those countries ride the train, and encourage train stations to be built in their neighborhoods instead of blocking them. The lack of crime is why people in those countries embrace dense living arrangements, which in turn enables the walkable mixed-use urbanism that you can enjoy only on vacation.
Absolutely true. I feel it every time I go home to the US. And let’s be honest: violent crime is statistically associated with young black males. It is always strange and disheartening to return to the US to visit, and to have to revert to learned safety habits, such as being wary around young black males. This isn’t racism (though in some forms it could be); this is paying attention. 
In European cities, crime is heavily associated with migrants from Africa, the Middle East, Pakistan, and Afghanistan. It’s a painful but undeniable truth: the more European a city is, demographically, the safer it is. You aren’t supposed to notice this, but you have to be blinded by ideology not to. In 2022, an American friend came to visit me in Budapest with his son, then age 10. I couldn’t figure out why my friend got so tense every time his little boy got more than 60 feet or so in front of us. Then it hit me: Oh, right, he’s American. My friend didn’t yet realize the meaning of living in Budapest, where kids can run around without fear of bad things happening to them, as is normal in America. 
I wouldn’t have let my 10 year old in the US run free like that in a big city either. But Budapest is not an American big city. Nor a British or western European one. In Budapest, they can be honest with themselves about what causes crime and social disorder, and they don’t want any part of it. Same throughout Central Europe. It’s obvious what liberalism on culture, religion, and migration have done in the west.
Culture matters. I fear that here in Europe, in the coming decade, we are going to learn that the hard way. I don’t think we ever will in America, which is large enough for the middle classes — including the law-abiding black middle class — to keep moving away from the problem, which is unsolvable.
Unsolvable, that is, absent the Bukele Strategy, which is simply to lock the anti-social gangsters away in prison and throw away the key. A horrible solution, but a society with its back against the wall and a knife at its throat will be sorely tempted to take it. El Salvador is a good place to live now, because the men who made it impossible for decent people to thrive are now in jail. But it’s not really a liberal democracy as we are accustomed to think of liberal democracy. Maybe that’s the best future available to us. What’s brewing now in broken Britain is the end of liberal democracy, which depends on non-political cultural pillars that have now been kicked out from under it.

Sat, 28 February 2026, 19:00 (Berlin)

Tugan Sokhiev conducts Berlioz’s “Symphonie fantastique”     Felix Mendelssohn The Hebrides, overture

Johannes Brahms Concerto for Violin, Cello and Orchestra


In his Symphonie fantastique, Hector Berlioz set a dark drama of unrequited love to music with intoxicating intensity. It depicts contrasting scenes in visionary tonal colours: infatuation, a swirling ball, doubts that lead to a murder and an execution, and finally a sinister witches’ sabbath. Johannes Brahms’s Double Concerto is characterised not only by its emotional depth but also by its Classical clarity. The soloists under the baton of conductor Tugan Sokhiev are concertmaster Noah Bendix-Balgley and principal cellist Bruno Delepelaire.

Before the concert: Noah Bendix-Balgley, Bruno Delepelaire and Tugan Sokhiev in conversation

During the intermission: Tugan Sokhiev in conversation with Marie-Pierre Langlamet

Live

Sat, 28 Feb 2026, 19:00 (Berlin)

Sat, 28 Feb 2026, 13:00 (New York)

Sun, 01 Mar 2026, 03:00 (Tokyo)


Repeat

Sun, 01 Mar 2026, 12:00 (Berlin)

Sun, 01 Mar 2026, 06:00 (New York)

Sun, 01 Mar 2026, 20:00 (Tokyo)


New: Michael Sanderling and the piano duo Lucas & Arthur Jussen

The piano – sometimes visible, sometimes invisible – formed the thread running through this programme conducted by Michael Sanderling: the Philharmoniker presented the orchestral arrangement of Liszt’s virtuosic Mephisto Waltzes No. 1 and also of Brahms’s First Piano Quartet, in the version by Schoenberg. Two grand pianos were on hand for brothers Lucas and Arthur Jussen, who made their debut playing Mozart: “It was impressive how closely intertwined and connected they were, and their dedication and natural expression were striking” (Berliner Morgenpost).


In our film series, members of the Berliner Philharmoniker introduce themselves and their instruments – and give exclusive personal insights.


“Many people are afraid of Haydn’s Second Cello Concerto,” says Bruno Delepelaire, “as it’s a compulsory piece at almost every competition and audition – and when it’s performed under pressure, the work often loses its natural musicality and elegance”. In his 2019 performance of the piece with the Berliner Philharmoniker and conductor Michael Sanderling, the 1st principal cellist once again displayed his joy of playing in this classic work, which is characterised by its distinctive cantabile style.


Welcome the Berliner Philharmoniker into your home – on your TV, computer, tablet or smartphone. Live or on-demand from our archive.

The Digital Concert Hall is the online concert venue of the Berliner Philharmoniker. Here, their concerts are broadcast live and are also available as recordings in a video archive a few days later. The Digital Concert Hall is available on televisions, computers, smartphones, tablets and streaming devices. Its streaming partner is Internet Initiative Japan Inc. (IIJ).

 =?UTF-8?Q?voc=C3=AA_fa=C3=A7a_'vibe_coding'_de_ci=C3=AAncia?=

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 27.02.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  A OpenAI quer levar o “vibe coding” para os laboratórios. O Prism é um editor em LaTeX com ChatGPT embutido que ajuda a escrever artigos científicos, resumir papers, organizar citações e até transformar rabiscos em equações. A promessa é cortar horas do trabalho chato e deixar o pesquisador focar no que realmente importa. Mas, com a ciência já inundada de textos gerados por Inteligência Artificial, a pergunta é se vai acelerar descobertas, ou só aumentar o ruído.

   

POR: WILL DOUGLAS HEAVEN

O produto mais recente da OpenAI permite que você faça 'vibe coding' de ciência

 Prism é um editor de texto com tecnologia do ChatGPT que automatiza grande parte do trabalho envolvido na escrita de artigos científicos
 A OpenAI lançou o Prism, uma ferramenta gratuita que leva o ChatGPT para dentro do lugar onde muitos pesquisadores de fato trabalham. Em vez de usar um chatbot em outra aba, o cientista escreve no editor em LaTeX e conversa com a Inteligência Artificial na própria tela. A proposta é um “vibe coding” aplicado à ciência: deixar o modelo no centro do fluxo de escrita e automatizar tarefas repetitivas que consomem tempo.
 Segundo a empresa, milhões de cientistas já fazem perguntas ao ChatGPT toda semana sobre tópicos avançados e o Prism nasce para transformar esse hábito em rotina. Ele roda com o GPT-5.2 e pode ajudar a redigir e polir trechos do artigo, resumir trabalhos relacionados, gerenciar citações, apontar erros de digitação e até bugs matemáticos. Também promete converter fotos de rabiscos em equações ou diagramas e servir como parceiro para discutir hipóteses e provas.

 
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Lei chinesa tentou ordenar ChatGPT a desacreditar o primeiro-ministro do Japão
2. IA da Meta está enviando denúncias inúteis a investigadores de abuso infantil
3. Juiz rejeita o processo da xAI contra a OpenAI
4. Especialistas estão preocupados com os efeitos dos vídeos de 'AI slop' no desenvolvimento infantil

5. Cerca de 400 milhões de pessoas estão vivendo com Covid-19 longa

Citação do dia

 
“Estamos usando cenouras e pedaços de madeira”
The Big Story


 Exportando água em tempos de seca recorde

Janeiro 2026



 No sudeste do Arizona, o Vale de Sulphur Springs parece infinito. Um deserto ventoso, cercado por montanhas, conhecido como as famosas ilhas do céu. A vida ali sempre dependeu de um tesouro invisível: um aquífero abastecido ao longo de milhares de anos por chuvas e neve. Foi essa reserva que permitiu às famílias cavarem poços, transformar areia em lavoura e manter o gado. Só que, na última década, a conta chegou. 
Ao lado das fazendas tradicionais, avançou uma agricultura industrial de exportação, com pomares de nozes e pistaches, alfafa, milho, rebanhos leiteiros e estufas, tudo sugando água com poços cada vez mais profundos, em um estado com pouca regulação. A seca histórica, agravada pelo aquecimento global, acelera o esgotamento, e lembra que o ciclo da água pode levar séculos. 

 


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil
Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-02-28 13:02:30+00:00
Subject: In the Swirl of Rage and Paranoia

You might have missed the headline, but there’s been another assassination attempt against President Trump. This time, a deranged 21-year-old man named Austin Martin tried to break into Mar-a-Lago with a shotgun and a gas canister. The Secret Service killed him.
The story would have dominated the headlines during any other period in American history, but in this instance, it seems to have passed through the discourse without much of a blip. Are we becoming desensitized to assassination culture, and if so, where does that leave us? What does the gradual normalization of conspiracy-driven violence mean for the future?
The evidence suggests that online radicalization was a major part of the alleged assassin’s spiral into violence. The would-be shooter seems to have been radicalized by Epstein conspiracy theories. According to messages obtained by TMZ, he had texted a friend saying, “I don’t know if you read up on the Epstein Files, but evil is real and unmistakable,” and “[t]he best people like you and I can do is use what little influence we have.”
Martin’s backstory reminded me of what we know about Thomas Crooks, the 20-year-old who nearly succeeded in killing Trump in Butler, Pennsylvania. In both cases, these were bookish young white men who had trouble fitting into the high school social order and who, if reporting is to be believed, graduated from high school as supporters of Trump. Then, for whatever reason, they had a psychological breakdown and ended up trying to assassinate the president.
The Cycle of Paranoia in American History
Paranoia and assassination have been a recurring, if unfortunate, feature of American politics. Conspiracy and counter-conspiracy are baked into our history, from the founding era to the Civil War to the cultural revolution of the 1960s, which saw the assassinations of figures like John F. Kennedy, Robert F. Kennedy, Martin Luther King, Jr., and Malcolm X. While any one incident can be blamed on a particular lunatic, the waves of violent paranoia that we have seen in specific periods of our history—and that we appear to be seeing now—are indicators of broader social distress, public anxiety, and latent political frustration...

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-02-28 15:02:09+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?B?Q29sZcOnw6Nv?= Reserva + MIT Technology Review Brasil

Estética em estado de inovação 


 Como o ato de vestir reflete a forma como pensamos o mundo? Em uma realidade moldada por Inteligência Artificial, ciência aplicada e novas dinâmicas de trabalho, a roupa deixa de ser apenas escolha estética. Ela passa a traduzir repertório, visão de futuro e alinhamento com um tempo em constante transformação.
 A Reserva apresenta a estampa MIT Ada Lovelace como expressão desse diálogo entre moda e conhecimento. Inspirada na pioneira da computação e no espírito investigativo que marca a cultura científica, a peça articula referências à tecnologia, à matemática e à inovação contemporânea. O resultado é um design que circula entre diferentes ambientes, combinando funcionalidade, conceito e identidade.
 Não se trata de tendência passageira, mas de um movimento mais profundo: vestir-se como extensão de pensamento crítico. A coleção estabelece um ponto de convergência com a MIT Technology Review Brasil ao propor criatividade fundamentada em reflexão, pesquisa e construção de futuro.
Conheça a coleção e descubra como a estampa MIT Ada Lovelace transforma ciência e inovação em linguagem estética para o presente.

 Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-01 09:40:14+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=94=AE_Exponential_View_#563:_The_Citrini_craze;_human_co?=
 =?UTF-8?q?gnition;_the_most_aggressive_AI_regulation;_OpenAI_spikes;_CO?=
 =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=A6?=

You offer a clarifying framing to what has changed in just the past 25 years, and for grasping both the scale and depth of that transformation. — Danie H., a paying member 
Hi all,
Welcome to the Sunday edition, in which we make sense of the week behind us.
Enjoy!
The future of human cognition
Autonomy is no longer simply “I chose this,” but “I chose this with a cognitive exoskeleton that is steerable, hackable, and designed by others.”
Everyone has a generative core that must be protected from offloading to AI. We should all understand where our best thinking happens and ring-fence it from automation.
As AI takes over the “visible” work, human institutions must focus on invisible work: building attention, intuition, social and embodied skills.
The billion-dollar overreaction
Citrini, of course, is not all wrong. The first flywheel has kicked off – cheap, fast software is already undercutting mid-market SaaS. But this focuses on the wrong part of the story. This month, someone on our team replaced a $40/month SaaS product in a couple of hours by building something that actually worked how they work. In this case, it’s not a job loss but a capability gain. We need to look at the gains and the pinch points of the transition. The collateral damage of railroads cannot be ignored, but nor can the fact that they didn’t just kill the canal companies; they also created an entirely new geography of economic possibility.
In creative destruction, markets always show what is destroyed first. And the markets are selling the canal phase of an AI transition that, like railroads, will destroy visible incumbents while building new economic possibilities. But that destruction will be slowed by the compute crunch, which buys time for adaptation that Citrini doesn’t consider.
See also:
The most aggressive AI regulator in the world
This week, the US government became, by a significant margin, the world’s most aggressive regulator of artificial intelligence. Poor Europe is losing even this ignominious title.
The deeper problem is the one I keep returning to – the laws governing this space were written for a different technological era. FISA was last substantially updated in 2008. Defence contracting frameworks assume software is a passive, bounded tool. Neither statute has a vocabulary for AI systems that can synthesise fragmented surveillance data across datasets in minutes, or for the specific danger zone Anthropic identified: capable enough for defence use, not safe enough for autonomous lethal decisions. Raw executive power rushed into that gap...

---
Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-02 03:57:16+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #259

AI is moving fast. Is your data platform ready?
AI is reshaping how data teams operate. But legacy pipelines, brittle workflows, and fragmented tooling weren’t designed for this shift.
Learn how leading teams are future-proofing their infrastructure before AI demands overwhelm it.
Confluent is hosting a free one-day conference with a catch: there’s no catch. It’s a single-track event with no sponsors and no product pitches—just technical talks for data engineers and architects.
🎙️ Speakers include Joe Reis, Holden Karau, and Max Beauchemin
🚫 No vendors. No sales pitches
✨ 100% free to attend 
📍 San Francisco 📅 March 26 
Netflix: DataJunction as Netflix’s answer to the missing piece of the modern data stack
Metric inconsistency and definition sprawl across distributed teams create onboarding bottlenecks and fragment analytics workflows. Netflix built DataJunction, an open-source semantic layer that decouples metric definitions from compute through a graph-based metadata model and SQL generation engine. This standardizes metrics across the experimentation platform, reducing onboarding from weeks to hours, while enabling expansion across all business verticals and LLM integration for auditable metric lineage.
Benoit Pimpaud: Specs Should Be Equations, Not Essays
As AI automates code generation, the engineering bottleneck shifts from writing implementation to defining precise specifications. the author argues that natural language specifications create compounding ambiguity when parsed by LLMs and proposes layered specifications that combine text, diagrams, and mathematical notation as constraint definitions for AI iteration. Mathematical specs eliminate interpretation drift, enabling AI agents to generate correct programs by satisfying invariants rather than reconstructing intent from prose.
Notion: Balancing cost and reliability for Spark on Kubernetes
Spark clusters on Kubernetes face a fundamental tension between aggressive cost optimization through spot instances and job reliability during capacity interruptions. Notion reduced compute costs by 60–90% through EKS migration with Karpenter bin-packing, then open-sourced Spot Balancer—a Kubernetes webhook that enforces stable spot-to-on-demand ratios per job, preventing cascade failures during AWS termination windows. Spot Balancer abstracts infrastructure trade-offs into developer-friendly stability tiers, enabling teams to optimize costs without sacrificing job completion rates.
Sponsored: Building a Cross-Workspace Control Plane for Databricks
As Databricks deployments scale, a familiar pattern emerges: multiple workspaces, multiple teams, and no reliable way to manage the dependencies between them.
In this hands-on deep dive, we'll show you how to build a cross-workspace control plane using Dagster on top of your existing Databricks environment. Demo-heavy and practitioner-focused, you'll leave with working patterns you can apply to your own platform the same day.
Apache Iceberg: Introducing the Apache Iceberg File Format API
Delta Lake: The next evolution of Delta - Catalog-Managed Tables
We went through the full cycle, from exposing the files directly through Hadoop to Snowflake-style cloud data warehouses, to Iceberg-style direct file access, back to catalog-managed tables. 
Nonetheless, it will be interesting to watch DuckLake-style catalog-managed tables vs object-store-style managed tables. 
Microsoft Fabric: Under the hood: an introduction to the Native Execution Engine for Microsoft Fabric
The Apache Gluten project is continually making an impact on the Spark ecosystem, bringing unique optimization and efficiency. Microsoft Fabric writes an under-the-hood story of adopting Apache Gluten in its Fabric platform. 
Pinterest: Piqama - Pinterest Quota Management Ecosystem
As companies scale, manual and static quota systems become bottlenecks, forcing engineers to choose between over-provisioning resources and managing brittle enforcement logic. Pinterest developed Piqama, a unified quota platform that dynamically right-sizes limits using historical data stored in Apache Iceberg, then applies custom enforcement strategies across batch schedulers and online services. Piqama centralizes resource governance across hardware and service metrics, enabling teams to optimize capacity allocation while linking consumption directly to financial costs.
LinkedIn: Engineering LinkedIn’s job ingestion system at scale
Ingestion systems struggle to scale source onboarding—hard-coded extraction logic creates engineering bottlenecks that slow integration of new data partners. LinkedIn shifted extraction logic from code to configuration files called Sitemaps, enabling AI tools and browser plugins to onboard sources without engineering deployments. At the same time, a transactional state machine enforces precise failure boundaries across parallel mining tasks. The configuration-driven approach reduces onboarding time from weeks to hours, allowing LinkedIn to ingest 20TB daily across thousands of global sources. 
Shopify: The generative recommender behind Shopify’s commerce engine
Recommendation systems traditionally treat purchases as isolated events, missing the temporal and causal structure that shapes buyer journeys across millions of products. Shopify transitioned to an autoregressive sequence model that treats commerce journeys as token sequences, implementing RoPE-inspired rotary encoding combined with relative attention bias to capture temporal gaps and seasonality across its catalog. The time-aware attention mechanism drove +0.94% order growth and +0.71% conversion lift while achieving 7.3x training speedup through optimized CUDA kernels, enabling Shopify to integrate richer context into a unified generative framework.
Alibaba: PostgreSQL Blink-tree Implementation
All rights reserved, Dewpeche Private Limited. I have provided links for informational purposes and do not suggest endorsement. All views expressed in this newsletter are my own and do not represent current, former, or future employers’ opinions.

Sender: ii@investidorinstitucional.com.br
Date: 2026-03-02 06:53:10+00:00
Subject: Planos abertos foram positivos em R$ 5,31 bi em 2025, diz Susep

Planos abertos foram positivos em R$ 5,31 bi em 2025, diz Susep
News Investidor Institucional

02/03/2026

‍Olá Tolis Vossos‍

‍‍Veja o que há de novo

Planos abertos foram positivos em R$ 5,31 bi em 2025, diz Susep

A previdência aberta — que inclui os planos VGBL (Vida Gerador de Benefício Livre), PGBL (Plano Gerador de Benefício Livre) e os tradicionais — encerrou 2025 com captação líquida positiva de R$ 5,31 bilhões. O resultado decorre de R$ 157,84 bilhões em aportes e R$ 152,53 bilhões em resgates, segundo dados da Superintendência de Seguros Privados (Susep)...


Robert van Dijk é o novo sócio da Rafter Investimentos

Robert van Dijk é o novo sócio da Rafter Investimentos, gestora fundada por Luiz Foz em 2013 e com R$ 1,75 bilhão em gestão de ativos em janeiro deste ano, segundo o ranking da Associação Brasileira das Entidades dos Mercados Financeiro e de Capitais (Anbima). Para van Dijk, a Rafter é uma gestora “estruturada no modelo de partnership, com foco na gestão de capital de famílias e investidores de longo prazo”...


Azis quer trazer para o CAE a análise dos investimentos dos RPPS

O senador Omar Aziz (PSD-AM) vai apresentar requerimento para que a Comissão de Assuntos Econômicos (CAE) do Senado realize um levantamento detalhado dos investimentos feitos pelos Regimes Próprios de Previdência Social (RPPS) de estados e municípios. O movimento ocorre após a identificação de aplicações relevantes desses institutos em letras financeiras do Banco Master, instituição que teve as operações encerradas pelo Banco Central em novembro do ano passado, em meio a investigações sobre fraudes e risco de insolvência...


Previc atualiza regras para publicação de balancetes contábeis

A Superintendência Nacional de Previdência Complementar (Previc) publicou na última sexta-feira (27/2) o “Manual de Orientações Técnicas para envio dos Balancetes Contábeis - 6.06” e “Regras de Consistência para Validação dos Balancetes Contábeis v2.0”. Os documentos atualizados visam atender às diretrizes estabelecidas na Portaria Previc 1071/2025:...


Anbima lança e-book sobre normas IFRS S1 e IFRS S2

A Associação Brasileira das Entidades dos Mercados Financeiro e de Capitais (Anbima) lançou na última semana um e-book para orientar analistas e gestores sobre a aplicação das normas IFRS S1 e IFRS S2 na análise de investimentos. As regras, emitidas pelo International Sustainability Standards Board, passam a ser obrigatórias no Brasil a partir do exercício de 2026, com divulgação dos relatórios em 2027, conforme regulamentação da Comissão de Valores Mobiliários...

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-02 09:39:58+00:00
Subject: America At War -- And The World Too?

This I made from Google Maps last night is the scariest map in the world right now. It shows the Old City of Jerusalem — the area roughly bounded by green spaces. The marker in red on the lower left side is where an Iranian missile struck last night. The path I’ve outlined is the walk from the site to the Temple Mount, where the Dome of the Rock, the holiest place to Muslims outside of Mecca stands. Next to it, as you can see, is the Kotel, the Western Wall, the holiest place to Jews. And not far away is the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, the built over the sites of Christ’s crucifixion and resurrection, and the holiest place to Christians.
You have to have been to Jerusalem to appreciate how small this space is. That walk I outlined will take you 18 minutes. It’s about half a mile from where that missile landed to the Temple Mount. Had that missile been a mere half-mile to the northeast, this war would, today, be apocalyptic — I mean, Apocalyptic, in the Biblical sense. 
If you think that the world’s Muslims would blame a Muslim country for blowing up the Dome of the Rock, instead of the “Zionists” who started this war, you are dreaming. Israel, the United States, and all the countries — including the Arab Muslim monarchies — that set itself against Iran would likely find themselves facing the hysterical rage of the global ummah. Or maybe not. I don’t want to find out. 
The destruction of the Dome of the Rock would mean that no force on earth could prevent Israel from rebuilding the Temple. I don’t subscribe to dispensationalist End Times theory, but you should know, if you don’t, that Christian dispensationalists believe that the reconstruction of the Temple is a clear sign of the imminent return of Christ. 
I woke up this morning as I usually do on my quiet street in old Budapest: to the sound the the church bells. It is quiet here. The war feels very far away. But the last words I heard in whatever I was dreaming was, “Oh no, not Ithaka!” Before I got out of bed, I reached for my phone to see if the Iranians had struck the island of Odysseus. They had not, but still, that’s how tense I am — and I bet you are too. If you aren’t, then you aren’t paying sufficient attention. I said all my morning prayers in bed, as if they needed to be said without delay.
What to think about this war? So many thoughts — so many conflicting thoughts. The situation is changing by the hour, so what you read here are a picture in time of how it looks to me on Monday morning, European time.
(What’s more, if you were alive at the time of the hostage crisis that started in 1979, and lived through the pain and humiliation of those 444 days, you almost certainly take satisfaction from seeing the mullahs get their comeuppance. I know I do.)
The only real question is: Was getting rid of them worth the cost? We don’t know that yet. I am eager for this operation to succeed — the cost of failure would be staggeringly high — but I am doubtful. Let me tell you why.
First, there is no controlling how this war will go. Iran is showing far more resilience than it did in the recent Twelve Day War. The regime knows that this is a war for its own survival. It is unleashing everything it has. For example, it’s hitting the Emiratis. Dubai’s economy is probably wrecked for some time to come. All the rich Western expats who moved there will probably be leaving, unless Iran is utterly defeated. The Westerners now know that there is no safety in Dubai or Abu Dhabi.
China gets 80 percent of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz. China cannot withstand the Strait being closed for long. If China grinds down, so will the world economy.
Iran’s expectation was that, by squeezing oil shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and disrupting air traffic, it would cause unbearable pain to the Gulf nations that depend so much on expatriate workers, tourism and overseas trade.
I’ve mentioned to y’all before in this space how startling it was to me back in 2006 or so, when I went to a conference in Dubai, to talk to Gulf Arabs and to learn how terrified they were of their Shia neighbor, a regional superpower. More from the WSJ:
“Iran is coming to the countries and people of the Gulf and saying: ‘You know, I am actually your number-one threat.’ This has long-term implications, regardless of whoever is actually in power in Iran,” Anwar Gargash, diplomatic adviser to the United Arab Emirates president, said in an interview. “Targeting Gulf states is completely irrational, and very shortsighted.”
The vast number of retaliatory attacks — and the array of sites being targeted, including nonmilitary sites in Arab nations across the Middle East — is concerning after so much of the regime’s top leadership was killed, this person continued. Officials are worried about the command and control of those weapons, the person added.
Inside the Pentagon, and among some members of the Trump administration, there was deepening concern Sunday that the Iran conflict could spiral out of control, said people familiar with the situation.
“The mood here is intense and paranoid,” one person said.
Let’s say you are a lower-ranking IRGC or Army officer, and you know that the Supreme Leader and the senior ranks have been killed. You perfectly well know that this is a do-or-die moment for the Islamic Republic. What’s to stop you from unleashing all the drones under your command onto every possible target in the region — oil rigs, luxury hotels, you name it? The reasoning being, If the Islamic Republic is going down, we’re going to take as many of our enemies down with us as we can.
Third, what about sleeper cells? It has long been believed by state security officials in the US and Europe that the Iranians and their proxies have in place throughout the West sleeper cells of terrorists waiting to be activated. If so, why wouldn’t what’s left of the Iranian regime activate them now? For that matter, why wouldn’t Islamists in the West undertake terrorist actions on their own, like the Senegalese Muslim man who shot up a bar in Austin over the weekend? 
If you know anything at all about AfD, it’s that they are strongly opposed to Islamic migration into Germany and Europe. There is zero real sympathy among those people for Islamist regimes. Europeans also know very well — even though governments won’t say it out loud, their security services know — that the large numbers of Muslims in western European countries pose a civil war threat. My guess — and that’s all it is — is that Weidel and her party are hoping to come to power and deport as many of these people as they can, before civil war starts. 
This is not an excuse not to attack Iran, if such a move is necessary for protecting America’s overall security. But it is a strategic reality that Europeans have to face. Well, not all Europeans. Here in Hungary, we are almost entirely free of Muslim migrants, as is Poland, and most of Central Europe. Western Europe and the UK, though, are in serious trouble. This is an unavoidable risk of blowback. The Iranians have now attacked a French naval base in the Gulf, and a British military installation in Cyprus. They are clearly trying to draw European powers into this war. Ask yourself why. The answer is: to activate Islamic rage in Europe.
Fourth, what’s the plan? When the US attacked and deposed Saddam Hussein, there was a plan in place for governing Iraq. It didn’t work well, but at least there was something. In this case, there is no plan. Trump has said so. We are doing Libya (NATO’s decapitating Qaddafi) on an incomparably larger scale: taking out the leadership and hoping for the best. Libya descended into barbarous warlordism. Iran is a far more sophisticated society, so maybe that won’t happen. But what guarantee do we have that it won’t? There are no guarantees. We might be in the process of delivering one of the world’s most important oil producing nation to anarchy and barbarism. 
Plus, Americans who think that all the Iranians hate the Islamist regime are fooling themselves, in the same we we lied to ourselves that all the Iraqi people would welcome Americans as liberators.
Fifth, the extreme political risk to Trump and the GOP. When George W. Bush took America into the idiotic war in Iraq, he at least prepared the American people, and got buy-in from the majority. When bombs began falling on Baghdad in 2003, 72 percent of Americans supported the war. 
Trump barely gets Republicans on his side here! He has zero margin for error in this war. Things had better go extremely well for the United States, or there will be political hell to pay for Republicans in the fall elections.
Meanwhile, antiwar Vice President JD Vance has been sidelined, it appears. While Marco Rubio was with the President in Mar-A-Lago as the strikes unfolded, the VP and antiwar DNI Tulsi Gabbard were in Washington. Says a lot. 
A friend of mine is angry over JDV’s public silence. I told him that no vice president, in any administration, can be expected to criticize the president in public, certainly not in a war situation. Nevertheless, whatever the VP’s personal views, he is Donald Trump’s man, for better or for worse. That’s the deal he made when he accepted the vice presidency. My belief is that like me, and like many of us who supported Trump in 2024, he thought we would get a president who was less eager to go to war. We were wrong. 
If the war ends with Trump triumphant, it seems to me that benefits a 2028 Marco Rubio candidacy. If it ends with a catastrophe, that will not redound to Vance 2028’s benefit, because he will have been part of an administration that brought on disaster. This has turned into a “heads they win, tails you lose” situation for Vance. 
Given the state of the economy, the Democrats are heading into the midterms with strength. Anything could happen between now and November, but as of this moment, Dems are expected to take back the House. The Senate is likely to remain in GOP hands, but it’s far from a sure thing. An Iran war disaster would surely shift those odds. An Iran war victory, with Trump having taken out the source of so many of America’s national security threats, would obviously be a tremendous boost.
We are living through what may be the biggest American gamble in our lifetimes. No patriot can possibly hope for an American loss. I devoutly hope and pray for a US victory. But hope cannot make the extreme risks, and the doubts they raise about the wisdom of this war of choice, go away.
Let’s just pray to God that no holy sites in Jerusalem’s Old City are hit by the Iranians. We will then be in uncharted waters, steaming straight into a typhoon.

---

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-03-02 14:02:31+00:00
Subject: O mito do roubo de alta tecnologia

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 02.03.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  Fazer um filme é como executar um grande roubo, segundo Steven Soderbergh. Planejamento minucioso, equipe afiada, precisão quase cirúrgica. Fora das telas, no entanto, os crimes mais bem-sucedidos passam longe de lasers e engenhocas futuristas. Pesquisas revelam que velocidade, logística e conluio superam qualquer alta tecnologia. O que os assaltos reais ensinam sobre poder, organização e até sobre o fascínio coletivo por desafiar sistemas é mais revelador do que Hollywood sugere.

   

POR: ADAM ROGERS

O mito do roubo de alta tecnologia

 A indústria cinematográfica faz você acreditar que ladrões têm um kit de ferramentas tecnológicas ilimitado
"Fazer um filme é como executar um grande roubo", provocou Steven Soderbergh, ao comparar bastidores do cinema e assaltos meticulosamente coreografados. A metáfora funciona em Hollywood, mas desmorona diante da realidade. 
 Pesquisas conduzidas por cientistas e historiadores mostram que, ao contrário do imaginário povoado por lasers e hackers geniais, crimes de alto valor dependem de planejamento obsessivo, ensaios exaustivos e soluções simples. 

 
 Outros destaques do dia

Vasculhamos a Internet para trazer para você as histórias mais divertidas/importantes/assustadoras/fascinantes sobre tecnologia de hoje

1. Os guerreiros online do Estado Islâmico estão publicando direto do túmulo
2. Instagram irá alertar os pais se adolescentes pesquisarem repetidamente por material sobre suicídio
3. ChatGPT Health falha regularmente em reconhecer emergências médicas
4. Ativistas que combatem abusos online foram barrados de entrar nos Estados Unidos

5. Você já está “chinamaxxing”?

Citação do dia

 
“Isso é tanto uma disputa política quanto uma questão de uso militar”
 The Big Story


 Omar Yaghi ainda tem sede

 Janeiro 2026



 Da infância sem água encanada em Amã, na Jordânia, à Universidade da Califórnia, nos Estados Unidos, Omar Yaghi transformou escassez em ciência de ponta. Vencedor do Prêmio Nobel de Química de 2025, ele desenvolveu estruturas metal-orgânicas, as MOFs, materiais porosos capazes de capturar moléculas do ar. 
 Agora, aposta em uma ambição que é quase ficção científica, fazer água potável surgir da atmosfera, mesmo em desertos. Sua empresa, a Atoco, corre para lançar máquinas que funcionam até sem energia elétrica, desafiando o processo de dessalinização caro e intensivo em energia. 

  


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil
Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001
