Sender: nathalia.sousa-campos@itau-unibanco.com.br
Date: 2026-03-12 20:30:20+00:00
Subject: =?iso-8859-1?Q?Cen=E1rio_atual:_o_que_est=E1_no_radar_dos_mercados_e_como?=
 =?iso-8859-1?Q?_estamos_posicionados?=

Olá , como vai? Espero que esteja bem.

Nas últimas semanas, tenho acompanhado um aumento natural das dúvidas e preocupações em relação ao cenário econômico e aos mercados financeiros. Conflitos geopolíticos, movimentos mais bruscos dos ativos e discussões sobre juros acabam gerando ruído - e é justamente nesses momentos que informação e estratégia fazem toda a diferença.

Gostaria de compartilhar uma leitura clara e objetiva sobre o cenário e, principalmente, como estamos olhando para as carteiras diante desse contexto.

1) Recuperação Extrajudicial (RE) - Raízen e Grupo Pão de Açúcar (GPA)
Nesta semana foi divulgado que Raízen e GPA (Grupo Pão de Açúcar) entraram com pedido de Recuperação Extrajudicial (RE). Em termos simples, a RE é um processo de reorganização de dívida, em que a empresa busca negociar com credores e ganhar fôlego para reorganizar a estrutura financeira, sem necessariamente recorrer a uma recuperação judicial tradicional.
De forma prática, durante esse processo há uma suspensão dos pagamentos das dívidas incluídas no plano enquanto a empresa trabalha a reorganização (prazo típico do rito divulgado: até 90 dias para buscar o quórum necessário e avançar nas negociações).
Esse tipo de evento costuma gerar volatilidade de curto prazo na classe de crédito privado (CRI, CRA e debêntures), especialmente por conta de marcação a mercado (PU/Anbima). É comum vermos oscilações pontuais - e, em geral, títulos mais longos tendem a oscilar mais do que os curtos.
Ponto importante: oscilação de preço na tela não significa, por si só, perda ou ganho efetivo. Em renda fixa de crédito, o resultado final depende do fluxo contratado e da capacidade do emissor honrar no vencimento. Se o título for carregado até o vencimento e o emissor pagar conforme o contratado, o investidor recebe o que foi acordado no momento da aplicação.

2) Conflito no Oriente Médio e impactos nos mercados
O agravamento do conflito no Oriente Médio voltou a trazer volatilidade aos mercados globais, principalmente por envolver uma região estratégica para a produção e o transporte de petróleo. Naturalmente, isso pode gerar movimentos de curto prazo em energia, câmbio e ativos de risco.
Historicamente, eventos geopolíticos como esse tendem a provocar reações imediatas, mas nem sempre se traduzem em mudanças estruturais de longo prazo. O principal ponto de atenção segue sendo a duração do conflito e seu impacto efetivo sobre os preços de energia.

3) Efeitos para o Brasil
Para o Brasil, o principal canal de transmissão desse cenário passa pelo preço do petróleo e seus reflexos sobre inflação. Até aqui, o que temos visto é um impacto que o mercado acompanha de perto, mas sem necessariamente alterar a lógica de construção de carteira: diversificação e prudência continuam sendo os pilares.

4) Juros no Brasil e início do ciclo de cortes da Selic
Mesmo com o cenário externo mais desafiador, o mercado segue acompanhando a dinâmica de juros e a possibilidade de cortes à frente. A taxa ainda elevada continua trazendo oportunidades interessantes, principalmente em renda fixa, ao mesmo tempo em que um ciclo de queda tende a abrir espaço gradualmente para outros ativos ao longo do tempo.

5) Como estamos olhando para as carteiras
Diante desse cenário, nosso foco tem sido:
* Preservação de capital em momentos de maior incerteza
* Diversificação como principal ferramenta de proteção
* Aproveitamento de boas oportunidades em renda fixa, ainda com juros elevados
* Ajustes pontuais e estratégicos, evitando decisões precipitadas baseadas em ruídos de curto prazo
Reforço que carteiras bem construídas atravessam períodos de volatilidade justamente porque foram pensadas para diferentes cenários.

Para quem quiser se aprofundar
Separei alguns materiais recentes que ajudam a contextualizar melhor o momento atual:
* Conflito no Oriente Médio, inteligência artificial e impactos no mercado
* Anúncios recentes e desdobramentos do conflito
* Possíveis impactos do conflito para a economia brasileira

Seguimos acompanhando o cenário de forma próxima e criteriosa. Caso queira entender como esse contexto se reflete especificamente na sua carteira - ou discutir eventuais ajustes - fico totalmente à disposição para conversarmos.

Conte sempre comigo.


Abraços,
"Sempre que receber nossa pesquisa de SMS participe, faça comentários. Sua resposta é fundamental para avaliar a experiência com o meu atendimento".

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-12 21:06:32+00:00
Subject: Postliberalism & Christian Revival At Oxford

On Wednesday, Mary Harrington kicked off the conference with a talk about what digital culture is doing to us. She drew on Marshall McLuhan and Walter Ong, to observe that we are all going through a tectonic, world-historical change away from the world that literacy made. Learning to read forms our neural architecture; the digital is forming (or de-forming) it in particular ways.
If Ong is right about the effects of literacy, she said, the culture now coming into being is going to be more emotional and tribal. We are becoming people who are formed by memes, she said — and I thought then about the American college student who told me last year that many of his male friends are being memed into anti-Semitism. Not argued into it — memed into it. The young man said that they get bombed all day on social media with bigoted, jokey memes — and then one day they wake up and realize they hate Jews. 
Mary, who converted to Christianity, says that “re-enchantment” is a natural response to the changed information environment — but warned that re-enchantment isn’t always a nice thing. She said some people today think of re-enchantment as “a polite secular modernity, but everybody goes to church and you can quote the Bible in parliament.” No, she said, re-enchantment also entails, for example, Muslims who really do believe in their faith, some of whom are willing to kill for it.
She spoke briefly about people she knows who have had weird, supernatural experiences online, such that they have ceased to believe in materialism. These people have run to communities where their experiences are taken seriously, and that can offer them protection. She’s talking about believing churches. 
“We have to have a coherent body of theology to cling to” as a rock in the flood of liquid modernity, she said. And: “The only remedy for Internet poisoning that I know is prayer. If you know, you know.”
Insofar as young people today are interested in religion, they do not want “non-mysterious” forms, said Mary. She cited Jonathan Pageau saying that this new era makes it possible once again to inhabit fully the faith. This is coming, Mary predicted. She foresees working-class Englishmen one day being willing to die for the faith, in ways polite bourgeois Christians will not.
“I view Christian faith as perhaps the only place left to stand,” she said. Yet in England “we are disenchanted, disaffected, disbelieving, and dispossessed.”
“Who will catechize the Anglo?” she asked.
Mary, an Anglican, said that “we need to rebuild the monasteries, in England most of all.” This, to restore the medieval practices of memory. But, she said, “This is no time for Christian nationalism. … The Holy Spirit really is for everyone — everyone who is willing to say yes.”
The Rev’d Fergus Butler-Gallie (Vicar of Charlbury with Shorthampton)
Wonderful young guy, booming voice, looks like he ought to be pulling pints at an old pub. “I am a pessimist,” he boldly declared. I want to have a pint with this guy! Utterly English.
“Should the Church of England fail, you will find it in my parish.” – I think he quoted Keble saying that
Beloved people, understand what is true: this world is in haste, and it draws near to its end, and therefore in the world it is ever the longer the worse. And so it must necessarily greatly worsen because of the people’s sins before the coming of Antichrist, and indeed it will then become terrible and horrible far and wide in the world.
Understand also well that the devil has led this people astray for many years now, and little loyalty has existed among men, though they spoke fair words, and too many injustices have ruled in the land. And there have not been many men who have considered the remedy as earnestly as they should, but day by day evil has increased after evil, and injustice has been raised up, and many unlawful things have spread far and wide throughout all this people.
And we have also paid for our sins with manifold injustices and with many breaches of loyalty and with many violations of law, both among clergy and laity alike. Nor has anyone among us upheld the law of God or of man as earnestly as he should; but there has been little justice and little fidelity among men since this world became so full of sin.
Wulfstan continued:
And now the punishment is very heavy upon us, and we suffer manifold afflictions and indignities. And if we wish to find any remedy, we must earnestly turn away from evil and turn to righteousness, and earnestly atone for our sins, and zealously amend our ways before God and man.But alas, too many are obstinate and refuse to do penance for their sins or to make amends, and therefore the misery increases day by day. And if we do not quickly turn to God and earnestly seek his mercy, then greater evils will befall us, as the prophets foretold long ago and as we now see with our own eyes.
Yet let us earnestly beseech God that he have mercy on us and turn away his anger from us, and grant us his peace and his blessing, and defend this land from its enemies. And may God help us. Amen.
In his talk, the vicar said that the same lesson applies to us today. Prayer and repentance is what we should do. He talked about how when he was an undergraduate, he was a hard partier, but would still drag himself to Sunday morning church service, despite his “smelling of God knows what.”
It was just him and “a bunch of old ladies, all probably dead now,” yet they repeated together the words of the Prayer Book. Said the vicar, “For me, it was a retreat to the edges as a way of being brought in to the life of Christ.”
Like Mary Harrington, Fr. Fergus-Gallie said we must return to prayer, and must believe it is efficacious. “The business of prayer is all of us,” he said. And if the national church is in a woebegone state, then strengthen the parish. He claimed for himself this quote from John Keble: “If the Church of England were to fail, it should be found in my parish.” 
I had not heard that before, but it represents a hard lesson I learned from losing my Catholicism. I paid way too much attention to what was happening at the institutional, global, and national level in the Catholic Church. That was a mistake. It wasn’t that the doings of the Pope, the bishops, and so forth, didn’t matter. It was that my attention was misdirected. This is why when I became Orthodox, I decided to focus on the parish, not the whole institution. The one time I violated that rule ended badly. 
Today’s conference began with a characteristically excellent talk by psychiatrist Iain McGilchrist. “In order to understand what’s happening in life, one needs myth,” he said. Today, at the heart of our civilizational crisis is that we are losing the ability to tell stories. And we stopped taking myths as embodiments of deep human wisdom. 
“It is because we have forgotten myths that we are powerless in the face of AI,” Iain said. And: “If we had been wise, we would not have sought Promethean power.”
Iain’s work on brain hemispheres speaks profoundly on the role of attention in creating our realities. “How you attend determines what it is you find,” he said. “What you find determines to what you will attend in the future.”
Iain said that there are three times in our history — in Ancient Greece, Ancient Rome, and in the Renaissance — when culture was marked by a balanced relationship between the brain hemispheres. These were times of great creativity, but of course it didn’t last.
He argued that cultures move from ideational forms in which transcendent truth claims and moral norms are the organizing principles of social life, to idealistic cultures, which blend ideational and “sensate” aims, to sensate cultures, which focus exclusively on sensory perceptions and experiences. This latter stage rejects transcendent values and slides into decadence and chaos, out of which is born a new ideational culture. The transitions between cycles are characterized by violent upheavals. Sorokin thought the period of World War II was such an upheaval, one which marked the end of a sensate cycle and presaged the dawning of a new ideational phase.
Sorokin introduced his theory in 1941, when he was on faculty at Harvard. He collaborated with his friend and fellow sociologist Carle C. Zimmerman, who was a sociologist of the family. I’ve mentioned Zimmerman a number of times in my writing. Zimmerman’s great book, Family And Civilization, puts forth a similar theory to Sorokin’s, but he ties the fate of civilization with family models. He argues that civilizations thrive when they exist midway between the “trustee” family (clans, which suppress individualism) and the “atomistic” family (in which the nuclear family tries to sustain itself without a web of family support networks). The ideal form is what Zimmerman calls the “domestic” family, which is to say, a nuclear family embedded in a supportive but non-oppressive kin network. Since the 18th century, said Zimmerman, the “atomistic” family has been the standard model in the West, and this, he believed, has a lot to do with our social disintegration.
I would love for Dr. McGilchrist to engage with the thought of Sorokin and Zimmerman, to see if there are points of contact with his own thinking. 
Anyway, at Oxford this morning, Iain warned that the emergence of AI is a strong example of the triumph of the left hemisphere, because it values “utility and power” above all. “AI promises us freedom, as do all seductive tyrannies,” he said.
Iain waxed philosophical, saying that “the ultimate goal of modern man is to be himself, unfettered by all bonds. … Yet is through constraint alone that we can be free, and create anything that is true, beautiful, and good.”
“We reject the service through which we could find freedom, and, as if hypnotized, we accept the servitude of the Machine.”
He said that we will not survive as a civilization without returning to Christianity. Sounding like an Old Testament prophet, the gentle Scotsman said that “our house is built on sand,” and when the fall comes, as it will unless we repent and return, it will be catastrophic. 
This return to Christianity must be more than “good intentions and private spiritual experience,” that it must be embodied in daily life. Yet as a Christian himself, Iain ended with a word of hope:
“Life can be snatched from the jaws of death. We matter. There is hope. That too is the Christian story.”
I didn’t hear a bad talk at all at this conference, but I didn’t take notes on every speech. I did on the one delivered by my dear friend Matt Burford, a Southern Baptist pastor and teacher of Great Books at Samford University in Birmingham, Alabama. Gotta tell you how great it was to be in that august Anglican chapel in Oxford and hear not only a man who knows how to preach, but one who does it in a rich Southern accent. After Matt’s session ended, everyone took a break for coffee, and I overheard so many people raving about how wonderful it was to hear a real Southern accent in Oxford. 
His speech was about how he incorporates Iain McGilchrist’s insights into both his pastoral and academic work in Christian higher education. 
“What you attend to you learn to love. What you love, you defend. And what you defend, you build institutions to protect,” Matt said. 
Institutions “are never neutral,” he went on, saying that they are built around what we love. If our attention is trained towards good and holy things, “then we build communities around sustained meaning.”
Because the conference was about the prospects of Christian revival in the context of postliberalism — that is, of a return to Christianity as an answer to the meaning crisis engulfing the West — it was weirdly great that we had a Southern Baptist preacher there. Revival, he said, is not just about increasing religious enthusiasm and Christian influence in the culture. 
“It is about the recovery of attention. It is about learning to see again. … Revival may be understood as the restoration of faithful attention. … It is about people being brought again under the presence of God.”
McGilchrist is so important, said the pastor-professor, because he reveals the roots of our cultural crisis. In teaching Great Books at Samford, he understands (in large part thanks to his reading of McGilchrist) that he’s not there to transfer information to his students, but to guide “the formation of attention … how to dwell with the text long enough for it to question them.”
Iain’s work, Matt said, taught him why attention is not secondary, but primary. “Students today are very skilled at scanning, sorting and reacting, but much less skilled” at paying attention, in dwelling deeply, in being fully present.
Iain, he went on, teaches us to cultivate “an openness to reality as more than what can be managed.”
“Revival is, in part, the healing of attention. It is the recovery of people who can see clearly enough to live rightly.”
“The crisis of our age is not simply that people hold bad ideas, but that we have been habituated into thin ways of seeing,” Matt said. If attention is thin, then love is thin. That’s why “the struggle for culture is at root a struggle for attention, and therefore over love.” 
The mission of both the church and higher education is not to pass on information, “but to form persons capable of faithful attention.”
Matt mentioned that at his Alabama church this past Sunday, a lady in his Bible study class came up to him and said: “Tell me about this McGilchrist fellow. I want to pray for him, and for God to use him.”
Little things like that is how revival starts, said Matt. And things like this Oxford conference. He concluded: “As a good Baptist, I am tempted to say that maybe this is where the Last Revival starts. And so I say, ‘Come, Lord Jesus, come.’”
Saw this on X earlier today. Kind of goes with the talks I heard at the conference:

Sender: news@digitalconcerthall.com
Date: 2026-03-13 12:16:46+00:00
Subject: =?utf-8?Q?New:_Daniel_Harding_conducts_Strauss=e2=80=99s_?=
  =?utf-8?Q?=e2=80=9cDon_Quixote=e2=80=9d?=

New: Daniel Harding conducts Strauss’s “Don Quixote”

For Daniel Harding, Wagner’s Tannhäuser overture is “the perfect work to open a concert” – it feels like a “warm welcome”. For his guest appearance with the Berliner Philharmoniker, Harding then conducted a tribute to Hans Werner Henze on the 100th anniversary of his birth: the orchestra played Henze’s Barcarola “with great precision, commitment and intensity”. In Strauss’s Don Quixote, 1st principal cellist Ludwig Quandt proved himself a “master of subtle nuance and intimate singing”, while his viola colleague Diyang Mei “exuded youthful freshness” as Sancho Panza (Berliner Morgenpost).


It has been 30 years since Daniel Harding first conducted the Berliner Philharmoniker – in our interview, he talks about this special collaboration.


Paul and his friend Lily set sail: they discover a lost island, a ship in the stars, a city on the sea, and a golden fish that reveals the secrets of the deep to them. Boris Friedewald’s story Pauls Reise zu den Fischen (Paul’s Journey to the Fish) is inspired by Paul Klee’s colourful seascapes – as is Sándor Veress’s sensuous Hommage à Paul Klee. Rufus Beck’s reading, Klee’s paintings and Veress’s music combine to create an enchanting interplay of storytelling, art and music.


Hugo Wolf was a fellow student and friend of Gustav Mahler, shared his exceptionally keen sense for setting poetry to music, and shortly before his death – having succumbed to madness as a result of syphilis – imagined himself as Mahler’s successor as director of the Vienna Hofoper. Today is the 166th anniversary of Wolf’s birth. Our recommendation invites you to discover three of the composer’s Romantic choral works, which Sir Simon Rattle presented before Mahler’s Resurrection Symphony in 2012.


Welcome the Berliner Philharmoniker into your home – on your TV, computer, tablet or smartphone. Live or on-demand from our archive.

                                                Digital Concert Hall vouchers


The Digital Concert Hall is the online concert venue of the Berliner Philharmoniker. Here, their concerts are broadcast live and are also available as recordings in a video archive a few days later. The Digital Concert Hall is available on televisions, computers, smartphones, tablets and streaming devices. Its streaming partner is Internet Initiative Japan Inc. (IIJ).



Berlin Phil Media GmbH

10117 Berlin

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-03-13 12:19:39+00:00
Subject: Quando a =?UTF-8?B?w6FndWE=?= pode vir do ar

EDIÇÃO ALIMENTOS


A tecnologia que pode transformar o ar em fonte de água potável

 


A escassez de água doce já afeta diferentes regiões do planeta e tende a se intensificar nas próximas décadas. Enquanto métodos como dessalinização exigem grande consumo de energia e infraestrutura complexa, novas abordagens científicas começam a explorar outra possibilidade: captar água diretamente da atmosfera.
 Pesquisas lideradas pelo químico Omar Yaghi avançam nesse caminho. Utilizando estruturas metal-orgânicas conhecidas como MOFs, cientistas estão desenvolvendo materiais capazes de absorver moléculas de água do ar e liberá-las posteriormente como água potável.

 A proposta é simples em teoria, mas poderosa em impacto. Esses materiais funcionam como esponjas microscópicas que capturam umidade mesmo em ambientes extremamente secos. Com pequenas quantidades de calor ou energia solar, a água pode ser liberada e coletada.
Se a tecnologia alcançar escala, poderá abrir novos caminhos para enfrentar a crise hídrica global, especialmente em regiões áridas ou com infraestrutura limitada.









  


Assine a MIT Technology Review Brasil e leia a edição Alimentos para entender como ciência e tecnologia estão redefinindo saúde, biologia e longevidade.



Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br

Subject: =?UTF-8?B?w4kgcG9zc8OtdmVs?= parar raios? Uma empresa diz que sim

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 13.03.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  Conter raios antes que virem incêndios florestais parece ficção, mas é a promessa da empresa canadense Skyward Wildfire. A startup afirma ter desenvolvido uma tecnologia para reduzir descargas em tempestades para assim, evitar focos em dias de risco extremo. A ideia ganhou força após a temporada devastadora de 2023, no Canadá e já atraiu milhões em investimentos. O problema é que especialistas ainda veem a proposta com cautela. 

  

POR: JAMES TEMPLE

Startup afirma que pode parar raios e evitar incêndios florestais catastróficos

Mas pesquisadores não se mostram tão confiantes quanto a empresa canadense
 Diante do avanço dos incêndios florestais por conta da crise climática, uma startup canadense promete um feito ambicioso: reduzir raios antes que eles atinjam o solo e iniciem novas chamas. A Skyward Wildfire diz ter desenvolvido uma tecnologia capaz de neutralizar cargas em nuvens de tempestade, com potencial para prevenir focos em dias críticos, justamente quando calor, seca e ventos transformam qualquer centelha em desastre.  
 A proposta atraiu investidores e ganhou força após a temporada devastadora de 2023 no país, quando raios foram os responsáveis pela maior parte da área queimada. A ciência, no entanto, ainda não acompanha o entusiasmo da empresa. Pesquisadores afirmam que faltam dados públicos, revisão por pares e clareza sobre a eficácia real do método, além de dúvidas sobre impactos ambientais e transparência nos testes.

  The Big Story


Homem de ferro

Janeiro 2026


 
 Venkatesh Mannar ajudou a levar o sal iodado a bilhões de pessoas, mas sua missão mais ambiciosa era outra: transformar o tempero de cozinha em uma arma também contra a anemia. De volta à Índia, após estudar e trabalhar nos Estados Unidos, ele percebeu que se o iodo já podia prevenir distúrbios graves de desenvolvimento, talvez pudesse ajudar a entregar ferro a populações que raramente têm acesso a suplementos ou carne.  
O desafio era enorme. O ferro reage com o iodo: altera cor, sabor e estabilidade do produto. Após décadas de pesquisa, testes de campo e avanços em microencapsulamento, o sal duplamente fortificado mostrou potencial para reduzir a anemia, sobretudo entre mulheres e crianças. 

 


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil
Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

Sender: rufo@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-13 16:13:24+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?Inside_San_Francisco=E2=80=99s_Racialist_Slush_Fund?=

Lurie’s office did not respond to multiple requests for comment.
Despite the euphemism and legal disclaimers, the program may violate federal law. “The courts have been extraordinarily clear for decades that ameliorating generalized societal discrimination does not constitute a compelling interest for the purposes of strict scrutiny, so cannot justify race-based action,” said Dan Morenoff, executive director at the American Civil Rights Project. (Morenoff is an adjunct fellow at the Manhattan Institute.) “Intent is always hard to prove, but where San Francisco tells us that it runs a program to do just that, it’s hard not to infer that—whatever disclaimers it otherwise attaches—the program’s grants do what the city says it’s trying to do, leaving the program with serious constitutional problems.”

Subject: =?utf-8?Q?AULA=20ABERTA=20|=20O=20concerto=20antes=20de=20Beethoven=20e=20os=20concertos=20do=20jovem=20Beethoven?=

** | Aula Aberta no Cultura Artística |
Encontros com Beethoven: O concerto antes de Beethoven e os concertos do jovem Beethoven
------------------------------------------------------------

Esta aula aberta inaugura a série Encontros com Beethoven, que acontecerá de março a julho no Cultura Artística. Nesta série, proposta pela Sala Jaú, professores de história da música analisam a integral dos concertos de Ludwig van Beethoven. Por meio da escuta de gravações, as aulas abordam o processo de criação das obras, seu contexto histórico e biográfico, seus temas e formas musicais, suas influências e suas inovações artísticas.


** Marcus Held
------------------------------------------------------------

É violinista e professor. Desenvolve pesquisa de pós-doutorado na Faculdade de Filosofia, Ciências e Letras de Ribeirão Preto com bolsa da FAPESP. Aperfeiçoou seus estudos na Escola Superior de Música da Catalunya (Espanha). Apresenta-se como recitalista e spalla convidado em instituições de prestígio no Brasil.
Público alvo: Todas as atividades da Sala Jaú são pensadas para o público em geral. As aulas são destinadas a quem gosta de música e deseja ampliar seu conhecimento e manter os ouvidos abertos para uma audição mais atenta aos detalhes. Não é necessário ter conhecimento de teoria musical.


** Informações:
------------------------------------------------------------


DATAS: 21/3

DIAS: Sábado

HORÁRIO: 11h às 13h

ENDEREÇO: Rua Nestor Pestana, 196 – Consolação, São Paulo - SP.

INSCRIÇÕES GRATUITAS.

AVISO: As atividades presenciais não serão gravadas.

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-13 17:32:48+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=94=AE_The_lantern_and_the_flame?=

AI is not a tool I pick up and put down. It’s become completely ambient, embedded in every process I run at work, daily.
Thinking is my livelihood. If I stop thinking new things, I’m not doing my job. This week, I want to share how I navigate this.
What I outsource
I've made an even bigger change to how I stress-test my reasoning. I’ll start writing and before I’ve finished the paragraph, an argument engine trained on 100,000 words of my writing might flag a structural weakness: I’m asserting where I should be evidencing. As a complement, House Views codify what we already believe at Exponential View, from learning curves to how Anthropic’s strategy differs from OpenAI’s, so a new argument faces challenge rather than confirmation. The friction is useful because it catches what I might miss.
On a bad day when I’m not doing my best writing, I will reach for a tool I built called The Stylometer, a Claude skill trained on 60,000 words of my prose, flags where sentences have gone slack and where the rhythm has drifted from my own voice. Synthetic editors interrogate the frame of the argument. The benchmark is always my own past best, not whatever I happened to produce that afternoon.
What I protect
What I’ve described above is the artificial scaffolding. It works because I deliberately and fiercely protect my actual thinking and what makes it mine. 
The first thing I safeguard is the space where ideas arrive before they’re shaped. For me that’s a walk, a long shower or a blank piece of A4 in landscape mode with a fountain pen. These are the conditions under which something genuinely new can appear. I let the process be non-linear, messy and iterative, because straightening it out too early kills what’s interesting.
These moments are profoundly personal. They depend on a world model that took decades to build, assembled from every conversation, every argument I’ve lost, every book that changed how I saw something. That specific arrangement of experience and association belongs to no one else and can’t be replicated. I safeguard that lived interiority carefully, because the things worth saying tend to come from it.
Once you figure out where your generative constituent of competence lives, that’s the thing you protect from offloading.
This is the best arrangement I’ve found for the work I need to do right now. Ten uninterrupted years of thinking would likely yield something different and perhaps better in many ways. And all of this will evolve in the coming years, so keep your minds open.
Azeem
Further reading

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-03-14 15:02:11+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?B?Q29sZcOnw6Nv?= Reserva + MIT Technology Review Brasil

Moda, tecnologia e imaginação criativa 


 O que a forma como nos vestimos revela sobre o momento em que vivemos? Em um tempo marcado por avanços em inteligência artificial, novas descobertas científicas e transformações no trabalho, a moda também passa a refletir essas mudanças. A roupa deixa de ser apenas uma escolha estética e se torna uma linguagem que expressa repertório, curiosidade e visão de futuro.
 É nesse encontro entre cultura, ciência e criatividade que surge a estampa EST MIT Cool Down, apresentada pela Reserva. A peça traduz visualmente referências que atravessam o universo da inovação, conectando elementos de tecnologia, pesquisa e pensamento contemporâneo. O resultado é uma proposta estética que dialoga com diferentes contextos e com uma geração que circula entre múltiplos ambientes.
 A coleção estabelece afinidade com o olhar investigativo que inspira a MIT Technology Review Brasil. A ideia é explorar como conhecimento, criatividade e experimentação também podem aparecer no cotidiano, inclusive na maneira de vestir.
Conheça a coleção e descubra como a estampa EST MIT Cool Down interpreta inovação e criatividade em linguagem visual para o presente.

 
 


Enviado por MIT Technology Review Brasil
 Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

 =?UTF-8?q?ercycle;_an_agentic_nation;_ChatGPT_Olympian,_seeing_fraud_&_?=
 =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=A6?=

I signed up because you are just getting better and better. – Amir, a paying member
Radical optimism
We argue that solar power could unlock a cascade of civilizational problems that cheap electricity can solve. Solar energy enjoys learning curves, cheaper with scale, while fossil fuels, for all their remarkable properties, face depletion curves. 
At three cents per kilowatt-hour, desalination will no longer be a luxury and water scarcity will stop being “a law of nature”. At one cent, carbon capture will approach economic viability and synthetic aviation fuel will close on fossil jet fuel.
An agentic nation
From DeepSeek in 2025 to OpenClaw now, Chinese media have been hammering one narrative non-stop: learn this AI tool, get a high-paying job.

Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-16 00:49:14+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #261

How to Orchestrate Databricks Across Multiple Workspaces
As Databricks deployments scale, a familiar pattern emerges: multiple workspaces, multiple teams, and no reliable way to manage the dependencies between them.
In this hands-on deep dive, we'll show you how to build a cross-workspace control plane using Dagster on top of your existing Databricks environment. Demo-heavy and practitioner-focused, you'll leave with working patterns you can apply to your own platform the same day.
Editor’s Note: Introducing Data Engineering After AI Podcast Series
I’m looking for guests who are in the trenches. If you have strong opinions on where the industry is heading, or if you are actively building solutions in this space (either in-house or as a product), let’s talk.
Please note: my goal is to foster an authentic discussion about how AI is reshaping data engineering from the ground up. This isn’t a space for promotional product pitches, and I want to keep the conversation strictly focused on the technology, the challenges, and the architectural shifts.
Joseph M. Hellerstein: AI and the Mixed-Consistency Future
Milan Mosny: Ontology, Taxonomy, Data Model, Context Graph & Friends
Context Engineering is the hot topic in the industry. I found the author did an excellent recap on ontology, taxonomy, data model & context graph. As the famous saying goes, it is all data engineering. 
Jason Cui & Jennifer Li: Your Data Agents Need Context
Contextual grounding—standardized terminology, data lineage, operational semantics—determines whether natural language agents answer analytics questions reliably. The authors propose a “Context Layer” combining LLM-powered metadata construction with human refinement to map business knowledge onto warehouse schemas. Organizations adopting context-aware agent architectures unlock self-serve analytics without brittleness, enabling agents to reason consistently across disparate schemas.
Sponsored: The AI Modernization Guide
AI is reshaping how data teams operate. But legacy pipelines, brittle workflows, and fragmented tooling weren’t designed for this shift.
Learn how leading teams are future-proofing their infrastructure before AI demands overwhelm it.
Robin Moffatt: Claude Code isn’t going to replace data engineers (yet)
We see some degree of success with the Claude Code in software engineering. Is it ready for the prime data engineering? The author noted the gap in trust & accuracy, silent data loss, non-determinism, technical flaws, and maintenance. There is a data engineering gap in building an efficient sandbox environment to bridge it, which is a must for brownfield projects. 
Snap: Agent Format: A Declarative Standard for AI Agents
Speed and Correctness in execution always have their own trade-off. Snap writes about how different teams adopted different AI frameworks to move fast and focus on standard interface design to make everything work together. I believe as long as the pendulum swings between speed and efficiency, the software engineering is safe. We will always build the next best abstraction.
LinkedIn: Engineering the next generation of LinkedIn’s Feed
Feed personalization at a massive scale requires unifying disparate retrieval signals into semantic representations while maintaining sub-second latency across billions of users. LinkedIn's architecture consolidates keyword matching, collaborative filtering, and engagement signals into a dual-encoder LLM retrieval paired with a Generative Recommender transformer that sequences 1,000+ historical interactions to capture professional trajectories. Custom infrastructure—Flash Attention variants, GPU-optimized data loaders, decoupled nearline pipelines—enables semantic ranking at sub-second latency for 1.3 billion members while reducing training memory by 37%.
Spotify: Inside the Archive: The Tech Behind Your 2025 Wrapped Highlights
Generating personalized narratives at a billion-scale requires balancing creative consistency, latency constraints, and data fidelity without requiring human review. Spotify's Wrapped Archive distills frontier LLM outputs into smaller production models via DPO, grounds narratives in heuristic-ranked "remarkable days" from distributed pipelines, and uses layered prompts to enforce tone while preventing hallucinations. Column-oriented storage with per-day qualifiers, pre-scaled compute, and automated Judge-model sampling of 165,000 reports enables 1.4 billion unique narratives at launch latency while catching systemic failures such as timezone bugs.
LinkedIn: Driving data enhancement & recruitment success with LinkedIn’s unified integrations
Recruitment data fragmentation—disparate ATS schemas, semantic conflicts, and partner integration overhead—blocks AI agents from reliably reasoning across hiring pipelines. LinkedIn's unified platform standardizes partner data into canonical schemas via hybrid push/pull models (BuildIn for speed, BuildOut with Temporal orchestration for reliability), assigns stable Integration IDs to decouple identity, and reconciles multi-source conflicts into single-truth serving layers. The system cut onboarding from 12 months to 4, expanded job field coverage 1.8x, and dropped resume gaps below 10%, enabling agents to reason and act consistently across enterprise hiring systems.
Uber: Transforming Ads Personalization with Sequential Modeling and Hetero-MMoE at Uber
Ads ranking at scale requires capturing sequential user intent over long behavioral histories while simultaneously optimizing competing objectives such as clicks and conversions. Uber's system pairs target-aware transformers with Multi-Head Latent Attention (reducing sequence complexity from O(N²) to O(N×L)) to compress engagement histories, then routes the compressed signals through Hetero-MMoE—blending DCN and CIN experts to capture low- to high-order feature interactions across multimodal inputs. Online experiments yielded +0.93% AUC on predicted CTR and +0.66% AUC on predicted click-to-order, validating sequential modeling at the ranking scale.
Databricks: LogSentinel: How Databricks uses Databricks for LLM-Powered PII Detection and Governance
PII discovery and compliance monitoring at a data warehouse scale requires automating label classification across schema evolution without manual audit cycles. Databricks’ LogSentinel orchestrates multiple LLM “experts” in parallel—augmented with Vector Search context and AI-generated column comments—to classify data across 100+ granular, hierarchical, and residency labels, selecting predictions by confidence voting. The system achieves 92% precision and 95% recall while reducing manual review cycles from weeks to hours, enabling real-time governance as schemas drift.
All rights reserved, Dewpeche Private Limited. I have provided links for informational purposes and do not suggest endorsement. All views expressed in this newsletter are my own and do not represent current, former, or future employers’ opinions.

TST julga Tema 24, que pode mudar disputas em planos deficitários

O Tribunal Superior do Trabalho (TST) deve julgar no próximo dia 23 de março o Tema 24, que discute se a corte trabalhista é competente para julgar ações indenizatórias de participantes de planos deficitários de previdência complementar contra as empresas patrocinadoras. Atualmente, o entendimento predominante é que ações envolvendo benefícios e disputas entre participantes e entidades previdenciárias devem ser julgadas pela Justiça comum e não pela trabalhista...


BNDES seleciona cinco ETF para receber aportes de até R$ 1 bilhão

O Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento Econômico e Social (BNDES) selecionou cinco Fundos de Índice (ETFs), entre 32 que se inscreveram, para receber aportes da instituição. Os aportes, que podem chegar a até R$ 200 milhões por fundo, num total de até R$ 1 bilhão, serão feitos através do BNDES Participações (BNDESPar) e limitados a até 50% do patrimônio do fundo..

No consolidado, a fundação fechou o mês com uma rentabilidade de xxx% e no acumulado dos dois primeiros meses do ano o retorno foi de de xxx%, para objetivos de retorno de xxx% e xxx%, respectivamente...


Ibovespa deve abrir com volatilidade nesta segunda-feira

Estrategistas de instituições financeiras internacionais projetam uma abertura volátil para o Ibovespa nesta segunda-feira (16/3), com os investidores monitorando principalmente a evolução da guerra no Oriente Médio e o comportamento do petróleo no mercado internacional...


MPS faz parceria com BNDES para capacitação em análises ESG

O Ministério da Previdência Social e o Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento Econômico e Social (BNDES) firmaram na última quinta-feira (12/3) uma parceria para capacitar gestores e analistas de fundos de pensão em investimentos sustentáveis. A iniciativa busca ampliar a capacidade das Entidades Fechadas de Previdência Complementar (EFPCs) de incorporar critérios ambientais, sociais e de governança (ESG) na análise de riscos e nas decisões de investimento dessa área...


Prevcom-MG publica rentabilidade de janeiro/26

A Prevcom-MG fechou janeiro/26 com seu principal plano, o Prevplan, rendendo 1,20% no mês e 13,15% no acumulado dos 12 meses, para índices de referência de 0,64% e 7,91%, respectivamente. Já o instituído Prev+ rendeu 1,17% no mês e 18,31% em 12 meses, para os mesmos índices de referência de 0,64% e 7,91%, nessa ordem...

** | curso on-line |
Frankenstein: dissecando a criação
------------------------------------------------------------
Frankenstein ou o Prometeu moderno (1818), de Mary Shelley, surpreende por seu pioneirismo e pela radicalidade das questões que suscita. Uma das criações mais extraordinárias do romantismo inglês, a obra é considerada o marco inicial dos romances de ficção científica, além de clássico da literatura gótica e protofeminista. Este curso abordará aspectos decisivos da biografia de Mary Shelley e discutirá o romance considerando suas influências literárias e seu contexto sócio-histórico. Em tempos de emergência da inteligência artificial, Frankenstein se revela uma fonte de reflexões sobre nossa relação com a ciência e com o que ela possibilita criar e destruir.


** Tarso do Amaral
------------------------------------------------------------

É professor de Literaturas de Língua Inglesa na Universidade do Estado do Rio de Janeiro. Como tradutor, integrou o Coletivo Finnegans, responsável por Finnegans Rivolta (Iluminuras, 2022), tradução integral para o português do romance Finnegans Wake, de James Joyce. É autor de duas coletâneas de poemas: Vela ao Sol e Ares de Guerrilha.
Programa:

Aula 1.
O mundo de Mary Shelley.

Aula 2.
Dissecando a criação.

Aula 3.
Frankenstein – Volumes I & II.

Aula 4.
Frankenstein – Volumes II & III.


** Informações:
------------------------------------------------------------


DATAS: 7/4, 14/4, 28/4 e 5/5

DIAS: Terças-feiras

HORÁRIO: 19h às 21h

Sender: newworkinphilosophy@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-16 13:12:16+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?"Iris_Murdoch=E2=80=99s_Moral_Philosophy:_Reframing_the_True,_t?=
 =?UTF-8?q?he_Real_and_the_Good"_-_Cathy_Mason_(Central_European_Unive?=
 =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=A6?=

Iris Murdoch’s philosophical fortunes over the past fifty years have been volatile. Early in her career, she was received enthusiastically into the world of analytic philosophy. She was invited, for instance, to give talks at prestigious philosophical meetings such as the Aristotelian Society and gave a conference paper at the Joint Session to which Gilbert Ryle, one of Oxford’s most respected philosophers, was a respondent. After this highly successful entrance, however, she gradually withdrew from academic philosophy, and for much of the latter half of her life she occupied a peripheral position within that world. She was a passionate moral realist, a novelist, and a woman at a time when all three located her firmly outside the philosophical mainstream, and after leaving St Anne’s College in the mid-1960s, she remained influential primarily in literary circles. Over the past decade, however, her philosophical significance has once again begun to be recognised, and she now occupies an increasingly significant place within the philosophical canon, even within analytic philosophy. It would no longer be eccentric to put Murdoch on a philosophy syllabus, for instance, and her example of ‘M and D’ is well on its way to becoming a stalwart of the philosophical imagination.
However, despite this resurgence of interest in Murdoch, she remains difficult to read, and difficult to situate on philosophy syllabi. Certain key ideas she discusses such as moral vision or attention have been relatively popular within contemporary philosophical debates, but discussing Murdoch within or alongside such debates can feel jarring. Read merely as part of these conversations, some of her comments can feel random and unconnected, leaping from one seemingly unrelated idea to another.
In my book, I work to unearth the foundations of Murdoch’s ethical thought, the broad metaethical system within which her ethical thought is situated. My hope in doing so is to do greater justice to Murdoch as a thinker of real depth and ambition, and simultaneously to offer an account of metaethics that is refreshingly novel and attractive for us today. Murdoch’s metaethical theorising, as I see it, revitalises metaethics. Far from being an obscure and abstract endeavour, distant from our everyday moral lives, her framework connects it with our deepest moral concerns, offering a compelling re-configuring of some of our most important metaethical ideas.
Chapter-by-Chapter Overview
In the first chapter of the book, I consider Murdoch’s conception of the role of moral philosophy. She affirms both that moral philosophy is aimed at truth and that it should make us morally better – two commitments that can seem puzzlingly at odds. For Murdoch, I suggest, these are two faces of a single task, and the connection can be illuminated by considering her notion of truth.
In the second chapter I thus argue that Murdoch is using a non-standard conception of truth. Philosophers typically think that the fundamental truth-bearers are propositions (or something similar); that truth and falsity are binary; and that truth can be fully understood quite apart from notions of human character and activity. Murdoch, I suggest, upends all these assumptions. In their place, she instead offers a conception of truth on which it is bound up with the notion of truthfulness, a concept found within a network of ethical terms.
Where does this leave her realism? In the third chapter, I argue that Murdoch has something very different to contemporary realists in mind. Realism, for her, is an ethical ideal for human life and vision involving a kind of morally laden answerability to the world, not simply a claim about the metaphysical status of moral properties.
In what sense does Murdoch thus regard the Good as ‘real’? In the fourth chapter, I offer an interpretation of Murdoch’s ‘ontological argument’ and a discussion of the thing it is supposed to prove, the Good. Her ontological argument, I argue, is an argument from our experience of ubiquitous degrees of goodness to the reality of a perfect ideal, the Good. The Good, I argue, is an ideal, and therefore the standards for its reality are essentially moral standards.
Chapter five turns to ask how knowledge, our grasp of the truth, might be connected with motivation on Murdoch’s picture. I suggest that on her picture, knowledge can only be the result of virtuous truth-seeking, and therefore anything that counts as knowledge always already brings with it motivation to act according to what one sees.
Finally, in chapter six I turn to Murdoch’s puzzling insistence that despite the supreme importance of morality, it has no purpose and is for nothing. The Good, she suggests must be sought and loved simply for its own sake. I suggest that virtue is pointless, on her account, precisely because the Good is sovereign. To view it as serving any further purpose would be to see it as standing in need of some kind of external vindication, which would diminish it.
Thanks for reading New Work in Philosophy! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

 baterias

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação.

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 16.03.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  A Donut Lab, uma startup finlandesa, afirma ter criado uma bateria de estado sólido capaz de ser recarregada em minutos, entregar autonomia muito maior, operar em temperaturas extremas e ainda custar menos que as atuais, de íon-lítio. A promessa acendeu o mercado de veículos elétricos, mas também provocou desconfiança entre especialistas, que veem contradições técnicas nas alegações. Agora, a empresa tenta provar que sua tecnologia é real, com testes e vídeos, enquanto a indústria observa, em meio a entusiasmo e ceticismo. 

 

POR: CASEY CROWNHART

Esta empresa afirma ter um avanço revolucionário em baterias de estado sólido

A Donut Lab agora precisa provar que fez uma revolução na área
A promessa da startup finlandesa Donut Lab soa como o sonho da indústria de veículos elétricos: uma bateria de estado sólido, com recarga em poucos minutos, densidade de energia muito acima do padrão atual, operação segura em temperaturas extremas, materiais abundantes e custo menor do que o das células de íon-lítio. 
 Se for comprovada, no mundo real, será uma virada histórica. O problema é que especialistas receberam o anúncio com forte ceticismo. A combinação de desempenho, durabilidade e preço desafia os limites conhecidos da química das baterias, e a empresa ainda se limitou a revelar poucos detalhes técnicos. Agora, a Donut Lab tenta sustentar suas afirmações com uma série de vídeos e testes independentes.

Passada e gelada: os segredos da comida fresca

Janeiro 2026

 

 Em "Passada e gelada: os segredos da comida fresca", Nicola Twilley desmonta a ideia de que “fresco” é sinônimo de natural. Do suco de laranja armazenado por anos às bananas e folhas embaladas em sistemas que controlam sua respiração, a autora revela a engrenagem invisível da cadeia do frio: uma rede de galpões, caminhões, vitrines e geladeiras que sustenta boa parte da alimentação moderna. Hoje, três quartos da dieta média dos estadunidenses dependem desse inverno artificial.

Sender: johnathanbi@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-16 14:02:55+00:00
Subject: You Live Free Because Your Ancestors Were Ruthless | Machiavelli

America violated the freedom of the natives in order to build the freest nation in history. According to Machiavelli, this is not hypocrisy; this is necessity. Be it Rome, Ancient Israel, or Persia, all great nations are founded by ruthlessness: there is no other way.
This is the interpretation of the great Machiavelli scholar Leo Strauss who writes: “America owes her greatness not only to her habitual adherence to the principles of freedom and justice, but also to her occasional deviation from them. [Machiavelli] would not hesitate to suggest a mischievous interpretation… of the fate of the Red Indians… there cannot be a great and glorious society without the equivalent of the murder of Remus by his brother Romulus.”
Strauss brings in Machiavelli to combat two dominant views on America’s founding. The right wants to whitewash and hide this founding murder: they blame disease; they point to how “backward” the natives were; they want this period of history hidden in textbooks to preserve America’s pristine moral image. The left, on the other hand, seeks to expose and guilt: they tear down statues; denounce their ancestors; and want this injustice seared into the minds of each American.
For Machiavelli, both sides are equally misguided, and for the exact same reason. The right buries its head in the sand and ignores the brutality. The left implicitly assumes that it could have been otherwise: that you can build your shining city on a hill, preserve the original city, while everyone sings kumbaya. Both voices are afraid to own up to the true cost of freedom. Machiavelli writes: “there is no secure mode to possess [a city] other than to ruin them. And whoever becomes patron of a city accustomed to living free and does not destroy it, should expect to be destroyed by it.”
It is tough and ruthless men, willing to violate their deepest principles, who get you to a state of strength where you can actually realize those principles. As the saying goes, tough men make peaceful times. But Machiavelli thinks that people living in peace tend to forget this. Because peaceful times are so easy, you delude yourself into thinking that toughness was never needed. So you either whitewash like the right or denounce like the left. Peaceful times make weak men. And so, to not let weak men make tough times, Machiavelli’s role as teacher is to constantly remind you of the treachery of founding and the true cost of politics. If he were writing a textbook on American history it would be filled with acts of America’s ruthlessness — not to be denounced but to be imitated — just as his books are filled with ruthless Roman leaders for his fellow Italians to learn from.
In this lecture, you will learn about the terrifying things you need to do to build a free, equal, and lawful society. And, as always, I will withhold my own opinion and critiques for the end after a charitable reconstruction.
Topics we cover:
To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a paid subscriber.

** | curso on-line |
Frankenstein: dissecando a criação
------------------------------------------------------------
Frankenstein ou o Prometeu moderno (1818), de Mary Shelley, surpreende por seu pioneirismo e pela radicalidade das questões que suscita. Uma das criações mais extraordinárias do romantismo inglês, a obra é considerada o marco inicial dos romances de ficção científica, além de clássico da literatura gótica e protofeminista. Este curso abordará aspectos decisivos da biografia de Mary Shelley e discutirá o romance considerando suas influências literárias e seu contexto sócio-histórico. Em tempos de emergência da inteligência artificial, Frankenstein se revela uma fonte de reflexões sobre nossa relação com a ciência e com o que ela possibilita criar e destruir.


** Tarso do Amaral
------------------------------------------------------------

É professor de Literaturas de Língua Inglesa na Universidade do Estado do Rio de Janeiro. Como tradutor, integrou o Coletivo Finnegans, responsável por Finnegans Rivolta (Iluminuras, 2022), tradução integral para o português do romance Finnegans Wake, de James Joyce. É autor de duas coletâneas de poemas: Vela ao Sol e Ares de Guerrilha.
Programa:

Aula 1.
O mundo de Mary Shelley.

Aula 2.
Dissecando a criação.

Aula 3.
Frankenstein – Volumes I & II.

Aula 4.
Frankenstein – Volumes II & III.

Golpes digitais com IA têm crescimento exponencial no Brasil 
 Hub de verificação inteligente reúne estratégias, ações e integrações para enfrentar fraudes com mensagens falsas, áudio e vídeo manipulados 
 


 Com a sofisticação dos golpes digitais, um dos pontos mais frágeis da cibersegurança passa a ser a falta de integração entre os diversos pontos da jornada. Além disso, estudos apontam e fazem o alerta para o crescimento de incidentes com deepfakes. E o Brasil figura como um dos principais alvos desse tipo de fraude.

 Por esse motivo, empresas pelo país já começaram uma mudança no paradigma da cibersegurança: para além da proteção dos sistemas, é fundamental atestar quem está do outro lado.
Mas, afinal, como reduzir esses riscos?

--------------------
## **Preciso falar antes que seja tarde **

Caption: 

Rory Sutherland é um dos **publicitários mais perigosos do mundo.**

**Ele não vende produto. **Ele engana o cérebro humano pra entender por que a gente toma decisão burra, e as maiores empresas do mundo pagam milhões pra ele explicar isso.

Esse cara criou um conceito que explica** **por que metade das empresas que tão **demitindo gente pra botar IA** **no lugar vai quebrar nos próximos anos.** E o pior: por que você pode estar destruindo o que tem de mais valioso no seu negócio achando que tá sendo eficiente.

Ele chama isso de **Falácia do Porteiro**.

### 🏨** A história do hotel que economizou 40 mil dólares**

Sutherland conta uma história pra explicar.

Imagina que o dono de um hotel cinco estrelas **contrata uma consultoria cara pra cortar custos e aumentar a margem.** O consultor olha a planilha, aponta pro porteiro e fala: esse cara custa 40 mil dólares por ano só pra abrir porta. Troca ele por uma porta automática e vocês economizam esse dinheiro todo.

“O hotel demite o porteiro, instala a porta automática, o consultor bota a grana no bolso e vai embora feliz.“ — 

**Alguns meses depois, o hotel entra em crise.**

Por quê? Porque o porteiro não apenas abria porta. Ele fazia segurança informal na entrada, chamava táxi pros hóspedes, carregava mala de idoso e grávida, reconhecia todo mundo pelo nome e fazia cada pessoa se sentir em casa. **Ele aumentava enormemente o nível de serviço do hotel** sem que isso estivesse descrito em lugar nenhum.

“**Ele fazia vinte coisas invisíveis que ninguém botou na planilha.** A porta automática faz uma: abre e fecha.“ — 

### **​**​⚠️​ **A distinção que muda tudo**

E aí o Sutherland crava uma distinção que eu acho genial e que todo empreendedor deveria tatuar na testa.

Tudo na vida tem dois valores:

“**Valor de Superfície:** o que você vê de cara, o que cabe numa planilha de Excel, o que o consultor aponta e diz "corta isso aqui".“ — 

“**Valor Real:** o que tá escondido embaixo — e que quase sempre é o que sustenta tudo de verdade.“ — 

Pensa comigo: **se alguém propõe um corte na sua empresa,** na sua rotina ou na sua vida e você aceita olhando só pro que é visível na planilha, você tá no grupo que pode destruir negócios e relacionamentos sem perceber.

Quando você para e pergunta **"o que mais essa decisão vai trazer de consequências que eu não tô enxergando?**", você tá no grupo que não toma decisão burra de curto prazo só com base em números e constrói patrimônio de verdade a longo prazo.

### 🤖 O que isso tem a ver com IA

Agora vem a parte que conecta com **a loucura da inteligência artificial que tá acontecendo agora.**

“Quantas pessoas tão usando IA pra escrever tudo? E-mail, post de LinkedIn, relatório, apresentação. Fica mais rápido, é claro. Economiza tempo. Parece eficiente.“ — 

Mas calma. **O valor real de escrever nunca foi o texto final.**

Era o pensamento que você era obrigado a organizar, estruturar e refinar pra produzir aquele texto. Era o processo de clarear a própria ideia enquanto escreve, reescreve e descobre o que você realmente pensa.

Você terceirizou a escrita pra IA e **perdeu a capacidade de pensar com clareza.** Você otimizou o texto e matou o raciocínio.

### 💬​ **Você não deveria ter pressa**

Uma escritora australiana resumiu isso de um jeito que eu nunca mais esqueci.

Um cirurgião perguntou pra ela **por que ela escrevia à mão em cadernos em vez de usar computador,** que é mais rápido e eficiente. Ela olhou pra ele e respondeu com uma frase linda:

“_**"Mas esse é o trabalho da minha vida. Eu não tenho pressa."**_“ — 

Tapa na cara, meus amigos.

Fazer as coisas mais rápido não significa que você tá fazendo as coisas certas.

### 💼 **O que você precisa fazer antes de cortar qualquer coisa**

Antes de demitir uma pessoa, cortar um processo, eliminar um ritual da sua vida ou da sua empresa, para e olha embaixo da superfície.

Pergunta: **o que mais isso faz além do óbvio?** Quem mais isso impacta além de quem eu tô vendo? Que consequência invisível eu posso estar ignorando?

“O porteiro não é só a porta. O jantar em família não é só a comida. A escrita não é só o texto. O vendedor não é só a venda. O atendente não é só o atendimento.“ — 

Sempre tem mais embaixo. **E quase sempre, o que tá embaixo é o que sustenta tudo**.

Fui!

### **Eu tenho dois presentes para você.**

Caption:

Toda segunda, o email que vocês já conhecem. 

Toda quarta, o **Email do Rony no modo #CabeçadeDono,** eu destrinchando cases, decisões, erros e acertos de quem constrói negócio de verdade. Sem palco. Sem ostentação. Sem guru. Do jeito que eu gosto de aprender e do jeito que eu sei que vocês gostam de receber.

==**O segundo:**== ==**amanhã, dia 17, às 20h, tem um anúncio importante lá no meu Instagram.**==** **Não vou antecipar nada aqui. Mas quem não me segue vai ficar de fora e vai entender o porquê depois, quando já for tarde.

## A maior turnê da história do Brasil começou vendendo só 650 ingressos.

E foi exatamente por isso que deu certo.

Caption: 

Em 2014, Thiaguinho estava no Rio gravando um programa da Globo e ia ficar quatro domingos na cidade. Foi quando ele olhou pros amigos, Rafael Zulu e Rafael Liporace, e soltou a ideia: "Irmão, vamos fazer um pagode?"

“Os caras ficaram sem entender na hora. **Mas como todo negócio grande nasce do nada, eles acreditaram.**“ — 

O primeiro show aconteceu em 2015 no Rio Beach Club. **Eles abriram mil ingressos mas venderam apenas 650. **Poderia ser visto como fracasso, mas não foi. Thiaguinho saiu do palco e falou pros sócios uma frase que mudou tudo: "Tinha gente chorando ali. A gente precisa continuar."

🥁 **O que a maioria teria feito**

**A maioria das pessoas nessa situação faria uma de duas coisas:** ou desistiria achando que não funcionou, ou tentaria crescer rápido demais pra compensar o começo morno.

**Thiaguinho fez diferente.** Ele dividiu o crescimento em cinco fases e cada fase tinha um objetivo claro.

**Fase 1**: Testar. Entender se a ideia funcionava de verdade.

**Fase 2:** Crescer devagar. Sem pressa, sem pular etapa, fazendo um show melhor que o anterior.

**Fase 3:** Parar. Quando a pandemia veio, eles não forçaram. Respeitaram o momento e pararam.

**Fase 4:** Retomar com inteligência. Voltaram só quando fazia sentido estar ali.

**Fase 5:** Liderar. Virar referência absoluta.

### 🚀** O resultado**

**1,5 bilhão de reais faturados em dez anos. **305 milhões só no ano passado. Atingindo 907 mil pessoas em 26 cidades. O show final em Interlagos reuniu 80 mil pessoas por quase 8 horas sem parar.

**A Tardezinha virou a maior turnê da história do Brasil.** E olha que agora eles vão dar uma pausa de dois anos porque, fazer o que, ninguém é de ferro. 

“**Thiaguinho sustenta 5 horas de show ininterruptas** sem sair do palco nem pra ir ao banheiro. Do outro lado existem milhares de funcionários que dão o sangue pro evento sair conforme o esperado pelo público.“ — 

### 👤** #CabeçaDeDono**

**O segredo não foi sorte. Foi cabeça de dono. **Thiaguinho não tentou virar grande rápido. Ele respeitou cada fase. Testou. Errou. Parou quando precisava. E voltou quando fazia sentido.

“Se você tá empreendendo e com pressa de explodir, para. Pensa comigo. **Qual fase você tá?**“ — 

**Você ainda tá testando? **Então testa de verdade. Tá crescendo?** **Cresce devagar. Precisa parar? Para.

**Cabeça de dono não é sobre correr. É sobre durar.**

Fui!



----------———————————————————————————

----------

### 📧 Como fazer a inteligência artificial organizar sua caixa de entrada automaticamente

Você perde tempo todo dia separando emails importantes de spam, marcando notas fiscais, procurando orçamentos?

O Google Workspace Studio deixa a IA fazer isso automaticamente. Ela lê cada email, identifica do que se trata e organiza tudo com etiquetas coloridas.

Caption: 

### 🤖 Como começar a usar:

**Passo 1: Entre no Workspace Studio**


→ Veja os modelos prontos ou crie sua automação do zero (clique no botão +).

**Passo 2: Configure a Automação**

→ Escolha "Quando eu receber um email" como gatilho.

→ Adicione a ação "Adicionar etiqueta".

→ Ative "Etiquetas com IA" (aqui a mágica acontece).

**Passo 3: Defina as Categorias**

→ Escolha quais tipos de email você quer que a IA organize. Exemplos:

* Notas fiscais e recibos

* Orçamentos de fornecedores

* Emails de clientes VIP

* Newsletters

→ A IA Gemini vai ler cada email que chega e colocar a etiqueta certa automaticamente.

**Passo 4: Teste**

→ Clique em "Ativar" e mande um email de teste para você mesmo.

→ Vá na aba "Atividade’’ do Workspace Studio para ver o que a automação fez com o email.

→ Se funcionou, pronto! Agora todo email que chegar vai ser organizado sozinho.

“**DICA DE OURO:**

Para automações mais avançadas, adicione a ação "Extrair com Gemini" para puxar informações específicas (como valor da nota fiscal, data de vencimento) e jogar direto numa planilha do Google Sheets.“ — 

Essa automação não substitui um assistente para emails complexos, mas para organização básica de caixa de entrada resolve muito bem.

E você para de perder 30 minutos por dia procurando emails.

Me conta se funcionou? Fui!

--------------------


Caption: 

Essa newsletter é semanal, **mas no Instagram eu tô postando todo dia.**

É lá que eu compartilho as coisas no momento que descubro, um app novo que testei, um insight de uma reunião, ou um livro que me impressionou.

Se você quer acompanhar em tempo real o que tá rolando, e não perder o que vem amanhã, me segue lá:

Caption: 

Claude Code é uma inteligência artificial que programa sozinha. Você fala o que quer em português normal e ela cria aplicativos, sites e ferramentas por você.

Não é aquele assistente que te ajuda enquanto você pergunta. Ela faz o trabalho inteiro.

Funciona assim: você descreve o que precisa, "preciso de uma planilha automatizada que calcula margem de lucro" e ela constrói. Você sai do computador por algumas horas e volta com tudo pronto e funcionando.

A diferença das outras ferramentas é que ela entende projetos complexos inteiros e faz tudo de uma vez sem quebrar nada.


Pra qualquer empreendedor que precisa de tecnologia no negócio.

Focus projeta alta relevante da inflação e juros ao final do ano

As projeções do mercado para inflação e juros voltaram a subir no horizonte de 2026 no boletim Focus divulgado nesta semana pelo Banco Central, indicando deterioração relevante nas expectativas de curto prazo. O IPCA esperado para 2026 passou de 3,91% para 4,10%, um salto de 0,19 pontos percentuais, enquanto a taxa Selic projetada para o fim do mesmo ano subiu de 12,13% para 12,25%...


MacapaPrev é invadida e polícia suspeita de “queima de arquivos”

A sede da Macapá Previdência (MacapaPrev), instituto responsável pela previdência dos servidores municipais de Macapá (AP), foi invadida durante a madrugada do último sábado (14/3). Segundo o boletim de ocorrência, foram levados notebooks utilizados por dois ex-dirigentes da entidade: o ex-diretor financeiro Fabiano Gemaque Valente de Andrade e a ex-chefe de gabinete da presidência, Karyna Santos Ramos..


Previ fecha 2025 com superávit consolidado de R$ 12,53 bilhões

O fundo de pensão dos funcionários do Banco do Brasil (Previ) apresentou os resultados de 2025 de seu plano de Benefício Definido (BD), o Plano 1. No ano, a rentabilidade do plano foi de 16,81%, superando a meta atuarial de 8,83% e gerando um superávit de R$ 15,7 bilhões no período. O superávit consolidado, porém, ficou em R$ 12,53 bilhões, após a dedução do déficit acumulado de 2024, que havia sido de R$ 3,16 bilhões...

O movimento esteve em linha com o desempenho positivo das bolsas internacionais, que também reagiram à percepção de menor risco de interrupção no fluxo global de energia...


Previc atualiza modelos de cláusulas de adesão automática

A Superintendência Nacional de Previdência Complementar (Previc) revisou e atualizou os modelos das cláusulas que tratam da adesão automática nos regulamentos dos planos de benefícios. Os novos modelos incorporam melhorias decorrentes da Resolução CNPC nº 63/2025 para planos patrocinados...

A Vexty, fundo de pensão que abriga empresas do antigo grupo Odebrecht e outras, divulgou a rentabilidade dos perfis de investimento do seu plano de Contribuição Definida (CD) em fevereiro de 2026. Com patrimônio de R$ 5,4 bilhões no mês, o plano disponibiliza 10 estratégias de investimento, sendo que a de melhor desempenho no mês foi a do perfil Data alvo 2065 e a de pior desempenho a do perfil ultraconservador, com 1,82% e 0,95%, respectivamente...

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-17 12:43:56+00:00
Subject: 'The Nazis Needed Tempestuous Times'

As I keep saying in this space, one surprise I’ve found in my research on this Weimar book is not that the Nazis took power in Germany, but that it took them as long as it did, given how tumultuous Germany of the 1920s and early 1930s was. I can’t get over the fact that despite all the terrible and destabilizing things Germany had been through during World War I and its aftermath, the Nazi Party still only polled just under 2.7 percent in the 1928 elections. There was a lot of ruin left in Germany, to paraphrase Adam Smith. Smith meant that a nation can bear a great deal of suffering before falling apart. But a year later, when the Great Depression struck, that ruin, in the sense that Smith meant, ran out. 
Every morning I wake up and take a look at the news to see what has happened overnight in the war. This morning I see that another oil tanker has been hit near the Strait of Hormuz, and that President Trump is talking about postponing his scheduled visit to China — this at a time when we really need to be talking to the Chinese. 
Trump is furious at NATO allies for not sending warships to help keep the Strait of Hormuz open. The Europeans are rightly saying: Why should we? We didn’t want this war, and we didn’t start this war: you did. This war is not an attack on a NATO country. You will remember that after 9/11, the US invoked Article 5 of the NATO treaty, for the first time ever. That is the treaty provision that activates a military response of the alliance, which regards an attack on one nation as an attack on all. 
All of this is playing out against the backdrop of a fractured, polarized, and angry America. The more I read about history of the Weimar era in researching my next book, the more I realize that “Weimar America” is a creation of both the American Left and the American Right. 
There’s a chapter in the Rees book about how in the 1920s, the fledgling Nazi Party leaned heavily into raising the racial consciousness of Germans, especially highlighting the “stab in the back” myth propagated by the German high command in World War I’s aftermath, to scapegoat Jews and socialists for their own mistakes that led to Germany’s defeat. There had always been anti-Semitism among the Germans, but the loss of the war, and the economic trauma of 1923’s hyperinflation, catalyzed it. Jews were slightly over one percent of Germany’s population, but according to the Nazis and many other nationalists, it was All Their Fault.
In contemporary America, we have been living through an era in this century in which whites, particularly white males, were openly and meaningfully stigmatized and discriminated against, in programs that were approved by institutional authorities, under what psychologists call the “just-world hypothesis” — that is, the theory that inflicting this harm will create a more just world. People of my generation and older will recall having been raised in the wake of the US civil rights movement, and taught that it is wrong to judge people by the color of their skin. The progressive Left abandoned that liberal principle, one that is absolutely necessary to keep a highly diverse society together. 
See, this is why in arguing out my thesis that America has reached a socio-psychological state eerily similar to Weimar Germany’s (and therefore that we are being prepared for totalitarianism of some sort), I contend that this is not only a right-wing phenomenon, but a left-wing one too. In Weimar Germany, the communists did not, obviously, wage a culture war as our wokes did, but they were nevertheless a serious force to be reckoned with. We don’t often think of this because Weimar produced right-wing totalitarianism, not the left-wing version, which took hold in Russia earlier after a time of immense turmoil. But the communist threat was not a fantasy invented by the Nazis, though they exploited it. The fact that communism was a real menace to Germany gave the Nazis a real enemy to hold up to the German people as a force from which they, the Nazis, offered deliverance. 
I think we are eventually going to see the radicalized Left and the radicalized Right, in America, using each other to advance their own goals, as the center shrinks — as the center did in early 1930s Germany, under pressure from economic disaster, and the political impotence of the centrist parties. As the centrist parties in the Reichstag proved incompetent to solve the country’s severe problems, both the Nazis and the Communist Party increased their vote totals. 
If, God forbid, domestic chaos, the loss of faith in democratic institutions, and immense economic suffering brings about conditions in America that usher in a totalitarianism of either Left or Right — and I honestly believe it could go either way, depending on conditions — then the illiberal racially discriminatory policies introduced by the Left during the Great Awokening, and embraced by public and private institutions, will have played a key role in dividing Americans by race. This is one key difference between Weimar Germany and Weimar America: in our own case, dividing and punishing people on the basis of race was a left-wing innovation.
As I’ve said, the breaking point for Germany, the thing that finally washed away the levees holding back radicalism and totalitarianism, was the Great Depression. Germany’s reserves of social cohesion and good sense had been exhausted by the time the Depression hit. Given the various social and psychological measures of our own decadent society, I fear how we would face that kind of suffering.
Years ago, talking about American decadence with a friend who was a military intelligence analyst when he was in the Army, he speculated that it would take America losing a real war — say, one with China — to usher in the kind of radicalism that overtook Germany after World War I. If memory serves — this was a long time ago — he focused on the psychological trauma that cracked the German people. They really didn’t think they could possibly lose World War I, in part because wartime propaganda misled them about the reality of the battlefield (which, significantly, was not on German territory). 
I’m wondering if the loss of the Iran war has the potential to accomplish something similar. Obviously Iran will not be able to defeat the US in the same sense that the Allies defeated Imperial Germany. But they don’t have to in order to deliver a sharp shock to the American people. All they have to do is to survive in such a way that resets the global order to knock the US off its pedestal.
Keep in mind too that if Iran is able to convulse the global economy through control of Persian Gulf oil and minerals passing through the Strait, this will reverberate through the American economy, and society. If the American people, who are already economically stressed, find themselves having to pay five dollars a gallon or more for gas, and higher prices across the board because of this conflict’s secondary and tertiary economic effects, who could blame them for asking why this America First president launched a war that was not in the interests of his own people — especially given that the country is deeply polarized, and the war was widely unpopular on its first day?

---
