Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-21 09:17:06+00:00
Subject: Against Christian Triumphalism

I posted this yesterday to X. Most of this will not be news to you longtime readers, but I felt it needed saying again:
Well, I haven’t been Catholic for twenty years, but I’ll tell you this: most faithful Catholics I know are not so snotty about their faith. The truth is, though, after my own Catholic conversion at age 26, I was a lot closer to Candace’s pride than I wish I had been. This was not the Catholic Church’s fault. The fault was mine. I received what I ought to have taken as a gift -- the Catholic faith -- and regarded it as some kind of shiny uniform with medals and epaulets that made me into a Superior Christian to all those poor Protestants. 
I didn’t see it at the time, but I was so, so triumphalistic. I thought, “I am now part of the Smart People’s Church.” True, most of the masses I attended (faithfully) were bland affairs in which the priests preached with all the depth and enthusiasm of a shelf full of Hallmark cards, and there was little to no fellowship, but I really didn’t care. Look at the books Catholics wrote! Look at our art! Look at our history! Look at how deep our public intellectuals (the conservative ones) are! Why would anyone who knew any better want to be Protestant? 
I loved talking about being Catholic way more than I loved actually being Catholic, in the sense that I was allowing the grace of God to convert my heart through sacramental living (though I was faithful to the sacraments, and frequently went to confession). I would get together with some of my other serious Catholic friends my age -- men in their twenties -- and we would talk endlessly of doctrine and the like, but mostly we would gossip about Church politics, and bitch about Catholic liberals, and how the Pope and the bishops were failing to be what we thought they should be. It got so bad at one point that after what I thought was a wonderful evening of Catholic food, fellowship, and discussion in our New York apartment (the women present were mostly quiet; they couldn’t have gotten a word in edgewise) that after we saw the last guest out, she said, “We need a lot less Peter in this house, and a lot more Jesus.” 
What she was telling me, though I was too proud to know it, was that I had turned Catholicism into an ideology, not a faith, and a way of life built around the faith. She too was a Catholic convert, and she wasn’t saying we should be less Catholic. What she was saying was that we never talked about the love of Christ, the Gospels, the lives of the saints, or any of the real substance of life in Christ in the Catholic Church. We just talked about the Church. That, and how pitiful non-Catholic Christians were, in a “bless their hearts” kind of way.
I didn’t change. Three months later, the bomb went off in Boston, with the Scandal. I threw myself into writing and reporting on it with real passion. And I was right to do so. For one of my first articles, I interviewed to Father Tom Doyle, who had poured out his entire life and priestly ministry to minister to and defend victims of clerical sexual abuse. After we finished the interview, he said to me, “I can tell that you are a serious Catholic. I need to warn you that if you keep going down this path” -- writing about the Scandal, he meant -- “you are going to go to places darker than you can imagine.”
I thanked the priest for his advice, but told him I felt I had to keep going, not in spite of being Catholic, but because of it. Besides, I told him, I’m a new father. I want to clean the church up for my kids. Great, he said, I’ll help you any way I can. But you need to know what you are in for.
I didn’t know, and could not have known, how dark it would be. As I descended to the depths of the satanic wickedness that had overtaken so much of the Church, I was confident that my faith was strong enough to take it. Why? Because I had all the arguments for Why Catholicism Is True clear in my head. Nothing could touch me.
After three years of that, I was a complete spiritual wreck. There was seemingly no end to the evil and the lies. By that time, I was white-knuckling it through mass, full of fear and anger. My wife, who had come into the Catholic Church before our marriage, from a solid Presbyterian church, told me in tears one day after mass, “For the first time in my life, I feel like I’m losing Christ.” I understood; I felt it too. But I kept holding fast to my intellectual convictions. 
We had moved to Dallas, an epicenter of the Scandal. The bishop was pig-headed and arrogant, and refused to recognize the reality of what lay Catholics were dealing with. The previous priest at our parish, we learned, had preached a sermon one day saying that the institutional Church had to change, had to take the Scandal more seriously. The bishop kicked him out of the parish. We moved from parish to parish, trying to find a place we felt safe, spiritually. Later, we found a priest we had thought was one of the good ones, because he was conservative, and preached the orthodox Catholic faith with conviction. We were getting close to him.
He turned out to be a fraud. He was, in fact, a manipulator who had been suspended in his home diocese in Pennsylvania, come to Dallas, his hometown, and talked his way into serving off the books in a solidly conservative parish. He convinced the pastor there that he had been falsely accused, and to let him serve, while keeping the bishop (this was in the Diocese of Fort Worth) uninformed of his presence. 
I found this out entirely by accident. When I came out of my home office and told my wife what I had learned about Father Chris, she collapsed on the floor of the living room, sobbing. “We can never trust them again!” I agreed.
We went back to our first parish, but we were spiritual zombies by then. I cannot adequately describe the emotional and spiritual pain of that experience. One day, we decided to attend the Divine Liturgy at the Orthodox cathedral. We had no intention of becoming Orthodox, and we knew we couldn’t receive communion. We just had to be in church where we knew (as the Catholic Church teaches about the Orthodox Churches) that Christ was truly present in the Eucharist. We also knew that the liturgy would be reverent -- and it was. 
We felt terribly guilty for going to an Orthodox church, but we kept going back, because it was a fresh and welcome experience to worship Christ absent all the anger, fear, and anxiety that had become a regular part of our Catholic church life. It had gotten so bad that I would walk out of mass during the homily and pray my rosary on the sidewalk, going back in for communion -- this, because it had gotten to the point where I was so angry that I felt that it would be sinful to receive Our Lord in the Eucharist with my heart so troubled.
One day, my wife said, “You can go back to the Catholic Church if you want, but I’m staying here.” I wanted to stay too, but I still had the framework of my intellectual convictions as a Catholic holding me in place. So I started doing my usual thing, and turning to books for the answer. I would read an Orthodox book about ecclesiology, papal primacy, and all that, and think, “This sounds right.” Then I would read a Catholic defense against Orthodoxy, and conclude, “Well, this sounds right.” 
Back and forth with the arguments in my head. Meanwhile, my soul was dying. One day, in prayer about all this, it hit me: if any of us are saved, it is not through doctrine and logic, but through a man, Jesus of Nazareth, who incarnated the Truth. If, because of my own brokenness, and the brokenness of the Catholic Church in this time and place, I cannot reach Him as a Catholic, then I must go, for my own soul and the souls of my children, where I can. I remember exactly where I was sitting, on our IKEA couch, when I imagined meeting God in the afterlife at my judgment. I would say, “Lord, I became a bitter, choleric man. I found no joy in my Christian life. My children grew up seeing their father like this, and when they were older, left the Church, because they saw what it had done to their father. But I stayed Catholic, Lord! So open the door and let me in!”
That day, I prayed, pouring my heart out to Jesus. I told Him that I was going to become Orthodox, and that if I was making a mistake, please have mercy on me, because I was doing it because I couldn’t live with Him. 
I entered the Orthodox Church with my family twenty years ago. But what a different man I was! The Lord, in His severe mercy, had shattered my intellectual and spiritual pride. So many of the Catholics who had followed my writing in the papers and online, and who used to send me e-mails telling me to keep fighting the bad guys in the Church, turned on me. I doubt any of them had ever lifted a finger to take a risk to fight the corruption. I accepted their hatred as penance for my pride. The worst were the Traditionalists, but it was easiest to accept their scorn. I had tried two or three times, years before, to become part of a Latin mass community, but I was strongly put off by the rigidity and anger I found there. Mind you, I have Trad Catholic friends now, and they’re wonderful people. This was over twenty years ago, and Trads like that were scarce on the ground in those days, at least where I looked. In fact, at my breaking point as a Catholic, I had them in mind when I imagined the kind of Catholic I was turning into: one whose only religious passion seemed to be in hating the Church that I refused to leave, but also thanking God that we weren’t Protestant. (The Orthodox never entered the picture, because there were so few of them around.)
After we became Orthodox, I asked our priest what books I should read to learn how to be Orthodox. He wisely said, “I could give you a reading list, but that’s not how you do it. You do it by showing up faithfully at liturgy, participating in the sacramental life, fasting, praying, and repenting.” I suppose all of that was available to me as a Catholic, but I rarely saw people doing it, and I wouldn’t have seen it, because I was too intellectual, and thought the substance of faith was assenting to the correct doctrines -- and, I’m ashamed to say, looking with contempt on Catholics who were too liberal. Funny, that: I found out during the Scandal years that some of the biggest liars and cowards were conservative priests and bishops, and some of the most courageous were liberal ones (like Fr Tom Doyle). That did not justify the liberals’ failing to uphold the magisterial teachings of the Church, God knows, but it did cast a certain light on the way people actually lived as Catholic.
I now knew that I had to be a different kind of Christian as an Orthodox. Sure, you could be triumphalistic if you wanted to be. In fact, back in 1995, two years into my Catholic journey, I visited a Greek parish where I was then living, to attend their parish festival. I went inside the church and was praying before some icons. A middle-aged parishioner approached and asked me if I was Orthodox. No, I said, I’m Catholic, but I respect the Orthodox. He took the occasion to launch into a lecture about how WRONG Catholics are about this and that. I left discouraged, thinking, “Is this how they are?” I knew that wasn’t true, because by then I had become friends with the wonderful Orthodox convert Frederica Mathewes-Green. I only bring that up to say that yeah, the Orthodox Church has its knothead triumphalists too.
I remember thinking at that time, shortly after my conversion, about my early days of my Catholic journey. I was living at the time in Baton Rouge, working at the newspaper. One of my colleagues, an older woman, told me she had heard that I was interested in Catholicism. She invited me to join her that Saturday at the Missionaries of Charity soup kitchen to do some volunteer work. Oooh, Mother Teresa’s order! Sure, I’ll come.
They put me to work scrubbing pots and peeling carrots. I don’t know what I was expecting, but it wasn’t that. I left that day thinking, “That kind of thing might be fine for some people, but I’m an intellectual. My time would be better spent reading theological books.” I never went back to the soup kitchen there, or anywhere. Over two decades later, with my Catholic faith in ruins, I reflected that if I had made going to the soup kitchen, or some other embodied practice of the Catholic faith, at least as much a part of my life as my reading and intellection, I might have been strong enough to resist the terrible fire of testing that came with the Scandal. But I didn’t, and there I was, out of the Catholic Church.
I learned something through the experience of losing my Catholic faith that nobody had told me could happen, and indeed that I didn’t think could happen. I was never argued out of Catholicism. What happened to me was like a man holding a cast iron skillet over a fire, with his bare hand. The day finally arrived when the pain was too great to hold on. I knew that analogy was true because I had lived it. But before the Scandal, I would have said that’s a false analogy, because as long as I have the arguments clear in my head, I could withstand anything. I even was so arrogant that I thought I was prepared to die for the Church. I never considered that I could die (spiritually, at least) from the Church. But I almost had, before the Lord rescued me through Orthodoxy. 
I knew that as an Orthodox Christian, I could not allow myself to get caught up in Church politics (I slipped once, early on, but didn’t make that mistake again). I knew also that I couldn’t start searching for corruption in the Orthodox Church, which was surely there, because no church is free of it, being full of sinful humans. I had learned that I, myself, did not have the strength for that work (though God bless those Orthodox who do, and who call it out.) Nor did I have any interest in apologetics, argumentation, or any of that. I was too broken and humiliated. My shiny Catholic uniform, the one I had worn so proudly as a young convert, had been turned into filthy rags. I knew that if I was going to make it as a Christian, I would have to get out of my head, and focus more on conversion of the heart.  This is something Orthodoxy emphasizes as part of its normal practice. It was exactly what I needed.
I quit looking down on Christians from other churches. After my failure as a Catholic, what right did I have to do that? I also knew that I could not make the convert’s mistake that I had in my twenties, and put the institutional church and the clergy on a pedestal. I hope I’m not cynical, but as an Orthodox, I have never expected much from the clergy. As a Catholic, I had done so -- and to be honest, this is one fault that the Catholic Church does encourage in its people. On reflection, though, the faithful Catholics I knew who were just as upset as I was about the abuse, but who held on to their faith in a healthy way, were cradle Catholics who grew up in the Church, and who were far more realistic about the realities of the clergy than I had been, as an idealistic convert.
My stance towards the world became: I’m not going to try to convert you to Orthodoxy. Yes, I believe that the Orthodox Church has the fullness of truth, and I think all Christians should be Orthodox. But after the very public humiliation of losing my Catholic faith, I know that nobody should listen to me. If you want to know what Jesus has done for me through living the Orthodox faith, I’ll tell you, and I’ll help you become Orthodox if that’s where the Lord is calling you. But that has to be that. I’m too broken, and don’t have it in me to do otherwise.
I changed the way I saw people in other Christian churches too. I have never been one of those people who believe that doctrinal distinctions don’t matter, and kum-ba-yah. I wasn’t that as a Catholic, and I’m not that as an Orthodox. In fact, I have more respect for Catholics and Protestants who know why their version of Christianity has the fullness of truth, while mine is not, and stand firm on that, than I do for indifferent Christians. A kindly Benedictine monk said to me once, “You must know that we pray for you to return to the Catholic Church.” I told him I have no problem with that, because in his way of seeing things, that is what is best for my soul, and because he cares for me, he wants what my good. He ought to pray for that! But I think it better that he pray for me to become more Christlike. 
See, as I learned painfully, the state of one’s heart matters more than the thoughts in one’s head. I began to approach other believers, Catholic and Protestant, with a lot more charity, as people I might learn something from about the way to follow Jesus, even as I adhere to Orthodoxy (which means “right belief,” after all). When I meet a Christian for the first time, and we talk about the faith, the thing I want to know more than anything else is: What has the Lord done in your life? How has He changed you? How is He changing you?  Maybe they know something I need to know. Or maybe we can just delight in the gift of faith, and the fellowship of someone else who knows Jesus as Lord. Later, if they want to talk about what Orthodoxy is, and why someone should choose that path, I am eager to tell them. But they have to ask, because I don’t condition my friendship or Christian fellowship on them agreeing with me, or prostrating themselves, in some way, in front of my superior way of being Christian. I used to be That Guy, and it makes me sick to think of it. Being torn down as a Catholic, and built back up as an Orthodox, made a huge difference in this way.
I don’t say all this to praise myself. God knows I am full of flaws, and still so broken that I don’t think I can ever allow myself to rest in trust of the institutional Church. And yes, I have been let down, even betrayed, by Orthodox priests I trusted (though not in an abuse-related way). I recognize that weakness about myself, and try to live through it. I say all this as a warning to all my fellow Christians -- Orthodox, Catholic, and Protestant -- to run far, far away from triumphalism. It’s deadly. Sometimes online I come across hot-headed young Calvinist men who have it all figured out, and who are eager to lecture you about why you are WRONG. And I wonder: was anybody ever shamed and bullied into becoming a Christian? 
Yes, in the United States, Catholics punch far above their weight in terms of intellectual accomplishment. It’s hard to deny that, and I see no reason to deny it. This has a lot to do with the way the Catholic mind is formed. I think we Protestants and Orthodox can learn something from them. But let’s not forget that Jesus did not call us to be masters of theological doctrine, or great lawyers or scholars (though I think it’s a fine thing to seek excellence in one’s calling). He calls us to love, to seek humility, to repent, and be of service to others, and all the things that I did not see, in my intellectual arrogance.
“You’re too smart to be Protestant,” Candace Owens says she told Charlie Kirk, and some Catholics in that room hooted and hollered. It’s so awful. How many non-Catholics watching that will make a note to themselves to stay away from the Catholic faith, to avoid people like that -- people who confuse holiness with assholiness?  Anyway, almost every Protestant intellectual I know is a mental giant compared to Candace Owens. And I know Protestants who aren’t intellectual, but who will probably receive crowns upon crowns, while people like Candace and me, if we make it to heaven, will only be able to look on them with admiration, and wish we had lived better, more Christ-like lives. 
I didn’t follow the Evangelical Charlie Kirk when he was alive, but in what I’ve learned of him after his death, he seems to have been a true man of God who won people to the faith through his conviction and his compassion. Did that make his Evangelicalism true, and Candace’s Catholicism, and my Orthodoxy, false? Of course not. I think Charlie missed some pretty important things, theologically, and I agree with Cardinal Newman, who left Anglicanism for Catholicism, when he said that to go deep into history is to cease to be Protestant. (It’s why Protestantism was never an option for me as a burnt-out Catholic; it is such a radical departure from the faith as it was known and lived for 1,500 years prior to the Reformation.)
But the most important things, Charlie got right, and demonstrated this in his life. Candace Owens might have the conviction, brittle though it is, but where is the love? Doesn’t she know that St. Paul teaches that if we have love, we have nothing?
Right now, I’m thinking about two of the most brilliant Christian intellectuals I know -- one Orthodox, one Catholic -- and they are cruel, miserable people. A couple of public intellectuals I read -- not top scholars, but clever and cutting and often right about important things -- are notably awful in the way they treat people (I know this from personal experience, and from the stories told by others who have been mistreated by them.) Was I ever like that? Maybe I was. Maybe God will show them one day the same severe mercy he showed me back in the day, to break my intellectualism and spiritual pride. It might save their souls. But it shouldn’t have to come to that.
Last point: in 2019, when I was traveling through the former communist countries of Europe interviewing Christians -- Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant -- who had stayed behind the Iron Curtain, and suffered for their faith, and when I was reading books about their testimonies (I’m thinking in particular of the saintly Catholic layman Dr. Silvester Krcmery, who served over a decade in a communist prison for his work in the underground church), I discovered something. In the gulag, the Christians imprisoned there discovered something. They found out that they were not thrown into prison and tortured by the communists because they were Catholic, Protestant, or Orthodox. They were made to suffer because they were Christians. They learned to pray together, to help each other, to bear each other’s burdens as brothers and sisters in Christ. 
If the secret police ever come for any of us, it won’t be because our Catholicism, our Orthodoxy, or our Protestantism. It will be because we confess Christ Jesus. There’s a lesson there for all of us. An important lesson. If we receive the gift of Christian faith, in whatever form, and wear it proudly, as something to lord over other Christians, or unbelievers, we are doing it wrong. The strongest Catholics, the strongest Orthodox, and the strongest Protestants, are the most humble ones, I think. As the saying goes, they have learned that they are nothing but beggars who are trying to show the other hungry people where to find the bread.
I had lunch today in Budapest with an American reader who stopped in town with his wife on a Viking river cruise. He too is an adult convert to Orthodoxy, and in fact is a carver of icons, like Jonathan Pageau. One thing he said to me stuck: “The longer I live and the deeper I go into the faith, the more aware I become of what I don’t know.” That would not have made sense to me at all at the age of 26, on the night I was received into the Catholic Church. It wouldn’t have made sense to me had I been received into the Orthodox Church instead that night, or into any Protestant Church. I was too young and full of myself. I hadn’t suffered. 
Well, I’m 59 now, and I have suffered (and so, in life, has my lunch companion). I don’t recommend it, but it has made all the difference. I think as I finish this of what that elderly Russian Baptist man told me as we stood in the snow just off Red Square: “Go back to America and tell the church: if you aren’t prepared to suffer for your faith, it means nothing.”
Candace will learn. Or she will perish, spiritually.
Lord have mercy on me, a sinner. No kidding.
Surprise! Iran Can Now Hit Europe With Missiles
We now know that Iran has twice the missile range than previously thought. The Iranians are sending a message to America and Europe: you think you’re safe, but you aren’t. Imagine the Islamic Republic of Iran developing nuclear bombs, and the capacity to put them on what is functionally an ICBM, and credibly threaten to take out Berlin, Rome, or Vienna? Possibly even Paris? 
You think I’m kidding about the apocalypticism of the Iranian regime? I asked Grok to summarize its vision, which is rooted in Twelver Shia eschatalogy:
This is not fringe or hidden but a foundational element of the regime’s ideology, explicitly promoted in official statements, military doctrine, and clerical teachings.
Core Belief: The Mahdi’s Return
Twelver Shia Muslims (the branch dominating Iran) believe the 12th Imam, Muhammad al-Mahdi (the “Hidden Imam”), went into occultation in the 9th century and will return as a messianic savior. His reappearance will follow apocalyptic events—global chaos, widespread war, martyrdom, suffering, and the defeat of “infidels” and oppressors—culminating in divine justice, Shia-led global rule, and the end of oppression. Jesus (Isa) is said to return and pray behind the Mahdi. Some hadiths describe massive casualties (e.g., 80% of humanity perishing in the final battles).
The Islamic Republic, established by Ayatollah Khomeini in 1979 via velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist), positions the Supreme Leader as the Mahdi’s deputy until his return. The state and its institutions (especially the IRGC) are tools to prepare the ground for this event—not passively wait, but actively build an “Islamic civilization” through resistance, revolution export, and struggle against perceived enemies (the U.S. as “Great Satan,” Israel as the “Zionist entity,” and Sunni rivals)
Please spare me the claim that “Christians believe in apocalyptic chaos in the Last Days too.” Yes, we do, though the different confessions have various ideas about when and how this will come about. But no Christians believes we have a religious duty to make it happen! Christian scripture is clear that when those days arrive, they will be horrific for Christians, who will face mass persecution and death. 
The Twelver Shia regime in Iran believes it does have a duty to bring about the Shia Muslim concept of the Apocalypse. And it has been working towards that for decades. As I’ve been saying in this space, I believe the Iranian regime is evil, and that it would be very much in our interests if it were to fall (and in the interest of the long-suffering Iranian people). The only real question I’ve had is whether or not the risk the US runs in launching a war to topple that regime would be worth the immense costs. 
I still don’t know, for myself, the answer. I don’t think anybody really does, on either side. Trump clearly was not prepared for this level of Iranian resistance and resilience, despite the fact that the Pentagon warned him in advance. Nevertheless, here we are. And as of today, the world now knows that these apocalyptic Islamic lunatics already have the missile capacity to strike most of Europe. The idea of deterrence assumes that we are dealing with rational actors. This would work with most states, but Iran isn’t most states; its leadership believes in bringing about the Apocalypse.
Now, imagine that same regime, a few years from now, with nuclear-armed missiles capable of evaporating European capitals, dictating terms to European governments about how they are to respond to Muslims living among them. If you have faith that the current ruling class in Europe would be capable of standing up to that kind of blackmail, well, you have not been paying attention. They would submit, to save their own capitals. Iranian nuclear bomb capacity + missiles capable of striking into the heart of Europe (which, I repeat, we know as of today they already have) = a situation looking a hell of a lot like checkmate. In such a scenario, the ayatollahs would have been able to pull off what no Islamic army since the days of Mohammed was able to do: conquer the heart of Christendom.
Does this sound fantastical to you? I don’t think it is. In any case, can we really risk hoping that it never happens, that the Iranian nuclear weapons program can be successfully deterred, forever? If they get the Bomb, it’s game over — not just for Israel, but for Europe. You could say, plausibly, that the West deterred the Soviet Union, which had the capacity to annihilate Europe and America, successfully during the Cold War. This is true — but recall, the Soviets, despite the evil of their ideology, were materialists and rationalists. The Iranians most definitely are not. I think we in the West suffer from a lack of imaginative capacity when it comes to the religious mind. We might comfort ourselves by thinking that for all their apocalyptic religious bluster, the Iranians are, deep down, rationalists. That would be a huge mistake. 
Consider a Christian case: why do Christians honor our martyrs? It makes no sense: why would you choose to die, often horribly, when denying your faith would save your life? It’s because you believe that there are things more important than saving your own life — and that testifying to the truth of these things, even if you have to pay the ultimate price (death), is worth it. This makes no sense to the secular rationalist mind. It makes perfect sense to the religious Christian mind. 
But again, we Christians do not believe it is our role to bring about the Apocalypse. The Twelver Shia masters of Iran do. Do you really think that they would hesitate to see their own capital, Tehran, vaporized by an American nuclear bomb if they judged that sacrifice would bring forth the Mahdi? Maybe they would, but I wouldn’t bet on it. Not after seeing them come close to destroying the Dome of the Rock. 
All of this is to say that today’s news moves me, with great reluctance, more towards supporting this war. I hate this war. It is costing America, Europe, and the world, plenty. I think there is a lot more pain yet to come. Trump is manifestly a poor commander-in-chief. But if you think this war is all about Israel, then the Iranians have demonstrated today, with their attempt to hit Diego Garcia 4,000km away, that the existential threat that Israel faces from a nuclear-armed Iran is now a matter for Europe too, and therefore for NATO, which means America. 
I know many of you will disagree. Let us discuss this in the comments civilly. Passions are high, but feel free to comment strongly. But if I see anybody ranting at me or anybody else, I will delete the comment. You — and you know who you are — have been warned.

---
Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-21 10:06:21+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?b?8J+UriBKZW5zZW7igJlzIE9wZW5DbGF3IHRoZXNpcw==?=

Jensen Huang delivered a stunning performance at this year’s GTC. I want to reflect on one thing Jensen said that tells us a lot about the near future of AI:
Every company needs an OpenClaw strategy.
OpenClaw is the most exciting piece of technology I have seen since the web browser in 1992. Jensen reached the same conclusion independently, from the other end of a trillion-dollar supply chain. Today, I want to explain what the shift Jensen described means for the AI economy, for your organization and for the window that is already closing.
The end of the training era
When ChatGPT arrived in late 2022, the underlying economics of AI were dominated by training. Building a large language model required astronomical compute – 10²³, 10²⁴ floating point operations or more. GPT-4 reportedly cost over $100 million to train. Meta’s Llama series consumed thousands of NVIDIA chips running for months. Data centers were configured for maximum parallelism, maximum throughput, everything optimized for the one-time act of building a model.
But training is, by definition, a one-time event. A model is trained once and then run — inferred against, in the technical vocabulary — millions or billions of times by actual users. And inference has completely different economics from training.
How large is large? As users shifted from simple chatbot interactions to reasoning models and now to agentic systems that run complex, multi-step workflows, compute applied per user interaction increased by a factor of 10,000. At the same time, the number of users deploying AI systems at scale increased by a factor of 100. The outcome is a million-fold expansion in inference demand over roughly two years.
A million times. In two years.
On Monday, working with R. Mini Arnold and its four sub-agents – R. Veblen for book research, R. Simmons for portfolio management, R. Bradlee for editorial analysis, R. Gulbenkian for AI economy frameworks – I applied 870 million tokens in a single day. Close enough to a billion. My average for the past week has been well above 200 million per day, seven days a week. And this is what work looks like when you have the right harness, OpenClaw.
The harness is the revolution
The standard debate about the AI investment and AI in general has mostly been about the wrong thing. The boosters and the sober-minded researchers at frontier labs argue that capability improvements are not near a wall. On the other end are the skeptics, who point to diminishing returns on training compute and say the AI wave is speculative. Both camps are measuring the performance curve and missing the diffusion curve. And the diffusion curve is the one that determines whether the trillion-dollar infrastructure bet is justified.
Consider the internal combustion engine. A mechanical engineer with access to a bare engine can do something useful – connect it to a generator, build a hedge trimmer, strap it to a bicycle. Impressive. But what makes the internal combustion engine transform an economy is the harness: the automobile. The car is not a clever engine. It’s a chassis, seats, a windscreen, a steering system, and yes, a terrible hi-fi. But that harness is what makes people use the engine constantly, for decades, at scale.
Would you rather have a race-tuned Ferrari engine sitting on your lawn, or a ten-year-old petrol engine actually in a car you can drive somewhere useful? The harness wins every time.
For AI, the harness moment happened at the tail end of 2025. Claude Code began to work reliably enough that you could leave it running overnight and trust what it had done in the morning. Not perfectly, but reliably enough. And that threshold, that “I can leave it to its own devices” threshold, changed everything. It changed what users asked AI to do. It changed how long tasks ran. It changed the token usage profile of every organization that crossed it. 
Now, OpenClaw is the harness for the next layer.
What the inference economy demands of organizations
The practical question is what all this means for a firm trying to make sensible decisions in 2026. And here I think the consensus response – more pilots, careful evaluation, measured rollout – is dangerously behind the curve.
Tokens are not an IT line item. They are a productive input, as fundamental to knowledge work as electricity or office space. The organizational error most large companies are making right now is treating token budgets as an IT function – a cost center to be rationalized and kept away from the business units. This is wrong. It is the equivalent of requiring engineers to book time on the engine through the facilities department...

Subject: =?UTF-8?B?Q29sZcOnw6Nv?= Reserva + MIT Technology Review Brasil

Quando inovação também se expressa no vestir 


 De que maneira o contexto tecnológico influencia escolhas cotidianas? Em um período marcado por avanços científicos, Inteligência Artificial e novas dinâmicas de trabalho, a forma de se vestir também acompanha essas transformações. A moda passa a refletir referências culturais, científicas e digitais que moldam a maneira como interpretamos o presente.
 A Reserva traduz esse diálogo por meio da estampa EST MIT Neon Bird. Inspirada pelo imaginário da inovação e pela estética que emerge da cultura tecnológica, a peça incorpora elementos visuais que remetem à experimentação, à criatividade e ao pensamento contemporâneo. O design propõe uma leitura visual que transita entre diferentes ambientes e repertórios.
 A proposta se aproxima do olhar investigativo que orienta a MIT Technology Review Brasil, ao explorar como ciência, tecnologia e imaginação criativa também podem aparecer em expressões do cotidiano. O vestir passa a funcionar como extensão desse ecossistema cultural em transformação.
Explore e descubra como a estampa EST MIT Neon Bird traduz inovação e criatividade em linguagem visual.

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-22 06:50:10+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=94=AE_Exponential_View_#566:_A_solar_shield;_AI_agents;_?=
 =?UTF-8?q?human_judgment;_China=E2=80=99s_robots++?=

Hi all, 
Happy Sunday. I’m en route to NYC for a week of meetings and speaking. If you’re travelling this week too, it’s a great moment to catch up on my latest podcast episode, where I explain why I changed my mind about Apple’s AI outlook:
Let’s go!
OpenClaw and the inference economy
We’re about to discover, the hard way, that most organizations are thinking about AI in entirely the wrong way.
In a training-first world, it made some sense to see AI as a capital project. But we don’t live in that world any more, as Jensen Huang made clear at NVIDIA GTC this week. We’ve shifted to an inference-first economy, where the dominant activity is running models – continuously, at scale, across millions of workflows and, increasingly, AI agents.
In that world, tokens are not an IT line item. They are a productive input, as fundamental to knowledge work as electricity, office space, or salary. I write about this at some length in my latest essay:
Pakistan’s solar shield
92% of countries would be more energy secure under a renewable paradigm than under a fossil one. Many countries used fossil fuels as a geopolitical weapon. From OPEC’s oil embargoes to Russia’s manipulation of natural gas, control over these resources gave them significant power. With renewables, this power dissipates. Once you have the solar panel, you have the energy – OPEC can’t block the sun.
Not delegating judgment
Last weekend, I spotted the AI legend, Andrej Karpathy , had vibe-coded an overview of AI labor market risk. It was rapidly spreading across X. As I strolled down to my studio, a three-minute mosey, I asked my OpenClaw agent, R Mini Arnold, to do this for more than just the US. 
After about 15 minutes, it came back with an interactive tool covering 25 countries. For each country, it grabbed employment by job category, as defined by the relevant statistical authority. Then it scored 1.4 billion jobs across several hundred categories in those two dozen-and-change countries.  You can see the screenshot of the version I built below. I’ve obfuscated some numbers.
I won’t get bogged down in whether Andrej was right or wrong to publish this exploratory tool the way he did.  But I will tell you why I didn’t publish my casual vibe-coded map of jobs at risk. In simplest terms, information and analysis matter. They carry consequences for how people understand their role in the world.  ...

Chicken Marbella (
)

(
)

Chicken Marbella—arguably the most famous recipe from the beloved
Silver Palate Cookbook (first published in 1982!)—has been a
staple at my family’s holiday table for as long as I can
remember. With Passover just around the corner, it’s the kind of
dish that feels both nostalgic and special, yet is easy enough to
pull off without much fuss.

The magic is in the flavor combination: chicken marinated with
garlic and herbs, then roasted with prunes, olives, capers, and a
savory-sweet wine sauce. It’s a little unexpected but completely
delicious—and the best part is that all the prep is done ahead,
so at dinnertime you just pop it in the oven.

actually quite simple. It has a crisp meringue shell with a soft,
marshmallowy center, topped with whipped cream and fresh fruit.
It’s a beautiful, make-ahead dessert that’s perfect for Passover
or Easter (and it just happens to be naturally gluten-free).

Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-23 01:31:45+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #262

This week: Orchestrating Databricks across multiple workspaces
In this hands-on deep dive, you'll learn how to build a cross-workspace control plane for Databricks using Dagster — connecting multiple workspaces, dbt, and Fivetran into a single observable asset graph with zero code changes to get started.
Pinterest: Building an MCP Ecosystem at Pinterest
Agent tooling at scale requires decentralizing context across domain-specific servers while maintaining security, discoverability, and governance across production systems. Pinterest’s MCP ecosystem deploys specialized servers (Presto, Spark, Airflow) behind a central registry, routes requests via JWT end-user tokens and SPIFFE mesh identities, and enforces human approval for sensitive actions such as data overwrites. The system handles 66,000+ monthly invocations while saving engineers 7,000 hours monthly, validating decentralized tooling as the production pattern for agentic workflows.
Julien Simon: Still Missing Critical Pieces
Tool standardization protocols face re-fragmentation when architectural constraints—token overhead, stateless scaling, weak auth—force enterprises toward custom implementations for production workloads. The author argues that MCP won adoption but lacks enterprise readiness: Cloudflare's native MCP costs 244,000 tokens versus 1,000 in "Code Mode"; sticky routing defeats load balancers; and missing governance leaves security to individual teams. Companies like Perplexity and Cloudflare are abandoning MCP's tool-calling layer in favor of direct APIs and code generation, signaling that production-scale enterprises require deterministic execution patterns that MCP cannot provide.
Databricks: Breaking the Microbatch Barrier: The Architecture of Apache Spark Real-Time Mode
Real-time analytics infrastructure traditionally required separate engines for throughput (Spark) and sub-100ms latency (Flink), leading to duplicated tooling and operational complexity. Apache Spark 4.1's Real-Time Mode eliminates this trade-off by using longer epochs with boundary checkpointing, concurrent map-reduce stages, and non-blocking operators that emit results continuously rather than buffering them. The unified engine handles both massive ETL and low-latency fraud detection while preserving Spark's lineage-based fault tolerance, consolidating the data stack for single-engine architectures.
Sponsored: The AI Modernization Guide
AI is reshaping how data teams operate. But legacy pipelines, brittle workflows, and fragmented tooling weren’t designed for this shift.
Learn how leading teams are future-proofing their infrastructure before AI demands overwhelm it.
Etsy: Making Ads Count: Using MMoE and Auxiliary Tasks to Better Connect Buyers & Sellers
Multi-objective ranking in marketplaces faces metric conflicts—optimizing for clicks often degrades conversions—thereby requiring task-specific expert routing while managing data sparsity across event hierarchies. Etsy's MMoE architecture routes CTR and purchase prediction tasks through specialized experts with gated selection, then bridges sparse purchase signals using auxiliary add-to-cart tasks that correlate strongly with intent. The system achieved a 3.5% lift in purchase AUC and a 1% lift in click AUC while reducing inference cost through model pruning, enabling more accurate auto-bidding for sellers.
Meta: Ranking Engineer Agent (REA): The Autonomous AI Agent Accelerating Meta’s Ads Ranking Innovation
ML model iteration at production scale requires balancing hypothesis generation, resource constraints, and infrastructure resilience across multi-day experiment cycles. Meta’s Ranking Engineer Agent combines a Dual-Source Hypothesis Engine (historical experiments + novel ML research proposals) with autonomous debugging and cost-aware planning to execute long-horizon ranking workflows without human supervision. The system doubled average model accuracy across six models while enabling three engineers to maintain eight production models—a 5x productivity gain over traditional team structures.
Rahul Garg: Context Anchoring
AI-assisted development degrades over long sessions as models lose reasoning context ("why") despite retaining technical choices ("what"), trapping developers in single conversations to avoid context loss. The author proposes Context Anchoring—externalizing decision rationale, rejected alternatives, and constraints into lightweight Feature Documents outside the chat interface. Teams adopting external anchoring achieve warm starts in seconds, reduce token costs by 98%, enable multi-developer alignment, and validate logic through forced documentation, eliminating session anxiety as a design anti-pattern.
Dropbox: How we optimized Dash’s relevance judge with DSPy
Relevance scoring at scale faces a model-cost-quality trade-off: premium models like o3 are accurate but expensive, while cheaper open-weight models degrade without model-specific prompt tuning. Dropbox's DSPy-based optimization automates prompt refinement against human judgments using NMSE metrics and GEPA feedback loops, reducing manual tuning from weeks to 1–2 days. The system cut relevance error by 45%, eliminated JSON formatting failures by 97%, and enabled 10–100x data scaling by shifting to cheaper models while maintaining quality through systematic prompt compilation.
Zalando: Search Quality Assurance with AI as a Judge
Search quality assurance in a new domain often lacks historical user data, forcing teams to rely on manual testing and reactive fixes post-launch. Zalando's framework automates evaluation by generating NER-clustered queries translated into target languages, then routes the results through GPT-4o as a multimodal judge that assesses product metadata and images against a 0–4 relevance scale. The system evaluates 1,500 search segments (37,500 results) in 3–5 hours for $250, enabling proactive root-cause identification across languages and ensuring Day 1 quality without local market expertise.
Andrey Novitskiy: Volga - A Rust Rewrite of a Real-Time AI/ML Data Engine (DataFusion, Arrow, SlateDB) with a Chronon + OpenMLDB–Style Architecture
Real-time ML feature computation requires unified streaming-batch execution with low-latency serving, forcing platforms to choose between specialized engines (Flink for latency, Spark for batch, Redis for serving). Volga's Rust implementation pairs DataFusion SQL execution with SlateDB (an embedded LSM on object storage) and Request Mode, embedding serving logic directly into operator state to eliminate external cache round trips. The system handles month-year windows via tiling, includes native ML aggregations (top-k, categorical sums), and achieves compute-storage separation by consolidating the feature pipeline, batch training, and real-time serving into a single Rust binary.
Max Halford: Lower your warehouse costs via DuckDB transpilation
Cloud warehouse compute costs escalate during development and testing cycles, incentivizing hybrid approaches that separate cheap storage from expensive query execution. Max Halford’s “Quack Mode” transpiles warehouse SQL (BigQuery → DuckDB) using SQLGlot, pulls only upstream dependencies into local DuckDB instances, and executes transformations at near-zero cost, optionally pushing results back. The pattern dramatically reduces development compute spend while maintaining warehouse portability, though pulling tables >100GB remains a bottleneck until zero-copy solutions like Iceberg are available.

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-23 09:09:03+00:00
Subject: The 'Me Generation', Fifty Years On

The Wolfe essay is a wonder, as much a work of prophecy as journalism, and funny as hell. If you have never read Wolfe, who died a few years back, this is a great introduction to him. 
It is my thesis that we in America have achieved a comparable level of social atomization as 1920s Germany. They got there the hard way: through a devastating war and hyperinflation, both of which wrecked the habits, traditions, and verities that held German society together. As Wolfe shows, America got there the easy way: through wealth and comfort, which created a culture of expressive individualism. Wolfe described what had emerged in the mid-Seventies as a “Third Great Awakening” — but this time, the object of worship was not God, but Self: From the essay:
The various movements of the current religious wave attempt very nearly the opposite [of binding together the masses]. They begin with … “Let’s talk about Me.” They begin with the most delicious look inward; with considerable narcissism, in short. When the believers bind together into religions, it is always with a sense of splitting off from the rest of society. We, the enlightened (lit by the sparks at the apexes of our souls), hereby separate ourselves from the lost souls around us. Like all religions before them, they proselytize—but always on promising the opposite of nationalism: a City of Light that is above it all. There is no ecumenical spirit within this Third Great Awakening. If anything, there is a spirit of schism. The contempt the various seers have for one another is breathtaking. One has only to ask, say, Oscar Ichazo of Arica about Carlos Castaneda or Werner Erhard of est to learn that Castaneda is a fake and Erhard is a shallow sloganeer. It’s exhilarating!—to watch the faithful split off from one another to seek ever more perfect and refined crucibles in which to fan the Divine spark … and to talk about Me.
Whatever the Third Great Awakening amounts to, for better or for worse, will have to do with this unprecedented post-World War II American development: the luxury, enjoyed by so many millions of middling folk, of dwelling upon the self. At first glance, Shirley Polykoff’s slogan—“If I’ve only one life, let me live it as a blonde!”—seems like merely another example of a superficial and irritating rhetorical trope (antanaclasis) that now happens to be fashionable among advertising copywriters. But in fact the notion of “If I’ve only one life” challenges one of those assumptions of society that are so deep-rooted and ancient, they have no name—they are simply lived by. In this case: man’s age-old belief in serial immortality.
The husband and wife who sacrifice their own ambitions and their material assets in order to provide “a better future” for their children … the soldier who risks his life, or perhaps consciously sacrifices it, in battle … the man who devotes his life to some struggle for “his people” that cannot possibly be won in his lifetime … people (or most of them) who buy life insurance or leave wills … and, for that matter, most women upon becoming pregnant for the first time … are people who conceive of themselves, however unconsciously, as part of a great biological stream. Just as something of their ancestors lives on in them, so will something of them live on in their children … or in their people, their race, their community—for childless people, too, conduct their lives and try to arrange their postmortem affairs with concern for how the great stream is going to flow on. Most people, historically, have not lived their lives as if thinking, “I have only one life to live.” Instead they have lived as if they are living their ancestors’ lives and their offspring’s lives and perhaps their neighbors’ lives as well. They have seen themselves as inseparable from the great tide of chromosomes of which they are created and which they pass on. The mere fact that you were only going to be here a short time and would be dead soon enough did not give you the license to try to climb out of the stream and change the natural order of things. The Chinese, in ancestor worship, have literally worshiped the great tide itself, and not any god or gods. For anyone to renounce the notion of serial immortality, in the West or the East, has been to defy what seems like a law of Nature. Hence the wicked feeling—the excitement!—of “If I’ve only one life, let me live it as a ———!” Fill in the blank, if you dare.
And now many dare it! In Democracy in America, Tocqueville (the inevitable and ubiquitous Tocqueville) saw the American sense of equality itself as disrupting the stream, which he called “time’s pattern”: “Not only does democracy make each man forget his ancestors, it hides his descendants from him, and divides him from his contemporaries; it continually turns him back into himself, and threatens, at last, to enclose him entirely in the solitude of his own heart.” A grim prospect to the good Alexis de T.—but what did he know about … Let’s talk about Me!
Tocqueville’s idea of modern man lost “in the solitude of his own heart” has been brought forward into our time in such terminology as alienation (Marx), anomie (Durkheim), the mass man (Ortega y Gasset), and the lonely crowd (Riesman). The picture is always of a creature uprooted by industrialism, packed together in cities with people he doesn’t know, helpless against massive economic and political shifts—in short, a creature like Charlie Chaplin in Modern Times, a helpless, bewildered, and dispirited slave to the machinery. This victim of modern times has always been a most appealing figure to intellectuals, artists, and architects. The poor devil so obviously needs us to be his Engineers of the Soul, to use a term popular in the Soviet Union in the 1920s. We will pygmalionize this sad lump of clay into a homo novus, a New Man, with a new philosophy, a new aesthetics, not to mention new Bauhaus housing and furniture.
But once the dreary little bastards started getting money in the 1940s, they did an astonishing thing—they took their money and ran. They did something only aristocrats (and intellectuals and artists) were supposed to do—they discovered and started doting on Me! They’ve created the greatest age of individualism in American history! All rules are broken! The prophets are out of business! Where the Third Great Awakening will lead—who can presume to say? One only knows that the great religious waves have a momentum all their own. Neither arguments nor policies nor acts of the legislature have been any match for them in the past. And this one has the mightiest, holiest roll of all, the beat that goes … Me … Me … . Me … Me . . .
Wolfe’s essay is fifty years old this year. The phenomenon he documented, the prose so vivid and alive it was as if written in neon, has made itself permanent. We now know much better where the Third Great Awakening has led: to an America that resembles Weimar, psychosocially, in terms of being radically atomized and unstable.
As you have read above, Wolfe saw 1970s America as thoroughly enchanted by narcissism. Sociologist Philip Rieff, in his 1966 book The Triumph Of The Therapeutic, identified the massive earthquake that had shaken the foundations, and Tom Wolfe documented the tsunami it produced, washing over our culture. We like to think today of Ronald Reagan’s election in 1980s as a backlash to all that, and in superficial ways it was. But only superficially: however much it might have slowed, the direction of the culture persisted onward, towards total liberation of the unencumbered self. 
We are at a turning point in our history. There are two paths to choose. One is a path I’ve warned about tonight, the path that leads to fragmentation and self-interest. Down that road lies a mistaken idea of freedom, the right to grasp for ourselves some advantage over others. That path would be one of constant conflict between narrow interests ending in chaos and immobility. It is a certain route to failure.
All the traditions of our past, all the lessons of our heritage, all the promises of our future point to another path -- the path of common purpose and the restoration of American values. That path leads to true freedom for our nation and ourselves. We can take the first steps down that path as we begin to solve our energy problem.
The speech went over badly. Nobody wanted to be lectured by a Sunday School teacher. A year and a half later, the Carters handed the keys of the White House over to the Reagans. Ronald Reagan was a great American president, one of the greatest in our history. Maybe you had to have been there to understand how he delivered America from the Carterian malaise. But we have to be honest: he succeeded in large part because he redefined and re-articulated the Third Great Awakening in a right-wing, libertarian mode. Reagan spoke with force and conviction about the freedom and dignity of the individual, and the agency a free American has. He embodied a certain kind of cultural conservatism, and, symbolically speaking, the hippies shaved, got a haircut, put on nicer clothes, and went to the office.
But the basic libertarianism of the Boomers did not reverse itself. Democrat Bill Clinton was the true heir of Reagan, in that he affirmed Reagan’s orientation towards free markets. Globalization took off under Clinton, and the Congressional Republicans who served in his time. I’m not prepared to argue that the statism and regulation characterizing the pre-Reagan economy was good for America. It wasn’t; we had stagnated. But at the same time, when we talk about how globalization destroyed American small towns and shipped manufacturing jobs abroad, we have to concede that this was Reagan’s doing. Clinton solidified the Reagan Revolution, at least economically — and so did the most important GOP figure of the 1990s, House Speaker Newt Gingrich.
I’m not going to get too deep into the particulars here, because hey, that’s what a book is for. Let me just say, though, that the history of the past fifty years, since Wolfe published his “Me Generation” essay, has been one of slow but steady social atomization. The one event that brought America together for a brief, solemn period — the 9/11 attacks — led to a disastrous war of choice waged by a Republican president, with 72 percent of Americans backing it on the day the bombs began falling in Baghdad. And what was President Bush’s advice to the American people in the immediate aftermath of the attacks? Go shopping. In a certain sense, this was reasonable. He was trying to prevent economic damage coming from the shock of 9/11. 
Still, that tells you something about how much America had changed as the Third Great Awakening hardened into a cultural consensus. You cannot imagine FDR saying such a thing days after Pearl Harbor.
The LGBT rights revolution would not and could not have occurred without the Third Great Awakening nullifying the religion of its predecessors as a binding and authoritative force in American life. Americans had come to regard sexual desire as intrinsic to identity. Sex was no longer merely something you did; it was who you were. Once this view had been popularized, it was only logical that gays and lesbians should be given what they demanded. This is why, twenty years ago, despite polls showing strong majorities of Americans opposing same-sex marriage, I was writing that the battle had been lost. I knew how, in a country that had come to the widespread belief in expressive individualism as normative, the convictions upon which the defense of traditional marriage depended were shallow indeed. They were rooted in sand, and though liberal judges accelerated the gay-rights revolution, the Me Generation and its children and grandchildren were bound to get there eventually, as they have done.
Wolfe had characterized the fundamental principle of 1970s popular culture as “Let’s talk about Me!” This gave rise in the 1990s to identity politics on the Left, in which individuals proclaimed their me-ness by membership in a collective of victims seeking political power. Gone was the old world of Martin Luther King Jr., who fought for racial justice in the name of Christian virtues and American constitutional principles. Now it was all about seizing power to create conditions under which one’s own self-identified tribe — organized by race, sex, and sexual identity — could more fully express themselves. This would eventually lead to the illiberal left-wing ideology we call wokeness; it is impossible to imagine the election of Donald Trump in 2016 absent the triumph of wokeness in the dominant cultural and corporate institutions of America. 
The popularization of the Internet in the mid-to-late 1990s, and the advent of the smartphone a decade or so later, marked the point of no return. If the hyperinflation of 1923 was the watershed event that made it irreversibly impossible, in a psychosocial sense,  for Germans to return to anything like the stability and cohesion they had before the Great War, then 2007 — when Apple gave the world the smartphone — was America’s 1923. 
Why? Because now every individual could carry in his pocket or her purse a personal computer that linked them to the virtual world of the Internet. To borrow from the late Neil Postman, when a child with a smartphone can access hardcore porn instantly, sitting in his backyard under a tree, then childhood as we have known it is over. So is society. 
Don’t misunderstand me here: it’s not simply a matter of socially destructive and immoral content coming to us through the Internet and smartphone. It’s far more radical than that. As Iain McGilchrist (and others) have said, what you pay attention to determines your own sense of reality. Prior to the Internet, Americans generally shared the same social reality. However distorted it was because of the work of information curators in newsrooms and Hollywood studios, it was still a shared culture. 
Where were you when, on February 28, 1983, CBS broadcast the final episode of M*A*S*H? Nielsen found that 106 million Americans were sitting in front of their televisions that night, watching the movie-length farewell episode. That means 45 percent of all Americans shared that experience. Can you imagine? It happened, and it was possible because back then, we only had three television networks, and cable television did not yet have the penetration that it would soon obtain. 
The M*A*S*H final episode’s viewership was historically extraordinary, but my point is that the media technology of the era meant that it was possible to have that kind of shared experience. That’s gone. I wouldn’t want to go back to the era of only three networks, and neither would you. My point is simply that the Internet has revolutionized what Charles Taylor calls our “social imaginary,” such that we no longer live in the same reality, because we no longer pay attention to the same things. 
We have all had the experience of finding ourselves at a dinner party or social gathering, and being shocked by how few people are aware of some event or phenomenon that we assumed everybody knew. Happens to me all the time, and it usually separates out the people who are on Twitter, versus those who aren’t. (And even then, the algorithm sorts us Twitter tribes out, and silos us.)
A people that do not and cannot share a broadly similar social reality are an atomized people, whether they want to be or not. I sit here in my Budapest apartment writing to you on my laptop. My psychological reality is determined by this machine. I spend my day reading and writing on it, and corresponding, and texting, with friends and colleagues across town, across borders, and across oceans. Though I have lived in this apartment for over two years, I don’t know my neighbors, and they probably don’t know each other. This is our common condition. 
In his bestselling 2018 book Why Liberalism Failed, political theorist Patrick Deneen posited that it has failed as a way to organize society precisely because it has succeeded in reaching its telos: liberating the individual from subjection to any unchosen obligations. The Me Generation, now ripening into senescence, had brought into a being in which “We” is an abstraction. It’s very hard to make a “We” out of 340 million Mes, all convinced of the rightness of their sovereignty. 
What’s next? Psychological research on the first generation raised fully within digital culture is extremely discouraging. We now know that heavy Internet exposure — which is the norm for young people today — causes organic changes in the brain. For one, we lose the ability to sustain attention. True, this is something all of us who are heavily online experience, but it is especially dangerous to those whose brains are still forming. We know that it affects the development of their prefrontal cortexes (where, among other things, our decision-making capabilities are located), and distorts the amygdala, which regulates emotional response. In short, we are probably forming, through our technology, the next generations of Americans to be slaves to their passions and their fears, and increasingly incapable not only of self-government, but of reversing social atomization except through something barbaric — like what the Nazis offered the German people. The triumph not of reason and love, but of the will, and hatred of the Other. 
The Nazis — Adolf Hitler, of course, but also his propaganda chief Joseph Goebbels — knew what the German people craved, and knew how to give it to them. Living in a socially atomized world was unbearable, especially when the Great Depression brought about widespread poverty and desperation. People wanted a return to community, meaning, and purpose. They wanted to regain a sense of agency. Hitler gave it to them. I’m telling you, the deeper you go into the history of the era, the easier it is to see how you, had you lived there, and suffered what they suffered, might have surrendered to the promises of Nazism too. The psychological and social pressures to do so were incredibly difficult to resist, especially as the centrist political parties proved incapable of dealing with the dire challenges of the Great Depression. 
Germany got to Hitler through losing a horrible war, the middle class seeing its savings dissolve into thin air through hyperinflation, and finally, the coming of the Great Depression. All of these things rendered the average German man feeling adrift, lost, purposeless, and afraid of the future. Again, we have arrived somewhere close to the same place, but we took the pleasant scenic route. Deprivation and hardship atomized 1920s Germany; abundance and comfort did the same for us. 
My great fear — a fear intensified by this war and its potential economic repercussions for the globe — is that America as we know it will not be able to survive a true economic collapse like the Great Depression. I could be wrong. I hope I’m wrong. But 1920s Germany shows us how vulnerable a people becomes when their social bonds, the ones both tying them together and anchoring them in a shared transcendent reality that provides them an authoritative basis for living together, dissipates. Time to take this seriously, and act as best we can to head it off.

---

Sender: newworkinphilosophy@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-23 13:47:06+00:00
Subject: "Alternatives to Violence" - Guy Crain (Rose State College)

Pacifist Dilemma: Smith is a bystander witnessing Jones being violently threatened by Williams. It is readily apparent to Smith that there is an urgent need to do something to prevent something bad from happening to Jones at the hands of Williams. What should Smith do?
Granted, such philosophers would admit that technically Smith does have other options—Smith could start dancing, tell jokes, or even join Williams in threatening Jones. Rather, in terms of what Smith ought to do, these philosophers assume that Smith has no options morally as good or better than committing violence against Williams. They do not argue this by way of exploring various alternative options available to Smith and concluding that each is morally inferior to violence. Instead, they take it as obvious by the mere description of the case that Smith’s only options are violence or nothing. Their sense of the obvious paucity of Smith’s options is taken to provide all the intuitive force necessary to conclude that agents sometimes ought to commit violence. (So much so that philosophers frequently do not describe such cases anywhere near as clearly as I have described Pacifist Dilemma but often take merely alluding to similar circumstances as sufficient to make their case.) And, given their intuitive force in support of that conclusion, cases like Pacifist Dilemma are aptly so named because they seem like decisive evidence against positions like pacifism, nonviolence, and nonresistance.
Social Experiment – The Joker, a psychopathic criminal, conducts a ‘social experiment.’ There are two ferries: one carrying commuters and the other transferring prisoners. With the boats in sight of each other, The Joker disables the engines. The Joker tells both sets of passengers their boats have been rigged with explosives. On the commuter ferry, there is a device that will detonate the bomb on the prisoners’ ferry, and vice versa. If the prisoners choose to use their detonator first, The Joker will let the prisoners live. If the commuters choose to use their detonator first, The Joker will let the commuters live. If neither detonator is activated within a set time limit, The Joker will detonate both bombs.
To understand the idea of refusing to legitimize assailants as assailants, it is important to review how Social Experiment played out in the movie. A considerable amount of time passes with neither ferry’s passengers deciding to use their detonators. A large intimidating prisoner approaches the prison warden who is in possession of their ferry’s detonator. The prisoner demands that the warden hand over the detonator because the prisoner will do what the warden should have done immediately; and, the prisoner says, the warden can afterward claim that the prisoner took the detonator by force. The warden slowly hands the detonator to the prisoners, and the prisoner immediately throws the detonator out of a window into the ocean.
In Social Experiment, The Joker presumes to dictate to the people on both ferries what their options are, the associated costs of those options, and to design the environmental experience the people on the boats will have while choosing among those options. The Joker presents this choice architecture in such a way that the people on the boats appear not to have the chance either to question the legitimacy of The Joker’s presumed role or to consider their own roles in granting that legitimacy. If the people on the boats participate according to The Joker’s choice architecture, they thereby overlook this important consideration. For the people on either ferry to decide to use their detonator would be tacit acceptance of The Joker’s terms and, consequently, an act that would legitimize The Joker as their choice architect. But in the movie, the prisoner’s discarding of the detonator constituted a refusal to confer such legitimacy to The Joker.
Something similar is going on in cases like Pacifist Dilemma. The assailant acts in such a way that presumes the role of choice architect for the agent. Granted, the assailant likely hopes that the agent will not intervene violently so that the assailant can unimpededly accomplish whatever purpose the assailant had when initiating a threat of violence toward the target in the first place. But, by posing a looming violent threat, the assailant has, at least in effect, presumed to put the agent in a situation in which it appears the agent has only a certain set of options—commit violence or allow bad things to happen. If the agent were, in fact, bound by that choice architecture, then perhaps violence would likely be the agent’s morally best option. But there is something wrong with choice architecture itself. Sometimes to express how bleak a situation is, people say things like, “Why did it have to come to this?” This expresses a sense of there being something wrong with the fact that “it” has “come to this.” Another way to frame this expression is that people are judging that such situations should not happen in the first place.
If an agent faces such situations as though the only options are to commit violence or do nothing, that agent functionally grants to the assailant the role of being the agent’s choice architect. Granted, it does not seem as though there was some sort of negotiation between the agent and the assailant about whether the assailant may put the agent in that situation. The assailant’s behavior, rather, presumes this role—the assailant behaving as an assailant thereby constitutes an attempt to disempower the agent of the agency to decide whether to accept the apparent choice architecture of cases like Pacifist Dilemma. If the agent commits violence, the agent has legitimized the assailant as an assailant dictating the agent’s options.
Critics of pacifism assume that for the agent to refuse to commit violence entails accepting loss according to the choice architecture of a pacifist dilemma. This is short-sighted. Pacifist alternatives are morally better than violence because they constitute a refusal to contract into the situation—a refusal to allow the would-be assailant to put either party in situation that should not happen in the first place. Some pacifist alternatives communicate to the assailant that such situations do not merit the agent’s participation (and, when effective, that neither do they merit the would-be assailant’s initiation).
Here the thief was the assailant. Virtually everyone in the salon was a target. The agent could have been anyone in the room other than the thief. If any of them suspected that even if they handed over money that someone might still get harmed, that person might have come to believe that violence was their best option (especially if they were armed or confident in their prowess for violence). Yet, the actual unfolding of events shows that such agents would be mistaken. The salon patrons and employees—they neither committed MCV nor did nothing. They had alternative options that not only resulted in no one being harmed but also did not legitimize the assailant’s presumption to create a situation in which they had to participate.
In this case, White was the assailant, Staten was the target, and Black was the agent. Black did not commit violence against a violent assailant, but instead responded civilly—by expressing compassion toward White. Black’s civil act of compassion presented a stark contrast to the atmosphere of threat White had created. That contrast dissipated the atmospheric threat; White seemed to sense this, was disarmed by it (even literally), and, herself, became more civil. Black’s civility functioned as a weapon not in the sense of being an instrument with which to attack the assailant, but as a weapon against White’s assailant-ness—as a civilizing contagion.
Let me be clear—my argument is not that violence is never an agent’s optimal option for dealing with assailants; it is perfectly fine for my position if something like deontological pacifism turns out to be false. What I have argued is that cases like Pacifist Dilemma lack the degree of intuitive force in favor of violence that some philosophers presume them to have because of the mistaken notion that agents in pacifist dilemmas simply have no options as good or better than violence. I have shown that there are such alternative options. Surely, this shows at least that some philosophers’ intuitions are suspect if not also that they harbor violentist tendencies perhaps at the level of the tools they are using (Pacifist Dilemma-like thought experiments).
On its surface, Pacifist Dilemma seems like an innocuous conceptual tool with which to analyze the ethics of interpersonal violence. However, it seems that Pacifist Dilemma functions more like a stereotype that skews intuitions toward the approval of more rather than less violence. This is why it is crucial to point out that pacifist alternatives exist let alone that they are morally better than violence.
Thanks for reading New Work in Philosophy! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Computadores quânticos podem resolver problemas de saúde?

Após 30 meses de inovação acelerada, seis grupos de pesquisa esperam "encontrar ouro"
 Estou em pé, diante de um computador quântico construído com átomos e luz, no National Quantum Computing Centre do Reino Unido, nos arredores de Oxford. Sobre uma bancada de laboratório, uma complexa matriz de espelhos e lentes circunda uma célula do tamanho de um cubo mágico, na qual 100 átomos de césio são suspensos em formação de grade por um feixe de laser cuidadosamente manipulado.

POR: RAFAELA GUIMARÃES

Como proteger cérebros em formação: do ECA Digital ao redesenho da tecnologia na educação

Março 2026



A sanção da lei apelidada de "ECA Digital", nessa quarta-feira, levou o debate sobre a relação de crianças e adolescentes com as plataformas para um novo estágio. Ao estender princípios do estatuto ao ambiente digital, a legislação sinaliza que a proteção não pode ser tratada como exceção operacional, mas como premissa de design. 
 A proteção no ambiente online passa a orientar o próprio desenho de plataformas, aplicativos e ferramentas educacionais. Com isso, crianças e adolescentes passam a orientar decisões tecnológicas. Em vez de discutir se a tecnologia deve estar na escola, o debate muda de nível. Escolhas devem ser conscientes, compatíveis com a idade, e sustentadas por critérios claros de segurança, privacidade e desenvolvimento.

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com

Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=93=88_Data_to_start_your_week_=E2=80=94_Helium_special?=

Hi all,
The Iran War has created a chokepoint in the supply of helium, a byproduct of  natural gas processing and LNG production that’s used in more than 20 steps of semiconductor fabrication. Headlines are making the helium situation sound apocalyptic for chips, but the reality is more nuanced. 
Here’s what’s going on:
So what’s likely to happen? Even in a severe scenario where Qatar takes years to fully restore helium supply, the direct cost impact on semiconductors remains modest. Helium accounts for roughly 0.5-1% of fab costs; even a tripling of prices only lifts this to around 1.5-3%. For high-value chips, particularly AI accelerators, this is easily absorbed or passed through. If there is a potential constraint, it is the immediate logistics. SK Hynix procurement team may have to work longer hours over the next few weeks but we expect no significant threats to the AI economy in either the short or long term.
Thanks for reading!
