Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-26 13:14:01+00:00
Subject: We Live In 'The Information State'

The Information State is about how the US Government and its media and big-tech accomplices use sophisticated messaging techniques to manipulate the public. That statement sounds rather bland, to be honest — but when you get into the details presented by Siegel in this blockbuster book, you’ll understand how deep and dark and serious this is. It’s the kind of thing that makes you wonder if we still have a meaningful democracy at all — or, more subversively, if democracy as we have known it is even possible under these conditions. He writes:
What I call the information state refers to a form of government that replaces the democratic principle of consent with control. Most of us are familiar with the distinction between authoritarian regimes that impose their will by force and democracies that govern through the consent of their citizens. The information state represents a third form of political regime. It governs by controlling the codes and protocols of the digital public arena, which it uses to engineer the public’s compliance with its programs. Such a state rules by monopolizing attention.
In place of the cinematic spectacles of twentieth-century propaganda, it dictates the parameters of user experience on social media and other shared online domains to fractionally adjust the public’s perceptions and condition people to see certain outcomes as inevitable.
Siegel begins with a discussion of how modern government in the digital age has largely abandoned traditional democratic means of governance through the control and manipulation of information. In Siegel’s view, the ruling-class elites deploy both censorship and propaganda to monopolize attention and manipulate people’s perceptions. In a technocratic society (which is to say, all advance modern societies), information is the key to power. 
Because it commands our attention, the Internet is our main public square, and therefore also a battleground for politics. Because the Internet is largely an information free-for-all, it is difficult to know what is real and what isn’t, what is true and what is false. 
Online echo chambers not only contribute to political polarization, they produce a mutual incomprehension that makes it nearly impossible to engage in productive dialogue or conceive of political introduction cooperation toward common goals.
This confusion works to the advantage of the Information State, says Siegel. Sometimes — as under the Covid emergency — the State imposes censorship, formally and informally, to control the narrative, and thus the behavior of the public. 
Siegel has a chapter diving into the antecedents of the Information State, going back to the Scientific Revolution, which was the beginning of the modern project of using science and technology to master Nature. Jean-Baptiste Colbert, who headed the French navy under Louis XIV, the Sun King, was perhaps the first of the governing elite to understand how information management could be useful in controlling the populace. These insights would only grow with time, and the mechanization and bureaucratization of life (including extensive record-keeping) in the late 19th and 20th centuries. 
A key figure in creating modern propaganda techniques was Edward Bernays, an early 20th century figure known today as “the father of public relations.” He was a brilliant propagandist — so much so that Joseph Goebbels, the Nazi propaganda chief, kept copies of Bernays’ books on his shelf. Siegel quotes Bernays: “No serious sociologist any progress longer believes that the voice of the people expresses any divine or specially wise and lofty idea. The voice of the people expresses the mind of the people, and that mind is made up for it by the group leaders.” This was a century ago, mind.
What is “information”? In one of his best chapters, Siegel explains the theoretical basis for the Information State. He traces its development through 20th century giants like John von Neumann, Alan Turing, and Claude Shannon, the father of information theory. These men all conceived of information as a quantifiable thing, electronic signals shorn of meaning. Later, physicist John Wheeler observed that quantum mechanics indicates that information is the building block of reality. This was a conceptual breakthrough. And computers were the tool that allowed the mastery of information. 
Today, says Siegel, digital technologies have revolutionized control and organization. He writes about how the Pentagon depended on information theory to guide the conduct of the Vietnam War. As is widely known, the Internet was initially developed by the US military to enhance its warfighting capabilities. The Vietnam War ended with an American defeat, but the advances in information gathering and manipulation remained. Siegel offers this critical insight:
While the network of digital surveillance was designed to defeat primitive insurgencies, insurgents could too easily escape its grid of control. Such a system only reaches its ideal when it operates in an advanced technological society whose citizens view escape from the sensors as a form of punishment.
A few years back, I interviewed Kamila Bendova, one of the last surviving members of the leadership circle of the anti-communist Czech resistance. She is elderly, and refuses to use a smartphone. Bendova marvels over how everybody today is content to allow detailed personal information about themselves to be collected by corporations, governments, and so forth. She said (I paraphrase), “Don’t they realize that information means control? That every piece of information you give about yourself to someone enables them to control you, in principle?” 
I sat on her couch nodding along, with my smartphone in my pocket. I could not do my job without the device, even though I am perfectly aware of what it is doing in terms of information collecting. Same with my laptop. Per Siegel, I would feel “punished” if I was cut off from my iPhone and my laptop. The system is almost impossible to escape. We consent to our own informational exploitation and vulnerability, because most of us have no choice — and even those who do have a choice would not choose to be free of this technology, which enables us to participate in public life. 
Last week I went to a new Chinese restaurant in my neighborhood. I forgot my phone at home. Turns out they had no printed menus — you were expected to use your phone to scan an image affixed to the table, which would open the menu on your phone. I had only ever seen that as an option in restaurants. In this new place, it was mandatory. The server brought his phone over and opened the menu on it.
Siegel argues that this kind of thing makes us dependent, and creates a condition of mass servitude. Because we Americans are dispositionally optimistic about technology, we welcomed the Internet as expanding freedom and choice. And in many ways, it does. But there were, and are, serious downsides.
For one, the digitalization of our world dramatically reduces the power of an individual over his environment. It made globalization possible, and with it, the enervation of democracy:
By its nature, an information economy attempts to transcend the physical limitations of time and space. The nascent communications network allowed a person in Singapore or Boston to control a physical process in a third location. … The breakdown of physical boundaries underscored a paradox of the rising information state. As the digital networks built to advance US strategic interests collapsed the distance between core and periphery, they undermined the boundaries and with them the integrity of the nation. Wealth produced in one nation’s capital city could be instantly transferred to another capital within the frictionless global market. As a result, the rights and privileges attached to being the citizen of a particular place began to lose their value.
The 1990s was the key transitional period, when Information conquered society, and Zygmunt Bauman’s concept of “liquid modernity” reached perhaps its final form:
Over the course of the 1990s, the Internet evolved from a tool of the government and research industries into the primary medium of all social and economic activity, a dizzying transformation that inspired more worshipful odes than sober reflections. Information was the new gold. The new oil. Only it was better than both of them put together because gold and oil could only ever be gold and oil, while information could be anything.
In Siegel’s telling — remember, this book is largely, but not entirely, a historical account of how we got here — the ruling class uses information gathering and control to manipulate us. This probably won’t surprise most people, but boy, you need to read the details. It’s terrifying stuff. 
The 9/11 attack sent surveillance into the stratosphere. Siegel writes about how Adm. John Poindexter’s Total Information Awareness program got shut down after public criticism, but “the program quietly migrated to different government offices and out into the private sphere.”
Siegel’s detailed account of how the Patriot Act and all that followed empowered the government in ways that can only be called potentially totalitarian make for chilling reading. The National Security Agency began hoovering up all informatio — even domestic communications — working in conjunction with private tech corporations. (Edward Snowden also revealed how a lot of this works.) 
Another example that I’m feeling here today, where I am: the Bush administration’s Pentagon embraced launching disinformation as a weapon of war abroad, heedless of how that same information could be picked up by the US media and repeated to American audiences as truth. There’s no way to avoid that. Here in Hungary, with a hotly contested election coming up in just over a week, there’s an incredible information war going on between the Orban campaign and his challenger’s. It involved national and transnational actors, too. I don’t read Hungarian, so I can’t follow the details, but just the stuff I see in translation makes it clear that the public square is being flooded with disinformation, probably from both sides, as well as outsiders (e.g., the European Union desperately wants to be rid of Orban). 
How the hell is anybody supposed to know what is true? The ordinary person can’t. 
Siegel writes that what began as an extraordinary set of measures to help the US fight the Global War on Terror became Just How We Do Things Now:
Policies enacted in the name of security widened divisions in the country’s common civic life. What started as a state of exception after 9/11 turned into a permanent system. Indeed, it revealed a general rule in technocratic society: Any technology of social control introduced under a state of exception becomes permanent.
Barack Obama was the perfect candidate for people who believe in their right to control information, Siegel writes.
The average Obama voter was more likely than their Republican counterpart to trust in the power of informational systems. That made sense. The information economy had so far been good to many of them. And that was especially true for professional elites who still felt firmly in control of America’s cultural narrative. They were what the novelist Walter Kirn called “the class that makes the headlines—that writes the headlines, and the stories under them.” People who instinctively understood control over information and messaging as essential to their status and identity.
The Obama years marked the merger of the state with corporate (Silicon Valley) power, consolidating the rule of progressive technocracy. Despite claiming to be neutral, tech giants like Google and Facebook used their algorithms to push progressive activism. Snowden’s 2013 revelations showed how tech giants participated in bulk data collection, and the Obama administration used surveillance to spy on political adversaries and journalists. The NSA, we now know, sucks up everything online, and stores is. You can imagine how the power of AI is going to help them make sense of all that information they have on each and every one of us. And by the way, did you know that Obama’s Justice Department carried out more information-related prosecutions than all previous administrations combined? I did not. Jacob Siegel teaches us things we didn’t know, but need to.
The smartphone and the rise of social networks created algorithm-driven global networks. Whoever controls the algorithm has a great deal of control over what the individual pays attention to, and therefore his or her sense of what is real. And during the Great Awokening, those algorithms decidedly favored a progressive narrative.
The party transformation that Obama oversaw succeeded because it built on a fundamental transformation in the American social structure, which was the rise of a single national ruling class whose members made up what Lind called a “homogeneous national oligarchy, with the same accent, manners, values, and educational backgrounds from Boston to Austin and San Francisco to New York and Atlanta.”
America has always had regional elites. But the consolidation of a single national class whose members all passed through the same credentialing institutions and shared the same narrow range of cultural and political tastes represented something new. Being unified and homogeneous, the class was more powerful than earlier regional elites but also easier to manipulate. Once it was brought into formation on a particular issue, natural social pressures enforced a striking degree of conformity in tastes and values. As a whole, the class adhered to the tenets of progressive technocracy. Yet there was nothing that its members believed in more fervently than their own providential destiny to rule, regardless of their failures.
Obama left, Trump came to power — but this oligarchy remained (and remains) in place. And it turned on Trump with the Russiagate lies:
By 2016, the American press had been hollowed out to the point that it could be worn like a hand puppet by the US security officials and Democratic Party operatives who ran the messaging of the Russiagate operation. The framing of Trump was a top-down operation. It was led by the sitting president Barack Obama and his intelligence chiefs who used rumors started by the Clinton campaign as a pretext to spy on a political rival and tarnish Trump with doctored intelligence reports that they strategically leaked to the press.
More:
Russiagate united the various branches of the American elite against the common foe who appeared to threaten their institutional survival. In the process, it formalized a transition that was already underway, as the social bonds of the ruling class hardened into the formal structure of a ruling party.
Siegel does not mince words: “Russiagate was not a tragedy, but a crime against the country.” The crime was actors in the state and journalists turning disinformation techniques on an American politician and citizens who supported him. In other words, they used techniques and strategies intended to fight foreign adversaries on their own people.
Anytime you hear or see the word “disinformation,” you should be skeptical. Siegel shows that it’s a word and a concept that elites use to discredit information that does not serve their interests. Siegel doesn’t mention this, but here in Europe, at the 2025 Davos meeting, European Commission president Ursula von der Leyen described “disinformation” at the No. 1 threat to Europe. That tells you a hell of a lot. 
Siegel discuss how government and private elites exercised censorship and information control during the Covid pandemic, with facts and dissenting opinions suppressed in the name of “safety.” And then, when Joe Biden faced off against Donald Trump in the 2020 election, the information regulators put their thumbs on the scale to help Biden win the White House:
The outcome of the election proved something beyond the obvious point that many Americans were sick of the endless Trump drama. Biden’s victory demonstrated the ruling party’s power to instantly disappear countless discrete pieces of information across billions of screens while penetrating into people’s private communications. An effort to extinguish the public’s unruly beliefs had spawned a single digital apparatus of law, punishment, culture, and government that policed the political arena and crouched inside its subjects’ inner lives, where algorithms worked to invisibly edit their desires.
Get this — also under Biden:
With additional funding earmarked to fight domestic terrorism, Homeland Security pursued a range of initiatives reminiscent of Stalinist-era ideological techniques. One called “attitudinal inoculation” proposed to build “psychological antibodies” in people deemed vulnerable to wrongthink in order to strengthen their defenses against disinformation.
Even the Post Office got in on the action through a program called iCOP (the Internet Covert Operations Program) that collected Americans’ social media posts including those pertaining to political protests. Internal documents obtained by Yahoo News detailed how Post Office analysts would “trawl through social media sites to look for what the document describes as ‘inflammatory’ postings and then shar[e] that information across government agencies.”
Remember how the Canadian government “de-banked” the truckers protesting Covid restrictions, cutting off access to their bank accounts? It happened in the US to January 6 defendants. All of this is part of how the new Information State works:
It was not that banks and other financial institutions decided one day that they wanted to police their customers’ political views. They were pushed in that direction by government regulations enacted after 9/11 that required them to monitor the activity at their institutions for signs of terrorist financing. The legal and technical capacity established in response to anti-terrorism statutes then shifted as subsequent administrations added new categories of forbidden activity to be sanctioned.
The scholar Nathan Pinkoski observed that “just as civil rights law allows corporations to enforce [diversity, equity, and inclusion] ideology across the whole business world, so anti-terrorism law allows corporations to enforce political loyalty tests across the whole financial system.” The result was a decisive break with liberal norms and the consolidation of what Pinkoski described as a new mode of postliberal politics, in which “civil society, especially its economic dimension, is being weaponized.”
Siegel’s narrative takes us to the return of Trump to office, and the exposure, through the Twitter Files and the efforts of DOGE, of how the government collaborated with Big Tech to manipulate public opinion to help Democrats. Most of that has been swept away by Trump, Siegel says, but the state still retains the power to hoover up information (does anybody think the NSA has stopped doing that under Trump?), and the power of Big Tech and its algorithms to shape and steer public debate still exists. He ends this fascinating story by saying whatever AI will bring us, it will be far more powerful than what we’ve been living with so far. Good luck, folks.
Reading this book, which has a strong historical component, I kept trying to identify a point at which we, collectively, could have decided to stop it, to jump off the train. I couldn’t find one. The development of the Information State, as the control mechanism of the Information Society, seems inevitable. As with AI, if we don’t build it, the Chinese, who have no qualms about AI, will conquer us, just as the Soviets would have done in the Cold War had America not created computerized information systems. More deeply, the Internet and related digital technologies are only the latest iteration of the defining characteristic of the modern era, beginning with the Scientific Revolution, which sought to use science and technology to increase man’s power over Nature. 
You can go back even earlier in this narrative, to the Garden of Eden, when the serpent tempted Eve by saying, “Ye shall be as gods.” Not God as God, but you, through the acquisition of knowledge. Human nature does not change. But now, as we are more thoroughly merged with the Machine, it might, because at some point, we will cease to be human. 
You Cannot Teach The Bible On Homosexuality In Finland
The court imposed a fine on Räsänen and her Lutheran bishop, who was also charged with hate speech over the matter. No word yet as to whether not she plans to pay. This is truly shocking. Finnish gays and lesbians have had the right to civil partnerships since 2002, and marriage since 2017. And yet, activists and their allies in the state went all the way back to 2004 to dig up an ordinary pamphlet a Lutheran churchwoman wrote explaining why, in her view, Christians should not support gay marriage. The High Court demanded that all copies of the pamphlet be removed from the Internet and destroyed.
This is Europe in 2026. This is exactly what J.D. Vance talked about in his controversial Munich 2025 speech. In Finland today, they can convict Christians of hate speech simply for writing a pamphlet 22 years ago, explaining why Christians cannot support gay marriage. This is where we are over here (though not in Hungary, thank God). Finland has been so de-Christianized that people probably won’t protest. Worse, I fear that has been de-liberalized, such that most people won’t really care if religious believers lose their right to speak, because who wants to hear from those bigots anyway?
Cesar Chavez, MLK, And Other Sex Fiends
I had bad news for him: MLK was. Though I don’t blame Vince for not knowing. When David Garrow, the historian of the civil rights movement who won the Pulitzer Prize for his biography of King, tried to publish a big exposé on how his personal hero, MLK, had abused women, he couldn’t find a single publication in the US willing to do it. Garrow had to turn to a British magazine, Standpoint, to publish the piece. I think Standpoint is out of business now, but you can read the whole thing screenshot on Garrow’s personal website. It’s revolting stuff. Excerpts:
Newly-released documents reveal the full extent of the FBI’s surveillance of the civil rights leader Dr Martin Luther King in the mid-1960s. They expose in graphic detail the FBI’s intense focus on King’s extensive extramarital sexual relationships with dozens of women, and also his presence in a Washington hotel room when a friend, a Baptist minister, allegedly raped one of his “parishioners”, while King “looked on, laughed and offered advice”. The FBI’s tape recording of that criminal assault still exists today, resting under court seal in a National Archives vault. The FBI documents also reveal how its Director, J. Edgar Hoover, authorised top Bureau officials to send Dr King a taperecording of his sexual activities along with an anonymous message encouraging him to take his own life. 
The complete transcripts and surviving recordings are not due to be released until 2027 but when they are made fully available a painful historical reckoning concerning King’s personal conduct seems inevitable.
More, about the FBI bugging King’s hotel room at the Willard in Washington DC, in 1964:
In two other nearby rooms Bergeron and fellow Special Agent William D. Campbell set up “radio receivers and tape recorders” prior to when King and his friends first checked in on January 5. Staying in one of the two targeted rooms was King’s friend Logan Kearse, the pastor of Baltimore’s Cornerstone Baptist Church and, like King, the holder of a PhD from the Boston University School of Theology. Kearse “had brought to Washington several women ‘parishioners’ of his church”, a newly-released summary document from Sullivan’s personal file on King relates, and Kearse invited King and his friends to come and meet the women. “The group met in his room and discussed which women among the parishioners would be suitable for natural or unnatural sex acts. When one of the women protested that she did not approve of this, the Baptist minister immediately and forcibly raped her,” the typed summary states, parenthetically citing a specific FBI document (100-3-116-762) as its source. “King looked on, laughed and offered advice,” [FBI Assistant Director William] Sullivan or one of his deputies then added in handwriting. 
While that claim appears only as an annotation, other similar marginalia, e.g. “more on this” one page prior, suggest that Sullivan was seeking an expanded, more detailed indictment of King’s behaviour. The document’s recently released final pages, narrating events until March 30, 1968, suggest that the unfinished revision was abandoned following King’s assassination on April 4.
Without question Sullivan and his aides had both the microphone-transmitted tape-recording, and a subsequent full transcript at hand while they were annotating their existing typescript; in 1977 Justice Department investigators would publicly attest to how their own review of both the tapes and the transcripts showed them to be genuine and accurate. Throughout the 1960s, when no precedent for the public release of FBI documents existed or was even anticipated, Sullivan could not have imagined that his and his aides’ jottings would ever see the light of day. Similarly, they would not have had any apparent motive for their annotations to inaccurately embellish upon the actual recording and its full transcript, both of which remain under court seal and one day will confirm or disprove the FBI’s summary allegation. 
At the Willard Hotel, King and his friends’ activities resumed the following evening as approximately 12 individuals “participated in a sex orgy” which the prudish Sullivan felt included “acts of degeneracy and depravity . . . When one of the women shied away from engaging in an unnatural act, King and several of the men discussed how she was to be taught and initiated in this respect. King told her that to perform such an act would ‘help your soul’.” Sometime later, in language that would reflect just how narrow Sullivan’s mindset was, “King announced that he preferred to perform unnatural acts on women and that he had started the ‘International Association for the Advancement of Pussy Eaters’.” Anyone familiar with King’s often-bawdy sense of humour would not doubt that quotation.
One more quote from the piece:
Yet without any doubt the uppermost issue raised by the new documents concerns just how fundamental a reconsideration of Martin Luther King’s historical reputation will take place when the complete trove of still-sealed FBI tape recordings and attendant transcripts is released for public review. Until now, some voices in 2027 might have called for the physical destruction of all those historical records, notwithstanding how the FBI’s electronic surveillance of King was not, under the regrettably relaxed standards of that time, in any way illegal. 
But the FBI’s allegation that King “looked on, laughed and offered advice” as a forcible rape took place right in front of him makes that stance unsupportable by anyone. Dorothy Cotton, the most important woman in King’s life, went to her grave without ever giving an interview in which she explicitly discussed their relationship, and how many of the additional 40 or more women, such as Dolores Evans, Barbara Meredith and Barbara Moore, whom the now-public documents identify as King’s more occasional partners, might have something of value to offer the historical record?
Keep in mind that Garrow is a professional historian who won the Pulitzer for his biography of King. The piece in Standpoint is very long and quite detailed, and it was authored by a biographer who not only knows more about King’s life than almost anybody, but also someone who had nothing to gain and and immense amount of standing to lose by reporting these things. Also, as Garrow points out, there was no doubt that J. Edgar Hoover’s FBI tried to destroy King … but that does not make these data false. 
None of this information detracts from King’s status as an American hero for what he accomplished in the civil rights movement. But it complicates his legacy immensely, to put it mildly. See, I think it was wrong for the State of California to immediately begin erasing Cesar Chavez’s name from all public places, as they did after the Times published. Chavez was not honored for being a saint. He was honored for what he did to fight for the civil rights of exploited farm workers. I think he still should be, and that King should be for what he accomplished for black civil rights. The importance of that far outweighs his repugnant and hypocritical private life. But neither do those achievements negate his private wickedness.
People, especially great ones, are complex. If we start taking down statues and renaming streets because we discover our great men had messy, even disgraceful, private lives, not many will be left. We have to learn, somehow, how to deal with the fact that all men, even the most heroic and accomplished ones, are also sinners. Life is tragic, and so are human beings. 
Now, about those Confederate monuments…

Sender: contato@mittechreview.com.br
Date: 2026-03-26 19:53:11+00:00
Subject: O frio que sustenta o sistema alimentar

EDIÇÃO ALIMENTOS


Como a refrigeração transformou o que comemos

 


 Antes de chegar ao copo ou ao prato, muitos alimentos percorrem uma longa jornada. O suco de laranja, por exemplo, pode permanecer armazenado por meses ou até anos em tanques industriais, enquanto frutas, carnes e laticínios dependem de uma extensa rede de refrigeração para chegar aos supermercados. Essa infraestrutura, conhecida como cadeia do frio, conecta armazéns, contêineres, caminhões e vitrines refrigeradas, permitindo que alimentos perecíveis circulem globalmente.

 Entender essa estrutura, e como a refrigeração mudou profundamente a forma como produzimos, transportamos e consumimos alimentos, é fundamental para compreender os impactos ambientais, econômicos e culturais da forma como nos alimentamos hoje.
Confira a matéria "Passada e gelada: os segredos da comida fresca", que compõe a edição Alimentos da mais recente revista da MIT Technology Review Brasil.









  


Assine a MIT Technology Review Brasil e leia a edição Alimentos para entender como ciência e tecnologia estão redefinindo saúde, biologia e longevidade.



Avenida Rio Branco, 108 10º andar - Centro CEP: 20040-001

TCU rejeita recurso da Mongeral contra licitação da Funpresp-Exe

O Tribunal de Contas da União (TCU) decidiu na última quarta-feira (25/3) rejeitar, por unanimidade, pedido da Mongeral Aegon Seguros e Previdência contra a licitação que resultou na contratação da Icatu Seguros pela Funpresp-Exe para ofertar cobertura por invalidez e morte aos participantes dos planos ExecPrev e LegisPrev. É a segunda vez que a corte rejeita o pleito da seguradora...


Vivest supera objetivos de retorno em todos os planos, em 2025

A Vivest, maior fundo de pensão de patrocínio privado do país, encerrou 2025 com desempenho acima dos objetivos de retorno dos 25 planos de previdência que administra, de diferentes modalidades. A rentabilidade média dos planos de Benefício Definido (BD) foi de 11,5%, enquanto nos planos de Contribuição Definida (CD) ficou em de 14,2%...


Ibovespa recua 1,45% com tensões em alta; dólar sobe

O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta quinta-feira (26/3) em queda de 1,45%, aos 182.732,67 pontos, refletindo a piora do ambiente externo diante da escalada das tensões no Oriente Médio e da ausência de avanços nas negociações. O movimento esteve em linha com as bolsas internacionais, que também recuaram em meio ao aumento da aversão ao risco global, com o Dow Jones perdendo 1,01%, o S&P 500 recuando 1,74% e o Nasdaq caindo 2,38%...


Fundiágua reconduz diretor financeiro e empossa conselheiros

O diretor financeiro da Fundiágua, Rodrigo Araújo, foi reconduzido ao cargo para mais um mandato de quatro anos. Engenheiro elétrico com MBA em estratégia empresarial, Araújo é também AETQ da entidade onde está desde junho de 2021. Anteriormente esteve por 10 anos na Funcef, atuando como coordenador de mercado de capitais...

Sender: architect-newsletter@mail.infoq.com
Date: 2026-03-27 09:15:00+00:00
Subject: The Software Architects' Newsletter March 2026

[InfoQ Live Logo]

The Software Architects' Newsletter
March 2026

Welcome to the InfoQ Software Architects' Newsletter! We bring you essential news and experience on emerging patterns and technologies from industry peers each month.


This newsletter theme explores software architecture through multiple lenses, including technical, organizational, human, economic, and operational. From sociotechnical systems to developer experience, governance, and AI-augmented design, it examines how different perspectives shape architectural decisions and long-term outcomes. Understanding these lenses helps architects reason more clearly about trade-offs, constraints, and changes at scale.

News

QCon London 2026: Kleppmann on Mitigating Europe's Cloud Dependency with Local-First Software



QCon London 2026: Morgan Stanley Rethinks Its API Program for the MCP Era



Platform Engineering as a Practice of Sociotechnical Excellence


Spec-Driven Development – Adoption at Enterprise Scale


In the short term, SDD adoption requires integration with existing workflows, support for brownfield projects (existing codebases without specifications), and the ability to enable sophisticated techniques incrementally. Over the long term, engineers need to develop an intuitive understanding of how to use SDD tools effectively. This understanding includes managing context effectively to avoid being overwhelmed by additional feedback loops and validation.

Software Evolution with Microservices and LLMs: A Conversation with Chris Richardson


They first explored the problems of monolithic enterprise software and how to use microservices to evolve them to enable fast flow. Then the discussion focused on the challenges of using generative artificial intelligence to understand an existing codebase, as well as the usefulness of artificial intelligence in system architecture.


Case Study

The Oil and Water Moment in AI Architecture


Traditional software is built on predictability, where the same input produces the same output. This predictability permits testing, debugging, and clear guarantees. AI systems, particularly those powered by LLMs, break this model. Their outputs are inherently variable, shaped by probabilities rather than fixed logic. The result is an "oil and water" moment, where two fundamentally different behaviors must operate within a single architecture.

The implication is a shift in design mindset. We are no longer engineering purely for correctness, but for bounded uncertainty. This shift defines acceptable outcomes rather than exact ones, and introduces guardrails such as validation layers, human-in-the-loop checks, and deterministic fallbacks when confidence is low.

Observability also evolves, with latency and error rates that are no longer sufficient. Architects must track output quality, drift, and alignment with user intent. In practice, tracking pushes teams toward tighter feedback loops and more continuous evaluation, closer to managing a sociotechnical system than a traditional service.

A pragmatic pattern is emerging. Rather than embedding AI deep within critical paths, teams encapsulate it behind well-defined interfaces. This approach helps contain unpredictability while still enabling high-value use cases such as summarization, recommendation, and decision support.

The takeaway is clear: AI is not just another component. It introduces a different operational model, and architects who succeed will embrace probabilistic thinking, design for uncertainty, and treat AI as a constrained collaborator rather than a deterministic service.




Sponsored



Struggling with brittle, hard-to-debug data pipelines and inconsistent orchestration across your stack? Learn how to design, build, and operate reliable workflows with Apache Airflow. This comprehensive eBook walks through DAG design, scheduling, testing, and production best practices—equipping architects to standardize data pipelines, improve observability, and scale data-driven systems with confidence.

New: Sir Simon Rattle conducts Haydn’s “Creation” in Lucerne

Sir Simon Rattle was once asked, “If you could only conduct one piece, which would it be?”. Without hesitation, he replied, Joseph Haydn’s Creation. For him, the oratorio is one of the “most perfect pieces, one of the wisest and wittiest” – and the epitome of the musical Enlightenment. Simon Rattle and the trio of soloists Elsa Dreisig, Mark Padmore and Florian Boesch performed this masterpiece at both the opening concert of his final season as chief conductor of the Berliner Philharmoniker, and also shortly afterwards at the 2017 Lucerne Festival.


Dmitri Shostakovich’s works reveal striking contrasts: he composed symphonies that reflect the oppressive reality of his life during the Stalin era, at times with profound seriousness and at others through the use of parody. At the same time, he composed playful film and ballet scores, as well as the Suite for Variety Orchestra, better known as the “Jazz Suite No. 2”. Members of the Berliner Philharmoniker bring this diversity to life here – in exquisite chamber music arrangements. Arnold Schoenberg’s String Trio forms a stylistic counterpoint.


Bruno Delepelaire is the focus of the latest release in the Soloists of the Berliner Philharmoniker series. The 1st principal cellist performs concertos by Édouard Lalo and Joseph Haydn with tremendous enthusiasm – and, as he recalls, was “carried along by a constantly palpable wave of energy” from his colleagues during the recording. You can now experience this enthusiasm and energy for yourself – the album is available to download and via streaming services.


Welcome the Berliner Philharmoniker into your home – on your TV, computer, tablet or smartphone. Live or on-demand from our archive.

                                                Digital Concert Hall vouchers


The Digital Concert Hall is the online concert venue of the Berliner Philharmoniker. Here, their concerts are broadcast live and are also available as recordings in a video archive a few days later. The Digital Concert Hall is available on televisions, computers, smartphones, tablets and streaming devices. Its streaming partner is Internet Initiative Japan Inc. (IIJ).

The DownloadSua dose sobre tecnologia e inovação

Por MIT Technology Review Brasil  • 27.03.26 

 
 Olá! Confira hoje, na newsletter The Download:

  O assédio online chegou a outro patamar com o avanço da tecnologia: um agente de Inteligência Artificial publicou um post em um blog difamando um engenheiro de software. O motivo? A contribuição do grande modelo de linguagem para a biblioteca Matplotlib foi recusada pelo humano.
 Enquanto isso, Meta e YouTube são multados por seu design viciante, a IA dedicada a automatizar a pesquisa científica é revisada pelos pares e uma pesquisa revela que a clonagem não produz cópias perfeitas.

  

 POR: GRACE HUCKINS

 Agente difama engenheiro de software e coloca assédio online na era da Inteligência Artificial

Texto foi publicado em blog após contribuição ser recusada na biblioteca Matplotlib
 O engenheiro de software Scott Shambaugh não pensou duas vezes quando negou o pedido de um agente de Inteligência Artificial para contribuir com o Matplotlib, uma biblioteca de software que ele ajuda a administrar. Como muitos projetos de código aberto, ela tem sido sobrecarregada por uma enxurrada de contribuições e, por isso, Shambaugh e os colegas mantenedores instituíram uma política: todo código escrito por IA deve ser revisado e submetido à avaliação de um humano. Ele rejeitou o pedido e foi dormir.

  The Big Story


POR: RAFAELA GUIMARÃES

 Como proteger cérebros em formação: do ECA Digital ao redesenho da tecnologia na educação

Março 2026



 A presença da tecnologia na vida de crianças e adolescentes deixou de ser um fenômeno periférico para se tornar estruturante. Plataformas digitais, sistemas de recomendação e, mais recentemente, ferramentas baseadas em Inteligência Artificial passaram a mediar não apenas o entretenimento, mas também processos de socialização, aprendizagem e construção de identidade.
 Esse cenário impõe um desafio central para formuladores de políticas públicas, educadores e empresas de tecnologia sobre como ampliar o potencial educacional dessas ferramentas sem comprometer o desenvolvimento cognitivo, emocional e social de indivíduos em formação.

Subject: =?UTF-8?B?Q29sZcOnw6Nv?= Reserva + MIT Technology Review Brasil

Quando tecnologia e cultura também
aparecem na moda 


  Como a tecnologia influencia o que vestimos? Em um cenário marcado por avanços científicos, Inteligência Artificial e novas dinâmicas de trabalho, o vestuário também passa a refletir essas transformações. Referências digitais, estéticas futuristas e repertórios ligados à inovação começam a ocupar espaço no design e na forma como interpretamos o presente.
 Esse diálogo aparece na estampa EST MIT Shaping The Future, criada na parceria entre a Reserva e a MIT Technology Review Brasil. O design incorpora elementos associados ao imaginário da inovação e à estética que emerge da cultura tecnológica, conectando criatividade, pensamento contemporâneo e novas linguagens visuais.
 A proposta se aproxima da perspectiva editorial da MIT Technology Review Brasil, que investiga como ciência, tecnologia e cultura moldam a sociedade. Nesse contexto, o vestir também se torna uma forma de expressão desse ambiente em constante transformação.
Explore a coleção e descubra como a estampa EST MIT Shaping The Future traduz inovação e criatividade em linguagem visual.

The great AI reorg
Firms that reshape around agents have smaller teams, fewer silos and a spectrum of jobs that only humans can do better than machines. Those human roles include direction-setting, validation and verification, and accountability. Small, nimble teams, like EV’s, are already on this trajectory.
Development 2.0
What Gill is saying is extraordinary. The East Asian Miracle helped teach a generation of development economists to treat “picking winners” as exceptional, risky and mostly non-replicable. In the new report, the World Bank says industrial policy is “more replicable than previously thought” in a world with higher educational attainment, better macro management and more scope for targeted tools like industrial parks and skills programs than for tariffs and subsidies.
AI, a lab partner
 The AI Scientist isn’t churning out breakthroughs on demand. But this alpha test shows AI can conduct some science, not just echo it.

Hot Cross Buns (
)

(
)

Hot cross buns are soft, lightly sweet, spiced yeast rolls
studded with raisins and finished with the signature cross piped
on top. The name always makes me think of the nursery rhyme—and
my kids learning to play it on the recorder at school (when my
daughter struggled, my son suggested she just pretend to play
along with the class so the teacher wouldn’t know). They have
just enough sweetness to feel like a treat, and while they’re
traditionally associated with Easter, they’re just as welcome at
breakfast or with an afternoon cup of coffee.

Sender: dataengineeringweekly@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-30 02:18:14+00:00
Subject: Data Engineering Weekly #263

How data teams are solving multi-tenancy
As data teams grow and serve multiple teams, clients, or business units from a shared platform, maintaining isolation and velocity without sacrificing either becomes a defining architectural challenge.
In this Deep Dive, Dagster Labs and Brooklyn Data Co. will cover the patterns, trade-offs, and real-world implementations behind multi-tenant data platforms built on Dagster. Attendees will leave this session with practical guidance they can take back to their own teams.
Aurimas Griciūnas: State of Context Engineering in 2026
LLM reasoning degrades with oversized context, forcing developers to manage attention through structured context engineering rather than scaling model size. The author outlines five patterns—progressive disclosure, compression, routing, agentic RAG, and tool management—that control how context is selected and applied. Layered orchestration across discovery, activation, and execution enables complex agent behavior within fixed context limits while preserving reasoning quality.
Joe Reis: AI Is Here, But The Hard Parts Haven’t Changed
Hamel Husain: The Revenge of the Data Scientist
LLM API accessibility enables rapid AI feature development but obscures reliability requirements grounded in evaluation and experimental design. The author argues modern AI development reuses core data science practices: analyzing production traces, validating LLM-as-judge with precision and recall, grounding test sets in real data, and using domain experts to define criteria. Teams that avoid synthetic benchmarks and over-automation focus on inspecting data to identify failure modes, reinforcing the role of data scientists as reliability gatekeepers.
Sponsored: The Data Platform Fundamentals Guide
We wrote an eBook on Data Platform Fundamentals to help you be like the happy data teams, operating under a single platform. 
In this book, you’ll learn:
- How composable architectures allow teams to ship faster
- Why data quality matters and how you can catch issues before they reach users
- What observability means, and how it will help you solve problems more quickly
Figma: Redefining impact as a data scientist
Data science impact in mission-critical systems like billing depends on domain expertise and observability rather than experimentation, shifting focus from models to correctness and clarity. The author describes Figma’s full-stack approach, where data scientists build consistency checks, create applications that explain system behavior, and define correctness criteria. Embedding these practices into operational systems scales their impact through tools rather than reports.
BlaBlaCar: Beyond the dashboard: how BlaBlaCar PMs use AI to self-serve data
Data analyst bottlenecks in fast-moving organizations require enabling non-technical users to self-serve without compromising data integrity or introducing hallucinations. BlaBlaCar evolves its approach from generic LLM usage to structured JSON schema documentation and few-shot learning on expert query histories, teaching the system to map natural language to business rules. A three-zone autonomy framework—safe, risky, and dead zones—combined with SQL literacy training for PMs reduces error rates from 32% to 15% and shifts analysts from reactive ticket handling to strategic work.
Expedia: Operating Trino at Scale With Trino Gateway
Managing Trino at scale requires isolating workloads to prevent resource contention across analytical, ETL, and BI queries. Expedia writes about operating Trino Gateway—a fork of Lyft's Presto Gateway—as a single-endpoint proxy that routes queries to dedicated clusters using configurable rules. This design eliminates noisy-neighbor failures, supports zero-downtime deployments, and provides real-time visibility into cluster health.
LangChain: How we build evals for Deep Agents
Building reliable AI agents requires evals that target specific production behaviors rather than optimizing for aggregate benchmark scores. LangChain's Deep Agents harness defines behavior-first evals sourced from production errors, BFCL, and hand-written unit tests — then scores agents on correctness and Ideal Trajectory ratios for step and tool-call efficiency. Teams run tagged eval subsets via pytest in GitHub Actions and trace every run in LangSmith to isolate failure modes and control evaluation cost.
LinkedIn: The LinkedIn Generative AI Application Tech Stack: Personalization with Cognitive Memory Agent
AI agents lose personalization across sessions because they lack structured memory that separates conversational, episodic, semantic, and procedural signals. LinkedIn’s Cognitive Memory Agent ingests activity traces through streaming and batch pipelines, then uses an LLM-based orchestrator to retrieve and reason across all four memory layers. This architecture enables the Hiring Assistant to auto-populate role requirements and generate recruiter-specific insights from historical hiring activity.
Ilia Gusev: Change Data Capture: Stop Copying 50M Rows to Move 5K Changes
Nightly full-table copies introduce fragility and place increasing load on source databases as data volumes scale. The article contrasts timestamp, trigger, and log-based CDC approaches, recommending Debezium with Postgres WAL or MySQL binlog as the production standard for near-real-time replication without impacting OLTP performance. Log-based CDC captures hard deletes, handles DDL changes, and decouples replication throughput from transactional write load.
Micheal Lanham: The Markdown File That Beat a $50M Vector Database
Agentic workflows expose the cost and operational overhead of managed vector databases when used for single-threaded memory and state management. The author shows how Manus, OpenClaw, and Claude Code converge on Markdown files as the primary memory layer, leveraging KV-cache efficiency, filesystem hierarchy for scoped retrieval, and sqlite-vec for lightweight semantic search. This file-first architecture reduces token costs by nearly 10x and defers vector database adoption to scenarios that require multi-user concurrency.
All rights reserved, Dewpeche Private Limited. I have provided links for informational purposes and do not suggest endorsement. All views expressed in this newsletter are my own and do not represent current, former, or future employers’ opinions.

Sender: roddreher@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-30 09:50:59+00:00
Subject: Israel's Self-Sabotage

You all know very well that I am a strong backer of Israel, which is not such an easy thing to be these days. Christians living in Israel are far safer than Christians living in most Islamic lands. In the past few days, Islamists have carried out anti-Christian pogroms in Syria. Not a peep from most American Christians about this. It’s unjust, and it’s hypocritical.
The Israelis aren’t stupid. They can see how more and more people in the US, their indispensable ally, are turning against Israel. I hate this, because despite its sins and failings, the Jewish state deserves our support. I don’t want to argue that here today — it would be endless, and we on both sides have said all we can say — but I am simply laying that down as a marker to emphasize that I’m on Israel’s side.
That’s why the actions of Jerusalem police over the weekend were so insanely frustrating. If you haven’t heard, they forbade Cardinal Pierbattista Pizzaballa, the Latin patriarch of Jerusalem (that is, the leader of the Holy Land’s Roman Catholics), from entering the Church of the Holy Sepulcher on Palm Sunday to celebrate the mass. 
As you will recall, Israel closed off all the holy sites, of all religions for the time being, for safety’s sake. This makes sense, given that the Iranians have targeted the Old City. The most important church in Christendom, full of Christian celebrating the holiest days of the year, would be a tempting target for the Iranians. Besides which, as a Christian who has prayed at that site — built over both the hill on which Jesus died, and the tomb from which he rose to life — I do not want to see it blown up by Shia zealots.
But it is impossible to understand why a cardinal and a small group of priest should be denied access on Palm Sunday. Yet that’s what happened! Card. Pizzaballa, whom I met in Jerusalem in 2022, and who is a serene, sensible, diplomatic man, said afterward:
“It is true that the police had said that the orders from the internal command prevented any kind of gathering in places where there is no shelter, but we had not asked for anything public, just a brief and small private ceremony to preserve the idea of the celebration in the Holy Sepulchre. There were no clashes; everything was done in a very polite manner. I do not want to force the issue; we want to use this situation to try to clarify better in the coming days what to do, in respect for everyone's safety but also in respect for the right to prayer."
Italian PM Giorgia Meloni was more blunt:
“Preventing the Patriarch of Jerusalem and the Custos of the Holy Land from entering, especially on a solemnity as central to the faith as Palm Sunday, constitutes an offense not only to believers, but to every community that recognizes religious freedom"
Even US Ambassador Mike Huckabee, who is religiously pro-Israel (in both senses of the term), spoke out:
Netanyahu did the correct thing in response to all this:
He should also apologize, but I don’t really care about that. What I care about is that our clergy — this policy also covers Orthodox clergy, as well as Copts — are allowed into the most holy of Christian sites to celebrate Paschal services. I’m pleased that it will happen now, but as a matter of its own strategic interests, Israel cannot afford incidents like this. As Amb. Huckabee said, this egregious mistake is having “major repercussions” around the world. It was entirely avoidable, too. 
I said here last week that if the Iran war goes wrong, we can expect a contemporary version of the “stab in the back legend” — the conspiracy theory originating in the German high command, to scapegoat others for its loss in World War I. German leaders spread the story that Germany would have won the war if not for traitorous Jews and socialists back home. But there was no truth to it at all. In fact, many German Jews served honorably in their country’s army.
By contrast, though an American loss in the Iran war will be America’s fault — nobody held a gun to Donald Trump’s head and forced him to do this — it cannot be denied that if not for Israel’s lobbying, we almost certainly wouldn’t be here. True, the sycophant whispers in Trump’s ear of the very Gentile US Senator Lindsey Graham certainly have played a role. Nevertheless, unlike Germany in the first World War, it is undeniable that Israeli lobbying influenced Trump. 
To be clear: Donald Trump is a grown man, responsible for his own decisions. If the war fails, the fault will be his. You may be perfectly sure that if it results in a victory for the US, somehow, he will claim credit. Therefore, if this turns into a disaster, he must also bear blame. 
Nevertheless, not only will Israel be blamed by millions, those who are in a personality cult relationship to Trump will embrace a stab in the back legend, for the sake of transferring their rage and disappointment to a scapegoat. This will happen, human nature being what it is. Israel needs all the friends and supporters in America that it can muster. It cannot afford stupid mistakes like what was done to the cardinal and his small team. Nor can it afford to give these wicked settlers free reign to beat up and abuse Arab Christians. 
The Israeli leadership might not give a toss about Arab Christian villagers in the West Bank, and frankly, to their shame, very few American Christians have ever shown much real concern for the fate of Arab Christians all over the Middle East, anywhere. But all eyes are on Israel now, and if Netanyahu knows what’s good for himself and the country he leads, he will take decisive action against the settler bullies, and make sure that the American people see him doing so. 
Last night I did a phone interview with a DC-based reporter for the New Yorker, who is working on a piece about the rise of the Groypers among Gen Z conservative elites in and around the Trump administration. She told me that it’s been common for a while now for her to go to DC cocktail parties and to hear Holocaust jokes. She affirmed my insight that older conservatives like me are truly unaware of how bad this is among that generation, in DC. She said in her reporting, talking to these Groypers, the only group they hate as much as they do the Jews are Boomers — especially Trump, who they see as a failed Boomer. 
The times are evil. They require intelligence, discernment, and wisdom. More wisdom than both the US and Israeli governments are demonstrating right now. Israel in particular is playing into a narrative popular among Americans who hate her. It is incumbent on Israel’s Christian friends — like me — to say so. To speak with Trumpian bluntness, you can’t shit on your foreign friends and allies during the holiest week of their year without expecting blowback — especially if you are granting privileges to Jewish clergy that you deny to those of other faiths who share the holy city.

Sender: exponentialview@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-30 15:19:10+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?=F0=9F=93=88_Data_to_start_your_week:_The_AI_buildout?=

Hi all,
As regular readers of Exponential View know, the AI economy is scaling exponentially. Its physical dependencies – power, water, land, permits – do not. The gap between those curves defines the next phase of the build-out.
We dig into the numbers behind this gap in today’s Data edition.
The mainstream has long picked the wrong fight over water. Golf courses in the US consume more than 30x as much water as the entire country’s data center industry uses for cooling.
The pledges will help, but they address a resource that data centers use far less of than is commonly assumed. The externality that communities actually live inside is harder to offset with a corporate commitment: heat and jobs.
These concerns, concentrated in the communities hosting the infrastructure, have sparked something unusual in American politics right now – organized, bipartisan resistance that makes local democracies relevant again. 
This is an issue that’s bringing us all together and that, I think, gives me hope.
Thanks for reading!

Sender: nathan@productivitygame.com
Date: 2026-03-31 00:25:30+00:00
Subject: [Productivity Game] SUMMARY: Speed Reading

"Skilled readers almost never read a text cold. Instead, they
examine it first with preview." – Kam Knight
In this summary, you'll learn two pre-reading habits that will
significantly increase your reading speed. You'll also learn to
see words on a page in a whole new way.

(
)​
​Book Summary Video (
)​

​1-Page PDF Summary (
)​

Favorite Takeaway:

Most of us read word by word. Each word is an anchor we lock
onto, hold for longer than we need to, and then release before
locking onto the next word. We repeat this hundreds of times per
page without realizing it. Author Kam Knight breaks this habit by
looking at the spaces between the words while he reads. It seems
like a trivial shift until you try it.

You may find that you start gliding through the passage much
faster than you would normally. When you look at the spaces or
imaginary dashes between words, you're using your peripheral
vision to absorb the surrounding words. Studies show the brain
processes information from peripheral vision 25% faster than from
direct vision.

Have a productive week!

Nathan

O movimento reflete, entre outros fatores, a elevação das incertezas no cenário internacional, em meio ao agravamento do conflito no Oriente Médio”...


Previc quer ajustes em documentos da incoporação da Elos

A Superintendência Nacional de Previdência Complementar (Previc) solicitou à Elos, fundo de pensão dos participantes das empresas da Eletrosul, ajustes na documentação referente à sua incorporação pela Fundação EletrobrasPrev. A autarquia também pediu correções pontuais no material apresentado, relacionadas principalmente à padronização de textos, atualização e conferência de dados, além de aspectos ligados à comunicação e transparência com os participantes, bem como a inclusão de assinaturas adicionais...


Ibovespa sobe e dólar avança com tensão externa

O Ibovespa, principal índice da B3, fechou nesta segunda-feira (30) em alta de 0,53%, aos 182.514,20 pontos, refletindo um movimento de recuperação após duas quedas consecutivas, mesmo diante das incertezas envolvendo o aumento das tensões entre Estados Unidos, Israel e Irã. O desempenho positivo ocorreu em meio a um cenário externo ainda instável e destoou parcialmente das bolsas de Nova York, que fecharam mistas no dia, com Dow Jones subindo 0,11%, S&P 500 recuando 0,39% e Nasdaq caindo 0,73%...


Família Prev apresenta política de investimentos até 2030

A Família Prev, fundo de pensão multipatrocinado gaúcho, aprovou sua nova Política de Investimentos (PI) para o período de 2026 a 2030. Segundo a fundação, a revisão foi baseada em estudos técnicos de ALM (casamento de ativos e passivos) e de fronteira eficiente, que apontaram a priorização de títulos públicos indexados à inflação nos planos de Benefício Definido (BD) e a ampliação da diversificação, incluindo investimentos no exterior, nos planos de Contribuição Definida (CD)...

O valor do mandato não foi divulgado...

AWS introduced account-regional namespaces for S3, fixing global bucket name collisions that broke IaC automation for 18 years. The new format is {prefix}-{account-id}-{region}-an. CloudFormation gets the BucketNamePrefix property, and IAM gets the s3:x-amz-bucket-namespace condition key. This prevents confused-deputy attacks by making names unpredictable when there is no account ID. (News)

Digital sovereignty is about maintaining control of critical systems by limiting reliance on any single vendor. Sender: newworkinphilosophy@substack.com
Date: 2026-03-31 12:22:22+00:00
Subject: =?UTF-8?q?"Transformative_Aesthetic_Dimensions_in_Young_Boys'_War_Play:_E?=
 =?UTF-8?q?xploring_the_World_Through_Kinesthetic_Musicality"_=E2=80=A6?=

Prologue
It took me by surprise that our sons began engaging in war play at such a young age, already when they were around two years old. Despite what we considered a peaceful and “gender-neutral” upbringing, they gravitated toward violent scenarios and toys. Initially, I attempted to stop the play, but without success.
Recurring themes included superheroes, knights, battles with different kinds of toy or fictive weapons, Star Wars, Spider-Man, Ninja Turtles, Batman, the Incredible Hulk, as well as pirates and other combat-related characters. I observed a similar dominance of play involving strong forces, particularly among boys I encountered both in my work in preschools and schools and in my free time. I was critical and sceptical of war play from several perspectives. For example, there appeared to be a commercial interplay between the toy industry and popular culture, and war play seemed to represent masculine ideals that I feared could be normalised through this violent behaviour. What seemed to unite parents and educators was that we often felt provoked, embarrassed, angry, ambivalent, and frustrated when confronted with these seemingly unstoppable games. With mixed feelings of concern and a sense that something was not quite right, I began to observe more attentively how the adult world responds to children who engage in war play.
Most educators and parents I spoke to expressed disgust toward this type of play, ignored it entirely, or communicated their disapproval in other ways. I also discovered that it is very common for preschools and schools to adopt zero-tolerance policies toward war play. Educators in one municipality where I worked for a period even told me that their social services and the police advised preschools and schools to ban war play completely for preventive purposes. On the internet, I found numerous blogs, parenting magazines, and online forums where anxious and frustrated educators and parents discussed the issue. During these early explorations, I realized that the hostility toward young children’s war play was much greater than I had expected. This harshness was mainly directed towards boys. The combined force of this adult negativity eventually became overwhelming for me and made me wonder how such attitudes might be experienced by the children themselves. I tried to imagine what it would be like to encounter this kind of response from the adult world for an entire day, a week, a year, or even several years. I could find almost no examples of anyone defending war play, apart from “let-it-be” arguments such as “boys will be boys” or claims that nothing could be done about it. Above all, I never encountered the idea that war play might contain something meaningful or interesting and began to question whether this one-sided approach overlooked important aspects of the play.
These questions led me to seek answers from the children themselves. Instead of turning away when they played war or action games, I began filming them in order to observe the play more closely. What I saw surprised me. For example, the children were extremely careful not to touch one another during jumps, kicks, and fencing movements. They seemed able to calculate the distance to a friend with a margin of only a few centimetres – or less – while performing complex and dynamic movements. Movement appeared to be the most central and attractive aspect in early childhood war play (2021).
Introduction
Rough and tumble play, action play, superhero play and war play are distinguished by children using toy weapons, powerful words and emotions, a lot of movement and by playing a war or some kind of fight. It is not about real fights, but about pretend fights. According to previous research on war play I have found, it is mostly boys who are drawn to war play and therefore it is mostly boys who encounter prohibition, disinterest and even aggression from adults (Knutsdotter Olofsson, 2003; Malloy & Mc Murray-Schwartz, 2004; Tullgren, 2004; Halvars-Franzén, 2010; Lagerström-Dyrssen, 2018;
Niu, 2018). Despite this, boys continue to play war or hero-related games, generation after generation. Why is their passion for war play so strong? In both upbringing and education, we have a hard time getting out of habitual patterns. This leads to stereotypes being maintained, no matter how much we
want and try to get away from them. Does this one-sided negative adult view of war play risk alienating boys from our communities and is there a risk that something is lost if we never see any creative aspects that war play generates? War play is a complex phenomenon and there are of course aspects that can be problematic, just like with other kinds of play. It raises questions
that extend from private family relationships to major societal issues that concern how destructive masculinity norms are created and maintained. War play is played from generation to generation, but the findings in these studies suggest that in each transfer, each repetition, variations of its meaning and expression are transformed. What kind of new meaning do boys three to nine years old invent in their physical war play?
War play is a complex phenomenon that raises questions ranging from family relationships to broader societal concerns about violence and masculinity. Dodd (1992) warns that war play may normalize violence but also argues that children cannot be completely shielded from violent influences. Even without direct exposure, children encounter violence through the internet, movies, television, and computer games, which may lead them to incorporate aggression into their play. Therefore, teachers and parents need to understand war play better and develop strategies for managing violent elements within children’s play (Dodd, 1992). Research shows that war play is primarily practiced by boys, who therefore more often encounter adult disapproval, prohibition, or interference (Knutsdotter Olofsson, 2003). Despite this, war- and superhero-themed play continues across generations (Malloy & McMurray-Schwarz, 2004). Adults often respond in two contrasting ways: some accept it as a natural part of growing up, while others – especially in Sweden – view it as destructive and attempt to prohibit it. This raises questions about whether alternative approaches are possible and whether the creative aspects of war play are being overlooked. War play typically involves toy weapons, strong emotions, expressive language, and intense movement in simulated fights (Levin & Carlsson-Paige, 2006). Many adults in Sweden and elsewhere view such play with suspicion (Jelleyman et al., 2019). Although it involves pretend rather than real violence, it often worries parents and teachers (Rasmussen, 1992). Consequently, strict rules and zero-tolerance policies are common in schools and preschools. These restrictions partly reflect broader social concerns about violence, including the involvement of increasingly young boys in gang criminality in Sweden (BRÅ, 2024). However, such prohibitions may risk alienating boys and weakening their trust in adults and educational institutions.
Making Room for Children’s Perspectives
War play is transmitted across generations, but children reinterpret it each time. What meanings do young boys create in their physical war play in this period of time? To recognize new meanings, we must understand how they emerge. Wartofsky (1973) describes perception as plastic: representations and ways of seeing the world change over time. New worlds become visible when existing representations lose their dominance. To better understand children’s perspectives, I filmed boys engaging in war play. Early observations revealed that their movements were expressive and skilful and that they carefully avoided harming one another despite the dynamic physical activity. Because movement appeared central to the play, I chose to analyse it through a dance-theoretical framework, which revealed unexpectedly aesthetic qualities.
Movement Categories in War Play
I employed film ethnography as method, and the analysis was inspired by Grounded Theory. The filmed sequences included boys playing physical war play, influenced by fictional narratives such as Star Wars or Spiderman and games such as World of Warcraft and Minecraft. The movement patterns in these films and games are often originally inspired by movement from the Martial Arts, but the children seem to transform them further. The analysis initially produced forty-six movement categories, which were later organized into six broader categories connected through one core concept: kinesthetic musicality. The movement categories from war play in this study was formulated as follows:
Rhythm
Children create rhythmic patterns in their play, often imitating one another and producing short choreographies through repetition, variation, and improvisation.
Orchestrating Space
Children interact sensitively with the spatial environment. Objects and terrain become meaningful elements of play – a hole becomes a “death trap,” and a tree becomes a lookout tower. Space is not only a backdrop to the play.
Fictional Characters
Choosing a character also means adopting specific movement qualities. Characters such as old and small Yoda, the robot R2-D2, or the evil, heavily breathing Darth Vader offers distinctive, characteristic movement for children to imitate, explore and transform.
Movement Canon
Certain recurring movements form a shared repertoire, including pirouettes, fencing gestures, robot movements, playing dead, and using “the Force” (the outreached arm from Star Wars).
Phrases
When asked individually to demonstrate their actions in the play, the children transformed movements into improvised dance-like solo sequences involving jumps, kicks, and spins.
Aesthetic Attention
Children focus closely on the sensory and expressive qualities of movement, sound, and space, sometimes slowing movements down in order to refine them.
Kinaesthetic Musicality
Together, these categories contribute to a central, overlapping concept of kinaesthetic musicality. Analysing war play through dance theoretical frame, reveals a strong bodily sensitivity. The concept of kinaesthetic musicality describes children’s ability to explore and create relationships with the world through movement. It combines kinaesthesia – the sense of bodily movement – with musicality understood as an embodied awareness of rhythm, timing, and expression.
In this expanded sense, musicality is not limited to audible music but involves the coordination of sensation, movement, breath, and perception. Similar ideas appear in Ravn’s (2009) concept of “incorporated music,” in which dancers perceive music through their bodies and muscles. Children appear to use a comparable bodily awareness in everyday life. Infants rocking to the rhythm of household sounds or children moving with natural phenomena illustrate this embodied resonance with the world. Kinaesthetic musicality functions as a bridge between the child and the environment, enabling sensitive and creative interaction. In a demanding play such as war play, this sensitivity helps children coordinate movement, anticipate others’ actions, and avoid injury. To sustain the play, they must understand balance, timing, spatial relationships, and bodily limits. The tension and risk involved intensify their focus and sensory awareness.
The Aesthetic Energy of War Play
War play exists on a threshold between violence and aesthetics. Many of its movements originate in films, games, and martial arts traditions. Martial arts such as capoeira or aikido combine physical discipline with aesthetic and choreographic dimensions. Over time, practices such as kung fu and karate have evolved from combat training into sports or artistic forms (Ölme, 2013). These aesthetic layers may resonate with children more strongly than violent intentions. The movements often begin as imitation – such as fencing – but develop into explorations of balance, rhythm, and precision. Children may transform aggressive energy into aesthetic experimentation, illustrating Corsaro’s (2012) concept of interpretive reproduction, in which children adapt cultural material to their own concerns. Understanding movement also requires examining perception. Hämäläinen (2007) argues that perception is active and connected to action. Dancers develop rapid interaction between inner sensations and external perception—what she calls “bodily wisdom.” Philosophers such as Deleuze and Guattari (2015) describe perception as a dynamic encounter between subject and world. Improvisation, according to Deleuze and Guattari, involves risking engagement with the world and blending with it. Similarly, Manning (2012) describes bodies as rhythms in continuous interaction with their environments. In this perspective, movement and thought are inseparable. Sensations, memory, and anticipation merge as the body responds to the world. Manning (2012) describes this process as preacceleration, the gathering momentum that precedes movement.
Force Taking Form
Such body–world interactions are particularly visible in early childhood exploration. Children appear to have a more immediate bodily connection to their surroundings. Their kinesthetic musicality allows them to sense forces and potential movements before they fully take form – in gestures, sounds, or physical actions. War play provides an intense setting in which this sensitivity becomes especially visible. Through these interactions, children transform cultural expressions of violence into new forms of movement and meaning.
Conclusion
This study proposes kinaesthetic musicality as a theory describing young children’s embodied sensitivity to rhythm, movement, and environment. The concept highlights how children explore the world through physical and aesthetic engagement. Understanding this embodied musicality may help adults rethink their approaches to war play. Instead of focusing solely on prohibition, educators and parents might recognize how children transform cultural expressions creatively. In times marked by war and gender-based violence, I refer to hooks (2004) that calls for the necessity of creating new visions of masculinity informed by feminist perspectives, and I suggest also learning from children’s transformations of stereotyped expressions to develop these new visions. By engaging constructively with children’s embodied creativity, educators may support more inclusive learning environments and address challenges faced by many boys in educational systems worldwide (The Economist, 2022). A greater sensibility, attention and curiosity towards new generations, sometimes subtle, invitations and suggestions on how to challenge and rebuild stereotyped expressions can open for new ethical dimensions to explore together. To begin with – instead of automatically prohibiting children’s war play, we can observe it for a second longer, and try to identify what is actually being explored. A pre-school teacher that is exploring these findings together with her colleagues, recently told me that their broader, more informed gaze on physical war play, has started also to change their approaches and actions towards the war playing children, that they used to see as trouble makers.
Link to my homepage at the University of Stockholm:
ebba.theorell@buv.su.se [ mailto:ebba.theorell@buv.su.se ]
